topics.title: Features of 8051 Microcontroller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Features of 8051 Microcontroller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Intel 8051 Microcontroller** is an **8-bit single-chip microcontroller** introduced by Intel Corporation in 1980. It integrates a Central Processing Unit (CPU), Random Access Memory (RAM), Read Only Memory (ROM), Input/Output (I/O) ports, timers/counters, a serial communication port (UART), and an interrupt controller onto a single Integrated Circuit (IC) package using **Harvard Architecture** (separate program and data memory buses).

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBECT404, Module 1)**, the 8051 is studied as the foundational 8-bit embedded controller that forms the architectural basis for the entire MCS-51 family (e.g., 8052, 8031, AT89C51, AT89S52 by Atmel/Microchip).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 belongs to the *MCS-51 family* of microcontrollers. It uses a **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** design philosophy and operates on an 8-bit data bus with a 16-bit address bus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **fully furnished mini-apartment** versus an empty plot of land:
- A **microprocessor** (like the 8086) is the empty plot — you must build the kitchen (RAM), storage (ROM), plumbing (I/O), and electrical wiring (timers) externally.
- A **microcontroller** like the 8051 is the *fully furnished mini-apartment* — the kitchen, storage, wiring, and plumbing are all built in. You just plug it in, give it a program, and it starts working.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** "Microcontroller = CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals, all on one chip." This integration is what makes the 8051 ideal for embedded control applications like washing machines, microwave ovens, and automotive dashboards.

### 1.3 High-Level Block Summary

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Architecture | Harvard |
| Data Bus Width | **8-bit** |
| Address Bus Width | **16-bit** |
| On-chip ROM | **4 KB** |
| On-chip RAM | **128 Bytes** |
| I/O Ports | **4 × 8-bit (P0, P1, P2, P3)** |
| Timers/Counters | **2 × 16-bit** |
| Serial Port | **1 × Full-Duplex UART** |
| Interrupt Sources | **5** |
| Clock Frequency | **12 MHz** (typical) |
| Instruction Set | **CISC**, 255 opcodes, 1/2-byte instructions |
| External Memory | 64 KB Code + 64 KB Data |
| Special Feature | **Bit-addressable Boolean Processor** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The "Why" and "How"

The 8051 is engineered as a **self-sufficient embedded controller**. Below is the structural logic of its key features, broken down into the *core reason* each block exists.

#### 2.1.1 Central Processing Unit (CPU)
- **Why:** Needs an intelligent brain to fetch, decode, and execute instructions.
- **How:** Built around an **8-bit ALU** supported by an **Accumulator (A)**, a secondary register (**B**), a **Stack Pointer (SP)**, the **Program Counter (PC)**, and the **Data Pointer (DPTR)**.
- **Utility:** Used in industrial process controllers, smart sensors, and consumer electronics.

#### 2.1.2 Memory Organization (Harvard Architecture)
- **Why:** Separation of program and data memory allows simultaneous fetch of instruction and operand → faster execution.
- **How:**
  - **Program Memory (ROM):** 4 KB on-chip, expandable externally to **64 KB** via the 16-bit PC.
  - **Data Memory (RAM):** 128 bytes on-chip, expandable externally to **64 KB** via 16-bit DPTR.
- **Special:** The lower 128 bytes of RAM contain **32 working registers arranged in 4 banks** (Bank 0–Bank 3, each 8 bytes), addressable from address **0x00 to 0x1F**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-Addressable Memory:** Bytes from **0x20 to 0x2F** (16 bytes = 128 bits) are bit-addressable. This is the foundation of the 8051's famous **Boolean Processor**.

#### 2.1.3 Input/Output Ports
- **Why:** To interface external devices like LEDs, motors, keypads, and LCDs.
- **How:** Four bidirectional 8-bit ports, each with an internal **latch (SFR)** and **driver**.
  - **P0:** Used as multiplexed low-byte address/data bus (AD0–AD7) during external memory access; no internal pull-up.
  - **P1:** General-purpose I/O only.
  - **P2:** Provides high-byte address (A8–A15) during external memory access.
  - **P3:** Has **alternate functions** (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).

#### 2.1.4 Timers/Counters
- **Why:** To generate precise time delays or count external events without CPU intervention.
- **How:** Two 16-bit registers — **Timer 0 (TH0:TL0)** and **Timer 1 (TH1:TL1)** — each can operate in 4 modes:
  - **Mode 0:** 13-bit timer (legacy 8048 compatibility).
  - **Mode 1:** 16-bit timer (most commonly used).
  - **Mode 2:** 8-bit auto-reload timer.
  - **Mode 3:** Split timer (Timer 0 split into two 8-bit timers).

> [!NOTE]
> The timer clock = `Oscillator Frequency / 12`. A 12 MHz crystal gives a timer tick of **1 microsecond**.

#### 2.1.5 Serial Port (UART)
- **Why:** For asynchronous serial communication with PCs, other microcontrollers, and modules.
- **How:** A **full-duplex UART** implemented via the **SBUF** register and the **SCON** (Serial Control) SFR. Operates in **4 modes**, supporting variable baud rates (commonly 9600, 4800, 1200).

#### 2.1.6 Interrupt Subsystem
- **Why:** Allows the CPU to respond immediately to asynchronous hardware events.
- **How:** **5 interrupt sources** with a fixed priority order:
  1. **Reset** (highest, non-maskable)
  2. **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)**
  3. **Timer 0 Overflow**
  4. **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)**
  5. **Timer 1 Overflow**
  6. **Serial Port (TI/RI)** (lowest)

> [!NOTE]
> Two priority levels are supported via the **IP (Interrupt Priority)** SFR.

#### 2.1.7 Special Function Registers (SFRs)
- **Why:** To control peripherals, configure I/O, and hold CPU status flags.
- **How:** 128 bytes (only 21–26 are actively used) located in the upper RAM area from **0x80 to 0xFF**. Key SFRs include `A`, `B`, `PSW`, `SP`, `DPTR`, `P0–P3`, `TCON`, `TMOD`, `SCON`, `IE`, `IP`, `PCON`, `SBUF`, `TH0/TL0`, `TH1/TL1`.

#### 2.1.8 Boolean (Bit) Processor
- **Why:** Embedded control often requires manipulating individual bits (e.g., turning on a single motor).
- **How:** Dedicated hardware supporting single-bit instructions like `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C, /bit`, `ORL C, /bit`, and `MOV C, bit`. Operands are the 128 bit-addressable locations (0x20–0x2F) and the 16 bit-addressable SFRs.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Timer Tick Duration | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | Delay generation |
| Timer Delay (Mode 1) | $Delay = (65536 - Count) \times T_{tick}$ | seconds | Generating precise delays |
| Baud Rate (Mode 1 Timer 1 Auto-reload) | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bps | UART communication |
| External Code Memory Size | $2^{16} = 65536$ Bytes = **64 KB** | bytes | Maximum program space |
| External Data Memory Size | $2^{16} = 65536$ Bytes = **64 KB** | bytes | Maximum external RAM |
| On-chip RAM | $\vert 128 \vert$ Bytes | bytes | Working data memory |
| On-chip ROM | $\vert 4 \vert$ KB | bytes | Program storage |
| Bit-Addressable RAM | $\vert 16 \vert$ Bytes = $\vert 128 \vert$ Bits | bits | Boolean operations |
| Number of I/O Lines | $4 \times 8 = 32$ | lines | External interfacing |
| Machine Cycle | $12 \times T_{osc}$ | oscillator periods | Instruction timing |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 8051's feature set makes it the workhorse of:
- **Automotive:** Engine control units (older cars), dashboard clusters, anti-lock braking systems.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machines, microwave ovens, remote controls, TV tuners.
- **Industrial:** PLCs, motor drives, sensor signal conditioning.
- **Medical:** Blood pressure monitors, glucometers, infusion pumps.
- **IoT (modern derivatives):** AT89C51RE2, CC2530 (ZigBee SoC) — all are 8051-cores with wireless transceivers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Timer Delay Calculation (Mode 1 — 16-bit Timer)

**Problem:** Calculate the value to be loaded into TH and TL registers of Timer 0 to generate a delay of **50 milliseconds** using an **11.0592 MHz** crystal oscillator. The timer is configured in **Mode 1**.

**Step 1: Identify the timer tick duration.**

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 internally to drive the timers. Therefore:

$$
f_{timer} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12}
$$

$$
f_{timer} = 0.9216 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the period of one timer tick.**

$$
T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{timer}} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

$$
T_{tick} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{tick} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the number of timer counts required for 50 ms.**

$$
Count = \frac{Delay}{T_{tick}}
$$

$$
Count = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
Count = 46{,}082.949 \approx 46{,}082 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 4: Subtract from the maximum count to determine the preload (timer reload) value.**

In Mode 1, the timer is 16-bit, which means it can count from **0 to 65535** (a total of **65536** counts before overflow). The preload value is:

$$
Preload = 65{,}536 - Count
$$

$$
Preload = 65{,}536 - 46{,}082
$$

$$
Preload = 19{,}454
$$

**Step 5: Convert the preload value into the TH and TL byte values.**

The 16-bit preload is split into two 8-bit registers: `TH0` gets the high byte, `TL0` gets the low byte.

$$
19{,}454 \div 256 = 75 \text{ remainder } 254
$$

$$
TH0 = 75 = 0x4B
$$

$$
TL0 = 254 = 0xFE
$$

**Step 6: Verification.**

$$
Total\ Counts = 65{,}536 - 19{,}454 = 46{,}082
$$

$$
Delay = 46{,}082 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 0.04999 \, s \approx 50 \, ms
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The slight discrepancy (49.99 ms vs 50 ms) is because the timer can only count whole numbers. For higher accuracy, use an 11.0592 MHz crystal and round intelligently.

### 3.2 Derivation: Baud Rate Calculation for Serial Communication

**Problem:** Determine the value of **TH1** required to achieve a **9600 baud** rate using a **11.0592 MHz** crystal in UART Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud rate via Timer 1 auto-reload, Mode 2 of the timer).

**Step 1: Recall the standard UART Mode 1 baud rate formula.**

$$
Baud = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 2: Assume SMOD = 0 (default in PCON).**

$$
2^{SMOD} = 2^{0} = 1
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the known values and solve for TH1.**

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
(256 - TH1) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times 32 \times 9600}
$$

$$
(256 - TH1) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3{,}686{,}400}
$$

$$
(256 - TH1) = 3
$$

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why 11.0592 MHz crystals are the **de facto standard** in 8051 systems — they generate *exact* standard baud rates (2400, 4800, 9600, 19200) without fractional errors.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Feature-to-Application Mapping

| Feature | SFR / Register | Engineering Function | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 KB ROM | Code memory | Program storage | Washing machine cycle logic |
| 128 B RAM | Internal data | Variable storage | Sensor value buffering |
| Port 0 | P0 | Address/Data bus | Multiplexed with external memory |
| Port 1 | P1 | General I/O | LED indicators |
| Port 2 | P2 | High address byte | Address lines for external ROM |
| Port 3 | P3 | Alternate functions | UART, external interrupts |
| Timer 0 | TH0, TL0 | Time delay / event count | Traffic light sequencing |
| Timer 1 | TH1, TL1 | Baud rate generator | PC communication |
| UART | SBUF, SCON | Serial data transfer | GPS module interfacing |
| INT0, INT1 | TCON bits | Edge-triggered events | Emergency stop button |
| Boolean Processor | Bit instructions | Single-bit control | Relay driving |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 8051 Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["8-bit CPU ALU with Accumulator A and Register B"]
    PC["Program Counter PC 16-bit"]
    DPTR["Data Pointer DPTR 16-bit"]
    SP["Stack Pointer SP 8-bit"]
    ROM["On-chip ROM 4 KB Program Memory"]
    RAM["On-chip RAM 128 Bytes Data Memory"]
    SFR["SFR Block 128 Bytes 0x80 to 0xFF"]
    P0["Port 0 P0 8-bit Multiplexed Addr Data"]
    P1["Port 1 P1 8-bit General Purpose"]
    P2["Port 2 P2 8-bit High Address"]
    P3["Port 3 P3 8-bit Alternate Functions"]
    T0["Timer 0 16-bit TH0 TL0"]
    T1["Timer 1 16-bit TH1 TL1"]
    UART["Serial Port UART SBUF SCON"]
    INT["Interrupt Controller 5 Sources"]
    OSC["On-chip Oscillator"]
    CTRL["Control Unit and Timing"]

    OSC --> CTRL
    CTRL --> CPU
    CPU --> PC
    CPU --> DPTR
    CPU --> SP
    CPU --> ROM
    CPU --> RAM
    CPU --> SFR
    SFR --> P0
    SFR --> P1
    SFR --> P2
    SFR --> P3
    SFR --> T0
    SFR --> T1
    SFR --> UART
    SFR --> INT
    INT --> CPU
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Memory Map — Harvard Architecture Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PM["Program Memory Space 64 KB Max"]
        A1["0x0000 to 0x0FFF 4 KB On-chip ROM"]
        A2["0x1000 to 0xFFFF External Code Memory via PSEN"]
    end
    subgraph DM["Data Memory Space 64 KB Max"]
        B1["0x00 to 0x1F Register Banks R0 to R7 in 4 Banks"]
        B2["0x20 to 0x2F Bit Addressable 128 bits"]
        B3["0x30 to 0x7F General Purpose RAM"]
        B4["0x80 to 0xFF SFR Region 128 bytes"]
        B5["0x0000 to 0xFFFF External Data Memory via MOVX"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Interrupt Priority Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RES["RESET Highest Priority Non Maskable"]
    INT0["External Interrupt 0 INT0"]
    T0OV["Timer 0 Overflow TF0"]
    INT1["External Interrupt 1 INT1"]
    T1OV["Timer 1 Overflow TF1"]
    SER["Serial Port TI or RI Lowest Priority"]

    RES --> INT0 --> T0OV --> INT1 --> T1OV --> SER
```

```mermaid

    end
    subgraph PORT0["Port 0 Pins 32 to 39"]

    end

    end

    end
    subgraph PORT3["Port 3 Alternate Functions Pins 10 to 17"]
        P3L["P3.0 RxD P3.1 TxD P3.2 INT0 P3.3 INT1 P3.4 T0 P3.5 T1 P3.6 WR P3.7 RD"]
    end
    subgraph CTRL["Control Pins"]
        ALE["ALE Pin 30 Address Latch Enable"]
        PSEN["PSEN Pin 29 Program Store Enable"]
        EA["EA Pin 31 External Access"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any six salient features of the 8051 Microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**

The six salient features of the 8051 Microcontroller are:

1. **8-bit CPU** with a **16-bit address bus**, capable of accessing up to **64 KB** of both program and external data memory.
2. **On-chip memory:** **4 KB ROM** (program) and **128 bytes RAM** (data) integrated into a single chip.
3. **Four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) for interfacing with external devices.
4. **Two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1) that operate in 4 programmable modes for delay generation and event counting.
5. **Full-duplex UART** (serial port) for asynchronous communication with external peripherals at programmable baud rates.
6. **Five interrupt sources** with two priority levels, providing asynchronous event handling.
7. **Boolean (bit) processor** with dedicated single-bit instructions and 128 bit-addressable locations.

*(Any six points will be accepted; mention Harvard architecture for a 7th if needed.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the function of the PSW (Program Status Word) register in 8051.**

**Model Answer:**

The **PSW (Program Status Word)** is an 8-bit **Special Function Register** at address **0xD0** that holds the CPU status flags and selects the current register bank.

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| PSW.7 | CY (Carry) | Set on arithmetic carry/borrow |
| PSW.6 | AC (Auxiliary Carry) | Set on BCD-carry between bits 3 and 4 |
| PSW.5 | F0 | User-defined general-purpose flag |
| PSW.4–PSW.3 | RS1, RS0 | Register Bank Select (00=Bank 0, 01=Bank 1, 10=Bank 2, 11=Bank 3) |
| PSW.2 | OV | Overflow flag for signed arithmetic |
| PSW.1 | — | Reserved |
| PSW.0 | P | Parity flag — set if Accumulator has odd number of 1's |

**Significance:** PSW enables rapid context switching by changing RS1:RS0 and provides hardware-level flag information for decision-making (`JC`, `JNC`, `JZ`, etc.).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — Set 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **memory organization of 8051** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **Harvard and Von-Neumann architectures**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Harvard vs Von-Neumann Comparison**

| Parameter | Harvard | Von-Neumann |
|---|---|---|
| Bus structure | Separate code and data buses | Shared code and data bus |
| Simultaneous access | Yes (fetch + read) | No (single bus bottleneck) |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Memory type | Different ROM/RAM possible | Unified memory |
| Example | 8051, PIC, ARM (most) | 8086, Pentium |

**Step 2: 8051 Memory Map**

- **Program Memory:** 64 KB total
  - `0x0000 – 0x0FFF`: Internal 4 KB ROM
  - `0x1000 – 0xFFFF`: External ROM (accessed via PSEN)
  - `0x0000` is the **reset vector**.

- **Data Memory:** 64 KB total
  - **Lower 128 bytes (Internal RAM):**
    - `0x00 – 0x1F`: 32 bytes = 4 Register Banks × 8 bytes (R0–R7)
    - `0x20 – 0x2F`: 16 bytes = **128 bit-addressable** locations
    - `0x30 – 0x7F`: 80 bytes of general-purpose scratchpad RAM
  - **Upper 128 bytes (SFR region, 0x80 – 0xFF):** Contains A, B, PSW, SP, DPTR, P0–P3, TCON, TMOD, etc.
  - **External Data:** 0x0000–0xFFFF accessed via `MOVX` instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 uses a **unified address space for SFR and upper RAM** (both 0x80–0xFF), distinguished by addressing mode.

**Step 3: Why Harvard?**
- Eliminates contention between instruction fetch and data read.
- Allows different memory technologies (e.g., ROM for code, RAM for data) on the same chip.

---

**(b)** Describe the **I/O port structure of 8051**. Explain why **Port 0 requires external pull-up resistors**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: General Port Structure**
Each of the four 8-bit ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) consists of:
- An **SFR latch** (e.g., P0 at 0x80) that holds the output data.
- **Output driver** transistors.
- **Input buffer** that reads the physical pin state.
- A **pin control logic** that decides direction (read/write) and alternate function.

**Step 2: Port-wise Description**

- **Port 0 (Pins 32–39):** Open-drain outputs (no internal pull-up). Used as **multiplexed lower address/data bus (AD0–AD7)** during external memory access. During external access, the ALE signal latches the address into an external latch (e.g., 74HC573).

- **Port 1 (Pins 1–8):** Internal pull-ups present. Pure general-purpose I/O. No alternate functions.

- **Port 2 (Pins 21–28):** Internal pull-ups. Provides **high address byte (A8–A15)** during external memory access.

- **Port 3 (Pins 10–17):** Internal pull-ups. Each pin has a specific **alternate function**:
  - P3.0 = RxD, P3.1 = TxD
  - P3.2 = INT0, P3.3 = INT1
  - P3.4 = T0, P3.5 = T1
  - P3.6 = WR, P3.7 = RD

**Step 3: Why Port 0 needs external pull-ups**

Port 0 outputs use **open-drain transistors**. The internal FETs can only pull the pin **LOW**, not HIGH. To output a logic HIGH, an external **pull-up resistor (typically 4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ)** connected to VCC is mandatory.

Without the pull-up:
- Writing a `1` would leave the pin **floating** (high-impedance), not logic HIGH.
- Multiple open-drain outputs can share a bus safely (wired-AND logic).

Ports 1, 2, and 3 have **internal pull-ups**, so they do not need external resistors for general I/O.

---

**Question B — Set 2 (Alternative Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of 8051** and the function of each block. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Major Blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram for the visual).**

1. **ALU (8-bit):** Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷), logical (AND, OR, XOR, NOT), rotate, and Boolean operations.
2. **Accumulator (A):** Primary operand register for all ALU operations; address `0xE0`.
3. **B Register:** Used during MUL and DIV instructions; address `0xF0`.
4. **Program Counter (PC):** 16-bit, points to the next instruction; auto-increments; reset value = `0x0000`.
5. **Data Pointer (DPTR):** Two 8-bit SFRs (DPH `0x83` + DPL `0x82`) forming a 16-bit pointer for external data and look-up tables.
6. **Stack Pointer (SP):** 8-bit, points to the top of stack in internal RAM; default value after reset = `0x07`; address `0x81`.
7. **Program Memory (ROM):** 4 KB on-chip, expandable to 64 KB externally.
8. **Data Memory (RAM):** 128 bytes on-chip, expandable to 64 KB externally.
9. **SFR Block:** 128 bytes (0x80–0xFF) for peripheral control.
10. **Timers/Counters:** Two 16-bit timers for delays and counting.
11. **Serial Port (UART):** Full-duplex asynchronous communication.
12. **Interrupt Controller:** Handles 5 vectored interrupts.
13. **On-chip Oscillator:** Drives the CPU and timers; external crystal on XTAL1/XTAL2.
14. **I/O Ports:** P0, P1, P2, P3 for external interfacing.
15. **Control Unit:** Generates control signals (RD, WR, PSEN, ALE) and decodes opcodes.

**Step 2: Data Flow.**
- PC fetches instruction from ROM → decoded by Control Unit → ALU executes on A or memory operand → result stored back to A, RAM, or SFR.

---

**(b)** Explain the **Timer/Counter operation in 8051**. List the four modes of operation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Timer/Counters — Overview**
The 8051 has **two 16-bit Timers/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1) implemented using two pairs of 8-bit SFRs:
- Timer 0: `TH0` (`0x8C`) + `TL0` (`0x8A`)
- Timer 1: `TH1` (`0x8D`) + `TL1` (`0x8B`)

**Step 2: Timer vs Counter mode**
- **Timer mode:** Counts internal oscillator pulses (1 tick = `12 / f_osc`).
- **Counter mode:** Counts external pulses on the T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) pin.

The mode (timer or counter) is selected by the **C/T̄ bit** in the `TMOD` register.

**Step 3: TMOD Register (0x89)**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Function | GATE1 | C/T̄1 | M1.1 | M0.1 | GATE0 | C/T̄0 | M1.0 | M0.0 |

- **GATE:** Enables hardware control via INTx pin (used for pulse-width measurement).
- **C/T̄:** 1 = Counter, 0 = Timer.
- **M1, M0:** Mode selection bits.

**Step 4: Four Modes of Operation**

| Mode | M1 M0 | Description | Max Count | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 0 | 13-bit timer (8-bit TH + 5-bit TL) | 8192 | Legacy 8048 mode |
| 1 | 0 1 | 16-bit timer/counter | 65,536 | Most common, long delays |
| 2 | 1 0 | 8-bit auto-reload (TH reloads TL on overflow) | 256 | Baud rate generation, repeated intervals |
| 3 | 1 1 | Split timer (Timer 0 becomes two 8-bit timers; Timer 1 stops) | 256 | Extra timer for serial + parallel tasks |

**Step 5: TCON Register (0x88) — Control Bits**
- `TR0` (TCON.4) — Timer 0 Run control (set by software to start).
- `TR1` (TCON.6) — Timer 1 Run control.
- `TF0` (TCON.5) — Timer 0 Overflow flag (set by hardware, cleared by software).
- `TF1` (TCON.7) — Timer 1 Overflow flag.

**Step 6: Delay Formula Recap**
$$
Delay = (65{,}536 - Preload) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

For 11.0592 MHz → $T_{tick} = 1.085 \, \mu s$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in 8051 Feature Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing 128 bytes RAM with 4 KB ROM** — Many students incorrectly say "128 KB memory" or "4 KB RAM". The **on-chip RAM is 128 BYTES** and the **on-chip ROM is 4 KB**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Boolean Processor** — A signature feature of 8051 often omitted in answers. Mention the **bit-addressable memory (0x20–0x2F)** and single-bit instructions.
> 3. **Port 0 external pull-up confusion** — Students often say "P0 cannot be used" instead of correctly identifying the need for a **10 kΩ pull-up resistor**.
> 4. **Interrupt source count** — It is **5** (Reset, INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial) — but only **2 are external** (INT0, INT1) and **1 is non-maskable** (Reset). Some students incorrectly count 6 or 7.
> 5. **Bus width confusion** — 8051 has an **8-bit data bus** and a **16-bit address bus**, *not* a 16-bit data bus (that is the 8052 extended variant or 8086).
> 6. **Machine cycle vs instruction cycle** — One machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods. Not 6, not 4 (PIC).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 8051 Features**
>
> - **Architecture:** Harvard, 8-bit data, 16-bit address, CISC, 12 clocks per machine cycle.
> - **Memory:** 4 KB on-chip ROM, 128 B on-chip RAM, 64 KB external code + 64 KB external data.
> - **CPU Registers:** A (8-bit), B (8-bit), SP (8-bit), PC (16-bit), DPTR (16-bit), PSW (8-bit).
> - **I/O:** 4 ports × 8 bits = 32 lines. **P0 = open-drain** (needs pull-up); P1, P2, P3 = internally pulled-up.
> - **Port 3 alternate functions (memorize order):** RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD.
> - **Timers:** Two 16-bit, 4 modes each; Mode 1 = 16-bit (most used); Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload (for baud rate).
> - **Serial Port:** Full-duplex UART, 4 modes, Mode 1 is most common. **Use 11.0592 MHz crystal** for standard baud rates.
> - **Interrupts:** 5 sources; priority order = Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial. 2 priority levels via IP register.
> - **Boolean Processor:** Bit-addressable RAM at 0x20–0x2F (128 bits) + 16 bit-addressable SFRs. Instructions: SETB, CLR, MOV C, bit, ANL C, bit, ORL C, bit.
> - **SFRs (most important):** A (0xE0), B (0xF0), PSW (0xD0), SP (0x81), DPTR (0x82/83), P0–P3 (0x80/90/A0/B0), TCON (0x88), TMOD (0x89), SCON (0x98), IE (0xA8), IP (0xB8), PCON (0x87), SBUF (0x99), TH0/TL0/TH1/TL1.
> - **Pinout:** 40-pin DIP. VCC=40, VSS=20, XTAL1=19, XTAL2=18, RST=9, ALE=30, PSEN=29, EA=31.
> - **Reset Vector:** 0x0000 (Program execution starts here on power-on).
> - **Default Stack Pointer after Reset:** 0x07 (engineers typically move SP to 0x30 or 0x60 to free register banks for stack growth).
> - **Signal-to-remember pins:** ALE (latches address from P0), PSEN (reads external ROM), EA (1 = internal ROM preferred, 0 = external ROM only).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Diagram of 8051 - Program Status Word (PSW), Accumulator, Program Counter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Block Diagram of 8051 - Program Status Word (PSW), Accumulator, Program Counter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 8051 Microcontroller — Formal Definition

The **Intel 8051** is an **8-bit Harvard architecture** CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) microcontroller introduced in 1980. It is the cornerstone of embedded systems education in the KTU 2024 syllabus because it transparently exposes every internal register, making it ideal for learning how a CPU actually executes machine code.

The **Block Diagram of 8051** organizes the chip into functional units: **CPU (ALU + Registers + Control Unit)**, **4 KB on-chip ROM (Program Memory)**, **128 Bytes internal RAM (Data Memory)**, **Special Function Registers (SFRs)**, **I/O Ports (P0–P3)**, **Timers/Counters**, **Serial Port (UART)**, and **Interrupt Control Logic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 1):** Students must be able to draw the block diagram, label every bus, and explain the role of the **PSW, Accumulator, and Program Counter** in instruction execution. These three elements are the *heart* of the 8051 CPU.

## 1.2 The Three Critical Registers — Intuitive Overview

Let us demystify each register with a real-world analogy before diving into the bit-level architecture.

### 🔹 The Accumulator (ACC / A)
**Analogy — The Chef's Mixing Bowl:**
Imagine a chef in a kitchen. Before any dish can be prepared, ingredients must be placed *in a central bowl* (the Accumulator). The chef mixes them, adds spices, stirs, and finally serves the result back into the same bowl. In the 8051, **almost every arithmetic (ADD, SUBB, MUL, DIV) and logical (ANL, ORL, XRL) operation must use the Accumulator** as one of the source operands *and* the destination.

- **Size:** 8 bits
- **SFR Address:** `E0H`
- **Bit-Addressable:** Yes (bits `E0H` to `E7H`)

### 🔹 The Program Counter (PC)
**Analogy — The Reader's Bookmark:**
Imagine reading a 1,000-page novel. Your finger tracks the next line you will read. If you finish a paragraph, your finger automatically moves down. If someone says "go back 3 pages," you instantly move your finger back. The Program Counter is exactly this — **a 16-bit pointer that always holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched from code memory**.

- **Size:** **16 bits** (2 bytes)
- **Addressable Range:** `0000H` to `FFFFH` → **2¹⁶ = 65,536 = 64 KB**
- **SFR Address:** *Not part of SFR memory space* (PC is internal to the CPU)
- **Reset Value:** `0000H` (execution always begins at address `0000H`)

### 🔹 The Program Status Word (PSW)
**Analogy — The Car's Dashboard:**
A car's dashboard has small indicator lights: engine warning, oil pressure, parking brake, high-beam. These tiny flags tell the driver the *current condition* of the vehicle. The PSW is the **8051's 8-bit dashboard**. It does not store data; instead, each bit is a **flag** that gets set (1) or cleared (0) automatically by the ALU after every arithmetic, logical, or data-movement operation.

- **Size:** 8 bits
- **SFR Address:** `D0H`
- **Bit-Addressable:** Yes (bits `D0H` to `D7H`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these three together?** The Accumulator *processes* the data, the Program Counter *locates* the instruction, and the PSW *reports the outcome*. Together they form the minimal triplet required to execute even a single instruction in the 8051.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Block Diagram — Functional Walkthrough

The 8051 block diagram is conventionally drawn as a CPU core surrounded by memory, I/O, and peripheral blocks connected by a **16-bit Address Bus** and an **8-bit Data Bus**. Below is a structured breakdown of every block in the KTU-recommended architecture diagram.

| Block | Width | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Accumulator (A)** | 8-bit | Primary operand and result register for ALU |
| **B Register** | 8-bit | Used by `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` |
| **PSW** | 8-bit | Holds status flags (CY, AC, OV, P) and bank select bits |
| **SP (Stack Pointer)** | 8-bit | Points to top of stack in internal RAM |
| **DPTR (DPH+DPL)** | 16-bit | Data pointer for external memory access |
| **PC (Program Counter)** | 16-bit | Holds address of next instruction |
| **Internal RAM** | 128 Bytes | `00H`–`7FH` (data memory + 4 register banks) |
| **SFRs** | 128 Bytes | `80H`–`FFH` (special function registers) |
| **ROM (Program Memory)** | 4 KB | `0000H`–`0FFFH` (on-chip code) |
| **P0–P3** | 8-bit each | 32 I/O lines |
| **T0, T1** | 16-bit | Timers/Counters |
| **SBUF, SCON** | 8-bit | Serial port registers |
| **IE, IP** | 8-bit | Interrupt enable and priority |

## 2.2 The Program Status Word (PSW) — Bit-by-Bit Decoding

The PSW is a **bit-addressable 8-bit register located at SFR address `D0H`**. Each bit has a precise, fixed role dictated by the 8051 hardware. The following table is **the most exam-critical artifact in Module 1** — memorize it.

| Bit | Symbol | Name | Set Condition (1) | Cleared Condition (0) |
|:---:|:---:|---|---|---|
| **PSW.7** | **CY** | **Carry Flag** | Carry out of bit 7 during ADD; borrow during SUBB | No carry / no borrow |
| **PSW.6** | **AC** | **Auxiliary Carry** | Carry out of bit 3 into bit 4 (BCD correction) | No auxiliary carry |
| **PSW.5** | **F0** | **User Flag 0** | Set by user software (no hardware meaning) | Cleared by user software |
| **PSW.4** | **RS1** | **Register Bank Select 1** | Combined with RS0 to choose bank 0–3 | (see bank table below) |
| **PSW.3** | **RS0** | **Register Bank Select 0** | Combined with RS1 to choose bank 0–3 | (see bank table below) |
| **PSW.2** | **OV** | **Overflow Flag** | Signed arithmetic overflow (result out of −128 to +127) | No signed overflow |
| **PSW.1** | **—** | **Reserved** | User-defined (undefined behavior) | — |
| **PSW.0** | **P** | **Parity Flag** | Accumulator contains an *odd* number of 1s | Accumulator contains an *even* number of 1s |

### 2.2.1 Register Bank Selection Logic

The 8051 dedicates the lower 32 bytes of internal RAM (`00H`–`1FH`) to **four register banks**, each containing eight 8-bit registers named `R0`–`R7`. The RS1 and RS0 bits in PSW *swap* which physical bank is currently active.

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | RAM Address Range |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** (default after reset) | `00H`–`07H` |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | `08H`–`0FH` |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | `10H`–`17H` |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | `18H`–`1FH` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** On reset, PSW = `0000 0000B`, so RS1 = RS0 = 0 → **Bank 0** is active. To switch to Bank 2, you would execute `SETB PSW.4` (sets RS1=1, RS0=0).

### 2.2.2 The Parity Flag (P) — A Subtle Detail

The P bit is **automatically set/cleared after every instruction that writes to the Accumulator**. It does NOT reflect the parity of the entire PSW; it reflects the parity of the *Accumulator only*. For example, if `A = 0xA3` (`1010 0011B` → 4 ones, even), then `P = 0`.

## 2.3 The Accumulator (ACC) — Operational Roles

The Accumulator participates in instruction execution in four distinct ways:

1. **Source Operand:** e.g., `ADD A, R0` → adds R0 to A.
2. **Destination Operand:** e.g., `MOV A, #55H` → loads immediate 55H into A.
3. **Implicit Operand:** e.g., `CPL A` → complements A (only operand).
4. **Result Register:** All ALU operations deposit the result back into A.

Because ACC is bit-addressable, the 8051 also allows individual bit manipulation (e.g., `SETB ACC.0`) — a feature used heavily in single-bit control programs.

## 2.4 The Program Counter (PC) — Operational Behavior

The PC is the *only* 16-bit register that is *not* part of the SFR memory map. Its behavior is fully automatic:

- After **reset**, PC = `0000H` → CPU fetches the first opcode from `0000H`.
- After **every instruction fetch**, PC auto-increments by the instruction's length (1, 2, or 3 bytes).
- On **`LCALL addr16` / `AJMP addr11`**, PC is loaded with the new address and saved to the stack.
- On **`RET` / `RETI`**, PC is restored from the stack.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| **PC Addressable Space** | $2^n$ where $n = 16$ | $= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64\text{ KB}$ |
| **External Code Memory** | `0000H` to `FFFFH` | 64 KB maximum (combined on-chip + off-chip) |
| **External Data Memory** | `0000H` to `FFFFH` | 64 KB maximum (accessed via `MOVX`) |
| **Carry Flag (CY)** | $\text{CY} = C_{out}$ from bit 7 | Boolean (0 or 1) |
| **Auxiliary Carry (AC)** | $\text{AC} = C_{out}$ from bit 3 to bit 4 | Used in BCD (DA) operations |
| **Overflow Flag (OV)** | $\text{OV} = C_{7}^{\text{in}} \oplus C_{7}^{\text{out}}$ | XOR of carry into and out of MSB |
| **Parity Flag (P)** | $P = (A_7 \oplus A_6 \oplus \cdots \oplus A_0)' $ | 1 if number of 1s in A is **odd** |
| **Register Bank Size** | $4 \text{ banks} \times 8 \text{ registers} \times 1 \text{ byte}$ | $= 32 \text{ bytes}$ (`00H` to `1FH`) |
| **PSW Bit Layout** | `CY AC F0 RS1 RS0 OV – P` | bit7 → bit0 |
| **SFR Address of ACC** | $\texttt{E0H}$ | Bit addresses `E0H`–`E7H` |
| **SFR Address of PSW** | $\texttt{D0H}$ | Bit addresses `D0H`–`D7H` |

> [!NOTE]
> The overflow formula $\text{OV} = C_{7}^{\text{in}} \oplus C_{7}^{\text{out}}$ is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU ESE whenever signed arithmetic is discussed. Practice it until memorized.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why 16-bit PC = 64 KB Addressing

**Problem:** The 8051 has a 16-bit Program Counter. Prove that it can directly address exactly **65,536 bytes** of program memory.

**Step 1 — Define address bus width.**
The 8051 code memory is accessed via a **16-bit address bus** driven by the PC (with help from the DPTR for `MOVC`).

**Step 2 — Count unique combinations.**
A single bit can represent 2 states. With $n$ independent bits, the number of unique binary patterns is:

$$
\text{Total Addresses} = 2^n
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute n = 16.**

$$
\text{Total Addresses} = 2^{16}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$
2^{16} = 2^{10} \times 2^{6} = 1024 \times 64 = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kilobytes.**

$$
65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = \frac{65{,}536}{1024} \text{ KB} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 16-bit PC allows the 8051 to address a contiguous 64 KB code memory space from `0000H` to `FFFFH`. The on-chip 4 KB ROM occupies `0000H`–`0FFFH`; the rest is external.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overflow Flag (OV) from Carry In and Out

**Problem:** Derive the relationship between the Overflow flag and the carries into / out of the Most Significant Bit (MSB) during an 8-bit signed addition.

**Step 1 — Concept of two's-complement overflow.**
In signed 8-bit arithmetic, the valid range is $-128$ to $+127$ (i.e., `80H` to `7FH`). Overflow occurs when the true mathematical result lies *outside* this range but the 8-bit result lies *inside* it (i.e., the sign bit is corrupted).

**Step 2 — Define the two carries.**
Let $C_7^{\text{in}}$ = carry *into* bit 7 (MSB) from bit 6.
Let $C_7^{\text{out}}$ = carry *out of* bit 7 into the (non-existent) bit 8.

**Step 3 — Analyze the four cases.**

| $C_7^{\text{in}}$ | $C_7^{\text{out}}$ | Meaning | Overflow? |
|:---:|:---:|---|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Both operands positive, result fits in 7 bits | No |
| 0 | 1 | Positive + Positive → Negative (signed error) | **Yes** |
| 1 | 0 | Negative + Negative → Positive (signed error) | **Yes** |
| 1 | 1 | Both operands negative, carry matches sign | No |

**Step 4 — Express as XOR.**
Overflow happens *exactly* when the two carries differ. This is the XOR operation:

$$
\text{OV} = C_7^{\text{in}} \oplus C_7^{\text{out}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification example.** Add `0111 1111` (+127) + `0000 0001` (+1) = `1000 0000` (−128 in signed). Here $C_7^{\text{in}} = 0$, $C_7^{\text{out}} = 0$, so $\text{OV} = 0 \oplus 0 = 0$ (correct — no overflow in the hardware sense; the result is the true mathematical answer). Compare with `0111 1111` (+127) + `0000 0010` (+2): sum should be +129, which is unrepresentable. The result is `1000 0001` (−127). Here $C_7^{\text{in}} = 0$, $C_7^{\text{out}} = 0$, so $\text{OV} = 0$... wait, let us recompute carefully.

**Correction & Re-Verification:**
$+127 = 0111\,1111$, $+2 = 0000\,0010$.
$0111\,1111 + 0000\,0010 = 1000\,0001$.
$C_7^{\text{in}}$: adding bit 6 of the result $(1+0) = 1$, no incoming carry → $C_7^{\text{in}} = 0$.
$C_7^{\text{out}}$: bit 7 sum is $1+0+0 = 1$, no carry out → $C_7^{\text{out}} = 0$.
OV = 0 ⊕ 0 = 0.

But the true mathematical result is +129, which IS out of range. Why is OV = 0? Because in this specific case, the addition does not generate any internal carry chain distortion. The hardware overflow flag uses a *different* rule for ADD:

$$
\text{OV (ADD)} = C_6^{\text{out}} \oplus C_7^{\text{out}}
$$

where $C_6^{\text{out}}$ is the carry from bit 6 to bit 7.

For $+127 + 2$: $C_6^{\text{out}} = 1$ (bit 6: $1+0+1 = 10B$), $C_7^{\text{out}} = 0$. So $\text{OV} = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$. **Correct.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often quote the formula $\text{OV} = C_7^{\text{in}} \oplus C_7^{\text{out}}$ without realizing that for **ADD** the equivalent working formula is $C_6^{\text{out}} \oplus C_7^{\text{out}}$. For **SUBB**, it is $C_7^{\text{in}} \oplus C_7^{\text{out}}$. The 8051 hardware internally computes OV using the latter XOR. Memorize both.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — PSW Bit Updates for Common Operations

The following matrix shows how each PSW flag reacts to representative instructions. The values are computed using $\text{A} = 0x73$ and $\text{R0} = 0x2C$ (unless stated).

| Instruction | Operation | Result in A (hex) | CY | AC | OV | P |
|---|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `ADD A, R0` | 0x73 + 0x2C | 0x9F | 0 | 0 | 0 (no sign overflow) | 0 (4 ones) |
| `ADD A, #0x90` | 0x73 + 0x90 | 0x03 | 1 | 0 | 0 (signed in range) | 1 (2 ones) |
| `SUBB A, R0` | 0x73 − 0x2C − 0 | 0x47 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 (3 ones) |
| `SUBB A, #0x80` | 0x73 − 0x80 − 0 | 0xF3 | 1 (borrow) | 0 | 0 | 0 (6 ones) |
| `ANL A, #0x0F` | 0x73 AND 0x0F | 0x03 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 (2 ones) |
| `ORL A, #0x80` | 0x73 OR 0x80 | 0xF3 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 (6 ones) |
| `XRL A, R0` | 0x73 XOR 0x2C | 0x5F | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 (4 ones) |
| `INC A` | 0x73 + 1 | 0x74 | *unchanged* | *unchanged* | *unchanged* | 1 (3 ones) |
| `CPL A` | ~0x73 | 0x8C | *unchanged* | *unchanged* | *unchanged* | 0 (4 ones) |
| `RLC A` | rotate through CY | — | = old bit 7 | *unchanged* | *unchanged* | updated |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Logical operations (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`) and the `INC` instruction **do NOT modify CY or AC**. Only `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, and `DA` modify the carry flags. The P flag, however, updates whenever A is written to.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Adding `3CH` and `D4H`

**Given:** `A = 3CH`, execute `ADD A, #0D4H`. Find all affected PSW flags.

**Binary expansion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0x3C = 0011\,1100_B \\
\#0D4H &= 1101\,0100_B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bit-by-bit addition (LSB to MSB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0: } 0 + 0 + 0 &= 0,\ \text{carry}=0 \\
\text{Bit 1: } 0 + 0 + 0 &= 0,\ \text{carry}=0 \\
\text{Bit 2: } 1 + 1 + 0 &= 0,\ \text{carry}=1 \rightarrow \mathbf{AC = 1} \\
\text{Bit 3: } 1 + 0 + 1 &= 0,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 4: } 1 + 1 + 1 &= 1,\ \text{carry}=1 \rightarrow \mathbf{AC = 1} \\
\text{Bit 5: } 1 + 0 + 1 &= 0,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 6: } 0 + 1 + 1 &= 0,\ \text{carry}=1 \\
\text{Bit 7: } 0 + 1 + 1 &= 0,\ \text{carry}=1 \rightarrow \mathbf{CY = 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result in A:** `0001 0000B = 10H`.

**OV determination:** $C_6^{\text{out}} = 1$ (from bit 6: $0+1+1=10B$, carry out), $C_7^{\text{out}} = 1$ (from bit 7).
$\text{OV} = C_6^{\text{out}} \oplus C_7^{\text{out}} = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$.

**Parity (P):** `10H = 0001 0000B` has exactly **1** one → odd → $P = 1$.

**Final PSW after this instruction:**

$$
\text{PSW} = \underbrace{1}_{CY}\,\underbrace{1}_{AC}\,\underbrace{0}_{F0}\,\underbrace{0}_{RS1}\,\underbrace{0}_{RS0}\,\underbrace{0}_{OV}\,\underbrace{x}_{-}\,\underbrace{1}_{P} = \texttt{C1H} \text{ (if reserved bit = 0)}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Functional Block Diagram with Highlight on ACC, PSW, PC

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the 8051, with explicit emphasis on how the **Accumulator**, **PSW**, and **Program Counter** interconnect with the ALU, Code Memory, and external bus structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["CPU CORE OF 8051"]
        ALU["ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT\n8-bit ALU"]
        ACC["ACCUMULATOR A\n8-bit SFR at E0H\nBit-Addressable: E0H-E7H"]
        BREG["B REGISTER\n8-bit SFR at F0H\nUsed by MUL and DIV"]
        PSW["PROGRAM STATUS WORD\n8-bit SFR at D0H\nBit-Addressable: D0H-D7H\nCY AC F0 RS1 RS0 OV - P"]
        TMP1["TEMP REGISTER 1\nInternal 8-bit"]
        TMP2["TEMP REGISTER 2\nInternal 8-bit"]
    end

    subgraph REGS["REGISTER SECTION"]
        RB0["REGISTER BANK 0\nR0-R7 at 00H-07H"]
        RB1["REGISTER BANK 1\nR0-R7 at 08H-0FH"]
        RB2["REGISTER BANK 2\nR0-R7 at 10H-17H"]
        RB3["REGISTER BANK 3\nR0-R7 at 18H-1FH"]
        SP["STACK POINTER SP\n8-bit SFR at 81H"]
        DPTR["DPTR DPH+DPL\n16-bit SFR at 83H+82H"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL["CONTROL UNIT"]
        PC["PROGRAM COUNTER PC\n16-bit Internal\nRange: 0000H-FFFFH\n64 KB Address Space"]
        IR["INSTRUCTION REGISTER\n8-bit opcode buffer"]
        DEC["INSTRUCTION DECODER\nTiming and Control Logic"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["MEMORY SUBSYSTEM"]
        ROM["4 KB ON-CHIP ROM\nCode Memory\n0000H-0FFFH"]
        XROM["EXTERNAL CODE MEMORY\nUp to 64 KB\nvia PSEN signal"]
        IRAM["128 BYTES INTERNAL RAM\n00H-7FH Data Memory"]
        SFR["128 BYTES SFR AREA\n80H-FFH"]
        XRAM["EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY\nUp to 64 KB\nvia MOVX and WR/RD"]
    end

    subgraph IO["I/O AND PERIPHERALS"]
        P0["PORT 0\n8-bit P0.0-P0.7\nMultiplexed Addr/Data"]
        P1["PORT 1\n8-bit P1.0-P1.7\nGeneral Purpose I/O"]
        P2["PORT 2\n8-bit P2.0-P2.7\nHigh Byte Address"]
        P3["PORT 3\n8-bit P3.0-P3.7\nAlternate Functions"]
        TMR["TIMERS T0 AND T1\n16-bit each"]
        UART["SERIAL PORT UART\nSBUF at 99H, SCON at 98H"]
        INT["INTERRUPT CONTROLLER\nIE at A8H, IP at B8H"]
    end

    BUS_DATA["8-BIT INTERNAL DATA BUS"]
    BUS_ADDR["16-BIT INTERNAL ADDRESS BUS"]

    ACC -->|operand/result| ALU
    BREG -->|operand for MUL/DIV| ALU
    TMP1 --> ALU
    TMP2 --> ALU
    ALU -->|updates flags| PSW
    ALU -->|writes result| ACC
    PSW -.->|RS1 RS0 select| RB0
    PSW -.->|RS1 RS0 select| RB1
    PSW -.->|RS1 RS0 select| RB2
    PSW -.->|RS1 RS0 select| RB3

    PC -->|fetches opcode| ROM
    PC -->|fetches opcode| XROM
    PC -->|address 16-bit| BUS_ADDR
    ROM -->|opcode byte| IR
    XROM -->|opcode byte| IR
    IR --> DEC
    DEC -->|control signals| ALU
    DEC -->|timing| PC

    ACC <--> BUS_DATA
    PSW <--> BUS_DATA
    BREG <--> BUS_DATA
    SP <--> BUS_DATA
    DPTR <--> BUS_DATA
    IRAM <--> BUS_DATA
    SFR <--> BUS_DATA
    XRAM <--> BUS_DATA
    RB0 <--> BUS_DATA
    RB1 <--> BUS_DATA
    RB2 <--> BUS_DATA
    RB3 <--> BUS_DATA
    P0 <--> BUS_DATA
    P1 <--> BUS_DATA
    P2 <--> BUS_DATA
    P3 <--> BUS_DATA
    TMR <--> BUS_DATA
    UART <--> BUS_DATA
    INT <--> BUS_DATA

    style ACC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style PSW fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style PC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style ALU fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style ROM fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style XROM fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style IRAM fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style SFR fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 PSW Internal Bit Map (Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PSWREG["PSW REGISTER - SFR ADDRESS D0H"]
        direction LR
        B7["BIT 7\nCY\nCarry Flag"]
        B6["BIT 6\nAC\nAuxiliary Carry"]
        B5["BIT 5\nF0\nUser Flag 0"]
        B4["BIT 4\nRS1\nBank Select 1"]
        B3["BIT 3\nRS0\nBank Select 0"]
        B2["BIT 2\nOV\nOverflow Flag"]
        B1["BIT 1\nRESERVED"]
        B0["BIT 0\nP\nParity Flag"]
    end

    B7 --> B6 --> B5 --> B4 --> B3 --> B2 --> B1 --> B0

    B7 -.->|"Set on carry out of bit 7"| NOTE1["HARDWARE"]
    B6 -.->|"Set on carry out of bit 3"| NOTE2["HARDWARE"]
    B5 -.->|"Software controlled"| NOTE3["USER"]
    B4 -.->|"Select Bank 0-3"| NOTE4["RS1:RS0"]
    B3 -.->|"Select Bank 0-3"| NOTE4
    B2 -.->|"Signed overflow detector"| NOTE5["HARDWARE"]
    B1 -.->|"Undefined"| NOTE6["DO NOT USE"]
    B0 -.->|"Odd parity of A"| NOTE7["HARDWARE"]

    style B7 fill:#FF4500,color:#fff
    style B6 fill:#FF8C00,color:#fff
    style B5 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style B4 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style B3 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#FF1493,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#808080,color:#fff
    style B0 fill:#32CD32,color:#000
```

## 4.3 PC Execution Flow (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["RESET SIGNAL\nApplied to RST pin"] -->|Forces PC=0000H| B["PC = 0000H\n16-bit counter\nloaded with zero"]
    B --> C["FETCH PHASE\nOpcode byte read from\naddress PC"]
    C --> D["INCREMENT PC\nPC becomes PC+1 or PC+2 or PC+3\nbased on instruction length"]
    D --> E{"IS INSTRUCTION\nCOMPLETE?"}
    E -->|No - more bytes needed| C
    E -->|Yes| F["DECODE PHASE\nInstruction Decoder\nactivates appropriate control lines"]
    F --> G["EXECUTE PHASE\nALU operates on operands\nACC, B, Registers\nPSW flags updated"]
    G --> H{"PC NORMALLY\nINCREMENTED?"}
    H -->|Yes| B
    H -->|No - Branch taken| I["PC LOADED WITH\nBranch / Call / Return address"]
    I --> B

    style A fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
    style B fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFA500,color:#000
    style I fill:#FF69B4,color:#fff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Explain the Program Status Word (PSW) of the 8051 microcontroller. List all its bits with their functions.**

**Model Answer (≈ 130 words):**

The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an **8-bit Special Function Register** located at **SFR address `D0H`** in the 8051. It is **bit-addressable** (bit addresses `D0H`–`D7H`) and functions as the CPU's status dashboard, holding flags set/cleared automatically by the ALU.

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| PSW.7 | **CY** | Carry flag – set on carry out of bit 7 (ADD) or borrow (SUBB) |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | Auxiliary Carry – carry out of bit 3 (used by `DA A`) |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | General-purpose user flag, software-controlled |
| PSW.4 | **RS1** | Register bank select bit 1 |
| PSW.3 | **RS0** | Register bank select bit 0 |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | Overflow flag for signed arithmetic |
| PSW.1 | **—** | Reserved (undefined) |
| PSW.0 | **P** | Parity flag – 1 if ACC has odd number of 1s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Q1):** Students often write "F0 is hardware-controlled" — it is **NOT**; F0 is purely software-defined. Losing 1 mark here is very common.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**What is the role of the Program Counter in the 8051? Why is it 16 bits wide even though the ALU is 8-bit?**

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

The **Program Counter (PC)** is a **16-bit internal register** that holds the **memory address of the next instruction** to be fetched from code memory. After every instruction fetch, the PC **auto-increments** by 1, 2, or 3 bytes depending on instruction length. The PC is loaded with new values during branch (`SJMP`, `LJMP`, `AJMP`), call (`LCALL`, `ACALL`), and return (`RET`, `RETI`) instructions.

The PC is 16 bits wide (not 8) because code memory addressing is **decoupled** from data width. A 16-bit counter can uniquely identify $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64\text{ KB}$ locations, which is the 8051's addressable code space. The 8-bit ALU width affects only *data* operations, not *address* generation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Q2):** Writing "PC is 16 bits because ALU is 16 bits" is wrong. The 8051's ALU is 8-bit; the PC is 16 bits purely for *addressing* reasons. Marks will be deducted for confusing data width with address width.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Draw the **block diagram of the 8051 microcontroller** and explain the functions of the **CPU, Program Memory, Data Memory, and I/O Ports**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Program Status Word (PSW)** of 8051. List all its bits and describe the role of **CY, AC, OV, and P flags** with one example each. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.1** above (the Mermaid block diagram satisfies this requirement when printed).

**Functional Description:**

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** Contains the **8-bit ALU**, **Accumulator (A)**, **B register**, **Program Status Word (PSW)**, **Stack Pointer (SP)**, **Data Pointer (DPTR)**, and **four register banks (R0–R7)**. The ALU executes arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `INC`, `DEC`), logical (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`), and bit-manipulation instructions.

2. **Program Memory (4 KB on-chip ROM):** Stores the user's program code. Address range `0000H` to `0FFFH`. Can be extended externally up to 64 KB using the `PSEN` (Program Store Enable) signal. Reset vector is at `0000H`; timer interrupt vectors start at `000BH`.

3. **Data Memory:**
   - **Internal RAM (128 Bytes)** at `00H`–`7FH`:
     - `00H`–`1FH` → 4 register banks (R0–R7)
     - `20H`–`2FH` → 16 bit-addressable locations
     - `30H`–`7FH` → general-purpose scratch-pad RAM
   - **SFR area (128 Bytes)** at `80H`–`FFH`: Special function registers (ACC, B, PSW, SP, DPTR, ports, etc.)
   - **External data memory** (up to 64 KB) accessible via `MOVX` instructions.

4. **I/O Ports:** Four 8-bit ports **P0, P1, P2, P3** providing 32 I/O lines. P0 and P2 also serve as the multiplexed **address/data bus** during external memory access. P3 has alternate functions: RxD/TxD (UART), `INT0`/`INT1`, `T0`/`T1`, `WR`/`RD`.

---

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**PSW Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.2** (PSW Internal Bit Map) above.

**Detailed Flag Explanations with Examples:**

| Flag | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **CY (Carry, PSW.7)** | Set when an addition generates a carry out of bit 7, or a subtraction requires a borrow. | `A = 0xFF, ADD A, #0x01` → `A = 0x00, CY = 1` |
| **AC (Auxiliary Carry, PSW.6)** | Set when there is a carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (the lower nibble boundary). Used for BCD correction. | `A = 0x0F, ADD A, #0x01` → `A = 0x10, AC = 1` |
| **OV (Overflow, PSW.2)** | Set when signed addition produces a result outside −128 to +127, i.e., the sign bit is corrupted. | `A = 0x7F, ADD A, #0x01` → `A = 0x80, OV = 1` (since +127 + 1 = +128, out of range) |
| **P (Parity, PSW.0)** | Set when the Accumulator contains an **odd** number of 1s. | `A = 0x53` (`0101 0011B`, 4 ones = even) → `P = 0`; `A = 0x55` (`0101 0101B`, 4 ones = even) → `P = 0`; `A = 0x07` → 3 ones (odd) → `P = 1` |

The remaining bits are **F0** (user flag), **RS1, RS0** (register bank selection), and **bit 1 (reserved, undefined)**.

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **architecture of the 8051** with a block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Accumulator, B register, and Program Counter** in detail. (7 marks)

**(b)** With suitable examples, explain the function of the **Carry, Auxiliary Carry, and Overflow flags** of the 8051. Show how the **parity flag (P)** is computed for a given byte. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution — (a)**

**Architecture:** See **Section 4.1** (block diagram) for the visual reference.

**Role of the Three Key Registers:**

1. **Accumulator (A / ACC):**
   - An 8-bit register at SFR address **`E0H`**, bit-addressable from `E0H` to `E7H`.
   - The **most-used register** in 8051 programming. It is the implicit operand for most ALU operations.
   - For example, in `ADD A, R0`, the instruction internally performs `A ← A + R0` — the result is *always* stored in A.
   - It is also used for data transfer: `MOV A, #data`, `MOVX A, @DPTR`, and rotates: `RL A`, `RR A`, `RLC A`, `RRC A`.

2. **B Register:**
   - An 8-bit register at SFR address **`F0H`**, used **only** by two instructions: `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`.
   - Example: `MUL AB` multiplies unsigned A by unsigned B, stores the 16-bit result with high byte in B and low byte in A.
   - In all other contexts, B is a general-purpose scratch register (R6-like).

3. **Program Counter (PC):**
   - A 16-bit register that *always* points to the **address of the next instruction** to be executed in code memory.
   - On reset, PC ← `0000H`. CPU fetches the opcode at `0000H`, increments PC to `0001H` (or beyond, depending on instruction length), decodes and executes, then fetches the next.
   - Total addressable code memory = $2^{16} = 64 \text{ KB}$.
   - PC is *not* part of the SFR map; it is internal to the CPU.
   - Modified automatically by the hardware (fetch increment), or explicitly by control transfer instructions (`LJMP`, `SJMP`, `AJMP`, `LCALL`, `ACALL`, `RET`, `RETI`).

---

#### **Model Solution — (b)**

**Carry Flag (CY — PSW.7):**
Set by `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, and `DA A` instructions. For addition, CY = carry out of bit 7. For subtraction, CY = 1 indicates a borrow was needed.

**Example 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0xF0 = 1111\,0000_B \\
\text{ADD } A, \#0x20 \rightarrow 0x10 \text{ with } CY &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Auxiliary Carry (AC — PSW.6):**
Set when a carry is generated from bit 3 to bit 4 (between the lower and upper nibbles). It enables BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) arithmetic via the `DA A` (Decimal Adjust) instruction.

**Example 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0x08 = 0000\,1000_B \\
\text{ADD } A, \#0x08 \rightarrow 0x10, \text{ bit 3 to 4 carry occurred} \\
AC &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Overflow Flag (OV — PSW.2):**
Set when signed arithmetic produces a result outside the −128 to +127 range. Computed as:

$$
\text{OV} = C_6^{\text{out}} \oplus C_7^{\text{out}} \text{ (for ADD)}
$$

**Example 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0x7F = +127 \\
\text{ADD } A, \#0x01 &\rightarrow A = 0x80 = -128 \text{ (signed)} \\
OV &= 1 \text{ (since +128 is out of signed 8-bit range)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Parity Flag (P — PSW.0):**
The P flag reflects the **parity of the Accumulator only**. It is set (P = 1) when the Accumulator has an **odd** number of 1s; cleared (P = 0) when the count is even.

**Example 4:**

Given `A = 0xA5 = 1010 0101B`. Count of 1s = 4 (even) → `P = 0`.

Given `A = 0xA7 = 1010 0111B`. Count of 1s = 5 (odd) → `P = 1`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Major KTU Valuation Pitfall:** The parity flag counts 1s in the **Accumulator** — *not* in the PSW, not in memory, not in the operand. A common error is "P = 1 if A is even" — this is **wrong**. P is about the number of 1-bits, not the numerical value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — High-Yield Revision Points:**

- ✅ The 8051 is an **8-bit Harvard architecture** CISC microcontroller with **4 KB on-chip ROM** and **128 Bytes on-chip RAM**.
- ✅ The **Accumulator (ACC)** is an 8-bit SFR at **`E0H`**, bit-addressable, and is the **primary operand/result register** for nearly every ALU operation.
- ✅ The **Program Counter (PC)** is a **16-bit register** (NOT in SFR space) that holds the address of the next instruction; it can address $2^{16} = 64 \text{ KB}$ of code memory.
- ✅ After **reset**, **PC = 0000H** and **PSW = 00H** (i.e., Register **Bank 0** is active).
- ✅ The **PSW** is at SFR address **`D0H`**, bit-addressable from `D0H` to `D7H`. The bit layout from MSB to LSB is: **CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, (reserved), P**.
- ✅ **CY** = carry out of bit 7 (ADD) / borrow (SUBB). **AC** = carry out of bit 3 (BCD use). **OV** = signed overflow ($C_6^{\text{out}} \oplus C_7^{\text{out}}$ for ADD). **P** = 1 if A has an odd number of 1s.
- ✅ **RS1:RS0** select one of 4 register banks at `00H–07H`, `08H–0FH`, `10H–17H`, `18H–1FH`. Default = `00` (Bank 0).
- ✅ **F0** is a **user-defined** software flag, not hardware-controlled.
- ✅ **PSW.1** is **reserved / undefined**; never rely on its value.
- ✅ **INC A** does NOT modify CY; **CPL A** does NOT modify any flags except P; **MOV** to A updates only P.
- ✅ The 8051 has **32 I/O lines** distributed across P0, P1, P2, P3 (8 bits each).
- ✅ External code memory (up to 64 KB) is selected by the **`PSEN`** signal; external data memory (up to 64 KB) is selected by **`RD`** and **`WR`** signals.
- ✅ For KTU problems: when asked for PSW contents after an arithmetic operation, **always show bit-by-bit addition** with carry chain and explicitly state CY, AC, OV, and P.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Organization - RAM and ROM, Register Banks and Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Organization - RAM and ROM, Register Banks and Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Memory Organization in Microcontrollers — The Formal Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8051 family of microcontrollers** (the canonical 8-bit MCU studied in the KTU PBECT404 syllabus), **Memory Organization** refers to the structured, addressable allocation of storage resources that the CPU uses to fetch instructions, manipulate data, and manage the execution context. The 8051 follows a modified **Harvard architecture**, which physically separates **Program Memory (ROM)** from **Data Memory (RAM)**, while still allowing limited cross-access through specific instructions (e.g., `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The 8051 has a **16-bit Program Counter**, enabling it to address up to **64 KB (65,536 bytes)** of program memory, and an **8-bit Stack Pointer** that operates on an **8-bit Internal RAM (IRAM) of 128 bytes** plus **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** in the upper 128 bytes.

The two principal memory families inside any embedded system are:

| Memory Type | Volatility | Writable? | Role in MCU | 8051 Location |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RAM** (Random Access Memory) | **Volatile** (loses data on power-off) | Yes (read/write) | Temporary data, variables, stack, registers | Internal 128 B + External 64 KB |
| **ROM** (Read-Only Memory) | **Non-Volatile** | No (or limited writes) | Permanent program code, lookup tables | Internal 4 KB + External 64 KB |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Desk Office"

Think of the 8051's memory as a **clerk's office with two completely separate desks**:

- **ROM Desk (Program Memory):** This is the **"Policy Bookshelf."** It contains the immutable rulebook of the company (your firmware). The clerk can *read* the rules whenever a decision is needed, but cannot erase or rewrite them during a workday. When the office loses power overnight, the bookshelf is still there in the morning — this is **non-volatility**.
- **RAM Desk (Data Memory):** This is the **"Working Bench."** It is a messy, fast-access surface where the clerk scribbles intermediate calculations, lays out active papers (variables), keeps a running tally (counters), and maintains a **stack of pending memos**. If the office loses power, everything on the bench vanishes — this is **volatility**.

The **Register Banks** are like **four pre-labeled trays** (Bank 0, Bank 1, Bank 2, Bank 3) sitting on the workbench. Only *one* tray is "active" at a time, holding the eight most-used scratch values (R0–R7). The clerk can swap trays in milliseconds to handle a different task — this is the hardware context-switching mechanism.

The **Stack** is an **"Inbox Tray"** sitting on the same workbench. The clerk places urgent memos (return addresses, saved registers) on top of the stack using `PUSH`, and removes them from the top using `POP`. It grows upward like a stack of plates, and a pointer (`SP`) always tracks the top plate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** ROM = *Permanent Knowledge*, RAM = *Working Memory*, Register Banks = *Fast-Access Scratchpads*, Stack = *LIFO Memo Tray*. Together, they form the complete cognitive workspace of the embedded CPU.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **8051 Program Counter (PC) width:** **16 bits** → addressable program space = **64 KB**.
- **Internal RAM size:** **128 bytes** (addresses `00H` to `7FH`).
- **SFR space size:** **128 bytes** (addresses `80H` to `FFH`) — accessed only by direct addressing.
- **Total directly addressable internal data space:** **256 bytes** (`00H` to `FFH`).
- **External data memory ceiling:** **64 KB** (`0000H` to `FFFFH`).
- **Number of Register Banks:** **4** (Bank 0, 1, 2, 3), each containing **8 byte-sized registers** R0–R7 → **32 bytes total**.
- **Bit-addressable region size:** **16 bytes** (addresses `20H` to `2FH`) → **128 individually addressable bits**.
- **Default Stack Pointer value on reset:** **`07H`** (i.e., the first `PUSH` writes to location `08H`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 RAM — Random Access Memory

**RAM** is *random access* because any cell can be reached in **equal time** (independent of its physical address), in contrast to sequential media like magnetic tape. Within microcontrollers, two principal RAM technologies are relevant:

#### 2.1.1 SRAM (Static RAM)
- Built from **6 transistors per cell** (a flip-flop).
- Retains data as long as power is supplied — **no refresh needed**.
- **Faster** access time (≈ 1–10 ns).
- Used for **CPU registers, cache, and high-speed scratchpads** — i.e., the 8051's *internal* RAM is SRAM.

#### 2.1.2 DRAM (Dynamic RAM)
- Built from **1 transistor + 1 capacitor per cell**.
- Capacitor leaks charge → requires **periodic refresh** (every few milliseconds).
- **Higher density, lower cost per bit** — used in large external data memories and PC main memory.

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051's *internal* 128 B RAM is **SRAM**, but it can interface to **external DRAM or SRAM chips** (up to 64 KB) through ports P0 and P2 acting as the multiplexed address/data bus.

### 2.2 ROM — Read-Only Memory (Program Memory in MCU Context)

ROM stores the **firmware / machine code** that the 8051 fetches and executes. Variants encountered in MCU design:

| ROM Type | Erasure Method | Re-programmability | Typical MCU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mask ROM** | Not erasable (blowing fuses at fab) | None (one-time) | High-volume production |
| **PROM** | Not erasable | None | Prototypes (one-time) |
| **EPROM** | **UV light** (20–30 min) | Yes (with eraser) | Old development boards |
| **EEPROM** | **Electrical byte-wise** | Yes (≈ 1 M cycles) | Storing calibration, passwords |
| **Flash ROM** | **Electrical block-wise** | Yes (≈ 10 K–100 K cycles) | **Modern 8051 variants (e.g., AT89C51, P89V51)** — internal 4 KB flash |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The classic 8051 has **4 KB of internal Mask ROM** (addresses `0000H` to `0FFFH`) and can address up to **64 KB externally**. Modern Atmel/NXP flash-based derivatives (AT89C51, AT89S52) replace the mask ROM with **4 KB / 8 KB of in-system programmable flash**.

### 2.3 8051 Internal RAM Map — The "Three-Zone Workbench"

The 128 bytes of internal RAM (`00H`–`7FH`) are partitioned into three functionally distinct zones:

| Address Range (Hex) | Zone | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **`00H` – `1FH`** | Register Banks | **32 bytes** | Four banks × 8 registers (R0–R7) |
| **`20H` – `2FH`** | Bit-Addressable | **16 bytes** | 128 individually addressable bits (`00H`–`7FH` of bit space) |
| **`30H` – `7FH`** | General-Purpose RAM | **80 bytes** | User variables, scratchpad |

> [!NOTE]
> The **upper 128 bytes** (`80H`–`FFH`) are the **SFR (Special Function Register) space**, accessible only via direct addressing. Examples: Accumulator (`E0H`), B register (`F0H`), PSW (`D0H`), SP (`81H`), P0–P3 (`80H, 90H, A0H, B0H`).

### 2.4 Register Banks — The Four Scratchpad Trays

The 8051 dedicates **32 bytes** (`00H`–`1FH`) to **four Register Banks**, each occupying **8 consecutive bytes**:

| Bank | R0 Address | R1 | R2 | R3 | R4 | R5 | R6 | R7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bank 0** | `00H` | `01H` | `02H` | `03H` | `04H` | `05H` | `06H` | `07H` |
| **Bank 1** | `08H` | `09H` | `0AH` | `0BH` | `0CH` | `0DH` | `0EH` | `0FH` |
| **Bank 2** | `10H` | `11H` | `12H` | `13H` | `14H` | `15H` | `16H` | `17H` |
| **Bank 3** | `18H` | `19H` | `1AH` | `1BH` | `1CH` | `1DH` | `1EH` | `1FH` |

**Active bank selection** is governed by the **RS0 and RS1 bits of the Program Status Word (PSW)**:

| RS1 | RS0 | Selected Bank | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** (default after reset) | `00H` – `07H` |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | `08H` – `0FH` |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | `10H` – `17H` |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | `18H` – `1FH` |

This mechanism enables **fast hardware context switching** for interrupt service routines (ISRs) and subroutine parameter passing without pushing registers onto the stack.

### 2.5 The Stack — LIFO Memo Tray

The 8051 stack is **8-bit wide** (each `PUSH` writes one byte) and lives **inside the internal RAM** (it can technically be placed anywhere, but the reset default is `08H`). Key facts:

- **Stack Pointer (SP, SFR at `81H`):** Holds the address of the *last used* location. It **increments before write** (on `PUSH`) and **decrements after read** (on `POP`).
- **Direction:** Stack grows **upward** in memory (toward higher addresses).
- **Reset value of SP:** **`07H`** → The first `PUSH` increments SP to `08H` and stores data there, immediately colliding with Register Bank 1. Engineers almost always reinitialize SP (e.g., `MOV SP, #60H`) at the start of every program.
- **PUSH operand:** `PUSH direct` (always direct addressing — pushes the SFR or RAM byte).
- **POP operand:** `POP direct` (always direct addressing — pops into the SFR or RAM byte).

**PUSH execution micro-steps (8051):**
1. `SP ← SP + 1`
2. `RAM[SP] ← source_byte`

**POP execution micro-steps (8051):**
1. `destination_byte ← RAM[SP]`
2. `SP ← SP − 1`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the order. The 8051 stack **grows UP** (toward higher addresses) — opposite to many other architectures (x86, ARM). Mark this clearly.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary / Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addressable program memory | $2^{n}$ where $n$ = PC width | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB** | Determines max firmware size |
| Addressable external data memory | $2^{16}$ via DPTR (DPL + DPH) | **64 KB** | Max external RAM/IO chip count |
| Internal RAM size | $2^{7} = 128$ bytes | Addresses `00H`–`7FH` | Variable storage limit |
| SFR space size | $2^{7} = 128$ bytes | Addresses `80H`–`FFH` | Hardware control registers |
| Bit-addressable space | $16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 128$ bits | Addresses `20H`–`2FH`; bit addresses `00H`–`7FH` | Boolean flag manipulation |
| Register Banks | $4 \text{ banks} \times 8 \text{ registers} = 32$ bytes | `00H`–`1FH` | Hardware context switching |
| Bank R0 address | $\text{Base} = \text{Bank}_{\text{num}} \times 8$ | e.g., Bank 2 R0 = `10H` | Direct address computation |
| Bit address from byte | $\text{bit\_addr} = (\text{byte} - 20H) \times 8 + n$ | $n \in [0, 7]$ | Assembly-level bit manipulation |
| Stack pointer after k PUSHes | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + k$ | 8-bit, wraps at `FFH` → `00H` | Stack depth budgeting |
| Stack depth available | $255 - \text{SP}_{\text{init}}$ bytes | Default: $255 - 60H = 159$ bytes | Recursion / nested calls limit |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Register Banks** enable *zero-overhead task switching* in small RTOS kernels running on 8051 (e.g., RTX-51 Tiny uses Bank 1 for task contexts).
- **Bit-addressable RAM** allows direct `SETB` / `CLR` operations on individual I/O pins and flags, emulating a one-bit ALU — extremely useful for control logic in industrial automation.
- **Stack discipline** determines interrupt latency; mis-managed stacks cause catastrophic firmware crashes in automotive ECUs and medical devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Addressable Program Memory

The 8051 Program Counter is a 16-bit register. The number of unique addresses is:

$$
N_{\text{addresses}} = 2^{\text{PC width}} = 2^{16}
$$

Converting to kilobytes (1 KB = 1024 = $2^{10}$ bytes):

$$
N_{\text{bytes}} = 2^{16} = 2^{6} \times 2^{10} = 64 \times 2^{10} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

Therefore, the 8051 can address **64 KB** of program memory, spanning addresses `0000H` to `FFFFH`.

### 3.2 Derivation: Computing the Bit Address of a Bit-Addressable RAM Cell

**Problem:** Find the bit address (used in instructions like `SETB 07H`) of bit 3 of the byte at internal RAM location `2AH`.

**Step 1 — Identify the byte range.** Bit-addressable bytes live in `20H` to `2FH`. Our byte `2AH` lies in this range, so it is bit-addressable.

**Step 2 — Apply the bit-address formula.**

$$
\text{bit\_address} = \left(\text{byte\_address} - 20H\right) \times 8 + n
$$

where $n$ is the bit position (0 = LSB, 7 = MSB).

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{bit\_address} = \left(2AH - 20H\right) \times 8 + 3
$$

$$
\text{bit\_address} = \left(0AH\right) \times 8 + 3
$$

$$
\text{bit\_address} = 0AH \times 8 + 3
$$

**Step 4 — Convert `0AH` to decimal.**

$$
0AH = 10_{10}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 8.**

$$
10 \times 8 = 80 = 50H
$$

**Step 6 — Add the bit offset.**

$$
50H + 3 = 53H
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Bit address} = 53H}
$$

This means the assembler treats `SETB 53H` as a single-bit write to internal RAM location `2AH`, bit 3.

### 3.3 Derivation: Register Bank Switching via PSW

**Problem:** The current PSW value is `18H`. Determine which Register Bank is active.

**Step 1 — Convert PSW to binary.**

$$
18H = 0001 \, 1000_2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the PSW bit layout.**

The PSW (`D0H`) is an 8-bit SFR with the following bit positions:

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **CY** | **AC** | **F0** | **RS1** | **RS0** | **OV** | — | **P** |

**Step 3 — Read RS1 and RS0.**

From `0001 1000` we read:

- Bit 4 (RS1) = **1**
- Bit 3 (RS0) = **0**

**Step 4 — Map to bank number.**

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** |

Therefore, **Register Bank 2 is active**, and any instruction like `MOV A, R3` now accesses internal RAM address `13H` (since Bank 2 R3 = `10H` + 3 = `13H`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship between Bank number $B$ and the address of its R0 register is:
> $$\text{Addr}(R0_{\text{Bank } B}) = B \times 8$$
> Validated for Bank 2: $2 \times 8 = 16 = 10H$. ✓

### 3.4 Derivation: Stack Pointer Behavior Across PUSH and POP

**Problem:** SP is initialized to `5FH`. Show the SP value and memory contents after executing:
```
PUSH 30H      ; pushes contents of RAM[30H] (= 0AAH) onto stack
PUSH 31H      ; pushes contents of RAM[31H] (= 0BBH) onto stack
POP  32H      ; pops top of stack into RAM[32H]
```

**Step 1 — Initial state.**

$$
\text{SP} = 5FH
$$

**Step 2 — Execute `PUSH 30H` (data = `0AAH`).**

Step 2a: `SP ← SP + 1 = 60H`.
Step 2b: `RAM[60H] ← 0AAH`.

Result:

$$
\text{SP} = 60H, \quad \text{RAM[60H]} = \text{0AAH}
$$

**Step 3 — Execute `PUSH 31H` (data = `0BBH`).**

Step 3a: `SP ← SP + 1 = 61H`.
Step 3b: `RAM[61H] ← 0BBH`.

Result:

$$
\text{SP} = 61H, \quad \text{RAM[61H]} = \text{0BBH}
$$

**Step 4 — Execute `POP 32H` (read top of stack).**

Step 4a: `RAM[32H] ← RAM[SP] = RAM[61H] = 0BBH`.
Step 4b: `SP ← SP − 1 = 60H`.

Final state:

$$
\text{SP} = 60H, \quad \text{RAM[32H]} = \text{0BBH}, \quad \text{RAM[61H]} = \text{0BBH (still there, but logically popped)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 stack does *not* clear the popped memory — it only decrements the SP. Engineers exploiting this for "stack peek" should be aware that the data remains until overwritten.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing RAM, ROM, Register Banks, and Stack

| Feature | RAM (Data Memory) | ROM (Program Memory) | Register Banks | Stack |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volatility** | Volatile | Non-Volatile | Volatile | Volatile |
| **Access Instruction** | `MOV`, `XCH` | `MOVC` (read-only) | `MOV A, Rn` (direct) | `PUSH` / `POP` / `CALL` / `RET` |
| **Address Space** | Internal 128 B + Ext 64 KB | Internal 4 KB + Ext 64 KB | 32 B (`00H`–`1FH`) | User-defined in internal RAM |
| **Width** | 8-bit | 8-bit (instructions) | 8-bit | 8-bit |
| **Address Pointer** | R0/R1 (R0-R1 indirect), DPTR | PC | PSW[RS1:RS0] | SP |
| **Writes Allowed** | Yes | No (in basic 8051) | Yes (to active bank) | Yes (PUSH only) |
| **Primary Role** | Variable storage | Firmware code | Fast ALU operands | Subroutine linkage, ISR context |
| **Speed Class** | Fast (1–2 cycles) | Fast (1–2 cycles) | Fastest (1 cycle) | Moderate (2 cycles per PUSH/POP) |
| **Reset Default State** | Undefined contents | Code execution begins at `0000H` | Bank 0 active | SP = `07H` |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Address Boundary

**Problem:** An 8051 system uses an external ROM of size 32 KB and an external RAM of size 16 KB, both mapped contiguously. Find the ending address of each memory chip.

**External ROM (32 KB):**

$$
\text{End address} = \text{Size in bytes} - 1 = 32 \times 1024 - 1
$$

$$
32 \times 1024 = 32{,}768 = 8000H
$$

$$
\text{End address} = 8000H - 1 = 7FFFH
$$

So the ROM occupies `0000H` to `7FFFH`.

**External RAM (16 KB):**

$$
16 \times 1024 = 16{,}384 = 4000H
$$

$$
\text{End address} = 4000H - 1 = 3FFFH
$$

So the RAM occupies `0000H` to `3FFFH` in the external data space (note: this is a *separate* address space from program memory, accessed via `MOVX`).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8051 Memory Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cpuCore["CPU CORE"]
        aluBlock["ALU"]
        accBlock["Accumulator A"]
        bBlock["B Register"]
        pcBlock["16-bit Program Counter PC"]
        dptrBlock["16-bit DPTR DPH + DPL"]
        spBlock["8-bit Stack Pointer SP"]
        pswBlock["PSW with RS1 RS0"]
    end

    subgraph progMem["PROGRAM MEMORY (ROM)"]
        intROM["Internal 4 KB Mask ROM 0000H-0FFFH"]
        extROM["External ROM up to 64 KB 1000H-FFFFH"]
    end

    subgraph dataMem["DATA MEMORY (RAM)"]
        subgraph intRAM["INTERNAL RAM 128 B 00H-7FH"]
            bank0["Bank 0 00H-07H"]
            bank1["Bank 1 08H-0FH"]
            bank2["Bank 2 10H-17H"]
            bank3["Bank 3 18H-1FH"]
            bitZone["Bit-Addressable 20H-2FH"]
            gpRAM["General Purpose 30H-7FH"]
        end
        subgraph sfrSpace["SFR SPACE 80H-FFH"]
            sfrA["A at E0H"]
            sfrB["B at F0H"]
            sfrPSW["PSW at D0H"]
            sfrSP["SP at 81H"]
            sfrP0["P0 at 80H"]
            sfrP1["P1 at 90H"]
            sfrP2["P2 at A0H"]
            sfrP3["P3 at B0H"]
        end
        extRAM["External RAM up to 64 KB via MOVX"]
    end

    pcBlock -- "fetches opcode" --> progMem
    cpuCore -- "read write" --> intRAM
    cpuCore -- "read write direct only" --> sfrSpace
    dptrBlock -- "MOVX address" --> extRAM
    spBlock -- "PUSH POP target" --> intRAM
    pswBlock -- "selects active" --> bank0
    pswBlock -- "selects active" --> bank1
    pswBlock -- "selects active" --> bank2
    pswBlock -- "selects active" --> bank3
```

### 4.2 Register Bank Selection Flow (PSW-Driven)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```
