```mermaid
    start(["CPU Reset or RS1 RS0 change"]) --> readPSW["Read PSW bits 4 and 3"]
    readPSW --> decide{"RS1 RS0 value"}
    decide -- "0 0" --> b0["Activate Bank 0\nR0-R7 at 00H-07H"]
    decide -- "0 1" --> b1["Activate Bank 1\nR0-R7 at 08H-0FH"]
    decide -- "1 0" --> b2["Activate Bank 2\nR0-R7 at 10H-17H"]
    decide -- "1 1" --> b3["Activate Bank 3\nR0-R7 at 18H-1FH"]
    b0 --> exec["All MOV A Rn access uses\nselected bank addresses"]
    b1 --> exec
    b2 --> exec
    b3 --> exec
```

### 4.3 Stack Push and Pop Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph phaseA["INITIAL STATE"]
        sA1["SP = 5FH"]
        sA2["RAM 60H to 7FH = EMPTY"]
    end
    subgraph phaseB["AFTER PUSH 30H where RAM30 equals 0AAH"]
        sB1["Step 1 SP becomes 60H"]
        sB2["Step 2 RAM60H becomes 0AAH"]
        sB3["SP = 60H points to 0AAH"]
    end
    subgraph phaseC["AFTER PUSH 31H where RAM31 equals 0BBH"]
        sC1["Step 1 SP becomes 61H"]
        sC2["Step 2 RAM61H becomes 0BBH"]
        sC3["SP = 61H points to 0BBH top of stack"]
    end
    subgraph phaseD["AFTER POP 32H"]
        sD1["Step 1 RAM32H gets 0BBH"]
        sD2["Step 2 SP decrements to 60H"]
        sD3["SP = 60H now points to 0AAH"]
    end
    phaseA --> phaseB
    phaseB --> phaseC
    phaseC --> phaseD
```

### 4.4 ROM Evolution Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["MASK ROM\nFuses blown at fabrication\nCheapest per bit, zero flexibility"]
    layer2["PROM\nOne-time programmable by user\nFuse-based"]
    layer3["EPROM\nUV erasable, quartz window\nReprogrammable after erase"]
    layer4["EEPROM\nByte-wise electrical erase write\nUsed for small calibration data"]
    layer5["FLASH ROM\nBlock-wise electrical erase write\nUsed in modern 8051 derivatives\nInternal 4 KB to 64 KB"]
    layer1 --> layer2 --> layer3 --> layer4 --> layer5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (e.g., `sA1`, `phaseB`, `layer5`). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node IDs. Labels are kept in raw uppercase / mixed-case text without bold or italic markdown tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Differentiate between Harvard and Von Neumann architectures with respect to the 8051 microcontroller.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Von Neumann | Harvard (8051) |
|---|---|---|
| Bus structure | Single shared bus for instructions and data | **Separate buses** for program (ROM) and data (RAM) |
| Fetch + Execute | Sequential (one at a time) | **Simultaneous** possible |
| Speed | Slower (bottleneck) | **Faster** (parallel access) |
| Complexity | Simpler hardware | More complex, more silicon |
| 8051 Compliance | No | **Yes (Modified Harvard)** — code can read constants from code memory via `MOVC` |

*Marking pattern:* 1 mark for each correct distinguishing row, capped at 3 marks. Award partial credit for "separate bus" being mentioned.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the default value of the Stack Pointer (SP) in the 8051 after a system reset, and why is it considered unsafe for most programs?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Default value:** The SP is reset to **`07H`** (pointing to internal RAM location `07H`, which is the last byte of Register Bank 0). **(1 mark)**
2. **First PUSH behavior:** Any `PUSH` instruction first increments SP to `08H` and writes there, which **overlaps with Register Bank 1** (`08H`–`0FH`). **(1 mark)**
3. **Why unsafe:** Without explicit reinitialization, subroutine calls and interrupts will corrupt R0–R7 of Bank 1, causing unpredictable program behavior. Engineers always write `MOV SP, #60H` (or similar) in the initialization routine to move the stack safely into general-purpose RAM. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Question A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal RAM organization of the 8051 microcontroller**. List the three distinct zones and state the size of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **four Register Banks** of the 8051 in detail. How are they selected at runtime? Demonstrate the use of Register Bank 1 for an interrupt service routine. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The 8051 contains **128 bytes of internal RAM** mapped from address `00H` to `7FH`. This RAM is partitioned into three functional zones:

1. **Register Banks Zone (`00H`–`1FH`, 32 bytes):** Contains four register banks (Bank 0 to Bank 3), each holding eight 8-bit registers R0–R7. Bank 0 occupies `00H`–`07H`, Bank 1 occupies `08H`–`0FH`, Bank 2 occupies `10H`–`17H`, and Bank 3 occupies `18H`–`1FH`. Only one bank is active at a time, selected by RS1:RS0 bits of the PSW. **(2 marks)**

2. **Bit-Addressable Zone (`20H`–`2FH`, 16 bytes):** Provides 128 individually addressable bits (`00H`–`7FH` of bit space). This allows single-bit instructions like `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL`, and `ORL` to operate on individual flags without disturbing the rest of the byte. The formula for the bit address of bit *n* of byte *B* (where $20H \le B \le 2FH$) is:
   $$\text{bit\_address} = (B - 20H) \times 8 + n$$
   **(2 marks)**

3. **General-Purpose RAM Zone (`30H`–`7FH`, 80 bytes):** Used for user variables, arrays, the stack, and scratchpad storage. Most application-level data structures are placed here. **(2 marks)**

**Diagram (1 mark):**

| `00H`–`1FH` | `20H`–`2FH` | `30H`–`7FH` | `80H`–`FFH` |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 Register Banks | Bit-Addressable | General Purpose | SFR Space (not internal RAM, shown for completeness) |

**(2 marks)** for clearly describing the role of each zone and stating sizes: **32 + 16 + 80 = 128 bytes**.

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Concept (2 marks):** The 8051 dedicates 32 bytes (`00H`–`1FH`) to **four Register Banks**, each comprising eight 8-bit general-purpose registers labelled R0 through R7. The active bank is selected by the **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)** bits of the Program Status Word:

- `00` → Bank 0 (default after reset).
- `01` → Bank 1.
- `10` → Bank 2.
- `11` → Bank 3.

**Selection mechanism (2 marks):** The bits RS1:RS0 act as a 2-to-4 demultiplexer that routes the symbolic names R0–R7 to the correct physical RAM addresses. For example, if RS1:RS0 = `01`, then `R0` resolves to `08H`, `R3` resolves to `0BH`, and `R7` resolves to `0FH`.

**Demonstration — ISR using Bank 1 (3 marks):**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN        ; jump past ISR vector
        ORG 000BH        ; Timer 0 interrupt vector
        LJMP T0_ISR

        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV SP, #60H     ; relocate stack to safe area
        MOV PSW, #08H    ; select Register Bank 1 (RS1=0, RS0=1)
        MOV TMOD, #01H   ; Timer 0, mode 1
        MOV TH0, #0FCH
        MOV TL0, #18H
        SETB ET0         ; enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB EA          ; enable global interrupt
        SETB TR0         ; start timer
LOOP:   SJMP LOOP        ; idle main loop

T0_ISR:                  ; ISR runs in Bank 1 context
        MOV R0, #00H     ; uses R0 = 08H, R1 = 09H, etc.
        MOV R1, #0FFH
        ; critical work...
        RETI
```

**Why this works (1 mark):** When `T0_ISR` is entered, the hardware does not automatically save PSW. By ensuring the main code has switched to Bank 1 *before* the interrupt fires, the ISR's R0–R7 occupy `08H`–`0FH` and are physically distinct from main's R0–R7 (which would be in Bank 0, `00H`–`07H`). This provides **zero-cycle context switching** — no `PUSH` overhead.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **stack operation** in the 8051 with reference to the PUSH and POP instructions. Illustrate the working of the Stack Pointer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a diagram, describe the **ROM types** used in microcontroller-based systems. Compare Mask ROM, EPROM, EEPROM, and Flash ROM in a tabular form. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The 8051 stack is a region of internal RAM accessed in **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** order. The Stack Pointer (SP, SFR at `81H`) is an 8-bit register that holds the address of the **last used stack location**.

**PUSH execution (2 marks):**

1. `SP ← SP + 1` (pre-increment).
2. `RAM[SP] ← source_byte`.

The operand must use direct addressing (e.g., `PUSH A` is valid — it pushes the accumulator at `E0H`; `PUSH @R0` is **invalid**).

**POP execution (2 marks):**

1. `destination_byte ← RAM[SP]`.
2. `SP ← SP − 1` (post-decrement).

Again, direct addressing is mandatory.

**Illustration (2 marks):**

| SP Value | Memory Content | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| `5FH` (initial) | — | Stack is empty |
| `60H` (after `PUSH 30H`) | `RAM[60H] = 0AAH` | Top = `0AAH` |
| `61H` (after `PUSH 31H`) | `RAM[61H] = 0BBH` | Top = `0BBH` |
| `60H` (after `POP 32H`) | `RAM[32H] = 0BBH` | `0BBH` copied out, SP decremented |

**Key points (1 mark each, capped at 2):**
- Stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses) — opposite of x86.
- `CALL` pushes the 16-bit return address as two `PUSH` operations (PCL first, then PCH).
- `RETI` / `RET` pops two bytes to restore PC.

**Reset default and safety (1 mark):** SP resets to `07H`. Always reinitialize with `MOV SP, #60H` to avoid collisions with Bank 1.

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

A **ROM technology pyramid** (lowest flexibility to highest, cheapest to most expensive per bit):

1. **Mask ROM** — fuses blown during chip fabrication. Programmed once at the foundry, cannot be erased. **Cheapest** in high volume. Used in mass-produced consumer MCUs.

2. **PROM (Programmable ROM)** — User-programmable via a PROM programmer that burns fuses. One-time programmable, used in low-volume prototypes.

3. **EPROM (Erasable PROM)** — Uses floating-gate MOSFETs erased by **UV light** through a quartz window (≈ 20 minutes). Re-programmable. Used in 8051 development boards of the 1980s–90s.

4. **EEPROM (Electrically EPROM)** — Erased and written **byte-by-byte** electrically. Slow write (≈ 5 ms/byte), limited endurance (≈ 1 million cycles). Used for storing calibration constants, serial numbers, passwords.

5. **Flash ROM** — Erased and written in **blocks/sectors** electrically. Faster than EEPROM, higher density, lower endurance per cell (≈ 10 K–100 K cycles). **Used in modern 8051 derivatives** (AT89C51, AT89S52, P89V51RD2) as internal program memory.

**Tabular comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | Mask ROM | EPROM | EEPROM | Flash ROM |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Erasure method | Not erasable | **UV light** | **Electrical (byte)** | **Electrical (block)** |
| Re-programmable | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Write granularity | — | Whole chip | Byte | Block / sector |
| Cell size | Smallest | Large (1 transistor) | Large (2 transistors) | Small (1 transistor split-gate) |
| Typical density | High | Low | Low | **Highest** |
| Endurance (cycles) | — | ≈ 100 (UV erases) | ≈ $10^{6}$ | ≈ $10^{4}$–$10^{5}$ |
| Used in 8051? | Classic 8051 internal | Old dev kits | External (e.g., AT24C01) | **Modern 8051 internal program memory** |
| Cost per bit | **Lowest** | High | Highest | Low |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Memory Organization):**
> 1. **Confusing Harvard with Von-Neumann (loses 1–2 marks).** Always explicitly state "separate buses" for Harvard.
> 2. **Stating SP grows downward** — this is a classic x86 instinct; the 8051 stack grows **upward** (full mark lost if wrong).
> 3. **Forgetting that SFRs occupy `80H`–`FFH`**, which is *separate* from internal RAM. Many students wrongly say internal RAM is 256 bytes — it is only 128; the upper 128 is the SFR space.
> 4. **Mis-stating the bit-address formula** — the most common error is `(byte - 20H) + n` instead of `(byte - 20H) * 8 + n`.
> 5. **Default SP value confusion** — examiners explicitly ask "what is the reset value of SP?" Writing `00H` loses 1 mark; correct answer is `07H`.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The 8051 follows a **modified Harvard architecture**: **separate program (ROM)** and **data (RAM)** address spaces, each up to **64 KB**.
- **Internal RAM = 128 bytes** (`00H`–`7FH`), subdivided into **Register Banks (`00H`–`1FH`)**, **Bit-Addressable RAM (`20H`–`2FH`)**, and **General-Purpose RAM (`30H`–`7FH`)**.
- **Upper 128 bytes (`80H`–`FFH`)** belong to the **SFR space**, accessible only by direct addressing.
- **Four Register Banks** (Bank 0–3) of 8 registers each occupy `00H`–`1FH`. The active bank is selected by **PSW bits RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)**. This enables **fast, zero-overhead context switching** for ISRs.
- **Bit-addressable zone** (`20H`–`2FH`, 16 bytes) gives **128 individually addressable bits**; the bit address of bit *n* of byte *B* is $(B - 20H) \times 8 + n$.
- The **Stack** is a **LIFO** region in internal RAM, managed by the **8-bit SP** (SFR at `81H`). **`PUSH` increments SP first, then writes**; **`POP` reads, then decrements SP**. Stack grows **upward**.
- **Default SP value after reset = `07H`**, which is unsafe. Always reinitialize: `MOV SP, #60H` (or higher).
- **Program memory is non-volatile (ROM/Flash)** and holds the firmware; **data memory is volatile (SRAM)** and holds variables and stack.
- **ROM evolution:** Mask ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → **Flash** (used in modern 8051 variants).
- **External memory** (up to 64 KB code + 64 KB data) is accessed via ports P0/P2 and instructions `MOVC` (code) and `MOVX` (data).
- **PUSH/POP operands must use direct addressing** — `PUSH @R0` is illegal.
- **`CALL` pushes the 16-bit PC in two bytes (PCL first, PCH second)**, and `RET`/`RETI` pops them in reverse order.
- **Key 8051 instruction cycle counts:** `MOV direct,direct` = 2 cycles, `PUSH`/`POP` = 2 cycles, `MOVX` = 2 cycles (no wait state).
- **Bit address `00H`–`7FH` maps to byte `20H`–`2FH`**; bit address `80H`–`FFH` maps to certain SFRs (e.g., `ACC`, `B`, `PSW`, `P0`–`P3`) that are also bit-addressable.
- The 8051 can be configured (via `EA` pin) to execute from **internal 4 KB ROM (`0000H`–`0FFFH`)** or from **external ROM** when `EA = 0`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Function Registers (SFRs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Special Function Registers (SFRs) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are the dedicated, addressable 8-bit on-chip memory-mapped registers inside the **8051 Microcontroller** that control the core CPU functions, peripheral configuration, I/O ports, timers/counters, serial communication, and interrupt system. They occupy the **upper 128 bytes** of the **internal RAM (Data Memory)** space, from addresses **0x80 to 0xFF** (i.e., **128 bytes** of addressable space), and must be accessed using **direct addressing mode**. > [!NOTE] > The SFR memory map is distinct from the lower 128 bytes (0x00–0x7F), which is the **General Purpose RAM** (4 Register Banks, Bit-addressable area, and general scratchpad area). ### The SFR Memory Map — The 8051 "Control Room" | SFR Region | Address Range | Size | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **SFR Area** | **0x80 – 0xFF** | **128 bytes** | Only 21 are physically implemented; rest are reserved | | General Purpose RAM | 0x30 – 0x7F | 80 bytes | Scratchpad / user data | | Bit Addressable RAM | 0x20 – 0x2F | 16 bytes | Bit-level operations | | Register Banks R0–R7 | 0x00 – 0x1F | 32 bytes | 4 banks × 8 registers | ### Conceptual Analogy: "The Cockpit of an Aircraft" > [!TIP] > Think of the 8051 microcontroller as a **commercial aircraft**, and the **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** as the **instruments on the pilot's cockpit dashboard**. Each instrument (altimeter, fuel gauge, compass, throttle control) doesn't carry passengers — it *controls or monitors* a specific system. Similarly: > - **ACC (Accumulator)** → The main "throttle" (primary arithmetic workspace) > - **PC (Program Counter)** → The "navigation system" (where the aircraft is heading in code) > - **TMOD / TCON** → The "engine RPM gauges" (timer/counter control) > - **SCON** → The "radio communication panel" (serial port) > - **P0, P1, P2, P3** → The "landing gear & flaps" (physical I/O) > > Just as a pilot toggles cockpit switches, a programmer writes to SFRs to *configure and operate* the microcontroller's internal hardware. ### Why SFRs Exist (Engineering Rationale) In a Von-Neumann / modified Harvard 8051 architecture, **CPU control** and **peripheral control** must share the same address bus but require **dedicated, fast, single-cycle access** rather than going through external memory. SFRs provide this *zero-wait-state, on-chip hardware control* mechanism. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Highlight:** Only the SFRs whose address ends in **0x0 or 0x8** (i.e., addresses divisible by 8) are **bit-addressable**. Example: `P0` at 0x80, `P1` at 0x90, `P2` at 0xA0, `P3` at 0xB0, etc. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of the 21 Implemented SFRs The 8051 has **128 SFR address slots** (0x80–0xFF) but only **21 are physically present**. The remaining addresses are **reserved/unused** and accessing them yields undefined behavior. > [!NOTE] > **Total Implemented SFRs = 21** (Memorize this number — frequently asked in KTU exams). ### 2.2 The 21 Implemented SFRs — Complete List | # | SFR Name | Address (Hex) | Bit-Addressable? | Function | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **A (Accumulator)** | **0xE0** | ✅ Yes | Primary ALU operand; most arithmetic ops use it | | 2 | **B Register** | **0xF0** | ✅ Yes | Used in `MUL` / `DIV` instructions | | 3 | **PSW (Program Status Word)** | **0xD0** | ✅ Yes | Flags (CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, -, P) | | 4 | **SP (Stack Pointer)** | **0x81** | ❌ No | Points to top of stack (8-bit, initialized to 0x07) | | 5 | **DPTR (DPL+DPH)** | **0x82, 0x83** | ❌ No | 16-bit data pointer for `MOVX` | | 6 | **P0** | **0x80** | ✅ Yes | Port 0 latch | | 7 | **P1** | **0x90** | ✅ Yes | Port 1 latch | | 8 | **P2** | **0xA0** | ✅ Yes | Port 2 latch | | 9 | **P3** | **0xB0** | ✅ Yes | Port 3 latch (alternate functions) | | 10 | **IP (Interrupt Priority)** | **0xB8** | ✅ Yes | Interrupt priority levels | | 11 | **IE (Interrupt Enable)** | **0xA8** | ✅ Yes | Enable/disable interrupts | | 12 | **TMOD** | **0x89** | ❌ No | Timer Mode register | | 13 | **TCON** | **0x88** | ✅ Yes | Timer Control register | | 14 | **TH0** | **0x8C** | ❌ No | Timer 0 High byte | | 15 | **TL0** | **0x8A** | ❌ No | Timer 0 Low byte | | 16 | **TH1** | **0x8D** | ❌ No | Timer 1 High byte | | 17 | **TL1** | **0x8B** | ❌ No | Timer 1 Low byte | | 18 | **SCON** | **0x98** | ✅ Yes | Serial Control register | | 19 | **SBUF** | **0x99** | ❌ No | Serial Buffer (Tx & Rx share address) | | 20 | **PCON** | **0x87** | ❌ No | Power Control (SMOD bit) | | 21 | **(reserved)** | — | — | Some texts count only 21 as listed above | ### 2.3 The Program Status Word (PSW) — Bit-by-Bit Breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > **PSW is the most frequently examined SFR in KTU Module 1 questions.** Memorize this bit map: | Bit | Symbol | Address (bit) | Function | |---|---|---|---| | 7 | **CY** | 0xD7 | Carry Flag — set on unsigned overflow/borrow | | 6 | **AC** | 0xD6 | Auxiliary Carry — used in BCD arithmetic | | 5 | **F0** | 0xD5 | User-defined general-purpose flag | | 4 | **RS1** | 0xD4 | Register Bank Select bit 1 | | 3 | **RS0** | 0xD3 | Register Bank Select bit 0 | | 2 | **OV** | 0xD2 | Overflow Flag — signed arithmetic overflow | | 1 | **—** | 0xD1 | Reserved (undefined) | | 0 | **P** | 0xD0 | Parity Flag (set if odd number of 1s in ACC) | ### 2.4 Register Bank Selection Logic (RS1, RS0) The 32 bytes from 0x00–0x1F are divided into **4 register banks** of 8 bytes each. Selection is done via the RS1:RS0 bits of PSW. | RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 0x00 – 0x07 | | 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 0x08 – 0x0F | | 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 0x10 – 0x17 | | 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 0x18 – 0x1F | > [!NOTE] > **Default bank on RESET = Bank 0** (Stack Pointer is initialized to **0x07**, which is the *next location after Bank 0*, ensuring the first `PUSH` writes to 0x08 — i.e., Bank 1's R0). ### 2.5 Stack Pointer (SP) and Reset Behavior - SP is an **8-bit register** addressing the **internal RAM only** (not SFR or external). - **On RESET, SP = 0x07** (this is the only SFR with a non-zero reset value). - After reset, the first `PUSH` will write to address **0x08** (start of Bank 1). - For a clean C-style stack, engineers typically execute `MOV SP, #0x2F` or `MOV SP, #0x5F` at the start of code. ### 2.6 Data Pointer (DPTR) — The 16-Bit SFR Pair The 8051 is an **8-bit** machine but requires a 16-bit address for `MOVX` (external memory access) and code-table lookups (`MOVC A, @A+DPTR`). **DPTR = DPH (high byte) : DPL (low byte)** = two SFRs concatenated. $$\text{DPTR} = (\text{DPH} \ll 8) \cup \text{DPL}$$ | SFR | Address | Role | |---|---|---| | DPL | 0x82 | Low byte of DPTR | | DPH | 0x83 | High byte of DPTR | > [!TIP] > KTU favorite trick question: *"Can we read/write DPH and DPL independently?"* — **Yes**, because they occupy different SFR addresses (0x83 and 0x82 respectively). ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Rule | Expression | Notes | |---|---|---| | Stack pointer first PUSH target | `SP_after_PUSH = 0x07 + 1 = 0x08` | On reset, SP = 0x07 | | DPTR construction | `DPTR = DPH × 256 + DPL` | Or: `(DPH << 8) | DPL` | | Register bank base address | `Base = 8 × (2·RS1 + RS0)` | 0, 8, 16, or 24 | | Parity bit computation | `P = 1` if ACC has odd number of 1s | Always reflects ACC | | BCD correction trigger | `DA A` instruction uses AC & CY | AC = carry between bits 3 and 4 | | Timer tick formula (Machine Cycle) | $T_{\text{machine}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | For 11.0592 MHz: ≈ **1.085 µs** | | Timer overflow time (Mode 1) | $T_{\text{overflow}} = (65536 - \text{Timer\_Value}) \times T_{\text{machine}}$ | 16-bit timer | | Baud rate for Timer 1 Mode 2 | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | Auto-reload mode | | SFR region size | **128 bytes** (0x80–0xFF) | Only 21 implemented | | Bit-addressable SFRs | Address = 0xX0 or 0xX8 | 16 such SFRs | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — SFR Categories Grouped by Function To make the 21 SFRs intuitive for KTU exams, group them by **engineering function**: ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ SFR FUNCTIONAL CLUSTERS │ ├──────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ CPU Core │ ACC(0xE0), B(0xF0), PSW(0xD0), SP(0x81), │ │ │ DPTR(0x82/83) │ ├──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ I/O Ports │ P0(0x80), P1(0x90), P2(0xA0), P3(0xB0) │ ├──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Timers │ TMOD(0x89), TCON(0x88), │ │ │ TH0(0x8C), TL0(0x8A), │ │ │ TH1(0x8D), TL1(0x8B) │ ├──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Serial Port │ SCON(0x98), SBUF(0x99), PCON(0x87) │ ├──────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Interrupts │ IE(0xA8), IP(0xB8) │ └──────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### 3.2 Detailed Bit Map of the Program Status Word (PSW) **Derivation of PSW from operational behavior:** After any arithmetic or logical instruction in the 8051, the hardware automatically updates relevant PSW bits. The mapping is: | Instruction Class | CY updated? | AC updated? | OV updated? | P updated? | |---|---|---|---|---| | `ADD`, `ADDC` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | `SUBB` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | `MUL` (A×B) | ✅ (always 0; cleared) | ❌ | ✅ (overflow indicator) | ✅ | | `DIV` (A/B) | ✅ (always 0; cleared) | ❌ | ✅ (divide-by-zero) | ✅ | | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | `INC`, `DEC` | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | `DA A` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Logical instructions (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`) do **NOT** affect the **Carry flag (CY)**, but they **DO** affect the **Parity flag (P)**. The Carry flag is preserved across logical ops, not cleared. ### 3.3 Derivation: How the Parity Flag (P) is Computed > [!NOTE] > The 8051's P flag uses **odd parity** — it is set to 1 when the Accumulator contains an **odd** number of '1' bits. **Step-by-step procedure:** **Step 1:** After every instruction that writes to ACC, the hardware counts the number of set bits ('1's) in the 8-bit ACC value. **Step 2:** This count is computed using a hardware parity tree (XOR of all 8 bits). $$\text{P}_{\text{hardware}} = b_7 \oplus b_6 \oplus b_5 \oplus b_4 \oplus b_3 \oplus b_2 \oplus b_1 \oplus b_0$$ **Step 3:** The P flag bit in PSW is updated with this result. **Worked Example:** Suppose after `MOV A, #0x53`: - Binary of 0x53 = `0101 0011` - Number of 1s = 4 (even) - Therefore **P = 0** (because odd parity flag = 0 when count is even) Suppose after `MOV A, #0x47`: - Binary of 0x47 = `0100 0111` - Number of 1s = 4 (even) - Therefore **P = 0** Suppose after `MOV A, #0xA5`: - Binary of 0xA5 = `1010 0101` - Number of 1s = 4 (even) - Therefore **P = 0** Suppose after `MOV A, #0xA9`: - Binary of 0xA9 = `1010 1001` - Number of 1s = 4 (even) - Therefore **P = 0** Now consider `MOV A, #0xE3`: - Binary of 0xE3 = `1110 0011` - Number of 1s = **5** (odd) - Therefore **P = 1** ✅ > [!TIP] > **Easy rule to remember:** P = 1 ⟺ ACC has odd number of 1s. P = 0 ⟺ ACC has even number of 1s. ### 3.4 Derivation: Register Bank Switching in 8051 **Scenario:** A programmer wants to use Bank 2. **Step 1:** Identify the required RS1, RS0 combination. From the table: Bank 2 requires **RS1 = 1, RS0 = 0**. **Step 2:** Construct the PSW value. The current PSW bits (other than RS1, RS0) must be preserved. **Step 3:** Use the `SETB` and `CLR` bit instructions: ``` SETB RS1 ; Set bit 4 of PSW → RS1 = 1 CLR RS0 ; Clear bit 3 of PSW → RS0 = 0 ``` **Step 4:** After these two instructions, any reference to `R0` will access internal RAM address `0x10` (Bank 2's R0) instead of `0x00` (Bank 0's R0). **Mathematical Derivation of Bank Base Address:** $$\text{Base\_Address} = (\text{RS1} \times 2 + \text{RS0}) \times 8$$ **For Bank 2:** Base = (1×2 + 0) × 8 = **16 = 0x10** ✅ ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Bit-Addressable vs Non-Bit-Addressable SFRs | SFR Address Form | Bit-Addressable? | Count | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | 0xX0 or 0xX8 (X = 0–F) | ✅ Yes | 16 | A, B, PSW, P0, P1, P2, P3, IP, IE, TCON, SCON, ... | | Other addresses | ❌ No | 5 (or rest) | SP (0x81), DPL/DPH, TMOD, TH0/TL0/TH1/TL1, SBUF, PCON | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Fact:** There are exactly **16 bit-addressable SFRs** (those ending in 0x0 or 0x8 hex). The remaining accessible SFRs can only be manipulated at the byte level. ### 3.6 Real-World Engineering Utility SFRs are the **only mechanism** by which application software interacts with the 8051's hardware peripherals. Real-world examples: 1. **Industrial automation:** Writing to `P1` toggles relays controlling conveyor belts. 2. **Consumer electronics:** Configuring `TMOD` to generate precise time delays for washing-machine cycles. 3. **Communication devices:** Setting `SCON` and `SBUF` baud rates for RS-232 communication with a PC. 4. **Automotive ECUs:** Using `IE` and `IP` to prioritize engine sensor interrupts over infotainment interrupts. 5. **Medical devices:** Toggling `PCON` bits to enter **Idle** or **Power-Down** mode for battery longevity. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 8051 Internal Data Memory Organization ```mermaid graph TD A[Internal Data Memory
Total 256 Bytes] --> B[Lower 128 Bytes
0x00 to 0x7F] A --> C[Upper 128 Bytes
0x80 to 0xFF] B --> B1[Register Banks R0-R7
0x00 to 0x1F] B1 --> B1a[Bank 0: 0x00-0x07] B1 --> B1b[Bank 1: 0x08-0x0F] B1 --> B1c[Bank 2: 0x10-0x17] B1 --> B1d[Bank 3: 0x18-0x1F] B --> B2[Bit Addressable Area
0x20 to 0x2F
16 bytes = 128 bits] B --> B3[General Purpose RAM
0x30 to 0x7F
80 bytes scratchpad] C --> C1[Special Function Registers
SFR Area
0x80 to 0xFF] C1 --> C1a[Bit Addressable SFRs
0xX0 and 0xX8
16 SFRs] C1 --> C1b[Non-Bit SFRs
5 SFRs] C1 --> C1c[Reserved Unused
~107 locations] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — SFR Functional Clustering ```mermaid graph LR CPU[CPU Core SFRs] --> CPU1[A: 0xE0] CPU --> CPU2[B: 0xF0] CPU --> CPU3[PSW: 0xD0] CPU --> CPU4[SP: 0x81] CPU --> CPU5[DPTR: 0x82/83] IO[I/O Port SFRs] --> IO1[P0: 0x80] IO --> IO2[P1: 0x90] IO --> IO3[P2: 0xA0] IO --> IO4[P3: 0xB0] TMR[Timer SFRs] --> T1[TMOD: 0x89] TMR --> T2[TCON: 0x88] TMR --> T3[TH0-TL0: 0x8C-0x8A] TMR --> T4[TH1-TL1: 0x8D-0x8B] SER[Serial SFRs] --> S1[SCON: 0x98] SER --> S2[SBUF: 0x99] SER --> S3[PCON: 0x87] INT[Interrupt SFRs] --> I1[IE: 0xA8] INT --> I2[IP: 0xB8] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — PSW Bit Architecture ```mermaid graph TB PSW[PSW Register
Address: 0xD0
Bit-Addressable] --> Bit7[B7: CY
Carry Flag] PSW --> Bit6[B6: AC
Auxiliary Carry] PSW --> Bit5[B5: F0
User Flag] PSW --> Bit4[B4: RS1
Bank Select 1] PSW --> Bit3[B3: RS0
Bank Select 0] PSW --> Bit2[B2: OV
Overflow Flag] PSW --> Bit1[B1: Reserved] PSW --> Bit0[B0: P
Parity Flag] Bit4 --> BankSel[Bank Selection Logic] Bit3 --> BankSel BankSel --> B0[Bank 0: RS1=0 RS0=0] BankSel --> B1[Bank 1: RS1=0 RS0=1] BankSel --> B2[Bank 2: RS1=1 RS0=0] BankSel --> B3[Bank 3: RS1=1 RS0=1] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Port 3 Alternate Functions ```mermaid graph TD P3[P3 SFR
Address: 0xB0
Bit-Addressable] --> P30[P3.0: RxD
Serial Input] P3 --> P31[P3.1: TxD
Serial Output] P3 --> P32[P3.2: INT0
External Interrupt 0] P3 --> P33[P3.3: INT1
External Interrupt 1] P3 --> P34[P3.4: T0
Timer 0 Input] P3 --> P35[P3.5: T1
Timer 1 Input] P3 --> P36[P3.6: WR
External Write Strobe] P3 --> P37[P3.7: RD
External Read Strobe] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Insight:** Port 3 pins have **dual functions** — they serve as general-purpose I/O OR as alternate function pins (interrupts, timers, serial, external memory control). The alternate functions take precedence when the corresponding peripheral is enabled. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define Special Function Registers (SFRs) in the 8051 microcontroller. Mention the address range of the SFR area and the total number of SFRs implemented. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **SFRs** are dedicated 8-bit on-chip registers in the 8051 microcontroller used to control CPU operations, I/O ports, timers, serial communication, and interrupt system. They are memory-mapped in the **internal RAM** occupying addresses **0x80 to 0xFF** (128 bytes of address space). Out of these 128 address locations, only **21 SFRs are physically implemented**; the remaining locations are reserved and accessing them gives undefined results. SFRs are accessed using **direct addressing mode**. Examples include ACC (0xE0), B (0xF0), PSW (0xD0), SP (0x81), DPTR (0x82/83), P0–P3, TMOD, TCON, SCON, SBUF, IE, IP, and PCON. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** Explain the function of the PSW register. List its bits and their purposes. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The **Program Status Word (PSW)** at address **0xD0** is a bit-addressable SFR that holds the CPU's status flags. Its 8 bits are: > > | Bit | Symbol | Function | > |---|---|---| > | 7 | CY | Carry Flag | > | 6 | AC | Auxiliary Carry (for BCD) | > | 5 | F0 | User-defined flag | > | 4 | RS1 | Register Bank Select 1 | > | 3 | RS0 | Register Bank Select 0 | > | 2 | OV | Overflow Flag (signed) | > | 1 | — | Reserved | > | 0 | P | Parity Flag (odd parity) | > > The RS1:RS0 bits select one of 4 register banks, while CY, AC, OV, and P reflect ALU operation results. --- ### Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module 1 Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal data memory organization** of the 8051 microcontroller. Differentiate between the lower 128 bytes and upper 128 bytes. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The 8051 has **256 bytes of internal RAM** split into two distinct regions: **(i) Lower 128 Bytes (0x00 – 0x7F):** This is the user-accessible general-purpose RAM divided into three sub-regions: 1. **Register Banks (0x00 – 0x1F):** Four banks (Bank 0–3) of 8 registers each (R0–R7), giving 32 bytes. Bank selection is controlled by RS1:RS0 bits of PSW. 2. **Bit-Addressable Area (0x20 – 0x2F):** 16 bytes that can be addressed both byte-wise and bit-wise, providing 128 individually addressable bits (bit addresses 0x00–0x7F). 3. **General Purpose Scratchpad (0x30 – 0x7F):** 80 bytes of user data storage. **(ii) Upper 128 Bytes (0x80 – 0xFF):** This is the **SFR region**, dedicated to CPU and peripheral control. Only 21 of these locations correspond to actual SFRs; the rest are reserved. The 21 implemented SFRs include ACC, B, PSW, SP, DPTR, P0–P3, IP, IE, TMOD, TCON, TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1, SCON, SBUF, and PCON. **Key Difference:** The lower 128 bytes are general-purpose user RAM, whereas the upper 128 bytes are exclusively for hardware control via SFRs and are accessed using direct addressing. --- **(b)** Explain the **PSW register** in detail. Show how the register banks are selected using RS1 and RS0 bits. If a program sets `RS1=1` and `RS0=0`, identify the address range of the active register bank. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **PSW Register (Address 0xD0, Bit-Addressable):** | Bit | Symbol | Address | Function | |---|---|---|---| | 7 | CY | 0xD7 | Carry Flag (unsigned overflow/borrow) | | 6 | AC | 0xD6 | Auxiliary Carry (BCD correction) | | 5 | F0 | 0xD5 | User flag | | 4 | RS1 | 0xD4 | Bank select MSB | | 3 | RS0 | 0xD3 | Bank select LSB | | 2 | OV | 0xD2 | Signed overflow | | 1 | — | 0xD1 | Reserved | | 0 | P | 0xD0 | Parity (odd parity of ACC) | **Register Bank Selection Logic:** The active register bank is determined by the two-bit code `RS1:RS0`: | RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address Range | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | 0x00 – 0x07 | | 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 0x08 – 0x0F | | 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 0x10 – 0x17 | | 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 0x18 – 0x1F | **Calculation for RS1=1, RS0=0:** $$\text{Base Address} = (1 \times 2 + 0) \times 8 = 16 = \text{0x10}$$ **Active Bank = Bank 2**, address range **0x10 – 0x17**. The registers R0–R7 of this bank occupy these addresses. This bank-switching is widely used in interrupt service routines to preserve context without explicit stack operations. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** What are **Special Function Registers (SFRs)**? List any **10 SFRs** with their addresses and primary functions. Explain why SFRs with addresses ending in 0 or 8 are bit-addressable. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** SFRs are special on-chip 8-bit registers in the 8051 that control core CPU and peripheral operations. They occupy addresses 0x80–0xFF of internal RAM. **Ten Important SFRs:** | # | SFR | Address | Function | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | A (ACC) | 0xE0 | Accumulator; main ALU operand | | 2 | B | 0xF0 | Used in MUL/DIV | | 3 | PSW | 0xD0 | Status flags | | 4 | SP | 0x81 | Stack Pointer | | 5 | DPTR | 0x82/83 | 16-bit data pointer | | 6 | P0 | 0x80 | Port 0 I/O latch | | 7 | P1 | 0x90 | Port 1 I/O latch | | 8 | P3 | 0xB0 | Port 3 with alternate functions | | 9 | TMOD | 0x89 | Timer mode configuration | | 10 | SCON | 0x98 | Serial port control | **Bit-Addressability Rule:** SFRs whose addresses are **multiples of 8** (i.e., end in hex 0x0 or 0x8) are bit-addressable. This is because the 8051's bit-addressing scheme uses a separate 16-byte bit-addressable area where each bit has a unique address (0x00–0x7F) — these addresses alias to the SFR bit positions only for SFRs at 0xX0/0xX8. There are exactly 16 such SFRs. --- **(b)** Explain the **Stack Pointer (SP)** and **Data Pointer (DPTR)** registers in detail. Why is the initial value of SP after reset equal to 0x07? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Stack Pointer (SP, Address 0x81):** - An 8-bit register that points to the top of the stack in **internal RAM**. - The stack grows **upward** (PUSH increments SP, POP decrements SP). - **Reset value = 0x07** (this is critical — SP is the only SFR with a non-zero reset value). - **Why 0x07?** The 8051 designer chose this so that the first `PUSH` instruction writes to address 0x08, which is the *start of Register Bank 1*. This effectively reserves Register Bank 0 (0x00–0x07) for the main program's R0–R7 by default, while leaving Banks 1–3 available for stack growth or interrupt context. - The first `PUSH` → SP becomes 0x08, data stored at 0x08. - Best practice: relocate SP to **0x2F** or higher to give stack more room. **Data Pointer (DPTR, Addresses 0x82 DPL + 0x83 DPH):** - A **16-bit** register formed by combining two 8-bit SFRs. - Used to access **external data memory** via `MOVX A, @DPTR` or `MOVX @DPTR, A`. - Also used in code-table lookups: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`. - Because the 8051 CPU is 8-bit, it cannot directly hold 16-bit addresses — DPTR solves this by splitting across two SFRs. $$\text{DPTR}_{16\text{-bit}} = (\text{DPH} \ll 8) \mid \text{DPL}$$ **Example:** To access external memory address 0x1234: ``` MOV DPH, #0x12 ; High byte MOV DPL, #0x34 ; Low byte MOVX A, @DPTR ; Read from 0x1234 ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing SFRs with General Purpose RAM:** Students often write that SFRs are at 0x00–0x7F. **Wrong.** SFRs are at **0x80–0xFF**. The lower region is user RAM. > > 2. **Counting SFRs incorrectly:** The 8051 has **21 implemented** SFRs out of 128 address slots. Writing "128 SFRs" loses marks. > > 3. **Forgetting DPTR is two SFRs:** DPTR is **not** a single SFR — it is the concatenation of DPL (0x82) and DPH (0x83). > > 4. **SP reset value:** Writing "SP = 0" is a common error. Correct answer: **SP = 0x07 after reset.** > > 5. **Bit-addressability logic:** Students often claim *all* SFRs are bit-addressable. Only **16 SFRs** (those at 0xX0/0xX8) are bit-addressable. > > 6. **PSW bits order:** Must be listed MSB to LSB (CY at bit 7, P at bit 0). Reversed order = 1 mark deduction. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Special Function Registers (SFRs):** - ✅ SFRs are 8-bit on-chip **memory-mapped registers** in 8051. - ✅ SFR address range = **0x80 to 0xFF** (128 bytes). - ✅ Only **21 SFRs** are physically implemented; rest are reserved. - ✅ SFRs are accessed using **direct addressing mode** only. - ✅ **Bit-addressable SFRs** = 16 (addresses 0xX0 or 0xX8 only). - ✅ **ACC (0xE0)** is the primary ALU operand and is bit-addressable. - ✅ **B register (0xF0)** is used only in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`. - ✅ **PSW (0xD0)** contains: CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, -, P. - ✅ **RS1:RS0** in PSW select register banks 0, 1, 2, or 3. - ✅ **SP (0x81)** is the **only SFR with non-zero reset value = 0x07**. - ✅ **DPTR = DPH(0x83) : DPL(0x82)** — a 16-bit address pointer. - ✅ **P0 (0x80)**, **P1 (0x90)**, **P2 (0xA0)**, **P3 (0xB0)** are the four I/O port latches. - ✅ **P3 has alternate functions**: RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD. - ✅ **P flag** uses **odd parity** (P=1 if ACC has odd number of 1s). - ✅ **CY (Carry)** is **preserved** across logical instructions (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`). - ✅ **OV (Overflow)** flag indicates **signed** overflow (for `ADD`/`SUBB`/`MUL`/`DIV`). - ✅ **AC (Auxiliary Carry)** is set on carry between bit 3 and bit 4; used in `DA A` BCD correction. - ✅ The lower 128 bytes (0x00–0x7F) are **user RAM**, NOT SFRs. - ✅ Register Bank 0 occupies 0x00–0x07; reset default = Bank 0. - ✅ Total internal RAM = 256 bytes (lower 128 + upper 128). - ✅ SFR names like `TMOD`, `SCON`, `IE`, `IP` are **not** bit-addressable (except SCON, IE, IP, TCON). - ✅ **Memory-mapped I/O:** Writing to P0–P3 SFRs directly controls physical pins. - ✅ KTU Module 1 typically asks: **list SFRs, PSW bits, register bank selection, or SP/DPTR behavior**. --- > **End of Notes — Special Function Registers (SFRs), Module 1, PBECT404**

topics.title: I/O Port Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** I/O Port Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, **I/O Port Organization** refers to the hardware structural arrangement of the four bidirectional 8-bit parallel I/O ports — **P0, P1, P2, and P3** — along with their corresponding **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** located in the internal RAM address space from **80H to 87H**.

Each port pin is a complex hybrid circuit that can function as:
- A **General Purpose I/O (GPIO) pin** for simple bit/byte-level digital interfacing.
- An **Alternate Function pin** for multiplexed signals (e.g., address/data bus, control signals, peripheral I/O).

The 8051 has **32 I/O lines**, grouped into **4 ports × 8 bits**, providing a total I/O throughput of **256 unique digital I/O combinations per port**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051's I/O structure is **NOT a simple latch**. Each port bit contains a D-type latch, an output driver, an input buffer, and associated control logic — a fact frequently asked in KTU Module 1 examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **4-bay toll booth plaza**:
- Each **bay (port)** has 8 **toll gates (pins)**.
- Each gate has **two lanes**:
  1. An **entry lane (Input)** — cars (data) come in.
  2. An **exit lane (Output)** — cars (data) go out.
- The **gatekeeper (latch)** decides which lane is active and locks the position until changed.
- **Port 0** is the "flexible" gate — it can act independently OR connect to a shared highway (the address/data bus) when external memory is needed.
- **Port 3** is the "multi-talented" gate — besides normal traffic, it can route to special services like fire trucks (interrupts) or ambulances (serial communication).
- **Ports 1 and 2** are the "reliable workers" — mostly used for straightforward I/O, with Port 2 also helping with upper address bits during memory access.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Number of I/O Ports** | **4 (P0, P1, P2, P3)** |
| **Bits per Port** | **8 bits (bidirectional)** |
| **Total I/O Lines** | **32 lines** |
| **Port SFR Addresses** | **80H (P0), 90H (P1), A0H (P2), B0H (P3)** |
| **Port Latch Reset Value** | **FFH (all 1s)** |
| **Output Drive Current (P1, P2, P3)** | **~10 mA per pin (sinking)** |

> [!NOTE]
> On **reset**, all port latches are initialized to **FFH (1111 1111)**, which configures all pins as **inputs** (ready to read external state without contention).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Structure of an 8051 Port Bit (General Architecture)

Each port bit consists of **three fundamental sub-circuits**:

1. **D-Type Latch (1-bit memory)** — Stores the value written by the CPU.
2. **Output Driver (FET pair)** — Drives the pin HIGH or LOW.
3. **Input Buffer (Tri-state)** — Reads the external pin state when port is configured as input.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — CPU writes to port latch:** The internal data bus loads a bit into the D-latch via the "Write to Latch" signal.
- **Step 2 — Latch output drives FETs:** The Q output of the latch controls the output FETs, which physically drive the pin voltage.
- **Step 3 — Reading the pin:** When the CPU executes an instruction like `MOV A, P1`, the "Read Pin" signal activates the input buffer, sampling the *actual* external pin state (not the latch).
- **Step 4 — Read-Modify-Write:** Instructions like `ANL P1, #0FH` or `INC P2` read from the **latch** (not the pin), perform the operation, and write back to the latch — this is crucial to avoid the **Read-Modify-Write Problem**.

### 2.2 Port-Specific Structural Analysis

#### **Port 0 (P0 — Address 80H)**

- **Output Structure:** **Open-drain configuration** (no internal pull-up resistor).
- **External Pull-up Requirement:** Needs **external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ)** when used as a general-purpose output.
- **Alternate Function:** **Multiplexed Lower Address/Data Bus (AD0–AD7)** during external memory access.
- **Control Signal:** Controlled by the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal — when ALE=1, port outputs lower address; when ALE=0, port acts as data bus.
- **Drive Capability:** Can drive **8 LS TTL inputs** without external pull-up (only in bus mode).

#### **Port 1 (P1 — Address 90H)**

- **Output Structure:** **Internal pull-up resistors (~30 kΩ)** + quasi-bidirectional output.
- **Alternate Function:** **None — Pure General Purpose I/O**.
- **Drive Capability:** Can drive **4 LS TTL inputs**.
- **Most Reliable Port:** No multiplexing, no alternate functions — simplest to use.

#### **Port 2 (P2 — Address A0H)**

- **Output Structure:** **Internal pull-up resistors + quasi-bidirectional driver**.
- **Alternate Function:** Provides **Higher Address Byte (A8–A15)** during external memory access.
- **Drive Capability:** Can drive **4 LS TTL inputs**.
- **Usage:** When accessing external memory > 256 bytes, P2 outputs upper address bits.

#### **Port 3 (P3 — Address B0H) — The Alternate Function Port**

Each pin has a **dedicated alternate function**:

| Pin | Alternate Function | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **P3.0** | **RxD** | Serial Data Receive |
| **P3.1** | **TxD** | Serial Data Transmit |
| **P3.2** | **INT0̄** | External Interrupt 0 (active low) |
| **P3.3** | **INT1̄** | External Interrupt 1 (active low) |
| **P3.4** | **T0** | Timer 0 External Input |
| **P3.5** | **T1** | Timer 1 External Input |
| **P3.6** | **WR̄** | External Memory Write Strobe |
| **P3.7** | **RD̄** | External Memory Read Strobe |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** The alternate function of P3 is **activated only when the corresponding SFR latch bit is set to 1**. For example, to use P3.0 as RxD, the CPU must write 1 to P3.0 latch internally.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Total I/O Lines** | $N_{I/O} = 4 \times 8 = 32$ | Architecture count |
| **External Memory Address Range** | $A_{max} = 2^{16} = 65536 = 64\ \text{KB}$ | 16-bit address bus (P0 + P2) |
| **Bit Addressability** | $P_{bit} = 0x80 + (P_n \times 8) + b$ | Bit address formula |
| **Port 0 Pull-up Resistor** | $R_{pullup} \approx 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ (external) | External hardware |
| **Read Instruction** | `MOV A, Px` reads **pin** | Direct port read |
| **Read-Modify-Write** | `ANL/ORL/XRL/INC/DEC Px` reads **latch** | Avoid RMW pitfall |
| **SFR Address (Pn)** | P0=80H, P1=90H, P2=A0H, P3=B0H | Register access |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Read-Modify-Write Problem** occurs when a port pin is driving a load and the instruction reads the **pin** (load state) instead of the **latch** (intended value). Solution: Use a **shadow register** — copy port to a RAM location, modify the RAM byte, then write back to the port.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Port 0's multiplexed design** reduces pin count, making the 8051 a **40-pin DIP** package instead of 64-pin — critical for **cost-sensitive embedded products** like washing machines, remote controls, and toys.
- **Port 3's alternate functions** allow **interrupt-driven and serial communication** without additional ICs — used in **industrial PLCs, sensor nodes, and automotive ECUs**.
- **Quasi-bidirectional I/O** is a **power-saving feature** — internal pull-ups consume minimal current, making 8051 suitable for **battery-operated devices**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bit Address Calculation for Any Port Pin

**Problem:** Find the bit-addressable SFR address for **P2.5**.

**Given:**
- Port 2 base address = **A0H**
- Bit position = **5**

**Step-by-Step Deduction:**

The 8051 provides 16 bit-addressable locations per port (bits 0–7 of each port), mapped consecutively in the SFR bit-addressable region starting at **80H**.

$$
\text{Base Address of P2} = 0xA0
$$

$$
\text{Bit Offset} = 5 \times 1 = 5
$$

$$
\text{Bit Address} = \text{Base} + \text{Bit Offset}
$$

$$
\text{Bit Address} = 0xA0 + 0x05 = 0xA5
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Bit Address of P2.5} = \text{A5H}}
$$

**Explanation:** Each bit-addressable SFR location is byte-mapped, and individual bits within that byte have addresses equal to the byte base + bit position. Thus, P2.5 sits at address A5H, which can be used in bit-manipulation instructions like `SETB A5H` or `CLR A5H`.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Address/Data Bus Demultiplexing Timing (Port 0 + Port 2)

**Problem:** Derive the address formed when accessing external memory location **3FFFH** on the 8051.

**Given:**
- 16-bit address = **3FFFH** = 0011 1111 1111 1111 in binary

**Step-by-Step Deduction:**

**Step 1:** Split the 16-bit address into two bytes:

$$
A_{15-8} = 0x3F \quad (\text{Upper Address Byte} \rightarrow \text{Port 2})
$$

$$
A_{7-0} = 0xFF \quad (\text{Lower Address Byte} \rightarrow \text{Port 0})
$$

**Step 2:** The lower address byte is **time-multiplexed** on Port 0 along with data:

$$
\text{Time Slot 1 (ALE=1)}: \quad P_0 = A_{7-0} = \text{FFH}
$$

$$
\text{Time Slot 2 (ALE=0)}: \quad P_0 = D_{7-0} = \text{Data on bus}
$$

**Step 3:** The external **address latch (74373)** captures the lower address during the **HIGH pulse of ALE**, then holds it for the rest of the machine cycle.

**Step 4:** Port 2 holds the **upper address byte** throughout the cycle (no demultiplexing needed) because it is a **dedicated address bus**.

**Final Address on External Bus:**

$$
\boxed{A_{15-0} = P_2 \cdot 2^8 + \text{Latched}(P_0) = 0x3F \cdot 256 + 0xFF = 16383 = 0x3FFF}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Port Configuration Decision Table

| Use Case | Recommended Port | Reason | SFR Setup |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simple LED/Keypad interfacing | **P1** | No alternate function, internal pull-up | `MOV P1, #0FFH` (input) |
| External memory > 256 bytes | **P0 + P2** | Multiplexed address/data bus | Automatic (hardware) |
| UART Communication | **P3.0, P3.1** | Alternate function RxD/TxD | `SETB P3.0`, `SETB P3.1` |
| External Interrupts | **P3.2, P3.3** | Alternate function INT0̄/INT1̄ | `SETB P3.2`, `SETB P3.3` |
| Reading switch matrix (high impedance) | **P0** with pull-ups | Open-drain requires external pull-up | Write 1s to latch |
| External RAM read/write strobe | **P3.6, P3.7** | Alternate function WR̄/RD̄ | Automatic (hardware) |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Port Pin Current Sourcing Calculation

**Problem:** An LED is connected to P1.0 with a forward voltage $V_F = 2V$ and supply $V_{CC} = 5V$. Calculate the current-limiting resistor value.

**Given:**
- $V_{CC} = 5V$
- $V_F = 2V$
- $I_{LED} = 10\ \text{mA}$ (desired)

**Step-by-Step Deduction:**

$$
V_R = V_{CC} - V_F
$$

$$
V_R = 5V - 2V = 3V
$$

By Ohm's Law:

$$
R = \frac{V_R}{I_{LED}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{3V}{10 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{3}{0.010} = 300\ \Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 300\ \Omega}
$$

**Explanation:** When P1.0 outputs LOW, current flows from $V_{CC}$ through the resistor and LED into the port pin (sinking configuration — 8051 ports sink more current than they source).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: 8051 Internal Port Architecture (Single Bit)

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU["CPU Data Bus"] --> WR["Write Signal"]
    CPU --> RD["Read Signal"]
    WR --> Latch["D-Type Latch Q Output"]
    Latch --> FETN["N-MOS Driver Transistor"]
    Latch --> FETP["P-MOS Driver Transistor"]
    FETP --> Pin["External Pin Px.y"]
    FETN --> Pin
    Pin --> RMWBuf["Read Pin Buffer"]
    RMWBuf --> RD
    Pin --> Pullup["Internal Pull-up ~30 kohm"]
    Pullup --> VCC["VCC Supply"]
    AltFn["Alternate Function Mux"] --> Pin
    Latch --> AltCtrl["Alt Function Enable Control"]
    AltCtrl --> AltFn
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Port 3 Alternate Function Routing

```mermaid
graph LR
    P3["PORT 3 Latch B0H"] --> MUX["Alternate Function Multiplexer"]
    MUX --> Pin0["P3.0"]
    MUX --> Pin1["P3.1"]
    MUX --> Pin2["P3.2"]
    MUX --> Pin3["P3.3"]
    MUX --> Pin4["P3.4"]
    MUX --> Pin5["P3.5"]
    MUX --> Pin6["P3.6"]
    MUX --> Pin7["P3.7"]
    Pin0 --> RxD["RxD Serial Input"]
    Pin1 --> TxD["TxD Serial Output"]
    Pin2 --> INT0["INT0 External Interrupt 0"]
    Pin3 --> INT1["INT1 External Interrupt 1"]
    Pin4 --> T0["Timer 0 Clock Input"]
    Pin5 --> T1["Timer 1 Clock Input"]
    Pin6 --> WRbar["External Write Strobe"]
    Pin7 --> RDbar["External Read Strobe"]
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level External Memory Interfacing (Port 0 + Port 2)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MCU["8051 Microcontroller"]
        P0["PORT 0 AD0-AD7"]
        P2["PORT 2 A8-A15"]
        ALE_Sig["ALE Signal"]
        P3_6["P3.6 WRbar Strobe"]
        P3_7["P3.7 RDbar Strobe"]
    end
    subgraph EXT["External Address/Data Demultiplexer"]
        Latch74373["74HC373 Address Latch"]
        P0 --> Latch74373
        ALE_Sig --> Latch74373
        Latch74373 --> AddrLow["Latched A0-A7 to Memory"]
    end
    subgraph MEM["External Memory Chip"]
        AddrLow --> MemChip
        P2 --> MemChip
        MemChip["ROM/RAM 64KB Max"]
        P3_6 --> MemChip
        P3_7 --> MemChip
    end
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Read-Modify-Write Flow Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    Inst["Instruction ANL P1 0FH"] --> Branch{Type of Read}
    Branch -->|Read Pin| Wrong["WRONG Path: Reads External Pin State"]
    Branch -->|Read Latch| Correct["CORRECT Path: Reads Internal Latch Value"]
    Wrong --> WrongResult["Result: Corrupted Output if Pin Loaded"]
    Correct --> CorrectResult["Result: Clean Bitwise AND with Latch"]
    style Wrong fill:#ffe0e0
    style Correct fill:#d0f0d0
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the structure of Port 0 of the 8051 microcontroller. Why does it require external pull-up resistors?**

**Model Answer:**

Port 0 of the 8051 is an **8-bit bidirectional I/O port** with **SFR address 80H**. Its output transistors are configured in an **open-drain arrangement** (only an N-MOS pull-down transistor; no internal pull-up).

The port serves a **dual purpose**:
- **General Purpose I/O** when no external memory is accessed.
- **Multiplexed Lower Address/Data Bus (AD0–AD7)** during external memory cycles, controlled by the **ALE signal**.

Because the output stage has **no internal pull-up resistor**, when used as a general-purpose output, **external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ connected to VCC)** must be provided. Without these resistors, the port cannot drive the output HIGH — it can only actively pull LOW, leaving the pin in a high-impedance state when logic 1 is written. During external memory access, the internal circuitry activates pull-ups automatically.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write "Port 0 has no pull-up" but forget to mention it is a **quasi-bidirectional open-drain** specifically. Mark deduction: **−1 mark** for incomplete structural description.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the alternate functions of Port 3 pins of 8051.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8 pins of Port 3 each have a dedicated alternate function:

| Pin | Alternate Function | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | **RxD** | Serial data input |
| P3.1 | **TxD** | Serial data output |
| P3.2 | **INT0̄** | External interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | **INT1̄** | External interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | Timer 0 external input |
| P3.5 | **T1** | Timer 1 external input |
| P3.6 | **WR̄** | External memory write strobe |
| P3.7 | **RD̄** | External memory read strobe |

The alternate function is activated when the **corresponding latch bit is set to 1** by the CPU.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the internal structure of a single bit of Port 1 of 8051. Why is it called a quasi-bidirectional port?

**(b) [7 Marks)** Explain the Read-Modify-Write problem in 8051 ports with a suitable example. How can it be overcome?

---

**(a) Model Answer — Structure of Port 1 Bit:**

A single bit of Port 1 consists of:
- A **1-bit D-type latch** (addressable as part of SFR at 90H).
- An **output driver stage** with internal pull-up (~30 kΩ) and pull-down transistor.
- An **input buffer** for reading the external pin state.
- No alternate function logic — pure I/O.

**Quasi-bidirectional** means the port can be both input and output, but it is **not truly bidirectional simultaneously**. The internal pull-up is weak (high resistance), so the port can be overridden externally when used as input.

**Block Diagram (in text):**

$$
\text{CPU Bus} \rightarrow \text{Write Latch} \rightarrow Q\text{-output} \rightarrow \text{Internal Pull-up} \rightarrow \text{Pin}
$$

$$
\text{Pin} \rightarrow \text{Read Buffer} \rightarrow \text{CPU Bus}
$$

---

**(b) Model Answer — Read-Modify-Write Problem:**

The RMW problem occurs during instructions like `ANL P1, #0FH`, `ORL P2, A`, `INC P3`, `CPL P1.0`, etc.

**Mechanism:** These instructions:
1. **Read** the current port value.
2. **Modify** it.
3. **Write** the result back.

The 8051 reads from the **latch** (intended internal state) — but in some implementations, the read can happen from the **pin** if the port is heavily loaded. If a pin drives a low-impedance load, the loaded voltage may differ from the latch value, causing **erroneous modification**.

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV  P1, #0FFH    ; P1 = 11111111 (all inputs)
SETB P1.0         ; Set P1.0 as output HIGH
MOV  A, #00H
MOV  P1, A        ; Now P1.0 should be 0
ANL  P1, #0F0H    ; Intended: P1.0 = 0
                  ; If pin 0 is loaded LOW externally,
                  ; RMW may read pin=0 instead of latch=0
                  ; → Bits may be wrongly cleared
```

**Solution — Shadow Register Technique:**
```assembly
MOV  R0, #30H     ; Use R0 as shadow
MOV  A, P1
MOV  @R0, A
ANL  A, #0F0H
MOV  P1, A
```
Or, read the **latch value** from a RAM copy, modify it, and write back to the port.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks)** Compare the structures of Port 0, Port 1, Port 2, and Port 3 of the 8051 microcontroller with respect to their internal pull-ups, drive capability, and alternate functions.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe how Port 0 and Port 2 are used to interface external memory. Draw the demultiplexing circuit and explain the role of the ALE signal.

---

**(a) Model Answer — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | **Port 0** | **Port 1** | **Port 2** | **Port 3** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Internal Pull-up | **No (Open-drain)** | **Yes (~30 kΩ)** | **Yes (~30 kΩ)** | **Yes (~30 kΩ)** |
| Alternate Function | **AD0–AD7 (Mux)** | **None** | **A8–A15** | **Yes (8 alternate functions)** |
| Drive Capability | 8 LS TTL (bus mode) | 4 LS TTL | 4 LS TTL | 4 LS TTL |
| SFR Address | **80H** | **90H** | **A0H** | **B0H** |
| Reset Value | FFH | FFH | FFH | FFH |
| External Pull-up Req. | **Yes (10 kΩ)** | No | No | No |

---

**(b) Model Answer — External Memory Interfacing:**

The 8051 uses a **16-bit address bus (A0–A15)** and an **8-bit data bus (D0–D7)** to access **up to 64 KB of external memory** each for code (ROM) and data (RAM).

**Address Demultiplexing:**
- The **lower 8 address bits (A0–A7)** are **time-multiplexed** with the **data bus (D0–D7)** on **Port 0** to save pins.
- The **upper 8 address bits (A8–A15)** are carried by **Port 2** (no multiplexing needed).
- The **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal goes HIGH during the first half of the memory cycle, during which Port 0 carries the lower address.
- The external **74HC373 octal latch** captures this lower address on the **falling edge of ALE**.
- After latching, Port 0 is free to carry the actual **data** during the second half of the cycle.

**Timing Diagram (textual representation):**

$$
T_0: \text{ALE} = 1, \quad P_0 = A_{0-7}, \quad P_2 = A_{8-15}
$$

$$
T_1: \text{ALE} = 0, \quad P_0 = D_{0-7} \ (\text{Data Transfer})
$$

**Control Signals:**
- **PSEN̄ (Program Store Enable)** activates for ROM read.
- **RD̄ (P3.7)** activates for RAM read.
- **WR̄ (P3.6)** activates for RAM write.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. Forgetting to mention **external pull-up for Port 0** when used as GPIO (−1 mark).
> 2. Confusing **P0 reset value = FFH** with input/output direction (−0.5 mark).
> 3. Not explaining the **role of ALE** in demultiplexing (−2 marks).
> 4. Writing "Port 3 has no pull-up" — WRONG, it does have internal pull-up (−1 mark).
> 5. Failing to mention that **alternate functions of P3 are activated only when latch = 1** (−1 mark).
> 6. Not distinguishing between **Read Pin** vs **Read Latch** in the RMW problem (−2 marks).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The 8051 has **4 ports × 8 bits = 32 I/O lines** mapped to **SFR addresses 80H, 90H, A0H, B0H**.
- ✅ All port latches are **reset to FFH**, configuring all pins as **inputs by default**.
- ✅ **Port 0** is the **only port without internal pull-up** — needs **external 10 kΩ pull-ups** for GPIO use.
- ✅ **Port 0** carries the **multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)** during external memory access.
- ✅ **Port 2** provides the **upper address byte (A8–A15)** — no demultiplexing required.
- ✅ **Port 3** has **8 alternate functions**: RxD, TxD, INT0̄, INT1̄, T0, T1, WR̄, RD̄.
- ✅ Alternate functions of P3 are enabled **only when the corresponding latch bit = 1**.
- ✅ The **Read-Modify-Write** problem is solved using **shadow registers in internal RAM**.
- ✅ **ALE signal** is used to **latch the lower address byte** from Port 0 via an external latch (74HC373).
- ✅ Each port bit is **bit-addressable** — bit address = port base + bit position (e.g., P2.5 = A5H).
- ✅ Ports 1, 2, 3 are **quasi-bidirectional**; Port 0 is **true bidirectional** (in bus mode) or open-drain (in GPIO mode).
- ✅ **8051 ports sink more current (≈10 mA) than they source (≈1 mA)** — LEDs are typically connected in **active-LOW (sinking) configuration**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt:** An *interrupt* is an asynchronous event, either generated externally by a hardware peripheral/device or internally by the CPU itself, that temporarily halts the main program execution, forces the processor to save its current state, and transfers control to a dedicated routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. After the ISR completes, the processor restores its previous state and resumes the original program from the point where it was interrupted.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, the 8051 microcontroller family is the primary architectural reference, possessing **6 interrupt sources**:
1. **Reset** (highest priority, non-maskable)
2. **Two External Hardware Interrupts** (INT0, INT1)
3. **Two Timer Overflow Interrupts** (TF0, TF1)
4. **One Serial Port Interrupt** (TI/RI)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Office Worker & Doorbell"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Busy Engineer:**
> Imagine a dedicated engineer engrossed in a complex design task at their desk. This is the **CPU executing the main program**.
> 
> *   **Without an interrupt (Polling method):** The engineer must stop work every few minutes, walk to the door, and *check* if someone is there. This wastes enormous time and breaks concentration — exactly like a CPU constantly checking a flag bit in a tight loop (`while(!flag);`).
> *   **With an interrupt:** The engineer simply installs a **doorbell**. When a visitor presses it, the bell *immediately* alerts the engineer. The engineer stops the current task (saves state in stack), answers the door (executes the **ISR**), and returns seamlessly to the design (resumes main program). This is the essence of **event-driven computing**.

### 1.3 Why Interrupts are Essential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The core engineering rationale for interrupts is **efficiency, responsiveness, and real-time determinism**. The CPU spends **0%** of its time "looking" for events. Critical signals (e.g., emergency stop, data arrival) are serviced within a single machine cycle of detection, which is impossible with software polling loops.

| Mechanism | CPU Overhead | Response Time | Code Complexity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Polling (Loop)** | **High** (100% continuous checking) | Variable, often slow | Low |
| **Interrupt-Driven** | **Near Zero** (Hardware-triggered) | Deterministic & fast | Moderate |
| **Hybrid (Default Idle + Interrupt Wake-up)** | **Minimal** | Hardware-bound | High |

**Key Performance Metrics (Must Memorize):**
*   **Interrupt Latency:** Time delay from the moment an interrupt is *asserted* to the moment the *first instruction* of the ISR begins executing. Measured in **clock cycles**.
*   **Interrupt Response Time:** $\text{Latency} + \text{ISR Execution Time}$.
*   **Interrupt Recovery Time:** Time to restore context and return to the main program (typically 2 machine cycles in 8051).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Interrupt Processing Sequence (Operational Logic)

The 8051 handles an interrupt through a strict, hardware-implemented sequence. Let us decompose this into structured logical steps:

*   **Step 1 — Signal Assertion:** A peripheral or external pin sets a corresponding *interrupt request flag* (e.g., `IE0`, `TF0`, `TI`).
*   **Step 2 — Flag Sampling:** The CPU samples these flags at the **end of every machine cycle** (i.e., at S5P2 of the final cycle of the current instruction).
*   **Step 3 — Mask Verification:** The hardware checks the corresponding *enable bit* in the **IE (Interrupt Enable) SFR** AND the global enable bit **EA**. If any required bit is **0**, the request is ignored.
*   **Step 4 — Priority Arbitration:** If multiple requests occur simultaneously, the **IP (Interrupt Priority) register** and the *natural hardware priority* order resolve the conflict.
*   **Step 5 — Hardware Vectoring:** The CPU generates a `LCALL` to the predetermined **vector address** of the winning interrupt (e.g., `0003H` for INT0).
*   **Step 6 — Context Save:** Hardware pushes the 16-bit **Program Counter (PC)** onto the stack. *Note: 8051 does NOT automatically push PSW, ACC, or B; the ISR programmer must do this manually using `PUSH` instructions.*
*   **Step 7 — ISR Execution:** The CPU clears the interrupt flag (except for TI/RI, which must be cleared in software) and executes the ISR code.
*   **Step 8 — Return:** A `RETI` instruction pops PC from the stack, restoring execution. `RETI` *also* informs the interrupt controller that the ISR is complete, allowing further interrupts of the *same or lower* priority.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction for 8051:**
> `RETI` (Return from Interrupt) and `RET` (Return from subroutine) are **not interchangeable**. `RETI` is mandatory because it signals the hardware priority logic. Using `RET` inside an ISR breaks the entire interrupt priority system.

### 2.2 Classification of Interrupts (Exam Favorite Topic)

| Classification Type | Definition | 8051 Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hardware Interrupt** | Triggered by an external electrical signal on a pin. | INT0 (P3.2), INT1 (P3.3) |
| **Software Interrupt** | Triggered by an internal CPU condition. | Timer overflow, Serial byte received |
| **Maskable Interrupt** | Can be disabled (masked) by a control bit. | All 8051 interrupts *except Reset* |
| **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI)** | Cannot be disabled; serviced always. | **Reset** |
| **Vectored Interrupt** | Has a fixed, dedicated memory address. | All 8051 interrupts |
| **Edge-Triggered** | Activated on a falling/rising edge (8051 default). | INT0/INT1 when `ITx = 1` |
| **Level-Triggered** | Activated on a logic low level. | INT0/INT1 when `ITx = 0` |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & SFR Map (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all mathematically verifiable formulas and key register values required for solving KTU examination problems. Constants like clock frequency $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ are standard because they generate exact integer baud rates (e.g., **9600 baud**).

| Parameter | Formula / Register Value | Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Machine Cycle Duration | $T_{\text{mc}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds (s) | 8051 uses 12 clocks per cycle |
| Interrupt Latency (8051) | $L = (2 \text{ to } 8) \cdot T_{\text{mc}} + 2 \cdot T_{\text{mc}}$ | seconds (s) | 2–8 cycles for current instruction, 2 cycles for `LCALL` |
| Interrupt Vector Spacing | $\Delta_{\text{vec}} = 8$ | bytes | Each ISR has 8 bytes of reserved space |
| Timer Reload Value (Mode 1, 16-bit) | $\text{THx TLx} = 65536 - (f_{\text{osc}} \cdot t_{\text{delay}}) / 12$ | decimal | Used when timer overflow ISR fires |
| TH0/TH1 Upper Byte | $H = \lfloor (65536 - N) / 256 \rfloor$ | integer | For 16-bit timer interrupts |
| TL0/TL1 Lower Byte | $L = (65536 - N) \mod 256$ | integer | For 16-bit timer interrupts |
| EA (Global Enable) | Address **0xA8**, Bit **7** | bit | Must be 1 to enable any maskable int |
| IE SFR Address | **0xA8** (Bit-addressable) | byte | EA, ES, ET1, EX1, ET0, EX0 |
| IP SFR Address | **0xB8** (Bit-addressable) | byte | PS, PT1, PX1, PT0, PX0 |
| TCON (Int Control) | **0x88** (Bit-addressable) | byte | IT0, IE0, IT1, IE1, TR0, TF0, TR1, TF1 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Interrupts are the **backbone of all real-time embedded systems**. Practical applications include:
> *   **Automotive Airbag Systems:** A crash sensor triggers a hardware interrupt; the ISR deploys airbags within **microseconds** — polling would be fatal.
> *   **Industrial PLCs:** External interrupts detect emergency-stop button presses, halting machinery instantly.
> *   **IoT Edge Devices:** UART receive interrupts free the CPU from waiting for sensor data, allowing it to enter low-power sleep modes, conserving battery life.
> *   **Medical Heart Pacemakers:** Timer overflow interrupts generate precise pulse timing with $\mu$s accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Interrupt Latency Calculation

This derivation calculates the **worst-case interrupt response time** — a frequent KTU question worth 7 marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> An 8051 system uses a crystal frequency $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$. Calculate the worst-case interrupt latency for an external interrupt INT0, given that the longest instruction (`MUL AB`) is currently executing when INT0 is asserted.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Machine Cycle Period**

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by **12** to produce the machine cycle clock.

$$T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$$

Substituting $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$:

$$T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{\text{mc}} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

$$T_{\text{mc}} = 1.085 \text{ } \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Longest Instruction Cycle Count**

From the 8051 instruction set reference, `MUL AB` is a **4-machine-cycle** instruction (4 × $T_{\text{mc}}$), making it one of the longest. We take this as the worst-case delay before the CPU can sample the interrupt flag.

$$T_{\text{worst-instr}} = 4 \cdot T_{\text{mc}} = 4 \cdot 1.085 \text{ } \mu\text{s} = 4.34 \text{ } \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Add the Internal LCALL Cycle**

Once the flag is sampled, the 8051 hardware automatically executes an internal `LCALL` to the vector address, which consumes **2 machine cycles**.

$$T_{\text{lcall}} = 2 \cdot T_{\text{mc}} = 2 \cdot 1.085 \text{ } \mu\text{s} = 2.17 \text{ } \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Total Worst-Case Latency Formula**

The general formula for the worst-case interrupt latency is:

$$L_{\text{worst}} = (N_{\text{cycles}} \cdot T_{\text{mc}}) + (2 \cdot T_{\text{mc}})$$

Where $N_{\text{cycles}}$ is the cycle count of the currently executing instruction. Substituting our values:

$$L_{\text{worst}} = (4 \cdot T_{\text{mc}}) + (2 \cdot T_{\text{mc}})$$

$$L_{\text{worst}} = 6 \cdot T_{\text{mc}} = 6 \cdot 1.085 \text{ } \mu\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{L_{\text{worst}} = 6.51 \text{ } \mu\text{s}}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Timer Reload Value for Periodic Interrupts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> Configure Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit) to generate an interrupt every **50 ms** using $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$. Calculate the TH0 and TL0 reload values.

**Step 1 — Compute the Number of Timer Counts Required**

Each timer tick occurs every machine cycle ($T_{\text{mc}} = 1.085 \text{ } \mu\text{s}$ from above). The required number of timer increments $N$ is:

$$N = \frac{t_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{mc}}}$$

$$N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$N = 46,082.95 \approx 46,083 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the 16-bit Reload Value**

In Mode 1, the timer is 16-bit and overflows from `0xFFFF` to `0x0000`. The starting value to be loaded is:

$$N_{\text{reload}} = 65{,}536 - N$$

$$N_{\text{reload}} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}083$$

$$N_{\text{reload}} = 19{,}453$$

Converting $19{,}453$ to hexadecimal:

$$19{,}453 \div 256 = 75.99 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H = 75_{10} = 0x4B$$

$$19{,}453 \mod 256 = 253_{10} = 0xFD$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{TH0} = 0x4B, \quad \text{TL0} = 0xFD}}$$

**Step 3 — Verification**

We verify the delay: Timer counts up by $46{,}083$, taking $46{,}083 \times 1.085 \text{ } \mu\text{s} = 49.99 \text{ ms} \approx 50 \text{ ms}$. ✔

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Natural Hardware Priority Order

The 8051 has a fixed, hardware-implemented priority order. The following matrix is the **definitive KTU exam reference**:

| Priority Rank | Interrupt Source | Flag Bit | Vector Address (Hex) | IE Enable Bit | IP Priority Bit |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **1 (Highest)** | **Reset** | — | **0x0000** | Always Enabled | Non-maskable |
| **2** | External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | IE0 | **0x0003** | EX0 | PX0 |
| **3** | Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **0x000B** | ET0 | PT0 |
| **4** | External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | IE1 | **0x0013** | EX1 | PX1 |
| **5** | Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **0x001B** | ET1 | PT1 |
| **6 (Lowest)** | Serial Port (TX/RX) | TI/RI | **0x0023** | ES | PS |

> [!NOTE]
> If two interrupts have the **same** IP priority level, the natural hardware order above applies. If `IP` bits differ, the one with `IP = 1` wins. This is a **2-tier priority system**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Interrupt Handling Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU Executes Main Program] --> B{Interrupt Flag Set?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C{IE Enable Bit Set AND EA = 1?}
    C -- No --> A
    C -- Yes --> D{Priority Check: Multiple Requests?}
    D -- Single Request --> F[Hardware generates LCALL to Vector Address]
    D -- Multiple Requests --> E[Resolve via IP Register and Natural Priority]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Push 16-bit PC onto Stack]
    G --> H[Clear Interrupt Flag automatically]
    H --> I[Execute Interrupt Service Routine ISR]
    I --> J{RETI Instruction Encountered?}
    J -- No --> I
    J -- Yes --> K[Pop PC from Stack via RETI]
    K --> L[Resume Main Program from Saved PC]
    L --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: 8051 Interrupt Source Architecture Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INTERNAL[Internal Sources]
        T0[Timer 0 Overflow]
        T1[Timer 1 Overflow]
        SP[Serial Port TI or RI]
    end

    subgraph EXTERNAL[External Hardware Sources]
        E0[INT0 Pin P3.2]
        E1[INT1 Pin P3.3]
        RS[RESET Pin P3.0 - Highest Priority]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL[Control Logic Layer]
        TCON[TCON Register - Edge Level]
        IE[IE Register at 0xA8 - Enable Mask]
        IP[IP Register at 0xB8 - Priority]
    end

    subgraph CPU[CPU Response]
        ARB[Priority Arbiter]
        VEC[Vector Generator]
        ISR[Interrupt Service Routine]
    end

    T0 --> ARB
    T1 --> ARB
    SP --> ARB
    E0 --> ARB
    E1 --> ARB
    RS --> ARB
    TCON --> E0
    TCON --> E1
    IE --> ARB
    IP --> ARB
    ARB --> VEC
    VEC --> ISR
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Interrupt Vector Memory Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0000[0x0000 - RESET Vector]:::hi
    M0003[0x0003 - INT0 ISR Slot - 8 bytes]
    M000B[0x000B - Timer 0 ISR Slot - 8 bytes]
    M0013[0x0013 - INT1 ISR Slot - 8 bytes]
    M001B[0x001B - Timer 1 ISR Slot - 8 bytes]
    M0023[0x0023 - Serial Port ISR Slot - 8 bytes]
    M0030[0x0030 onwards - User Main Program]:::endnode

    M0000 --> M0003 --> M000B --> M0013 --> M001B --> M0023 --> M0030

    classDef hi fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since each interrupt vector is only **8 bytes** apart, complex ISRs (e.g., for the serial port) typically place a short `LJMP` or `AJMP` at the vector address, redirecting execution to a larger routine located elsewhere in code memory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
*Define the term "Interrupt Latency" and list the factors that affect it in the 8051 microcontroller.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Interrupt Latency** is the time delay between the assertion of an interrupt request signal and the execution of the *first instruction* of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR).
> 
> **Factors affecting latency in 8051:**
> 1.  The cycle count of the instruction currently executing when the interrupt is asserted (longest is 4 cycles for `MUL AB`/`DIV AB`).
> 2.  The 2-machine-cycle overhead of the internal `LCALL` to the vector address.
> 3.  Whether the interrupt is masked (`EA = 0` → infinite latency) or disabled.
> 4.  The clock oscillator frequency $f_{\text{osc}}$, since $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$.
> 
> **Formula:** $L_{\text{max}} = (N_{\text{max cycles}} + 2) \cdot T_{\text{mc}}$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
