A_{\text{new}} = \underbrace{0010}_{\text{New Upper}}\;\underbrace{0111}_{\text{New Lower}}\text{B} = 27\text{H}
$$

**Result:** $A = 27\text{H}$, $C = 0$ (SWAP clears the carry flag).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SWAP Efficiency:** Notice that `SWAP A` is the equivalent of 4 successive `RL A` operations. Since 8051 is a CISC-style 8-bit microcontroller, `SWAP` is implemented as a single-cycle opcode (opcode `C4H`), saving both program memory and execution time — a classic example of hardware optimization for a common operation.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Multiplication by 2 using RL (Pure Logic)

> **Problem:** Multiply `A = 05H` by 2 using only logical instructions.

A single-bit left shift of a binary number is equivalent to multiplication by 2.

$$
A = 05\text{H} = 0000\;0101\text{B}
$$

After `RL A`:

- Bit 7 → Bit 6 (1 → 0)
- Bit 6 → Bit 5 (0 → 1)
- Bit 5 → Bit 4 (0 → 0)
- Bit 4 → Bit 3 (0 → 0)
- Bit 3 → Bit 2 (0 → 0)
- Bit 2 → Bit 1 (1 → 0)
- Bit 1 → Bit 0 (0 → 1)
- Bit 0 → Bit 7 (1 → 0, wrap around)

$$
A_{\text{new}} = 0000\;1010\text{B} = 0\text{AH} = 10_{\text{decimal}}
$$

**Verification:** $5 \times 2 = 10$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Using `RL` for multiplication only works up to a single byte. If `A` is `80H` or larger (sign-bit set for signed numbers), `RL` will produce a wrapped-around incorrect result. For safe multiplication, use the `MUL AB` instruction instead.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — 16-Bit Left Shift using RLC

> **Problem:** Shift the 16-bit value stored in `R1:R0` (high byte in R1, low byte in R0) left by 1 bit using logical instructions only.

A 16-bit logical left shift can be done with just two RLC instructions:

```assembly
MOV  A, R0     ; Load low byte into A
RLC  A         ; Shift A left, MSB goes to Carry
MOV  R0, A     ; Store shifted low byte back
MOV  A, R1     ; Load high byte into A
RLC  A         ; Shift high byte left, Carry from low byte enters LSB
MOV  R1, A     ; Store shifted high byte
```

**Trace:** Suppose `R1 = 12H`, `R0 = 34H`. The 16-bit value is `1234H = 0001 0010 0011 0100B`.

- **First `RLC A` on R0 (`34H`):**
  - Old A = `0011 0100B`
  - Old Carry = **? (we need to know its initial state, let's assume C=0)**
  - New A = `0110 1000B = 68H`
  - New Carry = **0** (since the bit shifted out from D7 was 0)

- **Second `RLC A` on R1 (`12H`):**
  - Old A = `0001 0010B`
  - Old Carry = **0** (inherited from previous RLC)
  - New A = `0010 0100B = 24H`
  - New Carry = **0**

- **Final Result:** `R1:R0 = 2468H` = `0001 0010 0010 1000B`

**Verification:** Original `1234H` shifted left by 1 should be `2468H`. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why RLC and not RL?** If we used `RL` for the first byte, the bit that "fell off" the MSB of R0 would be **lost forever**. `RLC` saves that bit into the Carry flag, and the second `RLC` picks it up as the new LSB of R1 — preserving the 16-bit value across the shift. This is a fundamental technique in 8051 multi-byte arithmetic.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Logical Instruction

| Engineering Goal | Instruction to Use | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Force a specific bit to 0 | `ANL A, #mask` (with 0 at that bit) | AND with 0 always yields 0 |
| Force a specific bit to 1 | `ORL A, #mask` (with 1 at that bit) | OR with 1 always yields 1 |
| Invert a specific bit | `XRL A, #mask` (with 1 at that bit) | XOR with 1 inverts; XOR with 0 preserves |
| Test if a specific bit is 1 | `ANL A, #mask` → `JNZ` | Result non-zero only if bit was 1 |
| Test if a specific bit is 0 | `ANL A, #mask` → `JZ` | Result zero if bit was 0 |
| Multiply unsigned A by 2 | `RL A` | Single left shift = ×2 |
| Multiply unsigned A by 4 | `RL A` × 2 (or `SWAP A` if A ≤ 0FH) | Two left shifts |
| Multiply A by 10 (BCD packing) | `MOV B, #10` → `MUL AB` | Arithmetic, not logical |
| Rotate MSB of A into Carry | `RLC A` | Standard 9-bit shift mechanism |
| Invert all 8 bits of A | `CPL A` or `XRL A, #0FFH` | Both give 1's complement; flag effects differ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 8051 ALU Logical Operation Pipeline

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates how the 8051 ALU processes a logical instruction from fetch to write-back.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Instruction Fetch] --> B[Decode Opcode: ANL/ORL/XRL]
    B --> C{Source Operand Type}
    C --> C1[Immediate data #data]
    C --> C2[Register Rn]
    C --> C3[Direct address]
    C --> C4[Indirect @Ri]
    C1 --> D[Fetch Source Byte]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Read Accumulator A]
    E --> F{Operation Type}
    F --> F1[ANL: Bitwise AND]
    F --> F2[ORL: Bitwise OR]
    F --> F3[XRL: Bitwise XOR]
    F1 --> G[ALU Computes Result]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Write Result Back to A]
    H --> I[Clear Carry Flag to 0]
    I --> J[OV Flag Unchanged]
    J --> K[End: Fetch Next Instruction]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Bitwise Operation Visualization

This diagram shows how a single `ANL A, #0F0H` operation transforms the accumulator bit-by-bit. The upper nibble is preserved (because mask bits are 1) and the lower nibble is cleared (because mask bits are 0).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Areg[Accumulator A = A3H]
        A7[Bit 7: 1] --> A6[Bit 6: 0]
        A6 --> A5[Bit 5: 1]
        A5 --> A4[Bit 4: 0]
        A4 --> A3[Bit 3: 0]
        A3 --> A2[Bit 2: 0]
        A2 --> A1[Bit 1: 1]
        A1 --> A0[Bit 0: 1]
    end
    subgraph Msk[Mask #0F0H = 1111 0000B]
        M7[Bit 7: 1] --> M6[Bit 6: 1]
        M6 --> M5[Bit 5: 1]
        M5 --> M4[Bit 4: 1]
        M4 --> M3[Bit 3: 0]
        M3 --> M2[Bit 2: 0]
        M2 --> M1[Bit 1: 0]
        M1 --> M0[Bit 0: 0]
    end
    subgraph Res[Result A = A0H]
        R7[Bit 7: 1] --> R6[Bit 6: 0]
        R6 --> R5[Bit 5: 1]
        R5 --> R4[Bit 4: 0]
        R4 --> R3[Bit 3: 0]
        R3 --> R2[Bit 2: 0]
        R2 --> R1[Bit 1: 0]
        R1 --> R0[Bit 0: 0]
    end
    Areg --> ANDop{Bitwise AND}
    Msk --> ANDop
    ANDop --> Res
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Rotate Instruction Family Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Rot[Rotate Instructions] --> Rot8[8-bit Rotations]
    Rot --> Rot9[9-bit Rotations via Carry]
    Rot8 --> RL[RL A: Pure 8-bit Left]
    Rot8 --> RR[RR A: Pure 8-bit Right]
    Rot9 --> RLC[RLC A: 9-bit Left with Carry]
    Rot9 --> RRC[RRC A: 9-bit Right with Carry]
    Rot8 --> SW[SWAP A: Special 8-bit]
    RL --> Note1[Carry flag unaffected]
    RR --> Note2[Carry flag unaffected]
    RLC --> Note3[Carry flag updated with shifted-out bit]
    RRC --> Note4[Carry flag updated with shifted-out bit]
    SW --> Note5[Carry flag cleared to 0]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Logical Group Position in 8051 ISA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ISA[8051 Instruction Set Architecture] --> DataProc[Data Processing Group]
    DataProc --> Arith[Arithmetic Group]
    DataProc --> LogicalGroup[Logical Group - This Module]
    DataProc --> DataXfer[Data Transfer Group]
    LogicalGroup --> ByteOps[Byte-Level: ANL, ORL, XRL, CPL, CLR]
    LogicalGroup --> BitOps[Bit-Level: CLR, SETB, CPL, ANL C, ORL C]
    LogicalGroup --> RotOps[Rotate: RL, RLC, RR, RRC, SWAP]
    ByteOps --> Ex1[Example: ANL A, #0F0H]
    BitOps --> Ex2[Example: SETB P1.0]
    RotOps --> Ex3[Example: RLC A for 16-bit shift]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain the difference between the 8051 instructions `RL A` and `RLC A` with respect to the Carry flag.**

**Model Answer:**

The instruction `RL A` performs an **8-bit left rotation** of the accumulator contents. The bit shifted out from bit 7 (MSB) **wraps around** to bit 0 (LSB), and the **Carry flag is not modified** by this operation. It is a closed, self-contained rotation within the 8 bits of A.

In contrast, `RLC A` performs a **9-bit left rotation** that includes the Carry flag as a 9th bit. The bit shifted out from bit 7 of A is **transferred into the Carry flag**, while the **previous value of the Carry flag** is rotated into bit 0 of A. This makes `RLC A` suitable for implementing multi-byte shifts, where the carry propagates from one byte to the next.

The key difference is that `RLC A` **modifies the Carry flag**, while `RL A` **leaves the Carry flag unchanged**.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is the function of the instruction `ANL A, #0FH`? State one practical application.**

**Model Answer:**

The instruction `ANL A, #0FH` performs a **bitwise logical AND** between the accumulator A and the immediate data `0FH` (which in binary is `0000 1111B`). The result is stored back in the accumulator.

Since the upper nibble bits (D7, D6, D5, D4) of the mask are all 0, the AND operation will **force those upper bits of A to 0**. Since the lower nibble bits (D3, D2, D1, D0) of the mask are all 1, the lower nibble of A is **preserved unchanged**.

**Practical Application:** This instruction is commonly used to **mask the lower 4 bits (lower nibble) of the accumulator**, effectively extracting the lower nibble of a byte. It is widely used in applications such as **BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) digit extraction**, **display multiplexing on 7-segment displays**, and **analog-to-digital converter (ADC) result formatting** where only the lower 4 bits represent a useful value.

After the instruction, the Carry flag is **cleared to 0**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each and offer an internal choice between two questions. Each part typically has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The following provides both alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the 8051 Logical Group of instructions with suitable examples. List the various byte-level logical instructions and describe any **two** in detail, including their effect on the Carry and Overflow flags. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given that the accumulator contains `A = 5AH`, trace step-by-step the execution of the following 8051 code sequence. Show the contents of A and the Carry flag after each instruction. **(7 marks)**

```assembly
ANL  A, #0F0H
ORL  A, #0FH
XRL  A, #0FFH
CPL  A
RL   A
```

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

The 8051 Logical Group comprises instructions that perform bitwise Boolean operations (AND, OR, XOR, NOT) and bit manipulations on the accumulator, registers, memory, and individual bits. All byte-level logical instructions use the **accumulator (A) as one of the operands**, with the other operand drawn from any of the 8051's five addressing modes.

The byte-level logical instructions are: `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `CLR`. (Rotations `RL`, `RLC`, `RR`, `RRC`, and `SWAP` are also part of the logical group, but covered separately.)

**Detailed Description of Two Instructions:**

1. **ANL A, <src> — Logical AND:**
   - The source byte is fetched. A bitwise AND is performed between A and the source.
   - The result replaces A. Source is unchanged.
   - Example: If A = `F0H` and the instruction is `ANL A, #0FH`, then A becomes `00H` (upper nibble masked to 0, lower nibble preserved as 0).
   - **Flag effect:** Carry (C) = 0, Overflow (OV) unchanged.

2. **XRL A, <src> — Logical Exclusive-OR:**
   - A bitwise XOR is performed between A and the source.
   - The result replaces A.
   - Example: If A = `AAH` (= 1010 1010B) and the instruction is `XRL A, #0FFH`, then A becomes `55H` (= 0101 0101B).
   - **Flag effect:** Carry (C) = 0, Overflow (OV) unchanged.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Initial State:** $A = 5\text{AH} = 0101\;1010\text{B}$, $C = ?$ (assume initial $C = 0$).

**Step 1: `ANL A, #0F0H`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0101\;1010 \;\text{AND}\; 1111\;0000 \\
A &= 0101\;0000 \\
A &= 50\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

$C \leftarrow 0$ (cleared by ANL)

**Step 2: `ORL A, #0FH`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0101\;0000 \;\text{OR}\; 0000\;1111 \\
A &= 0101\;1111 \\
A &= 5\text{FH}
\end{aligned}
$$

$C \leftarrow 0$ (cleared by ORL)

**Step 3: `XRL A, #0FFH`**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 0101\;1111 \;\text{XOR}\; 1111\;1111 \\
A &= 1010\;0000 \\
A &= \text{A0H}
\end{aligned}
$$

$C \leftarrow 0$ (cleared by XRL)

**Step 4: `CPL A`**

$$
A = \overline{\text{A0H}} = \overline{1010\;0000} = 0101\;1111 = 5\text{FH}
$$

$C$ remains unchanged (= 0, since CPL does not affect the Carry flag).

**Step 5: `RL A`**

Starting with A = `5FH` = `0101 1111B`:
- Bit 7 (0) → Bit 6
- Bit 6 (1) → Bit 5
- Bit 5 (0) → Bit 4
- Bit 4 (1) → Bit 3
- Bit 3 (1) → Bit 2
- Bit 2 (1) → Bit 1
- Bit 1 (1) → Bit 0
- Bit 0 (1) → Bit 7 (wrap)

$$
A = 1011\;1110 = \text{BEH}
$$

$C$ remains unchanged (RL does not affect the Carry flag).

**Final State:** $A = \text{BEH}$, $C = 0$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the operation of `RLC A` and `RRC A` instructions. How are they used in implementing a 16-bit left shift? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to toggle bit P1.7 of Port 1 continuously using the logical instruction `CPL`. Assume an infinite loop is required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**`RLC A` (Rotate Accumulator Left through Carry):**

This instruction performs a 9-bit left rotation, where the accumulator (8 bits) and the Carry flag (1 bit) form a circular 9-bit shift register.

**Operation sequence:**
1. The MSB of A (bit 7) is copied into the Carry flag.
2. Every other bit of A is shifted one position to the left (bit $i$ moves to bit $i+1$).
3. The old value of the Carry flag is shifted into bit 0 of A.

$$
C_{\text{new}} \leftarrow A_7, \quad A_{i,\text{new}} \leftarrow A_{(i+1) \bmod 8} \text{ for } i = 0,1,...,6, \quad A_0 \leftarrow C_{\text{old}}
$$

**`RRC A` (Rotate Accumulator Right through Carry):**

This is the mirror operation, rotating the 9-bit register to the right.

$$
C_{\text{new}} \leftarrow A_0, \quad A_{i,\text{new}} \leftarrow A_{(i-1) \bmod 8} \text{ for } i = 1,2,...,7, \quad A_7 \leftarrow C_{\text{old}}
$$

**Schematic Representation:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Carry Flag] -->|Old value| A0
    A0[A bit 0] -->|Shift left| A1
    A1[A bit 1] -->|Shift left| A2
    A2[A bit 2] -->|Shift left| A3
    A3[A bit 3] -->|Shift left| A4
    A4[A bit 4] -->|Shift left| A5
    A5[A bit 5] -->|Shift left| A6
    A6[A bit 6] -->|Shift left| A7
    A7[A bit 7] -->|Shifted out| CNew[New Carry]
    CNew --> C
```

**16-bit Left Shift Implementation:**

To shift a 16-bit value stored in two registers (say `R1` = high byte, `R0` = low byte) left by 1 bit:

```assembly
MOV  A, R0     ; Load low byte into A
RLC  A         ; Shift left; carry = old bit 7 of R0
MOV  R0, A     ; Store shifted low byte
MOV  A, R1     ; Load high byte
RLC  A         ; Shift left; old carry (from R0) enters bit 0
MOV  R1, A     ; Store shifted high byte
```

The key insight: the bit that "falls off" the MSB of `R0` is preserved in the Carry flag, and the second `RLC A` rotates it into the LSB of `R1` — no data loss.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Program:** Continuously toggle P1.7 using the `CPL` bit instruction.

```assembly
ORG  0000H        ; Reset vector address
START:
      CPL  P1.7   ; Complement bit P1.7 (0->1 or 1->0)
      SJMP START  ; Jump back to START, creating infinite loop
      END         ; End of assembly source
```

**Explanation:**

1. `ORG 0000H` is a directive that tells the assembler to place the following code starting at program memory address `0000H`, which is the 8051's reset vector.
2. `CPL P1.7` is a bit-level logical instruction that **complements (inverts)** the current logic level on pin 7 of Port 1. If P1.7 was HIGH (1), it becomes LOW (0), and vice versa.
3. `SJMP START` is a Short Jump (2-byte, 2-cycle relative jump) that goes back to the `CPL P1.7` instruction, creating an **infinite loop**.
4. The result is a **50% duty cycle square wave** on pin P1.7, with a period equal to the execution time of two instructions (CPL + SJMP = 1 + 2 = 3 machine cycles = 36 oscillator periods at 11.0592 MHz ≈ 3.25 µs period, ≈ 307 kHz frequency).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Logical Group Questions:**
> 1. **Flag Confusion:** Many students write that `CPL A` clears the Carry flag. This is **WRONG**. `CPL A` affects **NO flags**. The same applies to `CLR A`. Only `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL` (clear C) and `RLC`/`RRC` (update C) affect the Carry flag.
> 2. **Confusing RL and RLC:** Students often state that `RL A` updates the Carry flag. **It does not.** Only `RLC A` and `RRC A` modify the Carry flag.
> 3. **Operand Limitation of Logical Instructions:** A common mistake is writing `ANL R0, #0FH` (logical AND of R0 directly with immediate data). This is **not a valid 8051 instruction**. The destination must always be the accumulator. The correct form is `MOV A, R0` followed by `ANL A, #0FH`.
> 4. **SWAP vs SWAP A:** Students sometimes write just `SWAP` without specifying the accumulator. The correct mnemonic is `SWAP A`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The 8051 **Logical Group** performs bitwise AND, OR, XOR, complement, clear, and rotation operations on bytes and individual bits.
- **All byte-level logical instructions** (ANL, ORL, XRL) use the **accumulator as the destination** and one other operand as the source. The result **always** overwrites A.
- **ANL, ORL, XRL clear the Carry flag** to 0. The Overflow flag is never affected.
- **CPL A and CLR A affect NO flags** — this is a frequently tested KTU point.
- **Bit-level logical instructions** (CLR bit, SETB bit, CPL bit) work on the 128 bit-addressable locations in internal RAM 20H–2FH and on the SFR bit-addressable space.
- **RL and RR** are **8-bit rotations** that do **not** touch the Carry flag.
- **RLC and RRC** are **9-bit rotations** that **do** update the Carry flag — they are essential for multi-byte shifting.
- **SWAP A** exchanges the upper and lower nibbles of A in a single cycle — it is the hardware-optimized equivalent of four `RL A` operations.
- **AND is for masking** (force bits to 0), **OR is for setting** (force bits to 1), **XOR is for toggling** (invert selected bits).
- **XOR with FFH equals CPL**, but the flag effects differ.
- For 16-bit shifts, the canonical pattern is: `MOV A, low_byte; RLC A; MOV low_byte, A; MOV A, high_byte; RLC A; MOV high_byte, A`.
- The **bit slash notation** `/bit` in `ANL C, /bit` means "use the complement of the bit as the input" — it does NOT mean the result is stored in complemented form.
- Carry is the **only flag** routinely modified by logical group instructions (and only by some of them). The Auxiliary Carry (AC) flag is **never** affected by logical instructions.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy for Logical Group Questions:**
> 1. Always state **operand types** clearly (Acc, Rn, direct, @Ri, #data) — examiners allocate marks for this.
> 2. Always state **flag effects** explicitly — even if the answer doesn't ask for it, mentioning it shows depth.
> 3. For tracing questions, write the **binary representation** of every intermediate value — it earns you process marks even if the final answer is wrong.
> 4. For rotate questions, **always show the bit-by-bit movement** (D7→C, D6→D7, ..., C→D0) for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branching Group
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Branching Group # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Intel 8051 Instruction Set Architecture**, the **Branching Group** (also known as the *Program Control Group* or *Jump Group*) is the categorical collection of machine-level opcodes that **deliberately modify the contents of the Program Counter (PC)**, thereby interrupting the natural sequential flow of instruction execution. According to the official Intel MCS-51 Family Architecture manual, these instructions govern **all forms of unconditional transfers, conditional decision-making, subroutine linkage, and interrupt servicing**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Branching Group is examined under **Module 2 – 8051 Instruction Set & Programming**. Students are expected to identify the instruction size (bytes), machine cycle count, addressing mode, and the precise condition tested by each opcode. Questions frequently appear as *"Explain the branching instructions with examples"* (14 marks) or *"Write an 8051 ALP to... using conditional jumps"* (14 marks). The complete set of 8051 branching instructions can be classified into **three functional sub-groups**: | Sub-Group | Purpose | Example Opcodes | |---|---|---| | **Unconditional Jumps** | Always alter the PC | `LJMP`, `AJMP`, `SJMP`, `JMP @A+DPTR` | | **Conditional Jumps** | Alter PC only if a flag/bit condition is TRUE | `JZ`, `JNZ`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ` | | **Call/Return & NOP** | Subroutine linkage and timing control | `LCALL`, `ACALL`, `RET`, `RETI`, `NOP` | Mathematically, every branching instruction executes the operation: $$PC_{new} = PC_{current} + \text{Offset} \quad \text{or} \quad PC_{new} = \text{Target Address}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** A *Jump* updates only the Program Counter (linear control transfer), whereas a *Call* updates **both** the PC and the Stack Pointer (pushes return address onto stack) — enabling nested subroutine execution. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Railway Signal" Intuition Imagine a train running on a single straight railway track. Under normal conditions, it just keeps moving from one station to the next in order (this is **sequential execution**). Now imagine a **railway signal post** at every station: - A **red signal with a fixed arrow** → the train *always* diverts to a specific side-track (`LJMP`, `AJMP`). - A **sensor-based signal** that only diverts the train **if a passenger presses a button** → these are *conditional jumps* (e.g., `JZ` diverts only when Accumulator is Zero). - A **station master's token exchange** for sending the train into a maintenance depot and then bringing it back to the main line → this is `LCALL`/`RET` (subroutine call and return). - A **1-second pause button** that the driver presses but does nothing else → this is the `NOP` (No Operation). In the 8051, the "train" is the instruction pointer (PC), the "railway track" is the program memory (ROM), and the "signal" is the **opcode fetch-decode-execute cycle** of the CPU. ## 1.3 Program Counter — The Heart of Branching The 8051 uses a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** that can therefore address a full **64 KB** of code memory. Every instruction fetch begins with PC pointing to the next opcode byte. Branching instructions **overload this PC** with a new value. $$PC_{capacity} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ locations} = 64 \text{ KB}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **Stack Pointer (SP)** with the **Program Counter (PC)**. Remember — *SP tracks the RAM stack (where return addresses are saved)*, while *PC tracks the next instruction to be fetched from ROM*. Only the PC is changed by jumps; only the stack is changed by calls. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Unconditional Jump Instructions The 8051 provides **three distinct unconditional jump opcodes** to balance code size (ROM efficiency) against reach (memory range). This is a *design optimization* unique to the MCS-51 architecture. ### 2.1.1 `LJMP` — Long Jump (3 bytes, 4 machine cycles) - **Opcode byte:** `02H` - **Format:** `LJMP 16-bit address` - **Encoding:** `02H | addr_high | addr_low` - **Action:** `PC ← 16-bit address` directly - **Range:** Anywhere within the **full 64 KB** program memory space - **Use case:** Reaching distant routines or fixed memory-mapped interrupt vectors ### 2.1.2 `AJMP` — Absolute Jump (2 bytes, 2 machine cycles) - **Opcode byte:** `x1H` (where `x` is the upper 3 bits of the 11-bit address) - **Format:** `AJMP 11-bit address` - **Encoding:** `a₁₀ a₉ a₈ 0 0 0 1 | addr_low` - **Action:** The low 11 bits of the PC are replaced, while the **upper 5 bits of PC remain unchanged** - **Range:** Within the **same 2 KB page** as the instruction following `AJMP` - **Use case:** Memory-efficient jumps within a localized code block > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 2 KB Page Rule:** After the AJMP instruction, the PC's upper 5 bits form the page number. The 11-bit absolute address can therefore point to any of the $2^{11} = 2048$ bytes within that 2 KB page. Crossing the 2 KB boundary **without aligning** causes wrap-around errors. ### 2.1.3 `SJMP` — Short Jump (2 bytes, 2 machine cycles) - **Opcode byte:** `80H` - **Format:** `SJMP rel` where `rel` is a **signed 8-bit offset** - **Action:** $PC \leftarrow PC_{after\;SJMP} + rel$ - **Range:** **−128 bytes to +127 bytes** relative to the byte following the SJMP - **Use case:** Tight loops, small forward/backward jumps (most common in real code) The signed range formula: $$rel \in [-128,\; +127] \quad \text{(two's complement 8-bit)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why does SJMP exist?** In an embedded 8051 with limited ROM (e.g., 4 KB AT89C2051), saving 1 byte per jump × hundreds of jumps can save hundreds of bytes. SJMP is the workhorse of tight, hand-optimized assembly. ### 2.1.4 `JMP @A+DPTR` — Indirect Jump (1 byte, 2 machine cycles) - **Opcode byte:** `73H` - **Action:** $PC \leftarrow A + DPTR$ - **Use case:** Implementing **jump tables** (computed `goto` / `switch-case` dispatch) - **Example:** A state machine where `A` holds the current state index. ## 2.2 The Conditional Jump Instructions Conditional jumps test a flag or perform a comparison, and branch only when the condition is TRUE. If FALSE, the next sequential instruction is executed. All conditional jumps are **2-byte, 2-cycle** instructions (except `CJNE` and `DJNZ` which are 3-byte). ### 2.2.1 Flag-Condition Jumps | Instruction | Opcode | Condition Tested | Mnemonic Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | `JZ rel` | `60H` | A == 0 | **J**ump if **Z**ero | | `JNZ rel` | `70H` | A != 0 | **J**ump if **N**ot **Z**ero | | `JC rel` | `40H` | CY == 1 | **J**ump if **C**arry | | `JNC rel` | `50H` | CY == 0 | **J**ump if **N**o **C**arry | ### 2.2.2 Bit-Addressable Jumps These test a **single bit** in the bit-addressable RAM region (addresses `20H–2FH`) or in the **SFR bit-addressable** region. | Instruction | Opcode | Condition | |---|---|---| | `JB bit, rel` | `20H` | Jump if bit == 1 | | `JNB bit, rel` | `30H` | Jump if bit == 0 | | `JBC bit, rel` | `10H` | Jump if bit == 1, **then clear bit** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** `JBC` is the *only* jump that modifies the tested bit (it clears it). It is extremely useful in edge-triggered interrupt service routines for clearing the request flag atomically. ### 2.2.3 `CJNE` — Compare and Jump if Not Equal (3 bytes, 2 cycles) - **Format:** `CJNE , , rel` - **Action:** 1. Performs `op1 − op2` (without storing result) 2. Sets/clears the **Carry flag** based on the subtraction 3. Jumps to `PC + rel` if the operands are **NOT equal** - **Two addressing modes:** - `CJNE A, direct, rel` (opcode `B5H`) - `CJNE A, #data, rel` (opcode `B4H`) - `CJNE Rn, #data, rel` (opcode `B8H–BFH`) - `CJNE @Ri, #data, rel` (opcode `B6H–B7H`) > [!NOTE] > **Carry Flag Trick:** After `CJNE A, #data, rel`, if `A < data` then CY = 1; if `A ≥ data` then CY = 0. This gives you a "less than" test "for free" — widely used in KTU lab examinations for threshold comparisons. ### 2.2.4 `DJNZ` — Decrement and Jump if Not Zero (2 or 3 bytes, 2 cycles) - **Format:** `DJNZ , rel` - **Action:** 1. Decrement the operand byte by 1 2. Jump to `PC + rel` if the result is **NOT zero** - **Addressing modes:** - `DJNZ Rn, rel` (1-byte operand, opcode `D8H–DFH`) - `DJNZ direct, rel` (2-byte operand, opcode `D5H`) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Use — Loop Counter:** `DJNZ` is the **canonical 8051 loop instruction**. A loop of N iterations is written as: > ``` > MOV R2, #N ; 1 byte counter > BACK: ... ; loop body > DJNZ R2, BACK ; 2 bytes, jumps back until R2 = 0 > ``` ## 2.3 Call and Return Sub-Group ### 2.3.1 `LCALL` — Long Call (3 bytes, 4 cycles) - **Opcode:** `12H` - **Action:** 1. $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$; $Stack[SP] \leftarrow PC_{high}$ 2. $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$; $Stack[SP] \leftarrow PC_{low}$ 3. $PC \leftarrow 16\text{-bit address}$ - **Use:** Calling subroutines anywhere in 64 KB memory ### 2.3.2 `ACALL` — Absolute Call (2 bytes, 4 cycles) - **Opcode:** `x1H` - **Action:** Same as `LCALL` but target limited to current 2 KB page - **ROM savings:** Saves 1 byte over `LCALL` ### 2.3.3 `RET` — Return from Subroutine (1 byte, 4 cycles) - **Action:** $PC_{low} \leftarrow Stack[SP]$; $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$; $PC_{high} \leftarrow Stack[SP]$; $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ ### 2.3.4 `RETI` — Return from Interrupt (1 byte, 4 cycles) - **Action:** Same as `RET`, but additionally **re-enables the interrupt system** (clears the internal "in-progress" priority latch) - **Use:** Mandatory at the end of every ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) ### 2.3.5 `NOP` — No Operation (1 byte, 1 cycle) - **Opcode:** `00H` - **Action:** Fetches and decodes but executes nothing; consumes **1 machine cycle (12 oscillator periods)** - **Use:** Precise timing delays, pipeline padding, software debouncing ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Program Counter size | $PC = 16$-bit | 0 to $2^{16}-1 = 65535$ | | 2 | Code memory addressable | $2^{16} = 64$ KB | 64 KB | | 3 | `LJMP` reach | Full 64 KB | address = 16-bit | | 4 | `AJMP` page size | $2^{11} = 2048$ bytes | 2 KB page | | 5 | `SJMP` relative offset | $-128 \le rel \le +127$ | bytes (signed 8-bit) | | 6 | `JMP @A+DPTR` target | $PC \leftarrow A + DPTR$ | 16-bit sum | | 7 | `CJNE` carry rule | $A < data \Rightarrow CY=1$ | boolean flag | | 8 | `DJNZ` loop iterations | $N = \text{initial byte value}$ | count | | 9 | `LCALL` / `ACALL` stack growth | $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$ | bytes pushed | | 10 | `RET` / `RETI` stack shrink | $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$ | bytes popped | | 11 | `NOP` delay | $T_{nop} = 12 \cdot T_{osc}$ | 1 µs at 12 MHz | | 12 | `LCALL` / `LJMP` time | $4 \cdot T_{machine} = 48 \cdot T_{osc}$ | cycles | | 13 | Conditional jump time | $2 \cdot T_{machine} = 24 \cdot T_{osc}$ | cycles | | 14 | `RETI` time | $4 \cdot T_{machine} = 48 \cdot T_{osc}$ | cycles | | 15 | Branch address (SJMP) | $PC_{target} = PC_{after} + rel$ | bytes (signed) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cycle-Time Conversion:** At the standard 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz crystal, 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods = 1 µs. This makes `DJNZ` and `NOP` the building blocks of all software delay routines. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **State Machines in Automotive ECUs** — `JMP @A+DPTR` dispatches among 16+ states in an engine control unit. 2. **Industrial Conveyor Sorting** — `CJNE` compares sensor ADC values against thresholds to divert packages. 3. **Watchdog-Based Reset Loops** — `SJMP BACK` creates the main super-loop of every bare-metal 8051 program. 4. **Real-Time Interrupt Service** — `RETI` is the gate-keeper ensuring deterministic exit from time-critical ISRs. 5. **Precision Timing** — `NOP` chains generate microsecond-accurate delays in PLCs and smart meters. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Computing the `SJMP` Target Address The 8051 assembler converts a symbolic label into a **signed 8-bit relative offset** $rel$. The relationship between the label address and the SJMP opcode address is: $$rel = \text{TargetAddress} - \text{AddressOfNextInstruction}$$ ### Worked Example 1: Forward Jump ```assembly ORG 0000H SJMP NEXT ; SJMP is at 0000H, occupies 2 bytes ; PC after SJMP = 0002H ORG 0050H NEXT: MOV A, #55H ``` **Step-by-step derivation:** $$rel = \text{TargetAddress} - \text{AddressOfNextInstruction}$$ $$rel = 0050H - 0002H$$ $$rel = 004EH = 78_{10}$$ Since $rel = +78 \le +127$, the forward jump is **valid**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students forget that the PC has *already* advanced past the 2-byte SJMP opcode before the offset is added. Always subtract the **address of the byte following the SJMP**, not the SJMP itself. ### Worked Example 2: Backward Jump (Loop) ```assembly MOV R3, #03H ; at 0000H BACK: NOP ; at 0002H NOP ; at 0003H DJNZ R3, BACK ; at 0004H, 2 bytes, next PC = 0006H ``` For the first execution of `DJNZ`, R3 = 3, then 2, then 1, then 0 → loop runs 3 times. $$rel = \text{BACK} - \text{AddressOfNextInstruction} = 0002H - 0006H = -0004H$$ In two's complement 8-bit: $$-4_{10} = 1111\,1100_2 = FCH$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why is the offset $-4$ and not $-2$?** Because PC at the moment of calculation is **0006H** (already past the DJNZ), and the target is **0002H**. The distance is 4 bytes backward, NOT 2. ### Worked Example 3: Computing `AJMP` Page Boundary ```assembly ORG 07FEH AJMP TARGET ; at 07FEH (2 bytes), PC after = 0800H ; ... TARGET: ; (located at 0810H) ``` The 11-bit address of TARGET relative to the current page (0x00–0x7FF within 64K = page index) is: $$a_{10..0} = 0810H \; \& \; 07FFH = 010H$$ Since PC after AJMP = `0800H`, the upper 5 bits of PC are `00000_xxxx` ... let us examine the page. The AJMP replaces only the low 11 bits of the PC, so: $$PC_{new} = (\text{PC after AJMP} \; \& \; F800H) \; \vert \; a_{10..0}$$ $$PC_{new} = (0800H \; \& \; F800H) \; \vert \; 010H = 0800H \; \vert \; 0010H = 0810H \;\; \checkmark$$ > [!WARNING] > **Boundary Failure:** If `TARGET` were at `1000H`, the 11-bit portion `000H` would point back to `0800H`, NOT `1000H`! AJMP can never cross a 2 KB page boundary. ## 3.2 Derivation: Subroutine Stack Frame on `LCALL` Suppose `LCALL SUB` is located at address `0123H` and SUB is at `0456H`. **Initial state:** $SP = 07H$, internal RAM `08H–7FH` available. **Execution of `LCALL 0456H`:** 1. Increment SP: $SP \leftarrow 07H + 1 = 08H$ 2. Push PC high byte: $\text{RAM}[08H] \leftarrow 01H$ (high byte of $0123H + 3 = 0126H$ is $01H$) 3. Increment SP: $SP \leftarrow 08H + 1 = 09H$ 4. Push PC low byte: $\text{RAM}[09H] \leftarrow 26H$ (low byte of $0126H$ is $26H$) 5. Load PC: $PC \leftarrow 0456H$ **Note carefully:** The PC value pushed is the address of the **byte following the LCALL**, not the LCALL's own address. This is the return address used by `RET`. When `RET` executes: 1. Pop PC low: $PC_{low} \leftarrow \text{RAM}[09H] = 26H$, $SP \leftarrow 08H$ 2. Pop PC high: $PC_{high} \leftarrow \text{RAM}[08H] = 01H$, $SP \leftarrow 07H$ 3. PC now equals `0126H` — execution resumes **after** the LCALL. ✓ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Choosing a Jump Opcode | Distance to Target | Best Opcode | Bytes Used | Cycles | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\vert rel \vert \le 127$ | `SJMP` | 2 | 2 | Tight loops, hot paths | | Within same 2 KB page | `AJMP` | 2 | 2 | ROM-critical code | | Outside 2 KB, within 64 KB | `LJMP` | 3 | 4 | Distant routines, vector table | | Computed (variable) | `JMP @A+DPTR` | 1 | 2 | State machines, switch dispatch | ## 3.4 Delay Loop Derivation Using `DJNZ` A classic 8051 software delay uses **nested `DJNZ` loops**: ```assembly DELAY: MOV R1, #FFH ; 1 machine cycle LOOP1: MOV R2, #FFH ; 1 machine cycle per inner iteration LOOP2: DJNZ R2, LOOP2 ; 2 machine cycles × 255 = 510 DJNZ R1, LOOP1 ; 2 machine cycles × 255 = 510 RET ; 2 machine cycles ``` **Total cycles** (excluding the initial `MOV R1` and the final `RET`): $$T_{delay} = \big(1_{outer} + 1_{inner\;setup} + 2 \cdot 255_{inner} + 2_{outer\;DJNZ}\big) \times 255_{outer} \times T_{machine}$$ $$T_{delay} = \big(1 + 1 + 510 + 2\big) \times 255 \times 1\,\mu s$$ $$T_{delay} = 514 \times 255 \times 1\,\mu s = 131{,}070\,\mu s \approx 131\,ms$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Lab Note:** At 11.0592 MHz, 1 machine cycle = 12/11.0592 µs ≈ 1.085 µs. The delay is correspondingly longer. KTU lab manuals almost always specify the **12 MHz** assumption for simplicity. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Branching Group Classification — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB A["8051 Branching Group"]:::root A --> B["Unconditional Jumps"]:::sub A --> C["Conditional Jumps"]:::sub A --> D["Call and Return"]:::sub A --> E["NOP and Control"]:::sub B --> B1["LJMP 02H - 3 bytes - 4 cycles"] B --> B2["AJMP x1H - 2 bytes - 2 cycles"] B --> B3["SJMP 80H - 2 bytes - 2 cycles"] B --> B4["JMP at AplusDPTR 73H - 1 byte - 2 cycles"] C --> C1["Flag Tests"] C --> C2["Bit Tests"] C --> C3["Compare Jumps"] C --> C4["Counter Jumps"] C1 --> C1a["JZ 60H - A equals 0"] C1 --> C1b["JNZ 70H - A not equal 0"] C1 --> C1c["JC 40H - Carry set"] C1 --> C1d["JNC 50H - Carry not set"] C2 --> C2a["JB 20H - Bit equals 1"] C2 --> C2b["JNB 30H - Bit equals 0"] C2 --> C2c["JBC 10H - Bit equals 1 then clear"] C3 --> C3a["CJNE - Compare and Jump if Not Equal"] C4 --> C4a["DJNZ - Decrement and Jump if Not Zero"] D --> D1["LCALL 12H - 3 bytes"] D --> D2["ACALL x1H - 2 bytes"] D --> D3["RET - 1 byte - 4 cycles"] D --> D4["RETI - 1 byte - 4 cycles"] E --> E1["NOP 00H - 1 byte - 1 cycle"] classDef root fill:#0B3D91,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold classDef sub fill:#1E6FBF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold classDef leaf fill:#F2F4F8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 class B1,B2,B3,B4,C1a,C1b,C1c,C1d,C2a,C2b,C2c,C3a,C4a,D1,D2,D3,D4,E1 leaf ``` ## 4.2 LCALL/RET Stack Operation — Sequential Block Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU as 8051 CPU participant SP as Stack Pointer participant RAM as Internal RAM participant PC as Program Counter Note over CPU,PC: LCALL SUB at address 0123H
SUB is at 0456H
Initial SP = 07H CPU->>SP: SP = SP + 1 (= 08H) CPU->>RAM: RAM[08H] = 01H (PCH of return addr 0126H) CPU->>SP: SP = SP + 1 (= 09H) CPU->>RAM: RAM[09H] = 26H (PCL of return addr 0126H) CPU->>PC: PC = 0456H Note over CPU,RAM: --- Subroutine SUB executes ---
--- RET encountered --- CPU->>RAM: PCL = RAM[09H] = 26H CPU->>SP: SP = SP - 1 (= 08H) CPU->>RAM: PCH = RAM[08H] = 01H CPU->>SP: SP = SP - 1 (= 07H) CPU->>PC: PC = 0126H (returns to instruction after LCALL) ``` ## 4.3 Conditional Jump Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR Start([Instruction Fetched]) --> Decode{Decode Opcode} Decode -->|JZ 60H| TestA{A equals 0?} Decode -->|JNZ 70H| TestB{A not equals 0?} Decode -->|JC 40H| TestC{Carry equals 1?} Decode -->|JNC 50H| TestD{Carry equals 0?} Decode -->|CJNE| Sub["Perform A minus data
Set CY flag
Compare for equality"] TestA -->|YES| JYes[Branch to PC plus rel] TestA -->|NO| JNo[Fall through to next sequential] TestB -->|YES| JYes TestB -->|NO| JNo TestC -->|YES| JYes TestC -->|NO| JNo TestD -->|YES| JYes TestD -->|NO| JNo Sub -->|Not Equal| JYes Sub -->|Equal| JNo JYes --> Cont1([Continue at Target]) JNo --> Cont2([Continue at PC plus 2]) ``` ## 4.4 SJMP Range Visualization (Memory Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR SJ["SJMP opcode at 1000H"]:::ins P1["1002H: Next instruction"]:::base P2["..."]:::base P3["107FH: Max forward jump +127"]:::max P4["1080H: Out of range"]:::oob P5["0F82H: Min backward jump -128"]:::max P6["..."]:::base P7["0F81H: Out of range"]:::oob SJ -->|rel = 00H| P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 SJ -.->|rel = FFH = -1| P5 P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7 classDef ins fill:#0B3D91,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold classDef base fill:#F2F4F8,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef max fill:#2E7D32,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold classDef oob fill:#C62828,stroke:#000,color:#FFFFFF,font-weight:bold ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between `LJMP`, `AJMP` and `SJMP` instructions of 8051.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | `LJMP` | `AJMP` | `SJMP` | |---|---|---|---| | Opcode | `02H` | `x1H` | `80H` | | Size | 3 bytes | 2 bytes | 2 bytes | | Cycles | 4 | 2 | 2 | | Address | 16-bit absolute | 11-bit (within 2 KB page) | 8-bit signed relative (−128 to +127) | | Range | Full 64 KB | Within 2 KB page | 256-byte window | | Use | Long-distance jumps | Memory-efficient within a page | Tight loops | ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Explain the function of the `RETI` instruction. Why can it not be replaced by `RET`?** **Model Answer:** `RETI` (Return from Interrupt) pops the return address from the stack into PC — just like `RET`. However, `RETI` additionally **clears the internal "interrupt-in-service" priority latch** in the 8051's interrupt priority hardware, thereby re-enabling further interrupts at the same or lower priority level. `RET` does not clear this latch. Substituting `RET` in an ISR can cause **nested interrupt malfunctions** and system lockup. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU Part B questions for Module 2 carry **14 marks** and offer an **internal choice** between two question variants. Both variants below are designed to mirror the KTU board style. ### Question A: [KTU University Exam – July 2024] **A. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **complete branching group** of instructions in 8051. List opcode, size, cycles, and function for each. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (Step-by-step):** The 8051 branching group is divided into **four sub-groups**: **1. Unconditional Jumps:** - `LJMP addr16` — opcode `02H`, 3 bytes, 4 cycles. Jumps to any address in 64 KB. - `AJMP addr11` — opcode `x1H`, 2 bytes, 2 cycles. Jumps within current 2 KB page. - `SJMP rel` — opcode `80H`, 2 bytes, 2 cycles. Relative jump in −128 to +127 byte range. - `JMP @A+DPTR` — opcode `73H`, 1 byte, 2 cycles. Indirect computed jump. **2. Conditional Jumps (Flag):** - `JZ rel` (`60H`), `JNZ rel` (`70H`) — test Accumulator. - `JC rel` (`40H`), `JNC rel` (`50H`) — test Carry flag. **3. Conditional Jumps (Bit):** - `JB bit,rel` (`20H`), `JNB bit,rel` (`30H`), `JBC bit,rel` (`10H`). **4. Compare and Counter Jumps:** - `CJNE op1,op2,rel` (3 bytes) — compare, set CY, jump if not equal. - `DJNZ byte,rel` (2 or 3 bytes) — decrement, jump if not zero. **5. Call / Return / NOP:** - `LCALL addr16` (`12H`, 3 bytes), `ACALL addr11` (`x1H`, 2 bytes). - `RET` (`22H`, 1 byte), `RETI` (`32H`, 1 byte). - `NOP` (`00H`, 1 byte, 1 cycle). (Diagrammatic classification table earns 2 of the 7 marks.) --- **(b)** Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program to **find the largest number in an array of 10 bytes** stored in internal RAM starting at address `30H`. Store the result in `R7`. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV R0, #30H ; R0 = pointer to array base MOV R1, #0AH ; R1 = counter = 10 MOV R7, #00H ; R7 = assumed maximum (0) DEC R1 ; process first element separately MOV A, @R0 ; A = first element INC R0 LOOP: CJNE A, @R0, NEXT ; compare A with next element NEXT: JNC SKIP ; if A >= new element, skip MOV A, @R0 ; else A = new element (new max) SKIP: INC R0 DJNZ R1, LOOP MOV R7, A ; store result HERE: SJMP HERE ; halt END ``` **Mark Allocation (per KTU scheme):** - Correct initialization (1 mark) - Correct loop counter logic (2 marks) - Correct compare & update logic (2 marks) - Proper use of `DJNZ` and conditional jumps (1 mark) - Final storage and termination (1 mark) --- ### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **B. (a)** Explain the **different addressing modes** used by the `CJNE` and `DJNZ` instructions with examples. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **`CJNE` — Compare and Jump if Not Equal** — supports 4 addressing modes: | Mode | Format | Example | |---|---|---| | Register | `CJNE Rn, #data, rel` | `CJNE R0, #05H, LABEL` | | Direct | `CJNE A, direct, rel` | `CJNE A, 30H, LABEL` | | Immediate | `CJNE A, #data, rel` | `CJNE A, #0FFH, LABEL` | | Indirect | `CJNE @Ri, #data, rel` | `CJNE @R0, #0AH, LABEL` | `CJNE` performs `op1 − op2` internally, sets `CY = 1` if a borrow occurs (i.e., unsigned `op1 < op2`), then jumps to `PC + rel` if the values are unequal. **`DJNZ` — Decrement and Jump if Not Zero** — supports 2 addressing modes: | Mode | Format | Example | |---|---|---| | Register | `DJNZ Rn, rel` | `DJNZ R3, BACK` | | Direct | `DJNZ direct, rel` | `DJNZ 30H, LOOP` | `DJNZ` first decrements the operand by 1, then jumps to `PC + rel` if the result ≠ 0. The register mode uses 2 bytes; the direct mode uses 3 bytes. Both execute in 2 machine cycles. **Key Distinction:** `CJNE` is a *comparator* — it does not modify the operands. `DJNZ` is a *counter* — it modifies the operand. --- **(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to **sort an array of 5 unsigned bytes** (stored in internal RAM starting at `40H`) in **ascending order** using the **bubble sort algorithm**. Use branching instructions for the inner loop. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV R3, #04H ; outer loop count = n-1 = 4 OUTER: MOV R2, #04H ; inner loop count MOV R0, #40H ; pointer to array INNER: MOV A, @R0 ; A = current element INC R0 MOV B, @R0 ; B = next element CJNE A, B, CHECK ; if A == B, no swap CHECK: JNC NOSWAP ; if A <= B (no borrow), skip swap ; SWAP MOV @R0, A DEC R0 MOV @R0, B INC R0 NOSWAP: DJNZ R2, INNER DJNZ R3, OUTER HERE: SJMP HERE END ``` **Mark Allocation:** - Correct outer/inner loop structure (2 marks) - Correct bubble sort comparison and swap (3 marks) - Correct use of `CJNE` + `JNC` for branch decision (1 mark) - Correct termination (1 mark) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deduction Points in 8051 Branching Group Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the 2 KB page rule for `AJMP`.** Markers deduct **1–2 marks** if you write `AJMP` for a target outside the current 2 KB page. > 2. **Using `RET` inside an ISR instead of `RETI`.** This is a **2-mark penalty** because the interrupt priority latch is never cleared. > 3. **Off-by-one in `SJMP` offset calculation.** Marks are lost when students compute `rel` using the SJMP's own address instead of the **address of the byte following the SJMP**. > 4. **Confusing `DJNZ` and `CJNE` semantics.** `DJNZ` decrements and tests for zero. `CJNE` compares and tests for inequality. Confusing these is a **fatal logic error** in sorting/searching programs. > 5. **Not initializing the Stack Pointer (`SP`).** Before any `LCALL`/`ACALL`, you **must** set `SP` (e.g., `MOV SP, #60H`). Missing this on the 8052/8032 family is a **1-mark penalty** in subroutine questions. > 6. **Incorrect carry flag reasoning after `CJNE`.** Examiners expect: "$A < \text{data} \Rightarrow CY = 1$". Reversing this costs **1 mark**. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ⚡ The **Branching Group** modifies the Program Counter (PC) to break sequential execution. - ⚡ `LJMP` is 3 bytes / 4 cycles / 16-bit address; **use for any 64 KB jump**. - ⚡ `AJMP` is 2 bytes / 2 cycles / 11-bit address; **limited to current 2 KB page**. - ⚡ `SJMP` is 2 bytes / 2 cycles / **signed 8-bit offset** (−128 to +127). - ⚡ `JMP @A+DPTR` enables **jump tables** and state-machine dispatch. - ⚡ `JZ`/`JNZ` test Accumulator; `JC`/`JNC` test Carry flag. - ⚡ `JB`/`JNB`/`JBC` test **bit-addressable** RAM (20H–2FH) and SFR bits. - ⚡ `JBC` is the **only jump that clears** the tested bit — ideal for ISR flag clearing. - ⚡ `CJNE` performs subtraction, sets CY based on borrow, jumps if unequal. - ⚡ **`CJNE` carry rule:** if $A < \text{data}$ then $CY = 1$. - ⚡ `DJNZ` is the **canonical loop counter** — decrements first, then tests. - ⚡ `LCALL` / `ACALL` push PC+3 / PC+2 onto stack and jump to subroutine. - ⚡ **`ACALL` reaches only within the current 2 KB page** — same page rule as `AJMP`. - ⚡ `RET` pops PC from stack; `RETI` also **clears the interrupt-in-service latch**. - ⚡ **Always initialize `SP`** (e.g., `MOV SP, #60H`) before any `CALL`. - ⚡ `NOP` consumes exactly **1 machine cycle** — useful for software delay padding. - ⚡ The **return address** pushed by `CALL` is the address of the byte *after* the CALL. - ⚡ Relative offset = `TargetAddress − AddressOfNextInstruction` (signed 8-bit). - ⚡ At 12 MHz, 1 machine cycle = **1 µs** — memorize this for delay calculations. - ⚡ Nested `DJNZ` loops produce delays $\approx N \times M \times 2\,\mu s$ per inner iteration. - ⚡ A **DJNZ** instruction is **2 bytes** in register mode, **3 bytes** in direct mode. ---

topics.title: Addressing Modes - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Addressing Modes - Types # 🧭 Addressing Modes — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📌 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Addressing Modes** in a microcontroller define the **method by which the operand(s)** of an assembly language instruction are **specified, located, and fetched** during program execution. They describe *how the CPU identifies the source of data* and *the destination where the result must be stored*, effectively acting as the **rule-book for operand resolution** between the programmer's instruction and the underlying hardware datapath. In the **Intel 8051 architecture** (the primary focus of KTU PBECT404 Module 2), the CPU uses the **Program Counter (PC)**, **Stack Pointer (SP)**, **Data Pointer (DPTR)**, and the **working register banks (R0–R7)** to compute what is called the **Effective Address (EA)** — the actual physical location in memory that the instruction will act upon. The mode chosen determines *how* this EA is derived. ## 🌍 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Think of addressing modes as different ways of giving directions to a delivery boy:** | 🗣️ You Say | 🧭 Addressing Mode Equivalent | 💡 What the Boy Does | |---|---|---| | *"Deliver this to House No. 25"* | **Immediate** | The data (`25`) travels *with* the instruction itself | | *"Deliver to my cousin Ramesh"* | **Register** | Uses a known internal helper (a CPU register) | | *"Deliver to the house opposite the bank"* | **Direct** | Refers to a *named/fixed* memory location (e.g., `30H`) | | *"Deliver to the house whose address is in this slip"* | **Register Indirect** | Uses a register that *holds the address* of the actual house | | *"Deliver to the (Nth) house from the post office"* | **Indexed** | Combines a base + an offset (`DPTR + A`) | **Key Insight:** The mode doesn't change *what* the CPU delivers — it only changes *how the CPU figures out **where** to deliver it*. Different modes exist because different programming situations demand different levels of flexibility, speed, and memory access pattern. ## 🎯 Why Addressing Modes Matter in Embedded Systems - **Code Density:** Smaller instructions (e.g., Register mode = 1 byte) save precious ROM space in resource-constrained MCUs. - **Execution Speed:** Register addressing is the **fastest** (no memory fetch required). - **Programming Power:** Indirect & indexed modes enable **loops, arrays, and pointer-based data structures** in assembly. - **Hardware Efficiency:** The 8051 supports **only 5 primary modes** because the silicon is hard-wired to decode them — adding more would inflate the die size and cost. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** The 8051 supports **5 standard addressing modes** (some textbooks combine *Indexed* with *Register Indirect*). For board exams, students **must know all 5 with at least two examples each** and the **exact opcode byte size** of representative instructions. # 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧠 The Five Addressing Modes of 8051 — Operational Breakdown ### 1️⃣ Immediate Addressing (`#data`) **Definition:** The operand is a **constant** (literal value) that is embedded *directly into the instruction* itself, following the opcode byte. - **Syntax:** `OPCODE Destination, #constant` - **Mechanism:** The constant is stored in **Program Memory (ROM)**, immediately after the opcode. The CPU fetches it during the instruction cycle. - **Use Case:** Loading known initial values into registers, setting timer values, masking operations. - **Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, #25H ; Load accumulator with 0x25 MOV R3, #0F0H ; Load R3 with 0xF0 MOV DPTR,#4521H ; Load DPTR with 16-bit address ORL A, #00001111B ; Logical OR with bitmask ``` ### 2️⃣ Register Addressing (Implicit Register) **Definition:** The operand is located **inside one of the CPU's internal registers** (R0–R7, A, B, DPTR, C). No memory access is needed. - **Syntax:** `OPCODE Rn` (where n = 0 to 7) - **Mechanism:** The **3-bit register field** in the opcode itself identifies which register. - **Use Case:** Fast arithmetic, register-to-register data movement, loop counters. - **Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, R0 ; Copy R0 into A ADD A, R5 ; A = A + R5 INC R2 ; R2 = R2 + 1 XCH A, R4 ; Exchange A and R4 ``` - **Note:** Only R0–R7 of the *currently selected* register bank participate. ### 3️⃣ Direct Addressing **Definition:** The operand's effective address is an **8-bit constant** provided directly within the instruction. It can address **any of the 128 internal RAM bytes** (00H–7FH) or the **SFR region** (80H–FFH). - **Syntax:** `OPCODE Register, address` - **Mechanism:** The instruction contains a **direct 8-bit address** field that points to the memory byte. - **Use Case:** Accessing SFRs (P0, P1, TCON, SCON, etc.), manipulating bit-addressable memory. - **Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, 30H ; A = contents of internal RAM[0x30] MOV P1, #55H ; Write 0x55 to Port 1 latches MOV 30H, A ; RAM[0x30] = A ANL P0, #0FH ; AND P0 with mask ``` ### 4️⃣ Register Indirect Addressing **Definition:** The operand's address is **not given directly**; instead, it is **contained in a register** (R0, R1 of the current bank, or SP, or DPTR). The `@` symbol indicates indirection. - **Syntax:** `OPCODE Register, @Ri` (i = 0 or 1) or `@DPTR` - **Mechanism:** The chosen register is treated as a **pointer**; the CPU fetches the address *from* the register, then fetches the data *from that address*. - **Use Case:** Array processing, stack operations, table lookups, pointer-based data movement. - **Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, @R0 ; A = contents of RAM[address in R0] MOVX A, @DPTR ; A = contents of EXTERNAL RAM at DPTR PUSH ACC ; Push A onto stack (SP is implicit pointer) MOV @R1, #42H ; RAM[address in R1] = 0x42 ``` ### 5️⃣ Indexed Addressing **Definition:** Used exclusively for **read-only Program Memory (ROM) access** via the `MOVC` instruction. The effective address is computed as the **sum of a base pointer (DPTR or PC) and the accumulator (A)**. - **Mechanism:** $$\text{Effective Address (EA)} = \text{Base Register} + \text{Accumulator (A)}$$ - **Use Case:** Lookup tables (LUTs), character generation, sin/cos tables, branch tables. - **Examples:** ```assembly MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; A = ROM[DPTR + A] MOVC A, @A+PC ; A = ROM[PC + A] (PC is auto-incremented) ``` ## 📊 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | 🔢 Mode | Effective Address Formula | Operand Location | Instruction Size (bytes) | Allowed Registers | |---|---|---|---|---| | Immediate | **EA = PC (post-fetch)** | In ROM, after opcode | 2–3 bytes | A, Rn, DPTR, 16-bit SFRs | | Register | **EA = Register (direct)** | Inside CPU | 1 byte | A, R0–R7, B, DPTR, AB, C | | Direct | **EA = 8-bit address field** | Internal RAM 00H–7FH; SFR 80H–FFH | 2 bytes (3 for 16-bit SFR) | All internal RAM + SFRs | | Register Indirect | **EA = (Ri) or (DPTR) or (SP)** | Internal RAM @Ri; XRAM @DPTR | 1–2 bytes | R0, R1, SP, DPTR | | Indexed | **EA = A + DPTR (or A + PC)** | Program Memory (ROM) only | 1 byte | A + DPTR / A + PC | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Conditions for 8051:** > - **Internal RAM** accessible via Direct/Register Indirect: **00H–7FH** (128 bytes) + **SFRs 80H–FFH** > - **External RAM** accessible only via `@DPTR` with `MOVX` instruction > - **Program ROM** accessible only via `MOVC` (indexed mode) > - **Code memory limit:** 64 KB (16-bit address bus via PC) ## 🛠️ Real-World Engineering Utility - **IoT Firmware:** Direct addressing used heavily for **SFR bit manipulation** (e.g., toggling GPIO pins). - **Signal Processing:** Indexed addressing is the **backbone of Lookup Table (LUT) based algorithms** (e.g., generating sine waves on a DAC). - **RTOS Kernels:** Register indirect mode is essential for **context switching** and **stack frame management**. - **Communication Protocols:** Immediate mode is used to load **baud rate constants** into timer registers for UART setup. # 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 📐 Derivation 1: Effective Address Calculation for Indexed Mode The 8051 `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` instruction computes the **Effective Address** as follows: ### Given Conditions - Accumulator `A = 05H` (the *index* or offset) - Data Pointer `DPTR = 2000H` (the 16-bit *base address* of the lookup table in ROM) ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Identify the base address** $$\text{Base} = \text{DPTR} = 2000\text{H}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the index (offset)** $$\text{Index} = A = 05\text{H} = 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0101_{2}$$ **Step 3 — Perform the 16-bit addition** $$\text{EA} = \text{DPTR} + A$$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{DPTR} & : \quad 0010\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000_{2} \;=\; 2000_{H} \\ \text{A} & : \quad 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0101_{2} \;=\; 0005_{H} \\ \hline \text{SUM} & : \quad 0010\ 0000\ 0000\ 0101_{2} \;=\; 2005_{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Fetch the byte from Program Memory** The instruction `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` will load into `A` the **contents of ROM location `2005H`**. ### Generalised Form $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EA}_{\text{indexed}} &= \text{Base}_{\text{16-bit}} + \text{Offset}_{\text{8-bit}} \\ &= \text{DPTR} + A \quad \text{or} \quad \text{PC} + A \end{aligned} $$ > **Note:** The accumulator `A` is *8 bits wide*, while `DPTR` is *16 bits wide*. The addition is therefore a 16-bit operation where `A` is **zero-extended** to 16 bits first, then added. **No carry flag is affected** because `A` is treated as unsigned and the result is naturally modulo $2^{16}$. --- ## 📐 Derivation 2: Memory Footprint Analysis **Problem:** A KTU question gives the following 8051 program fragment and asks the **total ROM bytes** consumed: ```assembly MOV A, #25H ; Immediate mode MOV R0, A ; Register mode MOV 30H, A ; Direct mode MOV A, @R0 ; Register Indirect mode MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; Indexed mode ``` ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Look up opcode size for each instruction from the 8051 instruction set reference:** | Instruction | Mode | Opcode Bytes | Operand Bytes | Total Bytes | |---|---|---|---|---| | `MOV A, #25H` | Immediate | 1 | 1 | **2** | | `MOV R0, A` | Register | 1 | 0 | **1** | | `MOV 30H, A` | Direct | 1 | 1 | **2** | | `MOV A, @R0` | Reg Indirect | 1 | 0 | **1** | | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Indexed | 1 | 0 | **1** | **Step 2 — Sum the total bytes** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total ROM} &= 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 \\ &= 7\ \text{bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute total machine cycles** Each instruction has a fixed machine cycle count. From the 8051 manual: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MOV A, \#25H} &: 1\ \text{cycle} \\ \text{MOV R0, A} &: 1\ \text{cycle} \\ \text{MOV 30H, A} &: 2\ \text{cycles (memory write)} \\ \text{MOV A, @R0} &: 1\ \text{cycle (internal RAM)} \\ \text{MOVC A, @A+DPTR} &: 2\ \text{cycles (ROM fetch)} \\ \hline \text{Total Cycles} &= 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 2 = 7\ \text{machine cycles} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — If crystal frequency $f_{OSC} = 12\text{ MHz}$**, the machine cycle period is: $$ T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\ \mu s $$ **Step 5 — Total execution time** $$ \begin{aligned} t_{exec} &= \text{Total Cycles} \times T_{MC} \\ &= 7 \times 1\ \mu s = 7\ \mu s \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is exactly how embedded firmware engineers **profile critical code paths**. When you must meet a real-time deadline (e.g., toggling an I/O pin every 10 µs for bit-banging), you choose **Register and Register-Indirect modes** to minimize cycle count. --- ## 📐 Derivation 3: Range of Accessible Memory per Mode The 8051 has a **16-bit address bus**, so theoretically $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes (64 KB) of addressable space exists. | Mode | Address Bus Width Used | Maximum Accessible Range | |---|---|---| | Register Indirect (`@R0/@R1`) | **8 bits** (internal) | 256 bytes of internal RAM (00H–FFH) | | Register Indirect (`@DPTR`) | **16 bits** (external) | 64 KB of XRAM | | Direct | **8 bits** | 256 bytes (00H–FFH, internal only) | | Indexed | **16 bits** (PC/DPTR) | 64 KB of ROM | | Immediate | N/A (data is in instruction) | Constrained by operand size: 8-bit or 16-bit literal | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Common Student Mistake:** *Register Indirect mode with `@R0` is **NOT** the same as Direct mode with address `R0`.* Indirect uses the **contents of R0 as a pointer**, while Direct uses a *fixed 8-bit literal* that happens to be in the instruction. The two instructions `MOV A, 30H` and `MOV A, @R0` (where R0 holds 30H) achieve the **same final effect**, but differ in **instruction size, execution time, and flexibility**. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 📊 Diagram 1: Hierarchical Classification of 8051 Addressing Modes ```mermaid graph TD A["8051 Addressing Modes"] --> B["Immediate
Operand is a constant"] A --> C["Register
Operand is a CPU register"] A --> D["Direct
Operand address is in the instruction"] A --> E["Register Indirect
Register holds the operand address"] A --> F["Indexed
EA = Base + A offset"] B --> B1["MOV A, #25H"] C --> C1["MOV A, R0"] C --> C2["ADD A, R5"] D --> D1["MOV A, 30H"] D --> D2["MOV P1, A"] E --> E1["MOV A, @R0"] E --> E2["MOVX A, @DPTR"] F --> F1["MOVC A, @A+DPTR"] F --> F2["MOVC A, @A+PC"] style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:3px style B fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style C fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff style D fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000 style E fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff style F fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff ``` ## 📊 Diagram 2: Effective Address Computation Flow (Register Indirect & Indexed) ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Instruction Fetched
Opcode Decoded"] --> Q1{"Mode?"} Q1 -->|Immediate| IMM["Fetch constant
from next ROM bytes
→ Store in destination"] Q1 -->|Register| REG["Read source register
directly from CPU
→ 0 memory access"] Q1 -->|Direct| DIR["Read 8-bit address
from instruction
→ Access internal RAM/SFR"] Q1 -->|Reg Indirect| RI["Read pointer register
R0, R1, SP, or DPTR
→ Get target address"] Q1 -->|Indexed| IDX["Read DPTR or PC
Read accumulator A
EA = Base + A"] RI --> RI2["If MOVX used
→ External RAM"] IDX --> IDX2["If MOVC used
→ Program ROM"] IMM --> EXEC["Execute Operation
Write to Destination"] REG --> EXEC DIR --> EXEC RI2 --> EXEC IDX2 --> EXEC EXEC --> END["PC advances to
next instruction"] style START fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style Q1 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000 style EXEC fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff style END fill:#6366f1,color:#ffffff style RI2 fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff style IDX2 fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff ``` ## 📊 Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Operand Resolution ```mermaid graph LR PC["Program Counter PC"] --> OF["Opcode Fetch Unit"] OF --> DEC["Instruction Decoder"] DEC --> AMU["Addressing Mode Unit"] AMU -->|Immediate| ROM1["Constant from ROM"] AMU -->|Register| REGFILE["Register File R0-R7, A, B"] AMU -->|Direct| IRAM["Internal RAM 00H-7FH"] AMU -->|Direct| SFR["SFR Region 80H-FFH"] AMU -->|Reg Indirect| IRAM2["Internal RAM via R0/R1"] AMU -->|Reg Indirect| XRAM["External RAM via DPTR"] AMU -->|Indexed| PROM["Program ROM via DPTR+PC"] REGFILE --> ALU["ALU
Arithmetic Logic Unit"] IRAM --> ALU IRAM2 --> ALU SFR --> ALU ROM1 --> ALU PROM --> ALU XRAM --> ALU ALU --> RESULT["Result Bus
→ Destination Register/Memory"] style PC fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff style AMU fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style ALU fill:#ef4444,color:#ffffff style RESULT fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff style PROM fill:#a855f7,color:#ffffff style XRAM fill:#f59e0b,color:#000000 ``` ## 🧠 Memory Map Visualisation (Text-Based Block Schematic) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CodeROM["PROGRAM MEMORY (ROM) - 64KB"] direction TB R0["0000H-0002H : Reset Vector"] R1["0003H : External Interrupt 0 ISR"] R2["000BH : Timer 0 Overflow ISR"] R3["0013H : External Interrupt 1 ISR"] R4["001BH : Timer 1 Overflow ISR"] R5["0023H : Serial Port ISR"] R6["0030H-7FFFH : User Code & Lookup Tables"] end subgraph IntRAM["INTERNAL RAM - 128 Bytes"] direction TB BANK0["00H-07H : Register Bank 0 R0-R7"] BANK1["08H-0FH : Register Bank 1"] BANK2["10H-17H : Register Bank 2"] BANK3["18H-1FH : Register Bank 3"] BF["20H-2FH : Bit-Addressable Area (16 bytes)"] GEN["30H-7FH : General Purpose RAM"] end subgraph SFRReg["SFR REGION - 128 Bytes"] direction TB P0["80H : Port 0 Latch"] SP["81H : Stack Pointer"] DPL["82H : DPTR Low Byte"] DPH["83H : DPTR High Byte"] PCON["87H : Power Control"] TCON["88H : Timer Control"] SCON["98H : Serial Control"] P1["90H : Port 1 Latch"] P2["A0H : Port 2 Latch"] IE["A8H : Interrupt Enable"] P3["B0H : Port 3 Latch"] IP["B8H : Interrupt Priority"] end style CodeROM fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style IntRAM fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#10b981 style SFRReg fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b ``` # 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### ❓ Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define addressing modes. List the addressing modes supported by the 8051 microcontroller with one example each.** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): **Definition (1 Mark):** > *An addressing mode specifies the rule by which the operand of an assembly instruction is located — i.e., how the CPU identifies the data on which the instruction will operate.* **List with Examples (2 Marks):** | S.No | Addressing Mode | Example Instruction | Operand Location | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Immediate | `MOV A, #0FH` | Constant `0FH` embedded in instruction | | 2 | Register | `MOV A, R0` | CPU register R0 | | 3 | Direct | `MOV A, 30H` | Internal RAM address 30H | | 4 | Register Indirect | `MOV A, @R0` | Address in R0 points to data | | 5 | Indexed | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | ROM address = DPTR + A | --- ### ❓ Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Register Addressing and Register Indirect Addressing with suitable examples.** #### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks): | Parameter | Register Addressing | Register Indirect Addressing | |---|---|---| | **Symbol** | `Rn` | `@Rn` | | **Operand Location** | Inside the CPU register itself | In memory; the *register holds the address* of the data | | **Memory Access** | None — direct from register | One extra memory read required | | **Instruction Size** | 1 byte (fastest) | 1 byte | | **Flexibility** | Static — fixed register | Dynamic — pointer can be changed at runtime | | **Use Case** | Fast arithmetic, counters | Array traversal, stack operations | | **Example** | `MOV A, R3` | `MOV A, @R0` (where R0 = 40H loads A from RAM[40H]) | **Key Distinction:** In Register mode, the operand *is* the register content. In Register Indirect mode, the register content is a *pointer* to the operand. (1 mark for this critical clarification.) --- ## 📝 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - ✘ **Do not** confuse the `@` symbol's placement — `@R0` ≠ `R0@`. The `@` is **mandatory** before the register to indicate indirection. > - ✘ **Do not** write `MOV A, #@R0` — this is **not a valid 8051 instruction**. The `#` and `@` cannot combine in the same operand. > - ✘ **Do not** forget that **Indexed mode uses `MOVC`**, not `MOV`. Using `MOV A, @A+DPTR` will assemble but is logically incorrect for ROM access. > - ✘ **Do not** state that `MOVX @DPTR, A` accesses ROM — `MOVX` is for **external RAM only**. > - ✘ **Do not** assume all 5 modes are interchangeable — each has specific restrictions on which registers and memory spaces it can address. --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Module 2]` **(a) Explain the five addressing modes of the 8051 microcontroller with syntax and at least two examples each. (7 Marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution: **1. Immediate Addressing (1.4 Marks):** - **Syntax:** `OPCODE dest, #data` - **Description:** The 8-bit or 16-bit constant is part of the instruction and stored in program memory immediately after the opcode. - **Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, #0FH ; Load 0F into A MOV DPTR, #2000H ; Load base address 2000H into DPTR ANL A, #11110000B ; Mask lower nibble ``` - **Key Feature:** No memory read of data — the constant *travels* with the instruction. **2. Register Addressing (1.4 Marks):** - **Syntax:** `OPCODE A, Rn` or `OPCODE Rn` - **Description:** Operands are in one of the eight working registers (R0–R7) of the currently selected bank, or in A, B, or DPTR. - **Examples:** ```assembly ADD A, R2 ; A = A + R2 XCH A, R4 ; Swap contents of A and R4 INC R0 ; R0 = R0 + 1 ``` - **Key Feature:** **Fastest mode** — only 1 byte, 1 machine cycle. **3. Direct Addressing (1.4 Marks):** - **Syntax:** `OPCODE A, address` or `OPCODE address, A` - **Description:** The 8-bit address of the operand is included in the instruction; allows access to any internal RAM location (00H–7FH) or SFR (80H–FFH). - **Examples:** ```assembly MOV A, 30H ; A = RAM[30H] MOV P1, A ; Copy A to Port 1 MOV 20H, #0 ; Clear bit-addressable RAM[20H] ``` - **Key Feature:** Only mode that can **directly access any bit-addressable SFR** (e.g., P0, P1, TCON). **4. Register Indirect Addressing (1.4 Marks):** - **Syntax:** `OPCODE A, @Ri` (i = 0 or 1) or `MOVX A, @DPTR` - **Description:** The register holds the **memory address** of the actual operand; the data is fetched by dereferencing the pointer. - **Examples:** ```assembly MOV R1, #40H MOV A, @R1 ; A = RAM[40H] MOVX A, @DPTR ; A = External RAM[DPTR] PUSH ACC ; Stack push (uses SP as implicit pointer) ``` - **Key Feature:** Essential for **loops over arrays** and **stack operations**. **5. Indexed Addressing (1.4 Marks):** - **Syntax:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` or `MOVC A, @A+PC` - **Description:** Effective address = base register (DPTR or PC) + accumulator (A). Used **only for ROM lookups**. - **Examples:** ```assembly MOV DPTR, #2000H ; Base of sine table in ROM MOV A, #45 ; Index = 45 degrees MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; A = sin(45°) lookup value ``` - **Key Feature:** The **only mode** that uses `MOVC` and the **only mode for ROM access** in 8051. --- **(b) Write an 8051 ALP to add two 8-bit numbers stored in internal RAM locations 30H and 31H. Store the result in 32H. Use appropriate addressing modes and comment on each line. Calculate the ROM size and execution time assuming f_osc = 11.0592 MHz. (7 Marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution: **Assembly Program (3 Marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H ; Reset vector origin START: MOV A, 30H ; Direct mode: load first number ADD A, 31H ; Direct mode: add second number MOV 32H, A ; Direct mode: store sum SJMP START ; Short jump back to start END ; End of assembly ``` **Line-by-Line Comment on Addressing Mode (2 Marks):** | Line | Mode Used | Why this mode? | |---|---|---| | `MOV A, 30H` | **Direct** | Operand is at known fixed address 30H | | `ADD A, 31H` | **Direct** | Operand is at known fixed address 31H | | `MOV 32H, A` | **Direct** | Destination is a known fixed RAM address | | `SJMP START` | **Relative** (a sub-type) | Offset encoded as signed 8-bit relative to PC | **ROM Size Calculation (1 Mark):** | Instruction | Bytes | |---|---| | `MOV A, 30H` | 2 | | `ADD A, 31H` | 2 | | `MOV 32H, A` | 2 | | `SJMP START` | 2 | | **Total** | **8 bytes** | **Execution Time Calculation (1 Mark):** With $f_{OSC} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$: $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.085\ \mu s$$ Machine cycles per instruction: - `MOV A, 30H` → 1 cycle - `ADD A, 31H` → 1 cycle - `MOV 32H, A` → 1 cycle - `SJMP START` → 2 cycles - **Total = 5 cycles** $$ t_{exec} = 5 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 5.425\ \mu s $$ --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - Supplementary 2023]` **(a) Explain Register Indirect and Indexed addressing modes in detail. Compare the two with a focus on memory spaces accessed, instructions used, and typical applications. (7 Marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution: **Register Indirect Addressing (3.5 Marks):** **Definition:** The 8-bit or 16-bit register (R0, R1, SP, or DPTR) acts as a **pointer** to the operand in memory. The `@` operator instructs the assembler to treat the register's contents as an *address* rather than as *data*. **Mechanism:** ``` Instruction → Reads register Ri → Contents are address → Fetches data from that address ``` **Permissible Forms:** - `MOV A, @R0` / `MOV A, @R1` → Internal RAM 00H–FFH - `MOVX A, @DPTR` → External RAM 0000H–FFFFH - `PUSH` / `POP` / `CALL` / `RET` use SP implicitly **Typical Applications:** Array iteration, stack manipulation, buffer pointers, indirect subroutine calls. --- **Indexed Addressing (3.5 Marks):** **Definition:** A specialized mode used **exclusively for reading from Program ROM** (read-only code memory) through the `MOVC` instruction. The accumulator holds an 8-bit offset, which is added to a 16-bit base (DPTR or PC) to form the effective ROM address. **Mechanism:** $$ \text{Effective Address (EA)} = \underbrace{\text{DPTR or PC}}_{\text{16-bit base}} + \underbrace{\text{A}}_{\text{8-bit index}} $$ **Permissible Forms:** - `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → Most common, DPTR is the table base - `MOVC A, @A+PC` → Compact form, PC is auto-incremented first **Typical Applications:** Lookup tables (sine, hex-to-7-seg, ASCII), branch tables for `JMP @A+DPTR`, character font generation for LCDs. --- **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Register Indirect | Indexed | |---|---|---| | Memory Accessed | **Data Memory** (internal/external RAM) | **Program Memory** (ROM only) | | Instruction Used | `MOV`, `MOVX`, `PUSH`, `POP` | `MOVC` only | | Base Register | R0, R1, SP, DPTR | DPTR, PC | | Offset | None (register *is* the pointer) | Accumulator A | | Read/Write | Both | Read-only | | Typical Use | Runtime data manipulation | Compile-time constant tables | --- **(b) Design an 8051 program to access a Lookup Table stored in ROM starting at address 0200H. The table contains the hex values of digits 0–9 (i.e., 3FH, 06H, 5BH, 4FH, 66H, 6DH, 7DH, 07H, 7FH, 6FH — for a common-cathode 7-segment display). The digit to be displayed is given in RAM location 40H. Write the program and explain the role of indexed addressing mode here. (7 Marks)** #### ✅ Model Solution: **Assembly Program (3 Marks):** ```assembly ORG 0000H START: MOV DPTR, #0200H ; Load base address of LUT MOV A, 40H ; Get digit (0-9) from RAM[40H] MOVC A, @A+DPTR ; Lookup the 7-seg code from ROM MOV P1, A ; Send code to Port 1 (connected to 7-seg) SJMP START END ``` **Role of Indexed Addressing (2 Marks):** 1. **DPTR is the base register** — points to the start of the LUT in ROM (`0200H`). 2. **A is the index** — holds the digit (0–9) that acts as the *offset* from the base. 3. **EA Computation:** The 8051 hardware adds `A` to `DPTR` internally: - If `A = 05`, then `EA = 0200H + 05H = 0205H` - The byte at `0205H` is `6DH` — the 7-seg code for digit **5**. 4. **Why this mode is mandatory:** Only `MOVC` (indexed) can read from ROM. The LUT must reside in code memory to save precious internal RAM. **ROM Used:** `0200H` to `0209H` = **10 bytes** of ROM for the table, plus program code (≈ 10 bytes) = **20 bytes total**. (1 Mark) **Execution Time (1 Mark):** - 4 instructions × ~1 cycle each + MOVC (2 cycles) = ~6 cycles - At 11.0592 MHz: $6 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 6.51\ \mu s$ **Engineering Application:** This is the **standard pattern** used in digital clock projects, scoreboard displays, and any embedded system where a microcontroller drives multiplexed 7-segment displays. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > 🎯 **Critical Takeaways for KTU 2024 Exam:** - 🔑 The 8051 supports **exactly 5 addressing modes**: Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, and Indexed. - 🔑 **Register mode is the fastest** (1 byte, 1 cycle) — use it for hot loops. - 🔑 **Immediate mode** uses the `#` prefix; without `#`, the assembler assumes a **direct address** (a very common bug). - 🔑 **Direct addressing** is the **only mode** that can access **all 128 SFRs** with a single instruction. - 🔑 **Register Indirect** uses `@R0`, `@R1` for internal RAM and `@DPTR` for external RAM (with `MOVX`). - 🔑 **Indexed mode** is the **only way to read ROM** in 8051 — uses `MOVC` with `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`. - 🔑 Effective address formula for indexed: $\text{EA} = \text{DPTR (or PC)} + A$ (8-bit A is zero-extended to 16 bits before addition). - 🔑 Machine cycle period: $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$. At **11.0592 MHz** (the KTU-recommended crystal for UART), $T_{MC} = 1.085\ \mu s$. - 🔑 **External RAM is ONLY accessible via `MOVX`** — `MOV` cannot reach it. - 🔑 **Stack-related instructions** (`PUSH`, `POP`, `CALL`, `RET`, `RETI`) implicitly use **Register Indirect mode via SP**. - 🔑 For **array traversal**, always initialise `R0`/`R1` with the starting address and use `INC`/`DEC` to step through. - 🔑 For **lookup tables**, the table **must** be in ROM (use `.DB` or `DB` directives) and accessed via `MOVC`. - 🔑 The 8051 has a **16-bit Program Counter**, allowing it to address up to **64 KB of code memory** ($2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes). - 🔑 In indexed mode, the **accumulator is NOT modified** by the addition (only the temporary internal adder uses it) — a subtle but exam-favourite point. - 🔑 **Common board trick:** `MOV A, 30H` ≠ `MOV A, #30H`. The first loads the *contents* of RAM[30H]; the second loads the *value* 30H itself.

topics.title: Accessing Data from Internal and External Memory
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Accessing Data from Internal and External Memory

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller**, memory access is performed using a **strictly partitioned Harvard architecture**. The system maintains **two physically separate address spaces**: one for **Program Memory (Code ROM)** and one for **Data Memory (RAM)**. Data can be fetched from three logically distinct sources:

1. **Internal RAM** — 256 bytes on-chip, comprising Lower 128 (00H–7FH) and Upper 128 (80H–FFH) SFR region.
2. **Internal ROM** — 4 KB on-chip program memory (0000H–0FFFH).
3. **External Memory** — Expandable up to **64 KB code memory** and **64 KB data memory** (total **128 KB** addressable), accessed via external bus cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):**
> The 8051 uses a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** for code memory and a **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)** for external data memory, allowing each memory space to address up to **65,536 (64 KB)** locations independently.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Two-Room Library" Concept

Think of the 8051 as a **library with two completely separate reading rooms**:

| Library Room | Analogy | Microcontroller Equivalent |
|--------------|---------|---------------------------|
| **Reference Room** (locked, read-only) | Stacks of instruction manuals, opened by a special librarian | **Code Memory (ROM)** — accessed via **PSEN** signal |
| **Workspace Room** (writable, scratchpad) | Notebooks and registers you scribble on | **Data Memory (RAM)** — accessed via **RD/WR** signals |

The librarian in the Reference Room hands you books only when you show a "PSEN pass."
