The librarian in the Workspace Room hands you notebooks only when you show an "RD pass" (or takes them with a "WR pass"). They are **physically isolated** and **cannot be confused**, even though both have addresses from 0000H to FFFFH. The **same numeric address refers to two completely different physical bytes** depending on which room you visit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two rooms?** This Harvard separation allows the CPU to fetch the next instruction **simultaneously** while reading a data byte — a major speed advantage over Von Neumann designs.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Internal RAM size:** 128 bytes (00H–7FH) for the basic 8051 family
- **SFR space:** 128 bytes (80H–FFH), accessible only by **direct addressing**
- **Internal ROM:** 4 KB (0000H–0FFFH)
- **Maximum external code memory:** 64 KB (0000H–FFFFH)
- **Maximum external data memory:** 64 KB (0000H–FFFFH)
- **Address bus width:** 16 bits (P0 = A0–A7, P2 = A8–A15)
- **Data bus width:** 8 bits (multiplexed with P0)
- **Number of general-purpose registers:** 32 (4 banks × 8 registers)
- **Number of bit-addressable locations:** 128 bits (in 16 bytes from 20H–2FH) + 128 bits in SFRs

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Data Memory (Internal RAM) — Layered Architecture

The 8051 internal RAM is **divided into three functional zones** in the lower 128 bytes, plus the SFR zone in the upper 128 bytes.

#### Zone 1: Register Banks (Addresses 00H – 1FH)

- **32 bytes** organized as **4 banks of 8 registers each (R0–R7)**.
- **Bank selection** is controlled by bits **RS0 and RS1** of the **Program Status Word (PSW)**.
- Only **one bank is "active"** at any given time.
- After **power-on reset**, **Bank 0** is selected by default (RS1=0, RS0=0).

| Bank | RS1 | RS0 | Register Range | Address Range |
|------|-----|-----|----------------|---------------|
| Bank 0 | 0 | 0 | R0–R7 | 00H–07H |
| Bank 1 | 0 | 1 | R0–R7 | 08H–0FH |
| Bank 2 | 1 | 0 | R0–R7 | 10H–17H |
| Bank 3 | 1 | 1 | R0–R7 | 18H–1FH |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bank-switching advantage:** Useful in interrupt-driven systems. When an ISR fires, it can switch to a private register bank so that the main program's R0–R7 are not corrupted — **zero stack overhead, zero PUSH/POP time**.

#### Zone 2: Bit-Addressable Area (Addresses 20H – 2FH)

- **16 bytes = 128 individually addressable bits** (bit 00H to bit 7FH).
- Instructions like `SETB 20H.3`, `CLR 30H.7`, `MOV C, 2FH.0` operate on individual bits.
- The bit address is calculated as:
  
  $$\text{Bit Address} = \text{Byte Address} - 20\text{H, then} \times 8 + \text{bit position}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Only this 16-byte region** in the lower 128 bytes supports single-bit access without affecting neighbouring bits. This is **why the 8051 is called a "bit-manipulation engine."**

#### Zone 3: General-Purpose RAM (Addresses 30H – 7FH)

- **80 bytes** of scratchpad RAM used for:
  - **Stack operations** (SP points here; reset value = 07H)
  - Variable storage
  - Buffer areas
- Cannot be accessed bit-wise, only byte-wise.

#### Zone 4: Special Function Registers — SFRs (Addresses 80H – FFH)

- **128 byte addresses**, but only **~21 are physically implemented** (rest are "holes" / reserved).
- Accessible **only via direct addressing mode** (not via `R0`/`R1` indirect).
- Includes ACC, B, PSW, SP, DPTR (DPH+DPL), P0–P3, TCON, TMOD, TL0, TH0, TL1, TH1, SCON, SBUF, IE, IP, PCON.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** The SFR region (80H–FFH) and the upper RAM (80H–FFH) **share the same addresses** but are accessed by **different addressing modes**. `MOV A, 80H` accesses SFR P0; `MOV A, @R0` with R0=80H accesses... **nothing in the standard 8051** (only the 8052 has upper RAM).

### 2.2 Internal Program Memory (Code ROM)

- **4 KB** on-chip, addresses **0000H–0FFFH**.
- Program Counter (PC) starts at **0000H** on reset.
- Fixed vectors:
  - `0000H` → Reset vector
  - `0003H` → External Interrupt 0 (INT0)
  - `000BH` → Timer 0 Overflow
  - `0013H` → External Interrupt 1 (INT1)
  - `001BH` → Timer 1 Overflow
  - `0023H` → Serial Port Interrupt
- Above 0FFFH, the 8051 automatically fetches from **external code memory** using PSEN.

### 2.3 External Memory Interface — The Bus Mechanism

When the CPU needs to access off-chip memory, the 8051 generates a **bus cycle** using **Ports 0 and 2**:

- **P0** acts as the **multiplexed Address/Data bus**:
  - First, the low-order address byte (A0–A7) is placed on P0 (latched by **ALE** signal).
  - Then P0 is repurposed as the data bus (D0–D7).
- **P2** holds the **high-order address byte (A8–A15)** for the entire cycle.
- **ALE** (Address Latch Enable) — signal used to latch the low-byte address externally using a 74HC373/74LS373 latch.
- **PSEN** (Program Store Enable) — read strobe for **external code memory**.
- **RD** and **WR** — read/write strobes for **external data memory**.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Limit |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Total internal RAM (basic 8051) | $128 + 128$ (SFR) | **256 bytes** |
| 2 | Total internal ROM | $4 \times 1024$ | **4 KB** |
| 3 | Max external code memory | $2^{16}$ | **64 KB** |
| 4 | Max external data memory | $2^{16}$ | **64 KB** |
| 5 | Max combined addressable | $64\text{K} + 64\text{K}$ | **128 KB** |
| 6 | Bit address in bit-area | $(N - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + b$, $N$ in 20H–2FH, $b$ in 0–7 | Range 00H–7FH |
| 7 | Active register bank | $B = 4 \cdot \text{RS1} + 2 \cdot \text{RS0}$ | $B \in \{0,1,2,3\}$ |
| 8 | Address latch formula | $\text{Latched A0–A7} = \text{74HC373 output}$ | From P0 + ALE |
| 9 | Number of bit-addressable bits (lower RAM) | $16 \times 8$ | **128 bits** |
| 10 | Number of bit-addressable SFRs | varies (e.g., P0, TCON, ACC, B) | **~16 SFRs** |
| 11 | Stack growth direction | $\text{SP} \uparrow$ on PUSH, $\downarrow$ on POP | **Upward** |
| 12 | SP after reset | 07H | First PUSH → 08H |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Industrial control:** Bit-addressable area allows direct I/O pin toggling in real-time control loops (motor, valve).
- **Communication stacks:** General-purpose RAM holds buffer arrays for UART reception.
- **Memory expansion:** Adding external EPROM (program) and SRAM (data) is essential in large embedded designs.
- **OS/RTOS in 8051:** Register banks emulate **zero-overhead context switching** — the heart of tiny real-time kernels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bit Address from Byte Address and Bit Position

**Problem:** Compute the bit address for byte address `2CH`, bit position `5`.

**Given:**
- Byte address: $N = 2\text{CH}$
- Bit position: $b = 5$

**Step 1 — Convert hex to decimal for clarity:**

$$N = 2\text{CH} = (2 \times 16) + 12 = 32 + 12 = 44_{10}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract 20H from the byte address to find the relative byte index:**

$$N_{\text{rel}} = N - 20\text{H} = 2\text{CH} - 20\text{H} = 0\text{CH} = 12_{10}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply the relative index by 8 to find the starting bit address of the byte:**

$$\text{Start bit} = N_{\text{rel}} \times 8 = 12 \times 8 = 96_{10}$$

**Step 4 — Add the bit position offset to find the absolute bit address:**

$$\text{Bit address} = (N - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + b = 96 + 5 = 101_{10}$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to hex:**

$$101_{10} = 6 \times 16 + 5 = 65\text{H}$$

**Final Result:** Bit `2CH.5` = bit address `65H`.

**Verification:** Bits 20H.0 → 20H.7 = 00H–07H; bits 2CH.0 → 2CH.7 = 60H–67H ✓ (bit 5 → 65H).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Bits are numbered **linearly** from 20H.0 (bit 00H) up to 2FH.7 (bit 7FH), forming a contiguous 128-bit memory space, even though they physically live in 16 separate bytes.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bank Selection Logic

**Problem:** What is the active register bank if PSW = `0001 1000` binary?

**Step 1 — Write out PSW bits** (PSW = D7H…D0H):

$$\text{PSW} = \begin{array}{cccccccc} \text{CY} & \text{AC} & \text{F0} & \text{RS1} & \text{RS0} & \text{OV} & - & \text{P} \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}$$

**Step 2 — Extract RS1 and RS0:**

$$\text{RS1} = 1, \quad \text{RS0} = 1$$

**Step 3 — Compute bank index using the linear formula:**

$$B = 4 \cdot \text{RS1} + 2 \cdot \text{RS0} = 4(1) + 2(1) = 6$$

But $B$ must be in $\{0,1,2,3\}$, so we use the mod-4 form:

$$B = (2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}) = 2(1) + 1 = 3$$

**Final Result:** **Bank 3 is active** → R0–R7 are at addresses 18H–1FH.

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum External Memory Addressable

**Step 1 — Address bus width for external memory:**

$$W = 16 \text{ bits (P0 gives low byte, P2 gives high byte)}$$

**Step 2 — Total locations:**

$$N = 2^W = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$$

**Step 3 — Convert to KB:**

$$N = \frac{65{,}536}{1024} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 4 — Apply separately to code and data memory (Harvard):**

$$\text{Code}_{\max} = 64\text{ KB}, \quad \text{Data}_{\max} = 64\text{ KB}$$

**Total addressable space:**

$$\text{Total} = \text{Code}_{\max} + \text{Data}_{\max} = 64 + 64 = 128 \text{ KB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Always state **both** memory spaces when asked "maximum memory of 8051." Students who say "128 KB of memory" **lose marks** — it is **two separate 64 KB spaces**, not a single 128 KB linear space.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Addressing Modes vs Memory Region

The following table maps **which addressing mode can legally access which memory region** — a classic KTU examination question.

| Addressing Mode | Syntax Example | Internal RAM 00H–7FH | SFR 80H–FFH | Internal ROM | External Code | External Data (XDATA) |
|-----------------|----------------|----------------------|-------------|--------------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Immediate** | `MOV A, #25H` | ✗ (target) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Register** | `MOV A, R0` | ✓ (R0–R7) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Direct** | `MOV A, 30H` | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **Register Indirect** | `MOV A, @R0` | ✓ (lower 128) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (with `MOVX`) |
| **Indexed (Base+Index)** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| **Relative** | `SJMP rel` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (PC-relative) | ✓ | ✗ |
| **Absolute** | `AJMP addr11` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (within 2K) | ✓ | ✗ |
| **Long** | `LJMP addr16` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (full 64K) | ✓ | ✗ |
| **MOVX** | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **MOVC** | `MOVC A, @A+PC` | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ |

### 3.5 The MOVX vs MOVC Distinction — A Subtle Derivation

The 8051 has **three "MOV" variants** that map to **three physical buses**:

1. `MOV` → internal RAM and SFR (using internal bus).
2. `MOVC` (move Constant) → reads **code memory** using the **PSEN** strobe; the suffix "C" means "Code."
3. `MOVX` (move eXternal) → reads/writes **external data memory** using the **RD/WR** strobes; the suffix "X" means "eXternal."

**Step-by-step execution trace of `MOVX A, @DPTR`:**

$$\text{Step 1: DPTR (16-bit)} \rightarrow \text{Address Bus A0–A15}$$

$$\text{Step 2: ALE pulses high} \rightarrow \text{74HC373 latches A0–A7 from P0}$$

$$\text{Step 3: P0 is released} \rightarrow \text{becomes input (read cycle)}$$

$$\text{Step 4: RD goes low} \rightarrow \text{external SRAM drives D0–D7 onto P0}$$

$$\text{Step 5: CPU reads P0} \rightarrow \text{latches byte into ACC}$$

$$\text{Step 6: RD goes high, ALE goes high} \rightarrow \text{cycle ends}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** `MOVX` can **only** use `@R0`, `@R1`, or `@DPTR` for indirect addressing. `MOVX A, @R2` is **illegal** and will fail to assemble.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Internal RAM Memory Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node0["Internal RAM Lower 128 Bytes 00H to 7FH"]
    node1["Register Bank 0 00H to 07H R0 to R7"]
    node2["Register Bank 1 08H to 0FH R0 to R7"]
    node3["Register Bank 2 10H to 17H R0 to R7"]
    node4["Register Bank 3 18H to 1FH R0 to R7"]
    node5["Bit Addressable Area 20H to 2FH 128 bits 00H to 7FH"]
    node6["General Purpose RAM 30H to 7FH Stack and Variables"]
    node7["SFR Region 80H to FFH Direct Addressing Only"]

    node0 --> node1
    node0 --> node2
    node0 --> node3
    node0 --> node4
    node0 --> node5
    node0 --> node6

    node8["Internal ROM 4KB 0000H to 0FFFH"]
    node9["Interrupt Vectors 0003H 000BH 0013H 001BH 0023H"]
    node8 --> node9

    node10["External Code Memory 64KB PSEN Strobe"]
    node11["External Data Memory 64KB RD WR Strobe"]

    node0 --- node8
    node8 --- node10
    node6 --- node11
```

### 4.2 External Memory Interfacing Schematic (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcu["8051 Microcontroller"]
    p0["Port P0 Multiplexed AD0 to AD7"]
    p2["Port P2 Address A8 to A15"]
    ale["ALE Pin"]
    psen["PSEN Pin"]
    rdwr["RD and WR Pins"]
    latch["74HC373 Address Latch A0 to A7 Latched"]
    rom["External Code ROM 64KB Max"]
    sram["External Data SRAM 64KB Max"]

    mcu --- p0
    mcu --- p2
    mcu --- ale
    mcu --- psen
    mcu --- rdwr
    p0 --- latch
    latch --- rom
    latch --- sram
    p2 --- rom
    p2 --- sram
    psen --- rom
    rdwr --- sram
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** When ALE = 1, P0 outputs A0–A7 and the latch is **transparent**. When ALE = 0, the latch **freezes** A0–A7 and P0 is free to carry D0–D7. This demultiplexing is the heart of the 8051 external bus design.

### 4.3 Memory Read Sequence — Functional Flow (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["CPU initiates MOVX A at DPTR"]
    s2["DPTR address placed on P0 low byte and P2 high byte"]
    s3["ALE pulses HIGH Latch A0 to A7 via 74HC373"]
    s4["P0 released Becomes high impedance input"]
    s5["RD signal driven LOW by 8051"]
    s6["External SRAM outputs data on D0 to D7 P0 pins"]
    s7["CPU reads P0 Latches into ACC"]
    s8["RD goes HIGH ALE goes HIGH Cycle Ends"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8
```

### 4.4 Register Bank Selection — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q1{"RS1 equals 0?"}
    q2{"RS0 equals 0?"}
    b0["Bank 0 Active 00H to 07H"]
    b1["Bank 1 Active 08H to 0FH"]
    b2["Bank 2 Active 10H to 17H"]
    b3["Bank 3 Active 18H to 1FH"]
    q3{"RS0 equals 0?"}

    q1 -- YES --> q2
    q1 -- NO --> q3
    q2 -- YES --> b0
    q2 -- NO --> b1
    q3 -- YES --> b2
    q3 -- NO --> b3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Explain the function of **PSEN** and **ALE** signals in 8051 with reference to external memory access.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **PSEN (Program Store ENable)** — Output signal from 8051, active LOW. It is the **read strobe for external code memory** (ROM/EPROM). When the CPU fetches an instruction from external ROM (addresses > 0FFFH in basic 8051), PSEN goes LOW to enable the external ROM's output drivers, allowing the opcode byte to be read via the P0 data bus.

- **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** — Output signal, active HIGH. It indicates that P0 is currently carrying the **low-order address byte (A0–A7)**. An external **74HC373 latch** captures this byte on the falling edge of ALE. After latching, P0 is freed to act as the data bus (D0–D7).

- **Together:** ALE demultiplexes the address/data bus; PSEN selects the external code memory device. (1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

What is the **bit-addressable area** in 8051 internal RAM? How many bits can be addressed?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The **bit-addressable area** occupies internal RAM addresses **20H to 2FH** (16 bytes).
- This region provides **16 × 8 = 128 individually addressable bits** with addresses 00H to 7FH.
- Bit-level instructions (`SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C, bit`, `MOV C, bit`) can target any of these bits without disturbing neighbouring bits.
- Examples: `SETB 20H.0` sets bit 0 of byte 20H; this is bit address 00H.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bonus mark tip:** Mention that 16 SFRs (e.g., P0, TCON, ACC, B, PSW) are also bit-addressable, adding **another 128 bits** for a total of **256 bit-addressable locations** in the 8051.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supple]**

**(a)** Draw the **internal RAM structure of 8051** and explain the function of each region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **external memory interfacing** of 8051 with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of signals PSEN, ALE, RD, WR, P0 and P2. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Internal RAM Structure — 7 marks**

```
00H ─────────── Register Bank 0 (R0–R7)
07H
08H ─────────── Register Bank 1
0FH
10H ─────────── Register Bank 2
17H
18H ─────────── Register Bank 3
1FH
20H ─────────── Bit-Addressable Area (128 bits, 00H–7FH)
2FH
30H ─────────── General Purpose RAM (Scratchpad / Stack)
7FH
80H ─────────── SFR Region (Direct addressing only)
FFH
```

**Explanation of regions:**

1. **Register Banks (00H–1FH, 32 bytes):** Four banks of 8 registers each. Active bank selected by PSW.3 (RS0) and PSW.4 (RS1). After reset, Bank 0 is active. Used to provide fast context switching in interrupts. *(2 marks)*

2. **Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH, 16 bytes):** Each of the 128 bits can be individually set, cleared, AND-ed, OR-ed, or moved. Bit address formula: $(N - 20\text{H}) \times 8 + b$. Crucial for single-pin I/O control in real-time systems. *(2 marks)*

3. **General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH, 80 bytes):** Used for variable storage, buffers, and the **hardware stack**. SP (default 07H) points into this region; PUSH increments SP, POP decrements it. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **SFR Region (80H–FFH, 128 addresses):** 21 implemented registers control CPU and peripherals (ACC, B, PSW, SP, DPTR, P0–P3, TCON, TMOD, SCON, IE, IP, etc.). Access only by direct addressing. *(1.5 marks)*

**(b) External Memory Interfacing — 7 marks**

The 8051 uses a **Harvard architecture** with **separate physical buses** for code and data memory.

**Pin Functions during external access:**

| Pin/Signal | Direction | Function |
|------------|-----------|----------|
| P0.0–P0.7 | Bidirectional | Multiplexed Address A0–A7 and Data D0–D7 |
| P2.0–P2.7 | Output | High address A8–A15 |
| ALE | Output | Address Latch Enable — latches A0–A7 into 74HC373 |
| PSEN | Output | Program Store Enable — read strobe for external code ROM |
| RD (P3.7) | Output | Read strobe for external data SRAM |
| WR (P3.6) | Output | Write strobe for external data SRAM |
| EA | Input | Access Internal/External ROM |

**Read cycle trace for `MOVX A, @DPTR`:**

1. CPU places DPTR's low byte on P0 and high byte on P2. *(1 mark)*
2. ALE pulses HIGH → external 74HC373 latch captures A0–A7. *(1 mark)*
3. P0 switches to high-impedance (input mode). *(0.5 marks)*
4. CPU drives RD LOW; external SRAM is selected and drives its data on P0. *(1 mark)*
5. CPU reads byte from P0 into ACC; RD goes HIGH; ALE goes HIGH. *(1 mark)*

**Code vs Data Memory distinction:**

- **Code memory** is read using **PSEN** (not RD).
- **Data memory** is read/written using **RD/WR** (not PSEN).
- Hence, the same address 0000H can refer to both a code byte (in ROM) and a data byte (in SRAM) without conflict. *(2 marks)*

**Maximum size:** Each external memory can be up to **64 KB**, accessed via 16-bit address on P0+P2.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different **addressing modes of 8051** with examples. Show how each is used to access internal RAM, SFR, and external memory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **operation of the MOVX instruction** for reading from external data memory. Why is MOVC used for code memory? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Addressing Modes — 7 marks**

The 8051 supports **five primary addressing modes** plus two special jump modes:

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand is a constant in the instruction.
   - `MOV A, #25H` → loads 25H into ACC.
   - Cannot access memory; constant is part of the opcode. *(1 mark)*

2. **Register Addressing** — Operand is one of R0–R7 of the active bank.
   - `MOV A, R3` → copies R3 into ACC.
   - Fastest mode; only accesses 8 working registers in 00H–1FH. *(1 mark)*

3. **Direct Addressing** — Operand is the 8-bit address of the byte.
   - `MOV A, 30H` → loads byte at internal RAM 30H into ACC.
   - **Only mode that can access SFRs** (e.g., `MOV 80H, A` writes to P0).
   - Accesses internal RAM 00H–7FH and SFR 80H–FFH. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — Address is held in R0/R1/SP/DPTR.
   - `MOV A, @R0` → loads the byte whose address is in R0.
   - `@R0`/`@R1` → accesses internal RAM (00H–FFH) or external data (with MOVX).
   - `@DPTR` → only used with MOVX for external data memory.
   - `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` → used with MOVC for code memory. *(1.5 marks)*

5. **Indexed Addressing** — Effective address = base + index.
   - `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → reads code memory at (DPTR + ACC).
   - `MOVC A, @A+PC`  → reads code memory at (PC + ACC).
   - Ideal for **lookup tables** (e.g., 7-segment decoder, ASCII conversion). *(1.5 marks)*

6. **Relative / Absolute / Long Addressing** — Used by jump instructions.
   - `SJMP rel` (relative, –128 to +127)
   - `AJMP addr11` (absolute, 2 KB page)
   - `LJMP addr16` (long, full 64 KB)
   - All target code memory. *(0.5 marks)*

**Summary table mapping modes to memory regions (analytical matrix from Section 3.4 should be reproduced here for full marks).**

**(b) MOVX and MOVC — 7 marks**

**MOVX (Move eXternal):** Used **only** for external data memory (XRAM).

- **Syntax:** `MOVX A, @DPTR` (read) or `MOVX @DPTR, A` (write).
- **Pin activity:**
  - P0 outputs low address, P2 outputs high address.
  - ALE pulse latches low address.
  - RD goes LOW (read) or WR goes LOW (write).
  - P0 acts as data bus.

**Diagram (must be drawn — refer to Section 4.2 block diagram).** *(3 marks)*

**Why MOVC for code memory?** *(4 marks)*

- `MOVC` stands for "Move from Code memory." It uses the **PSEN** strobe, not RD, because code memory is read using the PSEN signal.
- The "C" suffix also indicates the operand address is formed as `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`, which is a base+offset scheme optimized for **table lookups in ROM**.
- Code memory is read-only, so `MOVC` is read-only.
- Why two instructions (MOVC and MOVX) for what seems like two reads?
  - **PSEN vs RD** separation allows the 8051 to have **64 KB code + 64 KB data** in the same address range (0000H–FFFFH) without conflict.
  - A program can read code at 0000H (instruction fetch) and read data at 0000H (XDATA read) **at different times** without collision because the strobe signals are different.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing MOVX and MOVC:** `MOVX` accesses **external data RAM** (XRAM); `MOVC` accesses **code memory** (ROM). Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting that internal RAM above 7FH is SFR, not general RAM** in the basic 8051. Writing `MOV A, @R0` with R0=80H is **illegal** on 8051 (only 8052 has upper RAM).
> 3. **Saying "8051 has 128 KB memory":** Wrong. It has **64 KB code + 64 KB data** — two separate 64 KB spaces, total addressable = 128 KB but not one contiguous block.
> 4. **Missing the role of 74HC373/74LS373:** Examiners **expect the address-latch IC** in the external memory diagram. Skipping it = –2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing register bank addresses:** Bank 1 starts at 08H, not 00H. Bank 2 at 10H, Bank 3 at 18H.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Revision Notes — Memorize before exam:**

- 8051 uses **Harvard architecture** → separate code and data buses, **64 KB + 64 KB = 128 KB** total.
- **Internal RAM** = 256 bytes split into Lower 128 (00H–7FH) and Upper 128 SFRs (80H–FFH).
- **Internal ROM** = 4 KB (0000H–0FFFH); reset vector at **0000H**; interrupt vectors at **0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H**.
- **Register banks** at 00H–1FH, **4 banks × 8 registers**, selected by **PSW bits RS0, RS1**.
- **Bit-addressable area** = 20H–2FH (16 bytes = **128 bits**, addresses 00H–7FH). Bit formula: $(N-20\text{H})\times 8 + b$.
- **General-purpose RAM** = 30H–7FH (80 bytes) used for stack and variables; SP grows **upward** from 07H on reset.
- **SFRs** are accessed **only by direct addressing**; `@R0` cannot reach them.
- **P0** = multiplexed Address (low)/Data bus; **P2** = Address (high).
- **ALE** latches low-byte address into external **74HC373**; **PSEN** strobes external code ROM; **RD/WR** strobe external data SRAM.
- **`MOV`** = internal RAM/SFR; **`MOVC`** = code memory (uses PSEN, read-only, for `@A+DPTR`/`@A+PC`); **`MOVX`** = external data memory (uses RD/WR, via `@R0`/`@R1`/`@DPTR`).
- **Maximum external code memory = 64 KB**, **maximum external data memory = 64 KB**.
- **Same numeric address** in code space and data space refers to **two different physical bytes** — Harvard isolation.
- **Stack Pointer (SP)** defaults to **07H**; first PUSH writes to 08H. Reset sets SP = 07H, P0–P3 = FFH, ACC = 00H, PC = 0000H.
- **EA pin** = 0 → all code from external ROM; EA = 1 → internal ROM up to 0FFFH, external above.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to 8051 Assembly Language Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to 8051 Assembly Language Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Assembly Language** is a low-level symbolic programming language that represents the machine code instructions of a specific microprocessor (in this case, the **Intel 8051** family of microcontrollers) in a human-readable mnemonic form. Each assembly instruction corresponds **one-to-one** with a machine-level opcode executed by the CPU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *"8051 Assembly Language is a symbolic representation of 8051 machine instructions, using mnemonics (like `MOV`, `ADD`, `SJMP`) and assembler directives (like `ORG`, `END`, `EQU`) that are translated into executable machine code by an assembler (e.g., ASM51)."*

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary)

| Term | Formal Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Mnemonic** | Short, English-like code representing a machine instruction (e.g., `MOV A, #25H`) |
| **Opcode** | The numeric binary code stored in ROM that the CPU decodes |
| **Operand** | The data or address on which the instruction operates |
| **Directive** | A pseudo-instruction to the **assembler**, not the CPU (e.g., `ORG 0000H`) |
| **Label** | A symbolic name for a memory address or program line |
| **Comment** | Text after a `;` ignored by the assembler, used for documentation |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"

Think of the **8051 CPU** as a chef in a tiny, strict restaurant kitchen:

- The chef **only understands numbers** (machine code: `01110100`).
- **Assembly language** is the *recipe card* written in short codes the chef recognizes (`CHOP`, `BOIL`, `MOVE`).
- The **assembler** is the *translator* who converts the recipe card (`.asm` file) into the numbers the chef actually reads (`.hex` file).
- **Registers** (A, B, R0–R7) are the chef's **hands** — fast, limited work surfaces right next to the brain.
- **Memory (RAM/ROM)** is the **pantry shelves** — slower but larger storage.
- **Mnemonics** like `MOV` = "pick up ingredient"; `ADD` = "mix"; `SJMP` = "jump to step 7".

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 has **255 opcodes** (actual machine instructions) and supports **111 distinct mnemonics** — a relatively small but complete instruction set that makes it ideal for teaching embedded systems fundamentals.

## 1.4 Program Development Flow (KTU High-Yield Diagram Concept)

The path from human thought to silicon execution has **5 stages**:

1. **Source Code** → Write `.asm` file using editor.
2. **Assembler** → ASM51 translates mnemonics + directives into `.obj` (object) and `.lst` (listing) files.
3. **Linker** → Combines multiple object files into a single executable.
4. **Locator/Loader** → Assigns physical addresses in 8051 ROM.
5. **Burner/Programmer** → Writes the final `.hex` file into the 8051's flash/ROM.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an 8051 Assembly Instruction

Every line of valid 8051 assembly has **four fields**, separated by spaces/tabs:

```
[Label:]   [Mnemonic]   [Operand(s)]   [; Comment]
```

**Example (Fully Annotated):**

```
START:    MOV   A, #0FH      ; Load A with 15 decimal, A <- 0x0F
```

| Field | Purpose | Optional? |
|---|---|---|
| **Label** | Symbolic name for this line's ROM address | Yes |
| **Mnemonic** | What the CPU must do (e.g., `MOV`, `ADD`) | **Required** |
| **Operand** | The data/address(es) involved | Depends on instruction |
| **Comment** | Human-readable explanation (after `;`) | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Rule:** Labels must end with a **colon (`:`)** when on the same line as the instruction. The colon is **NOT** part of the label name itself.

## 2.2 Classification of 8051 Instructions (Board-Favorite)

The 8051 instruction set is grouped into **5 functional categories** — a frequent 3-mark and 7-mark question:

| # | Group | Examples | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Data Transfer** | `MOV`, `MOVC`, `MOVX`, `PUSH`, `POP`, `XCH` | Move data between registers, RAM, code & external memory |
| 2 | **Arithmetic** | `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL`, `DIV` | Mathematical operations on the accumulator |
| 3 | **Logical** | `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `CLR`, `RL`, `RR` | Bitwise AND/OR/XOR, rotate, complement |
| 4 | **Boolean (Bit)** | `SETB`, `CLR`, `ANL C`, bit-move | Operate on individual bits |
| 5 | **Program Branching** | `SJMP`, `LJMP`, `AJMP`, `JC`, `JNC`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `DJNZ`, `CJNE`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI` | Alter program flow (jumps, calls, returns) |

## 2.3 Assembler Directives (Pseudo-Instructions)

Directives instruct the **assembler**, not the 8051 CPU. They consume no ROM and produce no opcode.

| Directive | Full Form | Function | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ORG` | Origin | Sets the starting address of code/data | `ORG 0000H` |
| `END` | End | Marks the last line of source code | `END` |
| `EQU` | Equate | Assigns a constant value to a label | `PORT1 EQU 90H` |
| `DB` | Define Byte | Stores byte(s) in program (code) memory | `DB 0AH, 0BH` |
| `DATA` | Data Address | Defines symbol in internal RAM (0–127) | `TEMP DATA 30H` |
| `BIT` | Bit Address | Defines a bit-addressable symbol | `FLAG BIT 20H.0` |
| `DS` | Define Storage | Reserves N bytes of RAM (uninitialized) | `DS 10` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Common Board Mistake:** Writing `EQU` for a RAM variable — this is wrong. `EQU` is for **constants only**. For RAM variables, use `DATA` or `BIT`.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Memory & Instruction Sizing

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Program Counter (PC) size | $N_{PC} = 16$ | bits | Allows $2^{16} = 65536$ addresses |
| Max ROM addressable | $2^{16} = 65536$ | bytes (64 KB) | 8051 standard address space |
| Internal RAM | $128$ (8051) / $256$ (8052) | bytes | Directly addressable 00H–7FH |
| SFR space | $128$ | bytes | 80H–FFH, special function registers |
| Bits per byte | $8$ | bits | 8051 is an 8-bit controller |
| Registers R0–R7 banks | $4$ | banks | Selected via PSW bits RS1, RS0 |
| Register banks start | $00H, 08H, 10H, 18H$ | addresses | Bank 0, 1, 2, 3 |

## 2.5 Why Assembly for 8051? (Real-World Engineering Utility)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Speed & Size:** Assembly produces the smallest, fastest code — critical for 8-bit resource-constrained 8051 systems.
> - **Hardware Control:** Direct bit manipulation (e.g., `SETB P1.0`) is impossible in higher-level languages.
> - **Determinism:** Exact cycle-count knowledge enables **real-time** systems (washing machines, automotive ECUs, industrial PLCs).
> - **Education:** Teaches CPU architecture, registers, and memory model — a foundation for ARM/RISC-V learning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Instruction Format: How Mnemonics Become Bytes

The 8051 instruction is **1, 2, or 3 bytes** long. The first byte is always the **opcode**; subsequent bytes hold operands or addresses.

### 3.1.1 Derivation — Machine Code for `MOV A, #0FH`

**Step 1: Identify the opcode from the instruction table.**

The instruction `MOV A, #immediate` has the opcode byte `0111 0100` (binary) = `74H` (hex).

**Step 2: Append the immediate data byte.**

The immediate value `0FH` is appended directly after the opcode.

**Step 3: Construct the final machine code.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Opcode byte} &= \texttt{74H} \\
\text{Data byte} &= \texttt{0FH} \\
\hline
\text{Final machine code in ROM} &= \texttt{74H, 0FH}
\end{aligned}
$$

> So the assembler will store **two bytes** at consecutive ROM locations for this single line of assembly.

### 3.1.2 Derivation — Total ROM for a Sample Program

Consider this simple program:

```
        ORG    0000H
START:  MOV    A, #25H       ; 2 bytes
        MOV    R0, A         ; 1 byte
        ADD    A, R0         ; 1 byte
        END
```

**Total ROM consumed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{ROM} &= 2 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 4 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Instruction Size & Cycle Count (Top 20 KTU-Frequent Mnemonics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **must-memorize** table. The 8051 uses a **12-clock oscillator period** as one **machine cycle**.

| Mnemonic | Bytes | Machine Cycles | Time @ 11.0592 MHz (µs) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| `MOV A, #data` | 2 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `MOV A, Rn` | 1 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `MOV Rn, #data` | 2 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `MOV direct, A` | 2 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `MOV direct, #data` | 3 | 2 | 2.170 |
| `ADD A, Rn` | 1 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `ADD A, #data` | 2 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `SUBB A, Rn` | 1 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `INC A` | 1 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `INC direct` | 2 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `MUL AB` | 1 | 4 | 4.340 |
| `DIV AB` | 1 | 4 | 4.340 |
| `ANL A, #data` | 2 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `ORL direct, A` | 2 | 1 | 1.085 |
| `SJMP rel` | 2 | 2 | 2.170 |
| `LJMP addr16` | 3 | 2 | 2.170 |
| `AJMP addr11` | 2 | 2 | 2.170 |
| `JZ rel` | 2 | 2 | 2.170 |
| `DJNZ Rn, rel` | 2 | 2 | 2.170 |
| `LCALL addr16` | 3 | 2 | 2.170 |
| `ACALL addr11` | 2 | 2 | 2.170 |
| `RET` | 1 | 2 | 2.170 |

### 3.2.1 Derivation — Time Delay Calculation

For a 11.0592 MHz crystal, the **oscillator period** is:

$$
T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 0.0904 \; \mu s
$$

One **machine cycle** = **12 oscillator periods**:

$$
T_{mc} = 12 \times T_{osc} = 12 \times 0.0904 \approx 1.085 \; \mu s
$$

This matches the table above for 1-cycle instructions.

**Example — Total delay for a 2-cycle `SJMP` loop:**

$$
T_{loop} = 2 \times T_{mc} = 2 \times 1.085 = 2.170 \; \mu s
$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Sample Program Analysis

**Program:** Add two numbers stored in internal RAM and store the result.

```
        ORG    0000H        ; Program starts at ROM address 0000
        MOV    A, 30H       ; A <- contents of RAM[30H]
        ADD    A, 31H       ; A <- A + RAM[31H]
        MOV    32H, A       ; RAM[32H] <- A
        END
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

| Step | Address | Instruction | Action | A Register | Flag Updates |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0000H | `MOV A, 30H` | Load A from RAM 30H | `A = RAM[30H]` | None |
| 2 | 0002H | `ADD A, 31H` | Add RAM 31H to A | `A = RAM[30H] + RAM[31H]` | CY, AC, OV updated |
| 3 | 0004H | `MOV 32H, A` | Store A to RAM 32H | `A` unchanged | None |
| 4 | — | `END` | Assembler pseudo-op | — | — |

### Flag Bit Semantics for `ADD`

$$
\begin{aligned}
CY &= 1 \text{ if bit 7 carry-out occurred} \\
AC &= 1 \text{ if bit 3 carry-out occurred (BCD use)} \\
OV &= 1 \text{ if signed overflow} \\
P &= 1 \text{ if A has odd number of 1s (parity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Only the `ADD` and `ADDC` instructions explicitly update **all four** of CY, AC, OV, P. `INC` does **not** affect any flag.

## 3.4 Addressing Modes (7 Modes — KTU 7-Mark Favorite)

| # | Mode | Syntax Example | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | `MOV A, #25H` | Constant is the operand |
| 2 | **Register** | `MOV A, R0` | Operand is a register (R0–R7) |
| 3 | **Direct** | `MOV A, 30H` | Operand is the RAM/SFR address |
| 4 | **Register Indirect** | `MOV A, @R0` | R0 holds the address of the operand |
| 5 | **Indexed (Code)** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | A + DPTR forms code memory address |
| 6 | **Relative** | `SJMP LABEL` | 8-bit signed offset from PC+2 |
| 7 | **Absolute / Long** | `AJMP` / `LJMP` | 11-bit or 16-bit absolute address |

### Worked Example — Identifying Addressing Modes

```
MOV    A, #0AH      ;  Immediate addressing
MOV    A, R5        ;  Register addressing
MOV    A, 30H       ;  Direct addressing
MOV    A, @R1       ;  Register indirect addressing
MOVC   A, @A+DPTR   ;  Indexed addressing
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Assembly Language Development Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Developer writes .asm source file] --> B[Editor: saves human-readable code]
    B --> C[Assembler: ASM51 translates mnemonics]
    C --> D[Generates .obj object file]
    C --> E[Generates .lst listing file with addresses]
    D --> F[Linker: combines modules if any]
    F --> G[Locator: assigns physical ROM/RAM addresses]
    G --> H[Generates .hex Intel HEX file]
    H --> I[Burner: writes .hex into 8051 ROM]
    I --> J[8051 Microcontroller executes machine code]
    J --> K{Output correct?}
    K -- No --> L[Debug using simulator or emulator]
    L --> A
    K -- Yes --> M[Embedded System deployed in field]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — 8051 Internal Architecture (Assembly-Programming View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["CPU Core"]
        PC["Program Counter\n16-bit PC"]
        ACC["Accumulator A\n8-bit"]
        B_REG["B Register\n8-bit"]
        SP["Stack Pointer\n8-bit SP"]
        PSW["Program Status Word\nPSW: CY AC F0 RS1 RS0 OV - P"]
    end

    subgraph REGS["Register Banks"]
        B0["Bank 0: R0-R7\n00H-07H"]
        B1["Bank 1: R0-R7\n08H-0FH"]
        B2["Bank 2: R0-R7\n10H-17H"]
        B3["Bank 3: R0-R7\n18H-1FH"]
    end

    subgraph IRAM["Internal RAM 128 bytes"]
        DATA["General Purpose\n30H-7FH"]
        BITAREA["Bit Addressable\n20H-2FH: 128 bits"]
    end

    subgraph SFR["Special Function Registers"]
        P0["P0: 80H"]
        P1["P1: 90H"]
        P2["P2: A0H"]
        P3["P3: B0H"]
        DPTR["DPTR: 82H-83H"]
    end

    PC --> ROM["ROM\nCode Memory\n64 KB max"]
    ROM --> CPU
    CPU <--> REGS
    CPU <--> IRAM
    CPU <--> SFR
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Tree — Instruction Set Classification

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((8051 Instruction Set))
    Data Transfer
      MOV
      MOVC
      MOVX
      PUSH
      POP
      XCH
      XCHD
    Arithmetic
      ADD
      ADDC
      SUBB
      INC
      DEC
      MUL AB
      DIV AB
      DA A
    Logical
      ANL
      ORL
      XRL
      CLR
      CPL
      RL
      RLC
      RR
      RRC
      SWAP
    Boolean
      SETB bit
      CLR bit
      CPL bit
      ANL C
      ORL C
      MOV C
    Branch
      SJMP
      LJMP
      AJMP
      JZ JNZ
      JC JNC
      JB JNB JBC
      CJNE
      DJNZ
      ACALL
      LCALL
      RET
      RETI
      NOP
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence — How `ADD A, R0` Executes

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as Program Counter
    participant ROM as Code ROM
    participant DEC as Instruction Decoder
    participant ALU as Arithmetic Logic Unit
    participant ACC as Accumulator A
    participant R0 as Register R0
    participant PSW as PSW Flags

    PC->>ROM: Fetch opcode at PC address
    ROM-->>DEC: Opcode byte (e.g., 28H for ADD A,R0)
    DEC->>ALU: Decode: ADD operation
    DEC->>R0: Read operand
    R0-->>ALU: Operand value
    ALU->>ACC: Read current A value
    ALU->>ALU: A + R0
    ALU->>ACC: Write result back to A
    ALU->>PSW: Update CY, AC, OV, P flags
    PC->>PC: PC <- PC + 1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three assembler directives used in 8051 assembly language and state their functions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **`ORG`** — *Origin.* Specifies the starting memory address where the following code or data will be assembled. Example: `ORG 0000H`.
2. **`END`** — Marks the termination of the assembly source program. Anything after `END` is ignored by the assembler.
3. **`EQU`** — *Equate.* Assigns a constant numeric value to a symbolic name, used for clarity. Example: `PORT1 EQU 90H`.
4. *(Optional mention)* `DB` — *Define Byte* stores byte constants in program memory.

*(Each directive with correct function: 1 mark × 3 = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a mnemonic and a directive in 8051 assembly language.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Mnemonic | Directive |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Symbolic code for a CPU instruction | Pseudo-instruction to the assembler |
| **Executed by** | The 8051 CPU | The assembler (translation phase) |
| **Produces** | Machine opcode in ROM | No opcode; controls assembly |
| **Example** | `MOV A, #25H` | `ORG 0000H`, `END` |

*(Comparison 1.5 marks + Examples 1.5 marks = 3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from the choice provided. Each full question = 14 Marks, split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the structure of an 8051 assembly language program. List the **five functional groups** of 8051 instructions with two example mnemonics for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Program Structure** (3 marks):
A typical 8051 `.asm` program has:
- A **header/title** (commented).
- **`ORG 0000H`** to set starting address.
- **Main code section** with instructions, labels, comments.
- Optional **subroutines**.
- **`END`** directive to terminate.

**Five Instruction Groups** (4 marks — 0.8 mark per group, with 2 examples each):

| # | Group | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Data Transfer | `MOV A, #25H` | `PUSH 30H` |
| 2 | Arithmetic | `ADD A, R0` | `MUL AB` |
| 3 | Logical | `ANL A, #0FH` | `CPL A` |
| 4 | Boolean (Bit) | `SETB P1.0` | `CLR C` |
| 5 | Program Branching | `SJMP START` | `DJNZ R0, LOOP` |

---

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to add the values stored in internal RAM locations **20H** and **21H**, and store the result in **30H** (low byte) and **31H** (high byte — zero, since max sum of two bytes fits in 8 bits + carry). Comment each line. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Program:**

```
        ORG    0000H        ; Start program at ROM 0000H
        MOV    A, 20H       ; A <- value at RAM 20H
        ADD    A, 21H       ; A <- A + value at RAM 21H
        MOV    30H, A       ; Store 8-bit sum in RAM 30H
        MOV    A, #00H      ; Clear A
        MOV    31H, A       ; Store 0 (high byte) in RAM 31H
HERE:   SJMP   HERE         ; Infinite loop to halt
        END                 ; End of source
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- `MOV A, 20H` uses **direct addressing** to load the first operand.
- `ADD A, 21H` performs 8-bit addition; the **carry flag (CY)** indicates any overflow.
- Result stored; `SJMP HERE` is the standard "do-nothing" halt pattern because the 8051 has no `HLT` instruction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Lost Marks Common Zone):**
> - Forgetting the `END` directive → **−1 mark**.
> - Confusing `MOV A, 20H` (direct, fetches contents) with `MOV A, #20H` (immediate, loads literal 0x20) → **−2 marks**.
> - Not including `SJMP HERE` for an infinite loop — examiners deduct **1 mark** because the program must not "fall off" into invalid memory.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With neat examples, explain **any five addressing modes** of the 8051 microcontroller. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution** (1.4 marks per mode):

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand is a constant prefixed with `#`.
   `MOV A, #0FH` → Loads A with the literal value 15.

2. **Register Addressing** — Operand is one of R0–R7.
   `MOV A, R3` → Copies R3's contents into A.

3. **Direct Addressing** — Operand is the 8-bit RAM or SFR address.
   `MOV A, 30H` → Loads A from internal RAM address 30H.

4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — A register holds the address of the operand.
   `MOV A, @R0` → If R0 = 40H, A is loaded from RAM[40H].

5. **Indexed Addressing** — Used with `MOVC` and DPTR/PC.
   `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → A ← Code[DPTR + A]. Used for lookup tables.

*(Bonus: Mention Relative and Absolute addressing to score full 7 marks.)*

---

**(b)** Write an 8051 program to **clear the accumulator**, then **load it with 55H**, and **swap the upper and lower nibbles** using `SWAP A`. Show the final value of A. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Program:**

```
        ORG    0000H        ; Start at 0000H
        CLR    A            ; A <- 00H
        MOV    A, #55H      ; A <- 0101 0101
        SWAP   A            ; A <- 0101 0101  (swap nibbles)
        END
```

**Trace (2 marks):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{After `CLR A`}    &: A = 00H = 0000\;0000 \\
\text{After `MOV A, #55H`} &: A = 55H = 0101\;0101 \\
\text{After `SWAP A`}   &: A = 5AH = 0101\;1010 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final value: A = 5AH**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> - Using `MOV A, 55H` (which is **direct addressing** — loads from RAM 55H) instead of `MOV A, #55H` (immediate) → **−2 marks**.
> - Incorrectly stating that `SWAP A` rotates or shifts — it ONLY exchanges the upper and lower 4-bit nibbles → **−1 mark**.
> - Forgetting the result in HEX (`5AH`) and writing only the binary → may lose **0.5 mark**; always provide both.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize):**
> - Every 8051 assembly line = `[Label:] Mnemonic [Operands] [;Comment]`.
> - **Mnemonics** = executed by CPU; **Directives** = executed by assembler.
> - **5 instruction groups** = Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean, Branch.
> - **7 addressing modes** = Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed, Relative, Absolute/Long.
> - **Key directives** = `ORG`, `END`, `EQU`, `DB`, `DATA`, `BIT`, `DS`.
> - 8051 has **4 register banks** (R0–R7 each) selected by **PSW bits RS1, RS0** at addresses **00H, 08H, 10H, 18H**.
> - **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**; with 11.0592 MHz crystal, **1 machine cycle ≈ 1.085 µs**.
> - Instruction size: 1, 2, or **3 bytes** (e.g., `MOV direct, #data` is 3 bytes, 2 cycles).
> - Always end programs with `SJMP HERE` (infinite loop) — no `HLT` instruction exists.
> - **Flags affected by ADD/ADDC**: CY, AC, OV, P. **INC** does not affect flags.
> - `SWAP A` exchanges upper and lower nibbles — it is **not** a rotate.
> - Comments begin with `;` and are ignored by the assembler.
> - Labels end with `:` (colon) on definition lines.
> - The Stack Pointer (`SP`) initializes to **07H** on reset → stack grows upward from 08H.
> - `MUL AB` puts 16-bit product in **B:A**; `DIV AB` puts quotient in **A**, remainder in **B**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Types and Directives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Types and Directives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Data Types in 8051 Assembly

In the context of the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** and its assembly language programming, a **data type** is a classification that specifies the **type of data** a variable or memory location can hold, the **amount of memory** it occupies, and how the CPU interprets the bit pattern stored in it. Unlike high-level languages (C, Java) which have rich, abstract data types (int, float, struct, class), the 8051 assembly language is hardware-oriented and supports a small, well-defined set of elementary data types directly mapped onto the physical memory architecture of the device.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The 8051 assembly programmer works primarily with **bit-addressable**, **byte-addressable**, and **word-sized** data. Understanding the data type is essential because the 8051 has specialized memory spaces (Internal RAM, SFRs, CODE, XDATA) that accept only specific data widths.

The fundamental 8051 data types recognised by the KTU 2024 scheme are:

| S.No | Data Type | Width | Memory Footprint |
|------|-----------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | **Bit** | 1 bit | 1/8 byte |
| 2 | **Nibble** | 4 bits | 1/2 byte |
| 3 | **Byte** | 8 bits | 1 byte |
| 4 | **Word** | 16 bits | 2 bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only these four?** Because the 8051 is an **8-bit microcontroller** — its Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU), accumulator, and internal data bus are all 8 bits wide. Every data manipulation in the CPU eventually degrades to operations on 8-bit bytes (and 1-bit flags), which is why the data type system is so minimalistic.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Toolbox and Container" Intuition

Imagine you are packing for a journey (a program). You have:

- A **matchbox** (a **Bit**): holds 1 piece of information — `ON` or `OFF`, `1` or `0`. Used for tiny yes/no flags like "door open?", "motor running?".
- A **small pouch** (a **Nibble**): holds 4 such pieces. A single hexadecimal digit `0–F` fits here. Useful for BCD digits when driving a 7-segment display.
- A **standard shoebox** (a **Byte**): holds 8 pieces. The native "word" of the 8051. Every register, every SFR, every Internal RAM location is exactly this size.
- A **carton of two shoeboxes** (a **Word**, 16 bits): used when you need a counter above 255, a 16-bit address pointer (like `DPTR`), or a 2-byte arithmetic result.

This **"container-size" mental model** is precisely how the 8051 hardware engineer thinks — the silicon is physically built around the byte, with the bit and nibble being natural subdivisions of it.

## 1.3 Formal KTU-Standard Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definitions Box (High-Yield for 2-Mark Questions):**
> - **Bit:** The smallest unit of data, equal to a single binary digit (`0` or `1`). In the 8051, 128 bits in Internal RAM (`20H–2FH`) and 16 bits in the bit-addressable SFR region are individually addressable.
> - **Nibble:** A group of **4 bits**. Two nibbles together form one byte. The upper nibble (D7–D4) and lower nibble (D3–D0) are often manipulated separately (e.g., for ASCII conversion, BCD packing).
> - **Byte:** The **fundamental addressable unit** of the 8051. Equals **8 bits**. The range is `00H–FFH` (0 to 255 decimal). All Internal RAM (128 bytes), SFRs (128 bytes), and Program Memory bytes are 8-bit wide.
> - **Word:** A **16-bit** (2-byte) quantity used by the 8051 for the **Data Pointer (DPTR)**, **Program Counter (PC)**, and 16-bit arithmetic results. Range: `0000H–FFFFH` (0 to 65,535 decimal).

## 1.4 Introduction to Assembler Directives

A **directive** (also called a **pseudo-instruction** or **pseudo-op**) is a statement that gives instructions to the **assembler software** (e.g., ASM51), *not* to the 8051 CPU. Directives do not generate any machine code (mnemonic-to-opcode translation); instead, they control the **assembly process** itself — how the program is laid out in memory, where symbols are defined, what data is pre-loaded, and which memory segment is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **Instruction** → translated into **machine code (opcode)** → executed by the **CPU**.
> - **Directive** → consumed by the **assembler** → produces **no opcode** → affects the **listing file / hex file**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Counting the number of bytes used by a program by simply counting the lines of code. Each directive must be analysed separately for the bytes it consumes (some consume 0, 1, 2, or 3 bytes in the hex file).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four 8051 Data Types — Detailed Engineering View

### 2.1.1 Bit (1-Bit Data)

- The 8051 contains a **bit-addressable region** in Internal RAM from addresses `20H` to `2FH` (16 bytes × 8 bits = **128 bits**, addressable individually as bit addresses `00H` to `7FH`).
- Additionally, **16 special-function registers** in the SFR space (`80H`, `88H`, `90H`, `98H`, `A0H`, `A8H`, `B0H`, `B8H`, `C0H`, `C8H`, `D0H`, `D8H`, `E0H`, `E8H`, `F0H`, `F8H`) are bit-addressable, contributing another **128 bits** (`80H`–`FFH`).
- Instructions used: `SETB bit`, `CLR bit`, `CPL bit`, `JB bit,rel`, `JNB bit,rel`, `MOV C,bit`, `MOV bit,C`, `ANL C,bit`, `ORL C,bit`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** Bit variables are heavily used for **flag storage** (e.g., a custom "data_ready" flag), **port pin control**, and **boolean I/O** where every bit of RAM is precious.

### 2.1.2 Nibble (4-Bit Data)

- Formed by **4 bits**. Represents a single **hexadecimal digit** (`0–F`) or a **BCD digit** (`0–9`).
- Two nibbles constitute one byte. The 8051 has instructions that operate on nibbles implicitly:
  - `SWAP A` — exchanges the upper and lower nibbles of the Accumulator.
  - `XCHD A,@R0` — exchanges the **lower nibble** of A with the lower nibble of the RAM pointed to by R0.
- `DA A` (Decimal Adjust) operates implicitly on the lower nibble for BCD arithmetic.

### 2.1.3 Byte (8-Bit Data) — The Native Data Type

- Range: `00H` to `FFH` (0 to 255 unsigned; −128 to +127 signed in 2's complement).
- **All** general-purpose registers (R0–R7), the Accumulator, B register, PSW, and SFRs (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, SCON, etc.) are 8-bit wide.
- Most 8051 instructions operate on bytes (`MOV A,#55H`, `ADD A,R0`, `INC A`, etc.).

### 2.1.4 Word (16-Bit Data)

- The 8051 is fundamentally an **8-bit** machine, but it has dedicated 16-bit hardware:
  - **DPTR (Data Pointer):** 2 bytes (DPH + DPL), used for accessing external/XDATA and code-table lookups.
  - **Program Counter (PC):** 16-bit, allowing addressing of 64 KB of code memory (`0000H–FFFFH`).
  - **Stack Pointer (SP):** 8-bit on original 8051 (but the stack itself is a 16-bit address space since it lives in 16-bit addressable Internal + external RAM).
  - **Timer Counters (TH0/TL0, TH1/TL1):** can be read as 16-bit counters.
- 16-bit arithmetic is performed by combining two 8-bit operations (e.g., `ADD` then `ADDC`).

## 2.2 The Major 8051 Assembler Directives — Engineering View

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasises the following directives. Each is examined below with its syntax, purpose, and effect on the hex file.

### 2.2.1 `ORG` (Origin) Directive

```
ORG <address>
```

- Sets the **location counter** of the assembler to the specified 16-bit address.
- The next instruction or data statement will be placed at this address in the hex file.
- Multiple `ORG` statements are allowed to create **non-contiguous code sections** (e.g., reset vector at `0000H`, timer ISR at `000BH`).

### 2.2.2 `END` Directive

```
END
```

- Marks the **end of the assembly source file**.
- Anything written after `END` is **ignored** by the assembler.
- Must be the **last line** of every 8051 assembly program.

### 2.2.3 `DB` (Define Byte) Directive

```
LABEL: DB <byte1>, <byte2>, ...
```

- Reserves one or more bytes of memory in the **Program Memory (CODE segment)** and initialises them with the supplied values.
- Values can be numeric, ASCII strings (`'A'`), or expressions.
- Used to store **lookup tables** (e.g., 7-segment codes, sine wave samples).

### 2.2.4 `DW` (Define Word) Directive

```
LABEL: DW <word1>, <word2>, ...
```

- Similar to `DB` but reserves 16-bit (2-byte) words.
- Stored in **little-endian** format (low byte first, high byte second) on the 8051.

### 2.2.5 `EQU` (Equate) Directive

```
SYMBOL  EQU  <expression>
```

- Assigns a **constant numeric value** to a symbol. The symbol can then be used throughout the program.
- **No memory is allocated** — it is a pure textual substitution.
- The expression is evaluated at assembly time.

### 2.2.6 `DATA` Directive

```
SYMBOL  DATA  <expression>
```

- Defines a symbol that refers to an **8-bit Internal RAM address** (range `00H–7FH`, i.e., the lower 128 bytes).
- Allows symbolic access to a specific RAM byte (e.g., `MY_VAR DATA 30H`).

### 2.2.7 `IDATA` Directive

```
SYMBOL  IDATA  <expression>
```

- Defines a symbol for a location in the **Indirectly addressable Internal RAM** (range `00H–FFH`, i.e., the full 256 bytes of the 8052's extended internal RAM).
- Used with `@R0` / `@R1` indirect addressing.

### 2.2.8 `XDATA` Directive

```
SYMBOL  XDATA  <expression>
```

- Defines a symbol for a location in the **External Data Memory** (range `0000H–FFFFH`, 64 KB).
- Accessed via `MOVX` instructions.

### 2.2.9 `CODE` Directive

```
SYMBOL  CODE  <expression>
```

- Defines a symbol for a location in **Program Memory (ROM)**.
- Used to declare addresses in the code segment (e.g., lookup tables).

### 2.2.10 `BIT` Directive

```
SYMBOL  BIT  <bit-address>
```

- Assigns a **bit-addressable** location (in the range `00H–7FH` for Internal RAM bits, or `80H–FFH` for SFR bits) to a symbol.
- Example: `READY_FLAG BIT 00H` makes the symbol `READY_FLAG` refer to bit `00H` of Internal RAM (the LSB of byte `20H`).

### 2.2.11 `USING` Directive

```
USING <bank-number>
```

- Informs the assembler which **register bank** (0, 1, 2, or 3) is currently in use.
- Allows the assembler to compute correct absolute bit addresses for the `R0–R7` symbols.
- `USING 0` is the default and need not be specified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Footprint Reference:** In the standard 8051, Internal RAM is `128` bytes (`00H–7FH`), SFRs occupy `80H–FFH` (also `128` bytes), Program Memory is `64` KB (`0000H–FFFFH`), and External Data Memory is `64` KB (`0000H–FFFFH`).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it is a high-frequency revision aid for Module 3.**

| Symbol / Directive | Memory Used | Width | Segment | Addressing Range | Typical Use |
|-------------------|-------------|-------|---------|------------------|-------------|
| `BIT` | 1 bit | 1 | Internal RAM + SFR | `00H–FFH` | Boolean flags |
| Nibble | 4 bits | 0.5 | (sub-byte) | (within a byte) | BCD / hex digit |
| `DATA` (byte) | 1 byte | 8 | Internal RAM | `00H–7FH` | General variables |
| `IDATA` (byte) | 1 byte | 8 | Internal RAM (indirect) | `00H–FFH` | Stack / large buffers |
| `XDATA` (byte) | 1 byte | 8 | External RAM | `0000H–FFFFH` | Large arrays, LCD buffers |
| `CODE` (byte) | 1 byte | 8 | Program ROM | `0000H–FFFFH` | Lookup tables, strings |
| `DB` | 1 byte each | 8 per item | CODE | `00H–FFH` per byte | Constant data |
| `DW` | 2 bytes each | 16 per item | CODE | `0000H–FFFFH` per word | Address tables |
| `EQU` | 0 bytes | — | (no segment) | value `0000H–FFFFH` | Symbolic constants |
| `ORG` | 0 bytes | — | (location counter) | `0000H–FFFFH` | Set code origin |
| `END` | 0 bytes | — | (file marker) | — | End of source |
| `USING` | 0 bytes | — | (assembler hint) | `0–3` | Register bank selection |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Embedded firmware** for consumer electronics (washing machines, microwave ovens, remote controls) uses bits to track individual I/O states.
- **Lookup tables** in CODE memory using `DB` are the standard technique for fast mathematical functions (e.g., `sine(x)` approximations) on resource-constrained microcontrollers.
- **Symbolic constants via `EQU`** make firmware maintainable — changing a clock frequency in one place updates the entire program (e.g., `SYS_CLK EQU 11059200`).
- **Memory-segment directives** (`DATA`, `IDATA`, `XDATA`, `CODE`) are critical when the program is linked with a C-language project (Keil µVision / SDCC), where assembly routines are called from C.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Memory Footprint of a `DB` Block

**Problem.** Consider the following assembly snippet. Determine the total number of bytes consumed in Program Memory and list the memory addresses at which each byte is stored.

```asm
        ORG   2000H
TABLE:  DB    0A5H, 3CH, 'K', 7
```

**Solution (step by step).**

- **Step 1 — Interpret the `ORG` directive.** The assembler sets its location counter to `2000H`. The next byte emitted to the hex file will occupy address `2000H`.

$$
\text{Location Counter}_{\text{before}} = 2000\text{H}
$$

- **Step 2 — Process `DB 0A5H`.** This stores the byte `0A5H` at the current location counter, then increments the counter by 1.

$$
\text{Address}(0\text{A5H}) = 2000\text{H}, \quad \text{LC} \leftarrow 2001\text{H}
$$

- **Step 3 — Process `DB 3CH`.** Similarly:

$$
\text{Address}(3\text{CH}) = 2001\text{H}, \quad \text{LC} \leftarrow 2002\text{H}
$$

- **Step 4 — Process `DB 'K'`.** The ASCII code of `'K'` is `4BH` (since 'A' = `41H` and 'K' is the 11th letter → `41H + 0AH = 4BH`).

$$
\text{Address}('K') = 4\text{BH} \;\; \text{at}\;\; 2002\text{H}, \quad \text{LC} \leftarrow 2003\text{H}
$$

- **Step 5 — Process `DB 7`.** Plain decimal `7` is stored as `07H`.

$$
\text{Address}(07\text{H}) = 2003\text{H}, \quad \text{LC} \leftarrow 2004\text{H}
$$

- **Step 6 — Tally bytes.**

$$
\text{Total bytes used} = 4 \;\text{bytes}
$$

- **Final memory map:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2000\text{H} &: \;\; 0\text{A5H} \\
2001\text{H} &: \;\; 3\text{CH} \\
2002\text{H} &: \;\; 4\text{BH} \;\;(\text{ASCII of 'K'}) \\
2003\text{H} &: \;\; 07\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Little-Endian Storage of a `DW` Block

**Problem.** Determine the hex file contents and address layout for:

```asm
        ORG   1000H
TAB16:  DW    1234H, 00FFH
```

**Solution.**

- The 8051 stores 16-bit words in **little-endian** order: the low byte goes to the lower address, and the high byte goes to the higher address.

- For `1234H`:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low byte} &= 34\text{H} \;\longrightarrow\; 1000\text{H} \\
\text{High byte} &= 12\text{H} \;\longrightarrow\; 1001\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

- For `00FFH`:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low byte} &= \text{FFH} \;\longrightarrow\; 1002\text{H} \\
\text{High byte} &= 00\text{H} \;\longrightarrow\; 1003\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Final layout:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1000\text{H} &: \;\; 34\text{H} \\
1001\text{H} &: \;\; 12\text{H} \\
1002\text{H} &: \;\; \text{FFH} \\
1003\text{H} &: \;\; 00\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** The little-endian rule means that if you read memory sequentially as bytes (`34 12 FF 00`), the 8051's 16-bit loads (`MOV DPTR,#TAB16`) reconstruct the original words `1234H` and `00FFH` automatically.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: `EQU` Symbolic Substitution Chain

**Problem.** Evaluate the value of `TIME` and the assembly-time constants in the following snippet:

```asm
FOSC    EQU   11059200          ; Crystal frequency in Hz
DIVIDER EQU   12                ; 8051 machine cycle divisor
M_CYC   EQU   FOSC / DIVIDER    ; Machine-cycle frequency
COUNT   EQU   256 - 50          ; Reload value for 50 ms delay @ 12T mode
TIME    EQU   COUNT             ; Alias
```

**Solution.**

- **Step 1:** `FOSC` is a textual label for the integer constant `11059200` (decimal). No memory is consumed.

- **Step 2:** `DIVIDER` is a textual label for the decimal `12`. No memory is consumed.

- **Step 3:** `M_CYC` is computed at assembly time:

$$
\text{M\_CYC} = \frac{11\,059\,200}{12} = 921\,600 \;\; \text{Hz (machine-cycle frequency)}
$$

- **Step 4:** `COUNT` is evaluated:

$$
\text{COUNT} = 256 - 50 = 206_{10} = \text{CEH}
$$

- **Step 5:** `TIME` is just an alias, so:

$$
\text{TIME} = \text{COUNT} = \text{CEH} = 206_{10}
$$

- **Final summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{FOSC} &= 11\,059\,200 \;\; (\text{decimal}) \\
\text{DIVIDER} &= 12 \\
\text{M\_CYC} &= 921\,600 \;\; \text{Hz} \\
\text{COUNT} = \text{TIME} &= \text{CEH} = 206_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4: Bit-Address Mapping for `BIT` Directive

**Problem.** The internal RAM byte at address `28H` is a bit-addressable region. If the following directive is issued:

```asm
ALARM   BIT   2EH
```

Determine (a) which RAM byte, (b) which bit position within that byte, and (c) the value written to that bit by the instruction `CLR ALARM`.

**Solution.**

- **Step 1 — Decode the bit address `2EH` into byte + bit.** The 8051 bit-addressing scheme for Internal RAM bits `00H–7FH` maps as:
$$
\text{Byte address} = 20\text{H} + \lfloor \text{bit\_addr} / 8 \rfloor, \quad \text{bit position} = \text{bit\_addr} \mod 8
$$

- **Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte address} &= 20\text{H} + \lfloor 2\text{EH} / 8 \rfloor \\
&= 20\text{H} + \lfloor 46 / 8 \rfloor \\
&= 20\text{H} + 5 \\
&= 25\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit position} &= 2\text{EH} \mod 8 \\
&= 46 \mod 8 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Step 3 — Identify the bit.** Bit `6` of Internal RAM byte `25H` (i.e., D6 pin of SFR P1 if it were an SFR).

- **Step 4 — Effect of `CLR ALARM`.** The bit is forced to logic `0`.

$$
\text{ALARM} \;\leftarrow\; 0
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Directive-to-Memory Mapping

The following matrix consolidates the effect of every directive discussed in this module. Use it during the last 10 minutes of revision.

| Directive | Example | Memory Consumed (Bytes) | Hex-File Address Range | Re-evaluable at Runtime? |
|-----------|---------|-------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| `ORG` | `ORG 0000H` | 0 | Sets LC | N/A |
| `END` | `END` | 0 | — | N/A |
| `EQU` | `PORT1 EQU 90H` | 0 | — | No (constant) |
| `DB` | `MSG: DB "HI"` | 2 (one per char) | Code segment | No (ROM) |
| `DW` | `TBL: DW 1234H` | 2 per word | Code segment (little-endian) | No (ROM) |
| `DATA` | `X DATA 30H` | 0 (symbolic only) | — | N/A |
| `IDATA` | `Y IDATA 80H` | 0 (symbolic only) | — | N/A |
| `XDATA` | `Z XDATA 0100H` | 0 (symbolic only) | — | N/A |
| `CODE` | `R CODE 0200H` | 0 (symbolic only) | — | N/A |
| `BIT` | `F BIT 00H` | 0 (symbolic only) | — | Yes (bit) |
| `USING` | `USING 1` | 0 | — | N/A |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The 8051 Assembler's Two-Stage Interpretation Process

The following flow illustrates how the assembler distinguishes between **instructions** (which become opcodes) and **directives** (which control the assembly process itself).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code Line Read] --> B{First Token Check}
    B -- Mnemonic like MOV ADD --> C[Translate to Opcode]
    C --> C1[Emit 1 to 3 Bytes to Hex File]
    C1 --> C2[Advance Location Counter by Opcode Length]
    C2 --> Z[Read Next Line]
    B -- Directive like ORG DB EQU --> D[Execute Assembler Action]
    D -- ORG --> E1[Set Location Counter to Address]
    D -- DB --> E2[Emit Byte Values to Hex File at LC]
    D -- DW --> E3[Emit Word Values Little Endian]
    D -- EQU --> E4[Create Symbol in Symbol Table Only]
    D -- BIT --> E5[Map Symbol to Bit Address]
    D -- END --> E6[Stop Assembly Process]
    E1 --> Z
    E2 --> Z
    E3 --> Z
    E4 --> Z
    E5 --> Z
    E6 --> F[Generate Final Hex and Listing Files]
    Z --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — 8051 Memory Architecture and Directive Targeting

This block-level diagram maps each memory segment to the directive that targets it.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CODE_SEG[Program Memory CODE 64 KB 0000H to FFFFH]
        A1[ORG Sets LC]
        A2[DB Stores Bytes]
        A3[DW Stores Words Little Endian]
        A4[CODE Directive Targets]
    end
    subgraph IRAM[Internal RAM 128 Bytes 00H to 7FH]
        B1[DATA Directive Byte Address]
        B2[BIT Directive Bit Address 00H to 7FH]
        B3[Register Banks 00H to 1FH]
        B4[Bit Area 20H to 2FH]
        B5[General Purpose 30H to 7FH]
    end
    subgraph SFR[SFR Space 128 Bytes 80H to FFH]
        C1[Bit Addressable SFRs 80H to FFH]
    end
    subgraph IDATA_SEG[Indirect Internal RAM 00H to FFH 8052]
        D1[IDATA Directive]
    end
    subgraph XDATA_SEG[External Data Memory 64 KB 0000H to FFFFH]
        E1[XDATA Directive]
    end
    USING[USING Directive] --> B3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — The 8051 Data Type Hierarchy (Bit → Word)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[8051 Fundamental Quantum] --> P1[BIT 1 bit]
    P1 --> P2[NIBBLE 4 bits]
    P2 --> P3[BYTE 8 bits]
    P3 --> P4[WORD 16 bits two bytes little endian]
    P1 -.->|used in flags, port pins| Q1[ALU PSW Bit Operations]
    P2 -.->|used in BCD, hex digit| Q2[SWAP XCHD DA A]
    P3 -.->|native of 8051| Q3[ACC, R0 to R7, P0 to P3, SCON, TCON]
    P4 -.->|used in addressing| Q4[DPTR, PC, Timer Counts]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Bit-Address Decoding Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bit Address 00H to 7FH] --> B[Divide by 8]
    B --> C[Quotient Added to 20H]
    C --> D[Final Byte Address in 20H to 2FH]
    B --> E[Remainder 0 to 7]
    E --> F[Bit Position D0 to D7]
    D --> G[Bit Located in Internal RAM]
    F --> G
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Directive Action Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[8051 Assembler Source File] --> S1[ORG Set Origin]
    S --> S2[Define Data DB DW]
    S --> S3[Symbolic Names EQU BIT DATA XDATA]
    S --> S4[Control USING END]
    S1 --> H[HEX File 0000H to FFFFH]
    S2 --> H
    S3 --> H
    S4 --> H
    H --> T[Target 8051 Microcontroller]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Three-mark questions demand a crisp definition plus a one-line example or formula. Avoid long essays; aim for 3–5 sentences.

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**List the four fundamental data types supported by the 8051 assembly language and state the memory width (in bits) of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 assembly language supports four fundamental data types:
1. **Bit** — 1 bit (used for boolean flags, e.g., `SETB P1.0`).
2. **Nibble** — 4 bits (used for BCD or hex digits, manipulated by `SWAP A`).
3. **Byte** — 8 bits (the native 8051 width; e.g., the Accumulator, all R-registers, all SFRs).
4. **Word** — 16 bits (used by `DPTR`, `PC`, and 16-bit timer counts).

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between an 8051 *instruction* and a *directive*. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Instruction | Directive |
|--------|-------------|-----------|
| Consumed by | The 8051 CPU (at run time) | The assembler (at assembly time) |
| Generates | Machine code (opcode) | No opcode — only controls assembly |
| Example | `MOV A,#25H` (2-byte opcode `74 25`) | `ORG 0000H` (no opcode; sets LC) |
| Effect at run time | CPU executes it | No effect — already consumed |

Example directive: `COUNT EQU 50H` — this symbol substitution produces no machine code.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### Question B — Option A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A. (a)** Explain the `DB` and `DW` assembler directives of the 8051 with suitable examples. How is the byte ordering handled in `DW`? **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to store the ASCII string `"KTU2024"` in Program Memory starting at address `0400H` using the `DB` directive. Determine the total memory used and sketch the memory map. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(a)

- **`DB` (Define Byte):** Reserves one or more bytes of **Program Memory (CODE segment)** and initialises them with the supplied 8-bit values. Syntax: `LABEL: DB <val1>, <val2>, …`. Each value occupies 1 byte. Values may be numeric, ASCII strings, or expressions.

  *Example:* `SQUARES: DB 0, 1, 4, 9, 16, 25` — stores 6 bytes of square numbers.

- **`DW` (Define Word):** Reserves one or more **16-bit (2-byte) words** in Program Memory. Syntax: `LABEL: DW <word1>, <word2>, …`.

- **Byte ordering in `DW`:** The 8051 uses **little-endian** order. The low-order byte of each word is stored at the lower address, and the high-order byte is stored at the next address.

  *Example:* `TABLE: DW 1234H, 0ABCDH` stored from address `0500H` produces:
$$
\begin{aligned}
0500\text{H} &: \; 34\text{H} \\
0501\text{H} &: \; 12\text{H} \\
0502\text{H} &: \; \text{CDH} \\
0503\text{H} &: \; \text{ABH}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution for A(b)

**Code:**

```asm
        ORG   0400H
MSG:    DB    'K','T','U','2','0','2','4'
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

- ASCII values: `'K' = 4BH`, `'T' = 54H`, `'U' = 55H`, `'2' = 32H`, `'0' = 30H`, `'2' = 32H`, `'4' = 34H`.

- Total memory used = number of characters = **7 bytes**.

- **Memory map:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0400\text{H} &: \; 4\text{BH} \;\; ('\text{K}') \\
0401\text{H} &: \; 54\text{H} \;\; ('\text{T}') \\
0402\text{H} &: \; 55\text{H} \;\; ('\text{U}') \\
0403\text{H} &: \; 32\text{H} \;\; ('\text{2}') \\
0404\text{H} &: \; 30\text{H} \;\; ('\text{0}') \\
0405\text{H} &: \; 32\text{H} \;\; ('\text{2}') \\
0406\text{H} &: \; 34\text{H} \;\; ('\text{4}')
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalent compact directive: `DB "KTU2024"` is accepted by most assemblers.

---

### Question B — Option B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**B. (a)** Explain the `EQU`, `BIT`, and `USING` directives with examples. How does `USING` affect the assembler's interpretation of register names? **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** For the snippet below, determine the bit position, the parent byte address, and the value stored after execution of `CLR FLAG`. **(7 marks)**

```asm
        ORG   0000H
FLAG    BIT   3FH
        MOV   A, #0FFH
        CLR   FLAG
```

#### Model Solution for B(a)

- **`EQU` (Equate):** Assigns a constant numeric value to a symbol. The symbol becomes a textual alias. No memory is allocated.

  *Example:* `PORT1 EQU 90H` — every later occurrence of `PORT1` is replaced by `90H`.

- **`BIT` Directive:** Maps a symbol to a specific **bit address** in the bit-addressable region of Internal RAM (`20H–2FH`, bit addresses `00H–7FH`) or the bit-addressable SFRs (`80H–FFH`).

  *Example:* `OVERFLOW BIT 0D7H` — refers to bit 7 of the PSW register (the Carry flag's SFR-bit equivalent is wrong; this points to PSW.7).

- **`USING` Directive:** Informs the assembler which of the four register banks (0, 1, 2, 3) is currently active. The 8051's PSW.3 and PSW.4 (`RS0`, `RS1`) determine the active bank at run time, but the assembler does not see PSW changes; it needs `USING` to compute the correct absolute RAM addresses for `R0–R7`.

  *Example:* `USING 1` — tells the assembler that the symbol `R0` corresponds to Internal RAM byte `08H` (start of register bank 1), `R7` to `0FH`, and so on. Without `USING`, the assembler assumes bank 0 (`R0 = 00H`).

#### Model Solution for B(b)

- **Bit address given:** `3FH` (decimal `63`).
- **Apply the bit-to-byte mapping formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte address} &= 20\text{H} + \lfloor 3\text{FH} / 8 \rfloor = 20\text{H} + 7 = 27\text{H} \\
\text{Bit position} &= 3\text{FH} \mod 8 = 63 \mod 8 = 7
\end{aligned}
$$

- **Result:** `FLAG` corresponds to **bit 7 (D7)** of Internal RAM byte **`27H`**.

- **Effect of `CLR FLAG`:** The bit is reset to logic `0`.

$$
\text{Memory}[27\text{H}].7 \;\leftarrow\; 0
$$

After execution, the binary value of RAM byte `27H` will have its D7 bit cleared (e.g., if it was `FFH` before, it becomes `7FH`).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Conflating `DB` with `EQU`.** `DB` allocates memory and stores data; `EQU` does neither. Writing "DB allocates no memory" or "EQU stores values" will be marked wrong.
> 2. **Big-endian vs little-endian in `DW`.** Many students wrongly assume the 8051 stores words in big-endian order. The 8051 (being little-endian) stores the **low byte first**. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Bit-address arithmetic errors.** Computing `2EH / 8` as `5.75` instead of using integer division to get quotient `5` and remainder `6`. Always use **integer (floor) division** for the byte part and **modulo** for the bit.
> 4. **Forgetting to declare `END`.** A program without an `END` directive is considered incomplete and may lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing `DATA`, `IDATA`, `XDATA`, and `CODE`.** The byte range differs for each: `DATA` → `00H–7FH`, `IDATA` → `00H–FFH` (indirect), `XDATA` → `0000H–FFFFH` (external), `CODE` → `0000H–FFFFH` (ROM).
> 6. **Not specifying the `USING` directive when using non-zero register banks.** If you `USING 1` and then use `R0` symbolically, the assembler will compute the wrong address if you forget to update it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The 8051 is an **8-bit** machine — every native data type is a multiple or sub-multiple of 8 bits (Bit, Nibble, Byte, Word).
- ✅ A **byte is the fundamental addressable unit**. Range `00H–FFH`.
- ✅ A **word is 16 bits** — used by `DPTR`, `PC`, and 16-bit timers. Range `0000H–FFFFH`.
- ✅ The bit-addressable area of Internal RAM is `20H–2FH` (16 bytes = 128 bits, addresses `00H–7FH`).
- ✅ 16 SFRs in the range `80H–FFH` are also bit-addressable, providing an additional 128 bits.
- ✅ **Bit-address to byte+bit formula:** Byte = `20H + (bit_addr ÷ 8)`, Bit = `bit_addr mod 8`.
- ✅ **Directives are NOT instructions** — they guide the assembler, not the CPU.
- ✅ `ORG` sets the location counter; `END` terminates the source file; both consume **zero** memory.
- ✅ `DB` stores 1 byte per item in CODE memory; `DW` stores 2 bytes per word.
- ✅ `DW` is **little-endian** — low byte at the lower address, high byte at the higher address.
- ✅ `EQU` is a pure symbolic constant — no memory allocated, no run-time cost.
- ✅ `BIT` maps a symbol to a single bit; `DATA`, `IDATA`, `XDATA`, `CODE` map symbols to specific memory segments.
- ✅ `USING` informs the assembler of the active register bank (0–3); without it, the assembler defaults to bank 0.
- ✅ Always end every 8051 assembly program with the `END` directive.
- ✅ Total addressable memory: 64 KB CODE + 64 KB XDATA + 256 bytes IDATA + 128 bytes SFR.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Subroutine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Subroutine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
