# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (Each 3 Marks)

### **Q1. Define Embedded C. List any four keywords specific to Keil C51 that are not part of standard ANSI C.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Embedded C** is an extension of the standard C programming language tailored for microcontroller programming. It includes additional keywords to access hardware-specific features like Special Function Registers, bit-level I/O, and memory-mapped registers.

Four Keil C51-specific keywords:
1. **`sfr`** — Declares an 8-bit Special Function Register.
2. **`sbit`** — Declares a single bit of an SFR or bit-addressable memory.
3. **`data / idata / xdata / code`** — Specify the 8051 memory model.
4. **`bit`** — Declares a single-bit variable in bit-addressable RAM.

*(Any four valid keywords accepted. Total 3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for keywords.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between `sfr` and `sbit` keywords in Embedded C for 8051 with one example each.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | `sfr` | `sbit` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Declares an 8-bit register | Declares a single bit |
| **Size** | 8 bits (1 byte) | 1 bit |
| **Target memory** | SFR map (0x80–0xFF) | Bit-addressable SFR or 0x20–0x2F |
| **Example** | `sfr P1 = 0x90;` | `sbit LED = P1^0;` |

*(2 marks for table, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Each 14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **six memory models** supported by Keil C51 for the 8051. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write an Embedded C program to **toggle all 8 bits of Port 1** continuously with a 200 ms delay. Assume 11.0592 MHz crystal. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — Memory Models (7 Marks)

The 8051 Harvard architecture has separate Code and Data spaces. Keil C51 allows programmers to place variables in any of the **six logical memory areas**:

1. **`code`** — Program ROM (64 KB). Used for constant lookup tables and string literals. Accessed via `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`. Does not occupy RAM.

2. **`data`** — Lower 128 bytes of internal RAM (0x00–0x7F). **Fastest access** because it uses direct addressing. Recommended for time-critical variables.

3. **`idata`** — Full 256 bytes of internal RAM (0x00–0xFF). Upper 128 bytes (0x80–0xFF) overlap with SFRs and are accessed via indirect addressing. Slightly slower than `data`.

4. **`bdata`** — Bit-addressable RAM (16 bytes, 0x20–0x2F = 128 bits). Allows bit-level variables mixed with byte variables. Most efficient for bit-flag manipulation.

5. **`pdata`** — One 256-byte **paged window** of external RAM. Uses port P2 to hold the high-byte page address. Faster than `xdata` because only one MOVX cycle for the low byte.

6. **`xdata`** — Full 64 KB external data memory. Accessed via `MOVX` instruction. **Slowest** but offers the largest storage. Used for buffers and large arrays.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — Toggle Port 1 Program (7 Marks)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms)
{
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 120; j++);   // ~1 ms inner loop
}

void main(void)
{
    P1 = 0x00;          // Initialize Port 1 to all OFF
    while (1)
    {
        P1 = 0xFF;      // All bits ON
        delay_ms(200);
        P1 = 0x00;      // All bits OFF
        delay_ms(200);
    }
}
```

**Explanation (3 marks extra for narrative):**
- The infinite `while(1)` loop implements the **super-loop** pattern typical of bare-metal 8051 firmware.
- `P1 = 0xFF` writes to SFR at address `0x90`, driving all 8 pins HIGH.
- The `delay_ms` function is a pure software busy-wait calibrated for 11.0592 MHz.
- The program occupies internal ROM after Keil compilation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

> **(a)** Explain the **`interrupt` and `using` function attributes** in Keil C51 with their syntax and purpose. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write an Embedded C program using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** to generate a **1-second** event (e.g., toggle P1.0) using **interrupt-driven** approach. Assume 11.0592 MHz crystal. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — Interrupt and Using Attributes (7 Marks)

**`interrupt n` Attribute (4 marks):**
- **Syntax:** `void ISR_name(void) interrupt <vector_number>`
- **Purpose:** Marks a function as an **Interrupt Service Routine**. The Keil compiler automatically generates the `RETI` instruction at the end and saves/restores registers (ACC, B, DPH, DPL, PSW) on the stack.
- **Vector numbers** (must memorize):
  - `0` → External Interrupt 0
  - `1` → Timer 0 Overflow
  - `2` → External Interrupt 1
  - `3` → Timer 1 Overflow
  - `4` → Serial Port (TI/RI)
- The vector number **n** maps to ROM address `0x0008 + 8n`.

**`using n` Attribute (3 marks):**
- **Syntax:** `void ISR_name(void) interrupt 1 using 1`
- **Purpose:** Selects which of the **four register banks** (0–3, located at 0x00–0x1F) the ISR will use.
- Each bank has R0–R7. Using `using 1` switches to bank 1, **avoiding register-push overhead** if the main code uses bank 0.
- Bank selection is done by setting the RS0 and RS1 bits of PSW.

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — Timer 0 ISR for 1 Hz Toggle (7 Marks)

**Pre-calculation (must show in answer):**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \,\mu s
$$

For 50 ms (timer is 16-bit, max 65 536):
$$
\text{TH0:TL0} = 65{,}536 - \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}080 = 19{,}456 = 0x4C00
$$

So `TH0 = 0x4C`, `TL0 = 0x00`. Twenty such overflows = 1 second.

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;
unsigned char tick = 0;

void timer0_ISR(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0x4C;       // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x00;       // Reload low byte
    tick++;
    if (tick >= 20)   // 20 × 50 ms = 1 second
    {
        tick = 0;
        LED = ~LED;   // Toggle LED
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;      // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0x4C;      // Initial value
    TL0  = 0x00;
    ET0  = 1;         // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;         // Global interrupt enable
    TR0  = 1;         // Start Timer 0
    LED  = 0;         // LED initially OFF
    
    while (1);        // Idle super-loop
}
```

**Program Walkthrough (3 marks for explanation):**
- `TMOD = 0x01` configures Timer 0 in 16-bit Mode 1.
- `ET0` and `EA` enable the Timer 0 interrupt and the global interrupt mask.
- `TR0 = 1` starts the timer.
- The ISR is automatically called every time the 16-bit counter overflows (≈50 ms).
- The variable `tick` counts 20 overflows to mark 1 second.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in 8051 Embedded C Programs:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `reg51.h`** → Compilation will fail because `P1`, `TMOD`, `SCON` are undeclared. **(-2 marks)**
> 2. **Using `auto` storage class** → Keil C51 defaults are `data`/`xdata`; writing `auto int x;` produces warnings and is **conceptually wrong** for embedded.
> 3. **Forgetting to reload TH0/TL0 in Mode 1 ISR** → Timer only fires once correctly, then re-fires at wrong interval.
> 4. **Writing `P1 = 0xFF` in SFR declaration line** → SFRs must be declared **outside** any function (global scope).
> 5. **Using `float` in ISR** → Causes stack overflow and 30+ cycle penalty per operation.
> 6. **Wrong interrupt number** → `interrupt 5` or `interrupt 10` does not exist on standard 8051.
> 7. **Not clearing TI/RI manually** → Subsequent transmissions/receptions get stuck.
> 8. **Declaring large arrays without `xdata` keyword** → Overwrites internal RAM and crashes program silently.
> 9. **Forgetting `while(1)` at end of main** → Program returns and jumps to address 0x0000, causing a reset loop.
> 10. **Confusing `P1^0` syntax with `P1.0`** — In Keil C51, **only `P1^0` (caret symbol)** is valid for `sbit` declaration.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ⭐ **Embedded C** = ANSI C + 8051-specific extensions handled by **Keil C51 compiler**.
- ⭐ **Six memory models:** `code` (ROM), `data` (lower 128 B fastest), `idata` (256 B), `bdata` (16 B bit), `pdata` (paged xdata), `xdata` (64 KB external).
- ⭐ **`sfr <name> = <addr>`** — Declares an 8-bit SFR (addr must end in 0 or 8 for bit-addressable).
- ⭐ **`sbit <name> = SFR^bit` or `= 0x<bit_addr>`** — Declares a single hardware bit.
- ⭐ **`bit`** keyword for 1-bit variables stored in 0x20–0x2F.
- ⭐ **`interrupt n`** — vector 0→Ext0, 1→T0, 2→Ext1, 3→T1, 4→Serial; auto-saves ACC, B, DPL, DPH, PSW; auto-emits `RETI`.
- ⭐ **`using n`** — selects register bank 0–3 to avoid stack-push overhead.
- ⭐ **Default storage in Keil C51 is `data`/`xdata`**, NOT `auto`.
- ⭐ **Standard SFR addresses (must memorize):** P0=0x80, P1=0x90, P2=0xA0, P3=0xB0, PSW=0xD0, A=0xE0, B=0xF0, SP=0x81, DPL=0x82, DPH=0x83, TCON=0x88, TMOD=0x89, SCON=0x98, SBUF=0x99, IE=0xA8, IP=0xB8.
- ⭐ **Timer reload formula:** `TH0/TL0 = 65 536 − (delay_time × f_osc / 12)`.
- ⭐ **Baud rate formula:** `TH1 = 256 − f_osc / (384 × baud)`. For 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz, TH1 = `0xFD` exactly.
- ⭐ **Super-loop pattern:** `while(1) { … }` — mandatory; 8051 has **no OS** to return to.
- ⭐ **11.0592 MHz crystal** is the universal standard because it divides evenly for all standard baud rates.
- ⭐ **Avoid `float` in ISR** — use scaled integers instead.
- ⭐ **No `TRIS` register** on 8051 — ports are quasi-bidirectional by default.
- ⭐ **Toolchain:** `.c → .obj → (Linker BL51) → .hex → (Programmer) → 8051 ROM`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Embedded C - Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Embedded C - Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded C** is a standardized extension of the ISO/ANSI C programming language, specifically tailored and optimized for programming embedded systems and microcontroller architectures. It incorporates additional features such as direct hardware access through memory-mapped I/O registers, bit-manipulation operators, fixed-point arithmetic support, and interrupt service routines, while retaining the structured, procedural, and modular nature of standard C.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PBECT404 – Microcontrollers & Embedded Systems**, Embedded C serves as the dominant high-level language used to program 8-bit, 16-bit, and 32-bit microcontrollers (e.g., **8051**, **ARM Cortex-M**, **PIC**, **AVR**). It acts as a hardware-software bridge, allowing engineers to write portable, readable, and maintainable firmware that directly manipulates the underlying hardware resources of a target device.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Multilingual Translator of a Smartwatch"**
> 
> Imagine a smartwatch. The physical components — the heart-rate sensor, the OLED display, the Bluetooth radio, and the tiny battery — are like a team of specialized workers who only understand a very specific set of electrical signals (1s and 0s, i.e., **Machine Code**). 
> 
> Communicating with them directly would be exhausting and error-prone, just like trying to manage a factory by yelling in raw binary. 
> 
> **Standard C** is like English — a powerful general language.
> 
> **Embedded C** is like a *specialized dialect of English* that has been customized for the factory floor. It adds specific words (keywords like `sbit`, `sfr`, `bit`, `interrupt`) that directly address the workers (hardware registers) by name, lets you control them with surgical precision (bit-level operations), and is stripped of unnecessary features (like dynamic memory allocation in many small MCUs) so that conversations (programs) are extremely fast and use minimal energy.

### Key Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Code Size Efficiency:** Typically **5–20% smaller** binary output compared to equivalent assembly language.
- **Execution Overhead:** Compiler-optimized Embedded C adds an average of **10–30%** cycle overhead versus hand-tuned assembly.
- **Portability:** A well-written Embedded C application can achieve up to **80–90%** source-code reuse across different microcontroller families.

### Why Embedded C Instead of Pure Assembly?

> [!NOTE]
> Modern KTU 2024 outcomes (mapped to **CO3** and **CO4**) emphasize that students must appreciate *why* a high-level language is preferred in industry for embedded development. Assembly is still used in extreme optimization routines, but Embedded C dominates because of **abstraction without losing control**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary Sheet

### Operational Breakdown — How Embedded C Functions Inside a Microcontroller

Embedded C programs execute through a well-defined software stack. The following structured logic explains the *why* and *how* behind each layer:

- **Step 1 — Source Code Authoring:** The programmer writes `.c` files using standard C syntax plus architecture-specific extensions (e.g., `REGXBYTE` macros, header files like `<reg51.h>` for 8051 or `<stm32f4xx.h>` for ARM Cortex-M4).
- **Step 2 — Pre-processing:** The preprocessor resolves directives such as `#include`, `#define`, and conditional compilation (`#ifdef`). This step is critical because Embedded C relies heavily on hardware abstraction layer (HAL) macros.
- **Step 3 — Compilation to Assembly:** The cross-compiler (e.g., **Keil µVision**, **IAR Embedded Workbench**, **GCC ARM Embedded**) translates the C code into the target MCU's native assembly instructions. This is where hardware-specific optimizations occur.
- **Step 4 — Assembly to Object Code:** The assembler converts mnemonics into machine-readable **object code** (`.obj` or `.o` files).
- **Step 5 — Linking:** The linker combines object files with startup code (`.s` files), hardware abstraction libraries, and the linker script. It resolves absolute addresses for memory-mapped peripheral registers.
- **Step 6 — Locating & HEX Generation:** The locator places code in specific memory regions (Flash, RAM, EEPROM) as defined in the linker script, producing a `.hex` or `.elf` file ready for flashing onto the microcontroller.
- **Step 7 — Execution on Target:** The program is loaded into non-volatile memory and the MCU begins fetching instructions from the reset vector. Interrupt-driven routines and main-loop polling manage real-time events.

### Core Advantages of Embedded C (High-Yield Summary Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **advantages of Embedded C** as required by the KTU 2024 Module-3 syllabus. KTU examiners frequently test these points directly (commonly as 3-mark short questions or 7-mark descriptive questions).

| **S.No** | **Advantage** | **Engineering Explanation** | **Real-World Utility** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Hardware-Level Access** | Allows direct manipulation of **Special Function Registers (SFRs)**, I/O ports, and individual bits using keywords like `sbit` and `sfr`. | Driving GPIO pins, configuring ADC, toggling relays. |
| 2 | **Portability Across MCUs** | Same C source can be recompiled for different microcontrollers by changing the header file and linker settings. | Code reuse between 8051 and ARM projects. |
| 3 | **Code Efficiency & Small Footprint** | Produces compact machine code, often within **2–4 KB** for typical 8051 applications. | Suitable for resource-constrained IoT sensor nodes. |
| 4 | **Faster Development Cycle** | High-level constructs (loops, functions) reduce development time by **40–60%** versus assembly. | Accelerates time-to-market for consumer electronics. |
| 5 | **Readability & Maintainability** | Structured programming (functions, modular `.c`/`.h` files) makes firmware easier to debug and update. | Easier firmware updates over-the-air (OTA). |
| 6 | **Rich Set of Operators** | Bitwise operators (`&`, `\|`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`) enable efficient bit manipulation. | Setting/clearing interrupt flags, masking registers. |
| 7 | **Interrupt Handling** | Native support for `interrupt` keyword (in compilers like Keil) simplifies ISR writing. | Real-time response in motor control, UART reception. |
| 8 | **Modular Programming Support** | Multi-file projects allow separation of drivers (e.g., `uart.c`, `adc.c`) from application logic (`main.c`). | Clean HAL/LL architecture in STM32CubeIDE projects. |
| 9 | **Wide Industry & Toolchain Support** | Supported by virtually all major IDEs and compilers (Keil, IAR, MPLAB X, Code Composer, GCC). | Standardized skill across the global embedded industry. |
| 10 | **Inline Assembly Capability** | Allows embedding of critical assembly snippets inside C code via `asm` or `__asm` blocks. | Optimizing time-critical DSP or cryptographic routines. |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Embedded C is the **de-facto industry standard** for firmware development in automotive ECUs, medical devices (pacemakers, glucometers), consumer electronics (TV remotes, washing machines), and industrial automation (PLCs, SCADA). The advantages listed above directly translate to **reduced BOM cost** (smaller Flash/RAM needed), **lower power consumption**, and **higher reliability** — all critical KTU design outcomes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — C vs Embedded C

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the topic "Introduction to Embedded C — Advantages" is **conceptual and introductory** rather than mathematically derivational, the "derivation" here is replaced with a **rigorous analytical comparison matrix**. This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam tool that demonstrates the structural and functional evolution from Standard ANSI C to Embedded C.

### Analytical Comparison: Standard C vs Embedded C

| **Parameter** | **Standard ANSI C** | **Embedded C** | **Why the Difference Matters** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Target Platform** | Desktop / General-purpose computers (Windows, Linux) | Microcontrollers (8051, ARM, PIC, AVR) | Resources are limited; language must adapt. |
| **Memory Model** | Assumes abundant RAM and stack memory | Fixed memory map; sizes are predefined in linker script | Prevents out-of-memory crashes in small MCUs. |
| **Hardware Access** | Indirect (via OS system calls, drivers) | **Direct** via memory-mapped registers and `sfr`/`sbit` | Enables real-time hardware control. |
| **Standard Library** | Full `stdio.h`, `stdlib.h`, `malloc()` supported | Restricted use; `printf` is heavy and often avoided; no `malloc` in small MCUs | Avoids unpredictable memory fragmentation. |
| **Bit Manipulation** | Possible via bitwise operators | **Native, first-class** support with `bit` data type and `sbit` | Critical for SFR control and flag management. |
| **Execution Environment** | Hosted (OS present) | **Freestanding / Bare-metal** (no OS) | Firmware must initialize hardware itself via startup code. |
| **Optimization Priority** | Readability and developer comfort | **Speed, size, and determinism** | Real-time systems need predictable timing. |
| **Code Size** | Typically 100 KB – several MB | Often **2 KB – 64 KB** | Fits within on-chip Flash of small MCUs. |
| **Compiler Type** | Native compiler (gcc on Linux) | **Cross-compiler** (host PC compiles for target MCU) | Development environment is decoupled from execution environment. |
| **Startup Code** | OS-provided (CRT0) | **Programmer-defined** vector table and reset handler | MCU boots from address 0x0000; programmer must set this up. |
| **Floating Point** | Floating-point is default and free | Floating-point is **expensive**; fixed-point or integer math preferred | Saves CPU cycles and power. |
| **Debugging** | Host debugger (gdb, Visual Studio) | In-circuit emulator (ICE), JTAG, SWD, on-chip debug | Requires physical hardware probes. |
| **Output Mechanism** | Console, GUI, files | GPIO pins, UART, LCD, SPI/I2C peripherals | Output is inherently physical, not visual. |

### The Structural Evolution — Logical Deduction

The transition from Standard C to Embedded C can be logically deduced through a sequence of engineering constraints:

$$
\text{Standard C} \xrightarrow{\text{Constraint: Limited Resources (RAM \leq 256B, Flash \leq 8KB)}} \text{Embedded C}
$$

$$
\text{Hosted Environment} \xrightarrow{\text{Constraint: No OS, Bare-Metal Execution}} \text{Freestanding Environment}
$$

$$
\text{Indirect Hardware Access} \xrightarrow{\text{Constraint: Real-Time Control Required}} \text{Direct SFR/Memory-Mapped Access}
$$

$$
\text{Floating-Point Default} \xrightarrow{\text{Constraint: CPU has no FPU or FPU is disabled to save power}} \text{Fixed-Point / Integer Math}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The advantages of Embedded C are not accidental — they are the *direct, logical response* to the four hard physical constraints of microcontrollers: **limited memory, no operating system, strict real-time deadlines, and severe power budgets**. Understanding this *causal chain* is what distinguishes a top-scoring KTU answer from an average one.

### Key Quantitative Relationship

A useful rule-of-thumb formula used by embedded engineers to estimate whether Embedded C will fit in a target MCU is:

$$
\text{Flash Utilization (\%)} = \frac{\text{Code Size after compilation (bytes)}}{\text{Available Flash (bytes)}} \times 100
$$

**Example Calculation (8051 with 8 KB Flash):**
$$
\text{Flash Utilization} = \frac{3.2 \text{ KB}}{8 \text{ KB}} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

This means the compiled Embedded C firmware uses only **40%** of available Flash, leaving **60%** headroom for future feature additions. This compactness is a direct consequence of Embedded C's efficiency advantages.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1 — Embedded C in the Firmware Development Toolchain

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how Embedded C source code flows through the cross-compilation toolchain to produce executable firmware on a target microcontroller. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as required by the KTU PREMIER ENGINE for complex topics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer Writes .c Source File] --> B[Editor IDE e.g. Keil uVision or STM32CubeIDE]
    B --> C[Preprocessor Handles include and define directives]
    C --> D[Cross Compiler Translates C to Target Assembly]
    D --> E[Assembler Converts Assembly to Object Code]
    E --> F[Linker Combines Object Files with Startup Code and Libraries]
    F --> G[Locator Assigns Absolute Addresses per Linker Script]
    G --> H[Generates HEX or ELF Firmware Image]
    H --> I[Programmer or Debugger Loads Image to Target MCU]
    I --> J[Microcontroller Executes Firmware from Flash Memory]
    J --> K[Hardware Peripherals Respond per Firmware Instructions]
    K --> L[Real World Sensors and Actuators Interface]
```

### Diagram 2 — Memory-Mapped Register Access Concept (Embedded C Advantage)

This diagram illustrates the **direct hardware access advantage** of Embedded C, showing how a programmer manipulates a single bit of a Special Function Register (SFR) without writing assembly language.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Embedded C Statement e.g. P1 = P1 or 0x01] --> N[Compiler Resolves to SFR Address 0x90]
    N --> O[Assembly Instruction SETB 90h]
    O --> P[Port 1 Latch Bit 0 Set to Logic 1]
    P --> Q[Physical Pin P1.0 Outputs 3.3V or 5V]
    Q --> R[External Device e.g. LED Glows or Relay Activates]
```

### Diagram 3 — Comparison: Assembly vs Embedded C Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Assembly Language Path]
        S1A[Manual Register Allocation] --> S1B[Manual Stack Management]
        S1B --> S1C[Manual Banking Handling for 8051]
        S1C --> S1D[Long Debug Cycle Hours per Bug]
    end
    subgraph S2[Embedded C Path]
        S2A[Compiler Handles Registers] --> S2B[Compiler Manages Stack]
        S2B --> S2C[Linker Script Manages Memory Banks]
        S2C --> S2D[Short Debug Cycle Minutes per Bug]
    end
    S1D --> X[Slower Development More Bugs]
    S2D --> Y[Faster Development Fewer Bugs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guidance for Students:** Diagram 1 shows the *full development pipeline* (highest KTU-yield for viva questions). Diagram 2 shows *why* Embedded C is powerful at the bit-level. Diagram 3 is a *productivity argument* that supports the "Faster Development Cycle" advantage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
> **Define Embedded C. List any four advantages of Embedded C over Assembly language in microcontroller programming.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Embedded C is a set of language extensions for the C programming language designed to program embedded systems and microcontrollers. It supports direct access to memory-mapped I/O registers, bit-level data types, and interrupt service routines.
- **Any four advantages (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Portability** — Code can be recompiled for different MCUs with minimal changes.
  2. **Faster Development** — High-level constructs reduce coding time significantly.
  3. **Readability & Maintainability** — Functions and modular files make firmware easier to update.
  4. **Rich Operator Support** — Bitwise operators enable efficient register manipulation.
  5. **Wide Toolchain Support** — Compatible with Keil, IAR, GCC, MPLAB X, etc.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Sessional Model]`
> **Differentiate between Standard ANSI C and Embedded C in terms of execution environment and memory model.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Execution Environment (1.5 Marks):** Standard C runs in a *hosted* environment (with an operating system providing services like `printf` to console). Embedded C runs in a *freestanding / bare-metal* environment where the programmer must initialize the hardware via a startup file and vector table.
- **Memory Model (1.5 Marks):** Standard C assumes a large, dynamically allocated memory (heap, large stack). Embedded C uses a *fixed memory map* defined by the linker script, with strictly partitioned Flash (code), RAM (data), and EEPROM (non-volatile storage) regions tailored to the specific MCU.

---

### Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **cross-compilation toolchain** used to convert Embedded C source code into executable firmware for a microcontroller. List the major stages and the output produced at each stage. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **advantages of Embedded C** with respect to (i) hardware-level access, (ii) portability, and (iii) code efficiency. Provide at least one real-world example for each advantage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Cross-Compilation Toolchain (7 Marks):**
The Embedded C toolchain is a *cross-platform* development system because the host (PC) compiles code for a different target (MCU). The major stages are:

1. **Editor / IDE Stage (0.5 Mark):** Programmer writes the source file (`main.c`, `uart.c`) in an IDE such as Keil µVision, MPLAB X, or STM32CubeIDE. *Output: Human-readable `.c` file.*

2. **Preprocessor Stage (1 Mark):** The preprocessor handles directives like `#include <reg51.h>`, `#define`, `#ifdef`. It performs macro substitution and conditional compilation, effectively tailoring the code to the target hardware. *Output: Expanded `.i` translation file.*

3. **Cross-Compiler Stage (1.5 Marks):** The cross-compiler (e.g., C51 for 8051, arm-none-eabi-gcc for ARM) translates C statements into target-specific assembly instructions and performs hardware-aware optimizations (loop unrolling, dead code elimination). *Output: `.s` assembly file.*

4. **Assembler Stage (1 Mark):** The assembler converts assembly mnemonics (`MOV A, #0x01`) into machine-readable binary. *Output: `.obj` or `.o` object file.*

5. **Linker Stage (1.5 Marks):** The linker combines all object files with the startup code (`.s` file containing the reset vector and stack pointer initialization) and library routines. It resolves external references and produces a single relocatable file. *Output: `.axf` or `.elf` file.*

6. **Locator / Object Copy Stage (1 Mark):** The locator uses the linker script to assign absolute memory addresses to code (Flash), data (RAM), and constants. It produces the final binary image. *Output: `.hex` file (Intel HEX format) ready for flashing.*

7. **Programmer / Loader Stage (0.5 Mark):** A hardware programmer (e.g., JTAG, SWD, UART bootloader, or ISP) writes the HEX file into the MCU's Flash memory.

**(b) Three Key Advantages (7 Marks — ~2.33 each):**

**(i) Hardware-Level Access:** Embedded C allows the programmer to directly read from and write to Special Function Registers (SFRs) using keywords like `sfr` and `sbit`. For instance, `P1 = 0xFF;` writes directly to Port 1 latch register at address 0x90 on the 8051. This enables real-time control of GPIO pins, ADCs, timers, and communication peripherals without writing any assembly code. *Real-World Example:* In a temperature controller, `ADC_VALUE = ADC_READ(0);` directly reads from the analog channel, processes the value, and drives a relay — all from C.

**(ii) Portability:** A well-structured Embedded C application (with HAL layers) can be recompiled for an entirely different MCU family by simply changing the device header file and the compiler flags. The application logic (e.g., a PID control algorithm) remains unchanged. *Real-World Example:* A heart-rate monitor firmware originally developed on PIC16F877A can be ported to an STM32F407 ARM Cortex-M4 with minimal code changes, often reusing **80–90%** of the source.

**(iii) Code Efficiency:** Modern cross-compilers produce highly optimized machine code with compact instruction sequences. Embedded C typically uses **30–50% less Flash** than equivalent functionally-correct assembly written by humans. *Real-World Example:* A washing machine control program requiring motor control, water-level sensing, and user-interface handling can fit in **8 KB of Flash** on a low-cost 8051 MCU, leaving headroom for additional features.

---

> **Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **(a)** Compare **Standard ANSI C** and **Embedded C** across at least **six parameters**. Justify why Embedded C is preferred for microcontroller programming. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **memory-mapped I/O** in microcontrollers. Describe how Embedded C uses `sfr` and `sbit` keywords to access hardware registers, with a suitable example for the 8051 microcontroller. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks — 1 Mark per relevant row + 1 Mark for justification):**

| **Parameter** | **Standard C** | **Embedded C** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Execution Environment | Hosted (with OS) | Freestanding (bare-metal) |
| Target | Desktop / Server | Microcontroller |
| Memory | Large, dynamic | Small, static, linker-defined |
| Hardware Access | Indirect via OS | Direct via SFRs |
| Standard Library | Full `stdio.h`, `stdlib.h` | Restricted subset |
| Floating Point | Default, free | Expensive; fixed-point preferred |
| Startup Code | OS-supplied (CRT0) | Programmer-defined |

**Justification (1 Mark):** Embedded C is preferred for MCUs because it provides the *right balance* — high-level abstractions for productivity **and** low-level register access for real-time hardware control. Standard C cannot guarantee deterministic behavior on bare-metal targets, and pure assembly is too tedious for complex projects.

**(b) Memory-Mapped I/O Concept (7 Marks):**

**Concept (3 Marks):** In microcontrollers like the 8051, the on-chip peripherals (I/O ports, timers, serial port, ADC) are mapped to specific addresses in the same address space as internal RAM. This is called **memory-mapped I/O**. Writing a byte to address 0x80 (SFR P0) actually sets the logic levels on the physical pins of Port 0. Similarly, reading from address 0x80 reads the current state of Port 0's pins.

**Role of `sfr` and `sbit` (2 Marks):** The `sfr` keyword declares an 8-bit Special Function Register and binds it to its absolute hardware address. The `sbit` keyword declares a single bit within an SFR, allowing bit-level access without bitwise masking.

**8051 Example (2 Marks):**
```c
#include <reg51.h>        // Header that defines SFR addresses
sbit LED = P1^0;          // Declares P1.0 as 'LED'

void main(void) {
    P1 = 0x00;            // Clear entire Port 1 (writes 0x00 to address 0x90)
    LED = 1;              // Set only P1.0 high (compiler generates SETB 90h)
    while(1);
}
```
Here, the statement `LED = 1;` directly manipulates bit 0 of SFR `P1` (address 0x90) without the programmer writing any assembly. This is a direct demonstration of the **hardware-level access advantage** of Embedded C.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "Embedded C" with "Embedded Systems"** — Embedded C is a *language*; Embedded Systems is the *field*. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this slip.
> 2. **Writing `printf("Hello")` as the first line** — This works on simulation but is *heavily discouraged* on real MCUs because `printf` consumes **~1.5–2 KB of Flash** and introduces nondeterministic timing. Always mention UART-based `printf` redirection or a custom `putchar` in serious answers.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the cross-compilation aspect** — The word "cross" is *critical*. A compiler that runs on a PC to produce code for an 8051 is a *cross-compiler*, not a compiler. Examiners specifically test this terminology.
> 4. **Listing advantages without real-world examples** — A 14-mark question expects *justification*, not just bullet points. Always tie advantages to engineering applications.
> 5. **Confusing `sfr` and `sbit`** — `sfr` is for the *full 8-bit register*; `sbit` is for a *single bit* within that register. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the linker stage** — Many students write only 5 stages of the toolchain, forgetting the linker. A complete answer must include **all 6 stages** (Preprocessor → Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Locator → Programmer).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Embedded C** = ISO C + hardware extensions (`sfr`, `sbit`, `bit`, `interrupt`, `using`).
> - It is the **most widely used** language for 8/16/32-bit microcontroller firmware.
> - Compiled using a **cross-compiler** (host PC → target MCU).
> - Runs in a **freestanding (bare-metal)** environment — no OS.
> - **Key advantages:** Hardware access, portability, efficiency, readability, faster development, modularity, rich operators, ISR support, industry-standard toolchains, inline assembly capability.
> - **Toolchain stages:** Preprocessor → Cross-Compiler → Assembler → Linker → Locator → Programmer.
> - **Header files** (`<reg51.h>`, `<avr/io.h>`, `<stm32f4xx.h>`) provide SFR definitions and are MCU-family-specific.
> - **Memory-mapped I/O** means SFRs share the address space with internal RAM.
> - `sfr` declares an 8-bit register; `sbit` declares a single bit of that register.
> - **Avoid** `printf`, `malloc`, `scanf` in production firmware — they bloat code and are nondeterministic.
> - **Prefer** integer/fixed-point math over floating-point on small MCUs.
> - **Linker scripts** define the memory map; always check Flash utilization to ensure future expandability.
> - KTU 2024 outcomes most commonly tested: **CO3** (use of Embedded C programming constructs) and **CO4** (firmware development and debugging methodology).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timer Registers - Timer0, Timer1
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Timer Registers - Timer0, Timer1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition
The **8051 Timer/Counter Subsystem** is an on-chip hardware peripheral consisting of two programmable **16-bit timers (Timer0 and Timer1)** used for precise time-delay generation, event counting, and baud-rate generation for serial communication. The operation of these timers is governed by three categories of **Special Function Registers (SFRs)**:

1. **TMOD Register (89H)** – Configures the *mode of operation* and *function* (Timer vs Counter) of both timers.
2. **TCON Register (88H)** – Controls the *start/stop* of timers and contains the *overflow flag bits*.
3. **Timer Count Registers (TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1)** – 8-bit High/Low byte registers that physically hold the *running count value* of the respective timer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Students must master the *bit-level architecture* of TMOD and TCON, the four operating modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3), and the *delay calculation formula*. These are recurring 14-mark questions in End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### Conceptual Analogy – The "Digital Stopwatch"
Imagine two **mechanical stopwatches** sitting inside your microcontroller:
- Each stopwatch has a **big dial** (TH) showing seconds and a **small dial** (TL) showing milliseconds – together they form a single 16-bit counter.
- A **side switch** (C/T bit in TMOD) decides whether the stopwatch is driven by the *internal clock* (Timer mode – measures time) or by an *external pulse train* on a pin (Counter mode – counts external events).
- A **mode knob** (M1, M0 bits in TMOD) selects how many digits the stopwatch has: 13, 16, 8, or split.
- A **start button** (TR0/TR1 bit in TCON) is pressed by your C/Assembly code to begin counting.
- When the stopwatch rolls over past its maximum value, a **buzzer** rings (TF0/TF1 overflow flag is set) to tell the CPU: *"Delay is over!"*

### Physical Constants & Key Metrics
- **Machine Cycle Frequency** = **Crystal Frequency ÷ 12**
- **Timer Tick Duration** = **12 ÷ Crystal Frequency**
- Standard KTU-approved crystal: **11.0592 MHz** (used for exact baud rates) and **12 MHz** (used for simple integer math).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** Because 11059200 ÷ 12 = **921600 Hz**, which is an exact integer multiple (×16 or ×32) of standard baud rates like 9600, 4800, 1200 – eliminating serial communication errors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The TMOD Register (Address 89H) – *Not Bit-Addressable*

The TMOD register is a **bit-pattern control register** split into two identical 4-bit nibbles. The **upper nibble (bits 7-4)** configures **Timer1**, and the **lower nibble (bits 3-0)** configures **Timer0**.

| Bit | Symbol | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 7 | GATE | **G**ating control for Timer1 | 1 = Timer runs only when INT1 pin is HIGH **and** TR1 = 1 |
| 6 | C/T | **C**ounter/**T**imer select for Timer1 | 0 = Timer mode (internal clock), 1 = Counter mode (external pin T1) |
| 5 | M1 | **M**ode bit 1 for Timer1 | Combined with M0 selects Mode 0, 1, 2, or 3 |
| 4 | M0 | **M**ode bit 0 for Timer1 | |
| 3 | GATE | Gating control for Timer0 | 1 = Timer runs only when INT0 pin is HIGH **and** TR0 = 1 |
| 2 | C/T | Counter/Timer select for Timer0 | 0 = Timer mode, 1 = Counter mode (external pin T0) |
| 1 | M1 | Mode bit 1 for Timer0 | |
| 0 | M0 | Mode bit 0 for Timer0 | |

#### Mode Selection Truth Table (M1, M0)

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Count Range |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer (8048 compatibility) | 0000H to 1FFFH (8192 counts) |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit Timer (Most commonly used) | 0000H to FFFFH (65536 counts) |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer | 00H to FFH (256 counts) |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split Timer (Timer0 only) | Two independent 8-bit timers |

### 2.2 The TCON Register (Address 88H) – *Bit-Addressable*

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 7 | **TF1** | 8FH | Timer1 Overflow Flag – set by hardware on FFFFH→0000H rollover |
| 6 | **TR1** | 8EH | Timer1 Run Control Bit – 1 = Start, 0 = Stop (software controlled) |
| 5 | **TF0** | 8DH | Timer0 Overflow Flag – set by hardware on rollover |
| 4 | **TR0** | 8CH | Timer0 Run Control Bit – 1 = Start, 0 = Stop |
| 3 | IE1 | 8BH | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag |
| 2 | IT1 | 8AH | External Interrupt 1 Type (Edge/Level) |
| 1 | IE0 | 89H | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag |
| 0 | IT0 | 88H | External Interrupt 0 Type (Edge/Level) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gating Logic (Critical Concept):** When GATE = 1, starting the timer requires a **hardware condition** (INTx pin HIGH) **AND** a **software condition** (TRx = 1). When GATE = 0, the timer runs purely under software control via TRx. For most delay-generation programs, **GATE = 0**.

### 2.3 Timer Count Registers (THx, TLx)

| Register | Address | Timer | Function |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **TL0** | 8AH | Timer0 | Low byte of the 16-bit Timer0 count |
| **TH0** | 8CH | Timer0 | High byte of the 16-bit Timer0 count |
| **TL1** | 8BH | Timer1 | Low byte of the 16-bit Timer1 count |
| **TH1** | 8DH | Timer1 | High byte of the 16-bit Timer1 count |

> [!NOTE]
> Although TL0 and TH0 are at addresses 8A and 8C respectively, in the 8051's SFR map, **TL1 is at 8B and TH0 is at 8C** – note the order! This is a famous address-mapping trick question in KTU exams.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Variables | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Machine Cycle Frequency | $f_{mc} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | $f_{osc}$ = crystal freq | Hz |
| Timer Tick Period | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $f_{osc}$ = crystal freq | seconds |
| Max Count (Mode 1) | $N_{max} = 2^{16} = 65536$ | – | counts |
| Max Count (Mode 0) | $N_{max} = 2^{13} = 8192$ | – | counts |
| Max Count (Mode 2) | $N_{max} = 2^{8} = 256$ | – | counts |
| Timer Count Required | $N = 2^{n} - \text{TimerValue}$ | $n$ = mode bits | counts |
| Required Initial Value | $\text{TimerValue} = 2^{n} - N$ | $n$ = mode bits | hex/decimal |
| **Generated Delay** | $T_{delay} = N \times T_{tick}$ | $N$ = count, $T_{tick}$ = tick period | seconds |
| **Finding N from Delay** | $N = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}} = T_{delay} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | – | counts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Timer0/1 in Mode 2 is the **workhorse of the 8051 serial port** – TH1 is auto-reloaded to generate precise baud rates (e.g., TH1 = 0xFD for 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz). Timer0/1 in Mode 1 is the **standard delay-generation mode** in real-time embedded applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Crystal Frequency to Delay

#### **Problem Statement (Standard KTU Model Problem)**
> An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal**. Configure **Timer0 in Mode 1** to generate a delay of exactly **50 ms**. Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0 and TL0.

#### **Step 1: Calculate the Machine Cycle Period**

The 8051 divides the external crystal oscillator frequency internally by 12 to produce its machine cycle clock. Each increment of the timer occurs once per machine cycle.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{1}{f_{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

Substituting $f_{osc} = 12 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$:

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds} = 1 \ \mu\text{s}$$

> **Logic:** Every "tick" of the timer is exactly 1 microsecond wide.

#### **Step 2: Calculate the Required Number of Timer Counts**

To produce a 50 ms delay, the timer must count up a specific number of ticks before overflowing.

$$N = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}}$$

$$N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

#### **Step 3: Verify Feasibility against Mode 1 Range**

Mode 1 is a 16-bit timer, so the maximum number of counts from 0000H to FFFFH is $2^{16} = 65536$.

$$50{,}000 < 65{,}536 \quad \checkmark \text{ Feasible}$$

#### **Step 4: Compute the Initial Timer Value**

The timer starts counting **up** from the loaded value. It overflows when it transitions from FFFFH to 0000H. Therefore, the number of counts *from the initial value* to overflow is:

$$N = 2^{16} - \text{InitialValue}$$

Solving for InitialValue:

$$\text{InitialValue} = 2^{16} - N = 65{,}536 - 50{,}000 = 15{,}536$$

#### **Step 5: Convert the Decimal Value to Hexadecimal**

We convert 15,536 to base 16:

$$15{,}536 \div 16 = 971 \text{ remainder } 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Least significant hex digit} = 0$$

$$971 \div 16 = 60 \text{ remainder } 11 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Next hex digit} = B$$

$$60 \div 16 = 3 \text{ remainder } 12 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Next hex digit} = C$$

$$3 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 3 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Most significant hex digit} = 3$$

Reading from most-significant to least-significant: **0x3CB0**

#### **Step 6: Split into TH0 (High Byte) and TL0 (Low Byte)**

$$\text{TH0} = 0x3C \quad \text{and} \quad \text{TL0} = 0xB0$$

#### **Step 7: Verify the Calculation**

$$N_{verify} = 65{,}536 - 15{,}536 = 50{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

$$T_{delay\_verify} = 50{,}000 \times 1 \ \mu\text{s} = 50 \ \text{ms} \quad \checkmark$$

#### **Step 8: TMOD Configuration**

For Timer0 in **Mode 1 (M1=0, M0=1)**, **Timer mode (C/T=0)**, **No gating (GATE=0)**; Timer1 is unused (set to 0):

$$\text{TMOD} = 0b00000001 = 0x01$$

#### **Step 9: Final 8051 C Code Structure**

```c
TMOD = 0x01;   // Timer0, Mode 1, Software Control
TH0  = 0x3C;   // Load High Byte
TL0  = 0xB0;   // Load Low Byte
TR0  = 1;      // Start Timer0
while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow flag
TR0  = 0;      // Stop Timer0
TF0  = 0;      // Clear overflow flag (software)
```

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix – Mode 2 Auto-Reload Derivation

#### **Problem Statement**
> Using an 8051 with **11.0592 MHz crystal**, generate a delay of **250 µs** using **Timer1 in Mode 2**.

#### **Step 1: Tick Period**

$$T_{tick} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \ \mu\text{s}$$

#### **Step 2: Required Count**

$$N = \frac{250 \ \mu\text{s}}{1.085 \ \mu\text{s}} \approx 230.4$$

Since the count must be an integer, we round to **N = 230**.

#### **Step 3: Initial Value**

For Mode 2 (8-bit, max 256):

$$\text{InitialValue} = 2^{8} - N = 256 - 230 = 26$$

$$\text{InitialValue in Hex} = 26 = 0x1A$$

#### **Step 4: Register Loading (Auto-Reload Behavior)**

In Mode 2, **TH1 holds the reload value** and **TL1 is the actual counter**. The hardware automatically copies TH1 → TL1 on every overflow:

$$\text{TH1} = 0x1A, \quad \text{TL1} = 0x1A$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is elegant:** Once started, the timer produces **exactly N counts of delay, repeatedly, forever**, without software reloading. This is why Mode 2 is the **preferred mode for serial baud-rate generation**.

#### **Step 5: TMOD Configuration**

Timer1 Mode 2, Timer mode, No gating; Timer1 uses upper nibble (M1=1, M0=0, C/T=0, GATE=0 → 0x20); Timer0 unused (0x00):

$$\text{TMOD} = 0x20$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TMOD Register Architecture (Bit-Field Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regTmod[TMOD Register Address 89H]
    regTmod --> t1Group[Upper Nibble Timer1]
    regTmod --> t0Group[Lower Nibble Timer0]
    t1Group --> gate1Node[GATE1 Bit7 External INT1 Gate]
    t1Group --> ct1Node[CT1 Bit6 Counter or Timer Select]
    t1Group --> m11Node[M11 Bit5 Mode High]
    t1Group --> m01Node[M01 Bit4 Mode Low]
    t0Group --> gate0Node[GATE0 Bit3 External INT0 Gate]
    t0Group --> ct0Node[CT0 Bit2 Counter or Timer Select]
    t0Group --> m10Node[M10 Bit1 Mode High]
    t0Group --> m00Node[M00 Bit0 Mode Low]
```

### 4.2 TCON Register Architecture (Bit-Field Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regTcon[TCON Register Address 88H Bit Addressable]
    regTcon --> tf1Node[TF1 Bit7 Timer1 Overflow Flag]
    regTcon --> tr1Node[TR1 Bit6 Timer1 Run Control]
    regTcon --> tf0Node[TF0 Bit5 Timer0 Overflow Flag]
    regTcon --> tr0Node[TR0 Bit4 Timer0 Run Control]
    regTcon --> ie1Node[IE1 Bit3 Ext Int1 Edge Flag]
    regTcon --> it1Node[IT1 Bit2 Ext Int1 Trigger Type]
    regTcon --> ie0Node[IE0 Bit1 Ext Int0 Edge Flag]
    regTcon --> it0Node[IT0 Bit0 Ext Int0 Trigger Type]
```

### 4.3 Timer0 Hardware Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    oscIn[Crystal Oscillator f_osc] --> prescaler[Prescaler Divide by 12]
    prescaler --> mcClock[Machine Cycle Clock f_osc over 12]
    mcClock --> muxNode{MUX C over T bit}
    t0Pin[External Pin T0 P3.4] --> muxNode
    muxNode --> t0Logic[Timer0 Counting Logic]
    gateSig[INT0 Pin P3.2 AND TR0 AND NOT GATE 0] --> t0Logic
    t0Logic --> tl0Reg[TL0 Register 8AH Low Byte]
    t0Logic --> th0Reg[TH0 Register 8CH High Byte]
    tl0Reg --> compareNode[All Ones Detector FFFFH]
    th0Reg --> compareNode
    compareNode --> tf0Set[Set TF0 Flag Bit 8DH]
    tf0Set --> cpuInt[Signals CPU via Polling or Interrupt]
```

### 4.4 Timer Mode Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Start Mode Selection] --> m1q{M1 Bit}
    m1q -->|0| m0a{M0 Bit}
    m1q -->|1| m0b{M0 Bit}
    m0a -->|0| mode0[Mode0 13 Bit Timer 8192 counts]
    m0a -->|1| mode1[Mode1 16 Bit Timer 65536 counts]
    m0b -->|0| mode2[Mode2 8 Bit Auto Reload 256 counts]
    m0b -->|1| mode3[Mode3 Split Timer Timer0 only]
    mode1 --> app1[Application Long Delays above 65ms]
    mode2 --> app2[Application Serial Baud Rates]
    mode0 --> app0[Application Legacy 8048 Code]
    mode3 --> app3[Application Extra 8 Bit Timer]
```

### 4.5 Timer Delay Generation Sequence (Software View)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant TMOD as TMOD Register
    participant THX as THx Register
    participant TLX as TLx Register
    participant TCON as TCON Register
    participant TFLAG as TFx Overflow Flag
    CPU->>TMOD: Configure Mode and C/T bit
    CPU->>THX: Load High Byte of Initial Value
    CPU->>TLX: Load Low Byte of Initial Value
    CPU->>TCON: Set TRx to 1 Start Timer
    TCON-->>CPU: Timer begins incrementing TLx then THx
    loop On every machine cycle
        TLX->>TLX: Increment counter
        Note over TLX: Rolls over THx incremented
    end
    TLX->>TFLAG: Set TFx when overflow occurs
    TFLAG-->>CPU: CPU detects via polling or interrupt
    CPU->>TCON: Clear TRx Stop Timer
    CPU->>TFLAG: Clear TFx flag by software
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: TMOD Register Architecture** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Draw the bit format of the TMOD register of 8051 and explain the function of the GATE and C/T bits.**

**Model Answer:**
The TMOD register at address 89H is an 8-bit register, **not bit-addressable**, divided into two 4-bit nibbles. The **upper nibble (bits 7-4)** controls **Timer1** and the **lower nibble (bits 3-0)** controls **Timer0**. Each nibble contains **GATE**, **C/T**, **M1**, and **M0** bits.

- **GATE Bit:** When set to **1**, the timer runs only when the corresponding INTx pin (P3.2 for Timer0, P3.3 for Timer1) is HIGH *and* the TRx bit is set. When **0**, the timer runs purely under software control via TRx.
- **C/T Bit:** Selects between **Timer mode (C/T = 0)** – the timer is clocked by the internal machine cycle, and **Counter mode (C/T = 1)** – the timer is clocked by external pulses on the T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) pin.

#### **Question 2: TCON Register Function** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the role of the TF0 and TR0 bits in the TCON register of the 8051 microcontroller?**

**Model Answer:**
The TCON register (address 88H) is a bit-addressable register controlling the timer operations:
- **TF0 (Bit 5, address 8DH) – Timer0 Overflow Flag:** This flag is **automatically set by hardware** when Timer0 rolls over from FFFFH to 0000H (in Mode 1). It can be polled by software or used to trigger an interrupt, and must be **cleared by software** before the next delay.
- **TR0 (Bit 4, address 8CH) – Timer0 Run Control Bit:** This is a **software-controlled** start/stop switch. Setting TR0 = 1 **starts** Timer0 (subject to the GATE condition), and clearing TR0 = 0 **stops** the timer.

---

### PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: Delay Calculation using Timer0** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a **12 MHz crystal**, write an 8051 C program to generate a square wave of **50 Hz** frequency on pin **P1.0** using **Timer0 in Mode 1**. Calculate the value to be loaded into TH0 and TL0. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the function of the **TMOD** and **TCON** registers in detail with their bit formats. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Calculate Half-Period Delay**
The 50 Hz square wave has a full period of:
$$T = \frac{1}{50} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}$$
Since the wave is symmetric, each HIGH or LOW half lasts:
$$T_{half} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Machine Cycle Period**
$$T_{tick} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \ \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Required Counts**
$$N = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 10{,}000 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 4: Compute Initial Value**
Mode 1 is 16-bit, so:
$$\text{InitialValue} = 65{,}536 - 10{,}000 = 55{,}536$$

**Step 5: Convert to Hex**
$$55{,}536 = 0xD8F0$$
Therefore: **TH0 = 0xD8, TL0 = 0xF0**

**Step 6: C Program**
```c
#include <reg51.h>
sbit WAVE = P1^0;
void timer0_delay() {
    TMOD = 0x01;    // Timer0, Mode 1
    TH0  = 0xD8;
    TL0  = 0xF0;
    TR0  = 1;       // Start
    while (TF0 == 0); // Wait
    TR0  = 0;       // Stop
    TF0  = 0;       // Clear flag
}
void main() {
    while (1) {
        WAVE = 1;
        timer0_delay();
        WAVE = 0;
        timer0_delay();
    }
}
```

##### **Solution to Part (b):**
*(See Sections 2.1 and 2.2 above for the complete TMOD/TCON bit-format explanation. The student should present a clear labeled diagram of both registers, describe the function of each bit, and state the SFR addresses 89H for TMOD and 88H for TCON.)*

---

#### **Question B: Mode 2 Auto-Reload for Serial Port** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the four operating modes of the 8051 timers. Why is **Mode 2** preferred for **serial communication baud-rate generation**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system with an **11.0592 MHz crystal** uses Timer1 in Mode 2 to generate a **9600 baud** rate. Calculate the value to be loaded into TH1 and explain the auto-reload mechanism. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to Part (a):**

| Mode | Bits (M1, M0) | Description |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Mode 0** | 00 | **13-bit Timer** – compatibility mode with the 8048 microcontroller. Uses 8 bits of TLx and lower 5 bits of THx. Range: 0 to 8191. |
| **Mode 1** | 01 | **16-bit Timer** – full 16-bit counter (THx and TLx). Range: 0 to 65535. Most commonly used for long delays. |
| **Mode 2** | 10 | **8-bit Auto-Reload Timer** – TLx counts from the THx value up to FFH, then overflows; hardware automatically reloads TLx from THx. |
| **Mode 3** | 11 | **Split Timer** (Timer0 only) – TL0 becomes an 8-bit timer, TH0 becomes another 8-bit timer, and Timer1 stops. |

**Why Mode 2 is Preferred for Baud-Rate Generation:**
1. **Auto-Reload**: The hardware automatically copies TH1 → TL1 on every overflow, so there is **no software overhead** for reloading. This ensures jitter-free, continuous baud ticks.
2. **Fixed 8-bit Period**: Each overflow is exactly 256 − TH1 machine cycles, providing a precise and stable time base.
3. **CPU Independence**: Once started, Timer1 free-runs in the background, freeing the CPU for other tasks.

##### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Baud-Rate Time Base**
For standard 8051 serial mode 1, the timer overflow rate must equal **16 × baud rate**:
$$f_{overflow} = 16 \times 9600 = 153{,}600 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Machine Cycle Frequency**
$$f_{mc} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12} = 921{,}600 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3: Required Count (N)**
$$N = \frac{f_{mc}}{f_{overflow}} = \frac{921{,}600}{153{,}600} = 6$$

**Step 4: Initial Value for TH1**
Mode 2 is 8-bit:
$$\text{TH1} = 256 - 6 = 250 = 0xFA$$

**Step 5: TMOD and SCON Configuration**
$$\text{TMOD} = 0x20 \quad \text{(Timer1 Mode 2, Timer mode, no gating)}$$
$$\text{SCON} = 0x50 \quad \text{(Mode 1, Receive enabled)}$$
$$\text{TH1} = 0xFA, \quad \text{TR1} = 1$$

**Auto-Reload Mechanism Explanation:**
On every overflow of TL1 (from 0xFF → 0x00), the hardware automatically transfers the contents of TH1 (0xFA) back into TL1. This means TL1 instantly resets to 250 and begins counting again to 255, producing exactly 6 counts per overflow cycle. The process repeats indefinitely without any CPU intervention, generating a rock-solid 153.6 kHz tick rate that the serial hardware divides by 16 to yield precisely 9600 baud.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Address Confusion:** Writing TMOD = 0x88 or TCON = 0x89 (they are swapped!). TMOD is **89H** and TCON is **88H**.
> 2. **Nibble Order Error:** For Timer1 control, students often set the *lower* nibble instead of the *upper* nibble. Example: For Timer1 Mode 1, TMOD should be **0x10**, not 0x01.
> 3. **Forgetting Software Reset of TF0/TF1:** Unlike external interrupt flags, timer overflow flags **must be cleared in software** before the next delay is generated.
> 4. **Off-By-One in Delay Calculation:** The final increment (FFFFH → 0000H) takes **one additional machine cycle** for the flag to set. Most textbooks ignore this; KTU expects you to mention it for full marks.
> 5. **TRx Reset:** Students often forget to set **TR0 = 0** after the delay, causing the timer to keep running and corrupting the next delay calculation.
> 6. **Confusing GATE Logic:** If GATE = 1, simply setting TRx is **not enough** – the corresponding INTx pin must also be HIGH.

---

### Topic Recap – Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] The 8051 has **two 16-bit timers** (Timer0 and Timer1) controlled by **TMOD (89H)**, **TCON (88H)**, and four count registers (**TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1**).
- [x] **TMOD** sets the *mode* and *function* of each timer; **TCON** starts/stops the timers and holds overflow flags.
- [x] **Upper nibble** of TMOD = Timer1; **Lower nibble** = Timer0.
- [x] **GATE bit** = 1 enables hardware control via INTx pin; 0 = pure software control.
- [x] **C/T bit** = 0 selects Timer mode (internal clock); 1 selects Counter mode (external pulses).
- [x] **M1, M0** select Mode 0 (13-bit), Mode 1 (16-bit), Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), Mode 3 (split timer).
- [x] **TF0/TF1** are overflow flags, set by hardware, cleared by software.
- [x] **TR0/TR1** are software-controlled run bits.
- [x] **Machine cycle** = 12 oscillator periods; tick period $T_{tick} = 12 / f_{osc}$.
- [x] Delay formula: $T_{delay} = (2^{n} - \text{TimerValue}) \times (12 / f_{osc})$.
- [x] **Mode 2** uses auto-reload from THx → TLx, ideal for serial baud-rate generation.
- [x] **Mode 1** is the standard choice for long software delays in C/Assembly.
- [x] For 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz, load **TH1 = 0xFA** with **TMOD = 0x20** and **TR1 = 1**.
- [x] Always clear TFx and reset TRx in software at the end of every delay routine.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configuration of Timer Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Configuration of Timer Registers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In the 8051 microcontroller architecture, **Timer Registers** are the dedicated Special Function Registers (SFRs) that govern the operation, mode selection, control, and counting behavior of the on-chip Timer/Counter hardware modules. The 8051 family integrates two identical **16-bit up-counters** designated **Timer 0** and **Timer 1**, each constructed by cascading two 8-bit registers (`THx` – Timer High byte and `TLx` – Timer Low byte).

The configuration of these timer registers is performed through the following primary SFRs:

| Register | Name | Function | Bit-Addressable? |
|----------|------|----------|------------------|
| **TMOD** | Timer Mode Register | Selects mode (0/1/2/3) and operation (Timer/Counter) | **No** |
| **TCON** | Timer Control Register | Controls run/stop flag, overflow flag, and external interrupt flags | **Yes** |
| **TH0 / TL0** | Timer 0 High / Low byte | Holds the 16-bit count value of Timer 0 | No |
| **TH1 / TL1** | Timer 1 High / Low byte | Holds the 16-bit count value of Timer 1 | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Configuration of Timer Registers is a core Module 4 topic. Students must memorize the bit positions of TMOD and TCON, and be able to write initialization sequences (C or Assembly) that configure a desired timer mode and delay.

## Conceptual Analogy — The Digital Stopwatch

Imagine a **digital stopwatch** used in a sports event:

1. The **TMOD register** is like the **mode dial** on the stopwatch — you set whether to count in milliseconds, seconds, or just raw events (external pulses).
2. The **TCON register** is the **START/STOP button and the alarm flag** — you press `TR0 = 1` to start, and the watch raises `TF0 = 1` when the time elapses.
3. The **THx / TLx registers** are the **display digits** that hold the running count, and you can pre-load them with a starting number (the "initial value" before timing begins).
4. The **Crystal Oscillator** is the stopwatch's **internal timebase** — just as a quartz crystal vibrates to keep time, the microcontroller's crystal generates the precise clock ticks that increment the timer.

When the count "overflows" (rolls over from `0xFFFF` to `0x0000`), the timer automatically raises the **TFx overflow flag**, which can be polled or used to trigger an interrupt — analogous to the stopwatch's buzzer.

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 timer operates at a **machine cycle** rate, which is **1/12th of the crystal oscillator frequency**. This is a hard architectural fact and the foundation of every delay calculation in KTU examinations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the TMOD Register (Address: 89H)

The **TMOD** register is an **8-bit, non bit-addressable** register. The upper nibble (`bits 7–4`) controls **Timer 1**, and the lower nibble (`bits 3–0`) controls **Timer 0**.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **GATE1** | **C/T1** | **T1M1** | **T1M0** | **GATE0** | **C/T0** | **T0M1** | **T0M0** |
| Timer | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 0} |

### Functional meaning of each bit

* **GATE (Gate Control):**
   * `GATE = 0` → Timer runs whenever the software-controlled run flag `TRx` is set. (Software triggering — most common)
   * `GATE = 1` → Timer runs only when `TRx = 1` **AND** the external interrupt pin (`INT0` or `INT1`) is **HIGH**. (Hardware triggering — for pulse width measurement)

* **C/T (Counter / Timer select):**
   * `C/T = 0` → Acts as a **Timer** — increments on internal machine cycles.
   * `C/T = 1` → Acts as a **Counter** — increments on external pulses on pin `T0` (P3.4) or `T1` (P3.5).

* **M1, M0 (Mode Select bits):** Choose one of four operating modes (see Section 2.3).

## 2.2 Anatomy of the TCON Register (Address: 88H, Bit-Addressable)

The **TCON** register is the only timer-related SFR that is **bit-addressable** — every flag can be set/cleared individually in C using names like `TR0 = 1;` or in Assembly using `SETB TR0`.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

* **TF1 (Timer 1 Overflow Flag):** Set **automatically by hardware** when Timer 1 overflows. Must be **cleared by software** before the next event.
* **TR1 (Timer 1 Run Control):** Set by software (`TR1 = 1`) to **start** Timer 1. Cleared (`TR1 = 0`) to **stop** it.
* **TF0 / TR0:** Same as above for Timer 0.
* **IE1, IT1, IE0, IT0:** Belong to external interrupts (not timers directly), but share the same register.

## 2.3 Timer Modes — M1 / M0 Encoding

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Width |
|----|----|------|-------------|-------|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer (8-bit Timer/Counter with 5-bit prescaler from TLx lower bits) | 13-bit |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | **16-bit Timer** — the most commonly used mode in KTU problems | 16-bit |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer — `TLx` counts, `THx` holds the reload value | 8-bit |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split Timer — Timer 0 becomes two independent 8-bit timers; Timer 1 stops | Special |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Mode 1 (16-bit) is the default mode in exam questions. You must compute the **initial value** that must be loaded into `THx:TLx` to produce a specific delay.

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet — Timer Delay Computation

The **master delay equation** for the 8051 timer is the single most-tested formula in Module 4:

$$
\boxed{\;T_{\text{delay}} = \bigl(N_{\text{bits}} - N_{\text{initial}}\bigr) \times T_{\text{machine cycle}}\;}
$$

Where the supporting sub-formulas are:

$$
\boxed{\;T_{\text{machine cycle}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{oscillator}}}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;N_{\text{bits}} = 2^{\text{mode bits}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Mode 1: } N_{\text{bits}} = 65536\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Initial value } (N_{\text{initial}}) = N_{\text{bits}} - \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{machine cycle}}}\;}
$$

### KTU Cheat-Sheet Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Formula / Value |
|----------|--------|------|-----------------|
| Crystal oscillator frequency | $f_{\text{osc}}$ | **Hz (MHz)** | Given (typically 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz) |
| Machine cycle period | $T_{\text{mc}}$ | **µs** | $12 \div f_{\text{osc}}$ |
| Timer count (Mode 1) | $N$ | counts | $2^{16} = 65536$ |
| Timer count (Mode 2) | $N$ | counts | $2^{8} = 256$ |
| Initial value | $N_{\text{initial}}$ | integer | $N - (T_{\text{delay}} / T_{\text{mc}})$ |
| THx (high byte) | — | hex | $N_{\text{initial}} \gg 8$ |
| TLx (low byte) | — | hex | $N_{\text{initial}} \,\&\, \text{0xFF}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **11.0592 MHz crystal**, $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^6 = \mathbf{1.085\,\mu s}$ — this is the most-used value in KTU exam questions. For a **12 MHz crystal**, $T_{\text{mc}} = 1\,\mu s$ (the simplest case).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

Timer-register configuration is the backbone of virtually every real-time embedded system:

* **Precision delays** (replacing software `for` loops in time-critical code).
* **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** for motor speed control, LED dimming, and audio generation.
* **Event counting** (counting objects on a conveyor belt, RPM measurement).
* **Baud-rate generation** for serial communication (UART) — `TH1` is commonly reloaded with `0xFD` for **9600 baud** at 11.0592 MHz.
* **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** emulation by chaining timer interrupts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Delay Equation

### Step 1 — Why 1/12th of Crystal Frequency?

The 8051 divides the external crystal oscillator by a fixed factor of **12** to produce the internal **machine cycle**. This division happens inside the **oscillator and clock-generation circuit**, not in software.

$$
\boxed{\;f_{\text{machine cycle}} = \frac{f_{\text{oscillator}}}{12}\;}
$$

### Step 2 — Period of One Machine Cycle

The period is the reciprocal of the frequency. Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{machine cycle}}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{osc}} / 12} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}
$$

### Step 3 — How the Timer Counts

The timer register (`THx:TLx`) **increments by 1 on every machine cycle**. So in Mode 1, the timer can hold values from $0$ to $65535$, i.e., a total of $65536$ distinct states. The next count after $65535$ (i.e., $0xFFFF$) is $0$, which causes the **overflow flag** `TFx` to be raised.

### Step 4 — Time per Timer Tick

Each increment of the counter consumes exactly one machine cycle:

$$
T_{\text{tick}} = T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}
$$

### Step 5 — Number of Ticks for a Desired Delay

If we want a delay $T_{\text{delay}}$, the number of timer ticks required is:

$$
N_{\text{ticks}} = \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{tick}}} = \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{mc}}}
$$

### Step 6 — Solving for the Initial Value

The timer starts counting from the pre-loaded value $N_{\text{initial}}$ and runs until it reaches the maximum count $N_{\text{bits}}$. So the **number of ticks performed** is:

$$
N_{\text{ticks}} = N_{\text{bits}} - N_{\text{initial}}
$$

Equating both expressions for $N_{\text{ticks}}$:

$$
N_{\text{bits}} - N_{\text{initial}} = \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{mc}}}
$$

Rearranging for $N_{\text{initial}}$:

$$
\boxed{\;N_{\text{initial}} = N_{\text{bits}} - \frac{T_{\text{delay}}}{T_{\text{mc}}}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental timer reload equation**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Compute Initial Value for 50 ms Delay

**Given:** 8051 with $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$, Timer 0 in **Mode 1** (16-bit).

**Goal:** Find the value to load into `TH0:TL0` to generate a $50\,\text{ms}$ delay.

### Solution

**Step A — Machine cycle period**

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

**Step B — Number of ticks needed**

$$
N_{\text{ticks}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46082.95 \approx 46083\,\text{ticks}
$$

**Step C — Initial count value (Mode 1, $N_{\text{bits}} = 65536$)**

$$
N_{\text{initial}} = 65536 - 46083 = 19453
$$

**Step D — Convert to hex and split into TH0 / TL0**

$$
19453_{10} = \text{4BFD}_{16}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;TH0 = 0x4B \qquad TL0 = 0xFD\;}
$$

**Step E — Verify by forward calculation**

$$
\text{Delay} = (65536 - 19453) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 46083 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 50.000\,\text{ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Mode 2 (Auto-Reload) for 200 µs Delay

**Given:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 12\,\text{MHz}$, Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload).

### Solution

**Step A — Machine cycle**

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1\,\mu s
$$

**Step B — Ticks needed**

$$
N_{\text{ticks}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 200\,\text{ticks}
$$

**Step C — Initial value ($N_{\text{bits}} = 256$ for Mode 2)**

$$
N_{\text{initial}} = 256 - 200 = 56_{10} = \text{38}_{16}
$$

**Step D — Register loading for auto-reload**

In Mode 2, `THx` holds the **reload value** and `TLx` does the counting.

$$
\boxed{\;TH1 = TL1 = 0x38\;}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Crystal | Mode | Delay | $T_{\text{mc}}$ | $N_{\text{ticks}}$ | $N_{\text{initial}}$ (dec) | $N_{\text{initial}}$ (hex) | THx | TLx |
|---------|------|-------|-----------------|--------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|-----|-----|
| 11.0592 MHz | Mode 1 | 10 ms | 1.085 µs | 9216 | 56320 | **0xDC00** | DC | 00 |
| 12 MHz | Mode 1 | 1 ms | 1 µs | 1000 | 64536 | **0xFC18** | FC | 18 |
| 11.0592 MHz | Mode 2 | 100 µs | 1.085 µs | 92 | 164 | **0xA4** | A4 | A4 |
| 24 MHz | Mode 1 | 5 ms | 0.5 µs | 10000 | 55536 | **0xD8F0** | D8 | F0 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Forgetting that the 8051 divides the crystal by 12. Students often wrongly use $T_{\text{mc}} = 1/f_{\text{osc}}$ and get a delay that is **12 times smaller** than required. Always insert the "12" in the denominator.

## 3.5 Complete Initialization Sequence (C51)

The standard three-step configuration idiom in **Keil C / SDCC** is:

```c
TMOD = 0x01;     // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), software-controlled, timer mode
TH0  = 0x4B;     // Load high byte of initial value
TL0  = 0xFD;     // Load low byte of initial value
TR0  = 1;        // Start Timer 0 — counting begins

while (TF0 == 0); // Wait here until overflow flag is raised
TR0 = 0;          // Stop the timer
TF0 = 0;          // Clear overflow flag (manual)
```

If using **interrupts** instead of polling:

```c
TMOD = 0x01; TH0 = 0x4B; TL0 = 0xFD;
ET0 = 1;   // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
EA  = 1;   // Enable global interrupt
TR0 = 1;   // Start timer — the ISR fires automatically on overflow
```

The corresponding **Assembly (8051)** version:

```assembly
MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1
MOV TH0,  #4BH      ; High byte
MOV TL0,  #0FDH     ; Low byte
SETB TR0            ; Start timer
WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT  ; Loop until overflow
CLR  TR0
CLR  TF0            ; Clear flag
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the 8051 Timer Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLOCK["Oscillator & Clock Divider"]
        OSC["Crystal Oscillator\nf_osc = 11.0592 MHz"] --> DIV["Divide by 12"]
        DIV --> MC["Machine Cycle Pulse\n1.085 microseconds"]
    end

    subgraph T0REG["Timer 0 Registers"]
        TMODREG["TMOD Register\n89H — Mode & Gate"]
        TCONREG["TCON Register\n88H — Run and Flag"]
        TH0REG["TH0\nHigh Byte"]
        TL0REG["TL0\nLow Byte"]
    end

    subgraph T1REG["Timer 1 Registers"]
        TH1REG["TH1\nHigh Byte"]
        TL1REG["TL1\nLow Byte"]
    end

    subgraph LOGIC["Counting & Detection Logic"]
        UPNC["16-bit Up-Counter\nIncrements every machine cycle"]
        OFD["Overflow Detector\nDetects FFFF to 0000 transition"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Outputs & Interrupts"]
        TF0FLAG["TF0 Flag\nSet on overflow"]
        INTREQ["Interrupt Request\nto CPU Vector 000BH"]
    end

    MC --> UPNC
    TMODREG -. Configures .-> UPNC
    TCONREG -. TR0 bit .-> UPNC
    TH0REG --> UPNC
    TL0REG --> UPNC
    TH1REG --> T1REG
    TL1REG --> T1REG
    UPNC --> OFD
    OFD --> TF0FLAG
    OFD --> INTREQ
    TF0FLAG --> TCONREG

    classDef reg fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#059669,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    class TMODREG,TCONREG,TH0REG,TL0REG,TH1REG,TL1REG reg
    class UPNC,OFD logic
    class TF0FLAG,INTREQ out
```

## 4.2 TMOD Configuration Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Configure TMOD"] --> GATEQ{"Need hardware\ngating via INTx pin?"}
    GATEQ -- "Yes" --> G1["Set GATE = 1"]
    GATEQ -- "No" --> G0["Set GATE = 0\nsoftware start via TRx"]

    G1 --> CTQ{"Count external\nevents on T0/T1?"}
    G0 --> CTQ
    CTQ -- "External events" --> CT1["Set C/T = 1\nCounter Mode"]
    CTQ -- "Internal timing" --> CT0["Set C/T = 0\nTimer Mode"]

    CT0 --> MODEQ{"Choose Mode"}
    CT1 --> MODEQ

    MODEQ -- "Mode 0" --> M0["M1=0, M0=0\n13-bit legacy mode"]
    MODEQ -- "Mode 1" --> M1["M1=0, M0=1\n16-bit (most used)"]
    MODEQ -- "Mode 2" --> M2["M1=1, M0=0\n8-bit auto-reload"]
    MODEQ -- "Mode 3" --> M3["M1=1, M0=1\nSplit Timer 0"]

    M1 --> LOAD["Compute N_initial\nLoad THx, TLx\nSet TRx = 1"]
    M2 --> LOAD
    M0 --> LOAD
    M3 --> LOAD

    LOAD --> POLL{"Wait via\npolling TFx?"}
    POLL -- "Yes" --> POLLACT["while TFx==0 ;\nclear flag manually"]
    POLL -- "No, use ISR" --> ISR["Enable ETx, EA\nWrite ISR routine"]

    class LOAD,POLLACT,ISR action
```

## 4.3 Configuration Sequence — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Define delay T_delay\nand crystal f_osc"] --> B["Step 2: Compute T_mc = 12/f_osc"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Choose Mode (1 or 2)"]
    C --> D["Step 4: N_initial = 2^bits - T_delay / T_mc"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Convert to hex\nSplit into THx, TLx"]

    F --> G["Step 7: TRx = 1 to start"]
    G --> H{"Step 8: Polling or\nInterrupt?"}
    H -- "Polling" --> I["Wait loop on TFx\nClear TFx, TRx"]
    H -- "Interrupt" --> J["Enable ETx, EA\nISR handles TFx"]
```
