# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the function of the TMOD and TCON registers in the 8051 microcontroller. List the timer modes selectable through M1 and M0 bits of TMOD.**

**Model Answer:**

The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** at SFR address **89H** is an 8-bit non-bit-addressable register used to select the operating mode of Timer 0 (lower nibble) and Timer 1 (upper nibble). Each timer has its own **GATE**, **C/T**, **M1**, and **M0** bits controlling gating, timer/counter selection, and mode.

The **TCON (Timer Control) register** at SFR address **88H** is bit-addressable. The upper nibble controls the timers: **TF1/TR1** for Timer 1 and **TF0/TR0** for Timer 0. Setting `TR0 = 1` starts Timer 0; `TF0` is set by hardware on overflow.

The four modes selectable through M1:M0 are:

* **Mode 0:** 13-bit timer
* **Mode 1:** 16-bit timer (most used)
* **Mode 2:** 8-bit auto-reload
* **Mode 3:** Split timer (Timer 0 only)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Calculate the value to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 of Timer 0 in Mode 1 to generate a delay of 1 ms. Assume a crystal frequency of 12 MHz.**

**Model Answer:**

* Machine cycle: $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / 12\,\text{MHz} = 1\,\mu s$
* Ticks required: $N_{\text{ticks}} = 1\,\text{ms} / 1\,\mu s = 1000$
* Initial value: $N_{\text{initial}} = 65536 - 1000 = 64536$
* Hex: $64536_{10} = \text{FC18}_{16}$

$$
\boxed{TH0 = 0xFC \qquad TL0 = 0x18}
$$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the bit format of the **TMOD register** of the 8051. Briefly describe the role of the **GATE** and **C/T** bits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 1** to generate a delay of **25 ms**. Calculate the values of TH1 and TL1, and write the complete initialization sequence in both C and Assembly. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) TMOD Register Format**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol | GATE1 | C/T1 | T1M1 | T1M0 | GATE0 | C/T0 | T0M1 | T0M0 |

* **GATE bit:** When `GATE = 0`, the timer runs purely under software control via the `TRx` bit. When `GATE = 1`, the timer runs only when `TRx = 1` **AND** the corresponding `INTx` pin is HIGH — used for pulse-width measurement.
* **C/T bit:** `C/T = 0` selects internal timer mode (counts machine cycles); `C/T = 1` selects external counter mode (counts pulses on `T0`/`T1` pin).
* **M1, M0 bits:** Select one of four modes: Mode 0 (13-bit), Mode 1 (16-bit), Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), Mode 3 (split timer for Timer 0).

**(b) Delay Calculation**

* Machine cycle: $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / 11.0592 \times 10^{6} = 1.085\,\mu s$
* Ticks: $N_{\text{ticks}} = 25 \times 10^{-3} / 1.085 \times 10^{-6} = 23041$ (approx)
* Initial value: $N_{\text{initial}} = 65536 - 23041 = 42495$
* Hex: $42495_{10} = \text{A5FF}_{16}$

$$
\boxed{TH1 = 0xA5 \qquad TL1 = 0xFF}
$$

**C initialization:**

```c
TMOD = 0x10;   // Timer 1, Mode 1, software start, timer mode
TH1  = 0xA5;
TL1  = 0xFF;
TR1  = 1;      // Start Timer 1
while (TF1 == 0);
TR1 = 0;
TF1 = 0;
```

**Assembly initialization:**

```assembly
MOV  TMOD, #10H
MOV  TH1,  #0A5H
MOV  TL1,  #0FFH
SETB TR1
WAIT: JNB TF1, WAIT
CLR  TR1
CLR  TF1
```

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Timer Mode 1 and Mode 2** of the 8051. Mention a typical application for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an 8051 with **24 MHz crystal**, configure **Timer 0 in Mode 2** to produce a **200 µs** delay. Compute TH0, TL0 and the reload sequence. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Mode 1 vs Mode 2**

| Aspect | Mode 1 | Mode 2 |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Width | **16-bit** (THx + TLx) | **8-bit** (TLx counts) |
| Reload | **Manual** (software must reload) | **Automatic** (THx reloads TLx on overflow) |
| Max delay (12 MHz) | ~65.5 ms | ~255 µs |
| Typical use | Long, one-shot delays | Repetitive, precise intervals (baud rate, periodic sampling) |
| Counter range | $0$ to $65535$ | $0$ to $255$ |

**(b) Mode 2 Calculation at 24 MHz**

* Machine cycle: $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / 24 \times 10^{6} = 0.5\,\mu s$
* Ticks: $N_{\text{ticks}} = 200 \times 10^{-6} / 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 400$

Since Mode 2 is 8-bit (max 256 ticks), 400 ticks **overflows** the 8-bit range. Therefore, the design is **invalid** with a single timer tick; we must either (i) use Mode 1, or (ii) cascade two timer intervals.

**Correct approach using Mode 1 instead:**

* $N_{\text{initial}} = 65536 - 400 = 65136$
* Hex: $65136_{10} = \text{FE70}_{16}$

$$
\boxed{TH0 = 0xFE \qquad TL0 = 0x70}
$$

**C initialization (Mode 1, corrected):**

```c
TMOD = 0x01;
TH0 = 0xFE; TL0 = 0x70;
TR0 = 1;
while (TF0 == 0);
TR0 = 0; TF0 = 0;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the divide-by-12:** This is the **#1 reason for wrong answers**. Always write $T_{\text{mc}} = 12/f_{\text{osc}}$ explicitly. Marks are lost here.
> 2. **Mode 2 overflow confusion:** When the required delay exceeds 256 ticks, Mode 2 cannot be used with a single timer. Either switch to Mode 1 or use a software counter to extend the delay.
> 3. **Confusing TMOD with TCON:** TMOD is for **mode selection** (write-only-style in practice); TCON is for **run/flag control** (bit-addressable). Mixing these up costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to clear TFx:** The overflow flag is **not auto-cleared**. If you don't clear it, the next `while(TF0==0)` loop exits **immediately** — the timer never produces a delay on the second call.
> 5. **Hex / decimal conversion mistakes:** Many students compute the initial value correctly in decimal but mess up the hex split between `THx` and `TLx`. Always double-check with a calculator.
> 6. **Not setting TRx = 1:** Writing to TMOD/THx/TLx is meaningless unless `TRx = 1` is executed to actually **start** the timer.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **TMOD (89H)** is *not* bit-addressable — write the whole byte (e.g., `TMOD = 0x01;` for Timer 0 Mode 1).
* **TCON (88H)** is bit-addressable — `TR0`, `TR1`, `TF0`, `TF1` are the timer-specific bits.
* The 8051 always divides the **crystal frequency by 12** to produce the machine cycle — this is **not programmable**.
* **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the workhorse: range $0$ to $65535$ counts, manual reload.
* **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** is best for repetitive, fixed periods (e.g., UART baud generator).
* **Master delay equation:** $T_{\text{delay}} = (N_{\text{bits}} - N_{\text{initial}}) \times (12/f_{\text{osc}})$.
* **11.0592 MHz** is preferred over 12 MHz in real designs because it generates **exact standard baud rates** (9600, 4800, 1200) with zero error.
* **Always clear `TFx`** in software after detecting the overflow.
* **GATE = 1** enables hardware-controlled timing using the `INTx` pin (used in pulse-width measurement).
* **C/T = 1** switches the timer to external event counter mode on pin `T0` (P3.4) or `T1` (P3.5).
* The **high byte of the initial value** goes into `THx`; the **low byte** goes into `TLx`.
* For 12 MHz crystal, machine cycle = **1 µs** (easiest for exam arithmetic).
* For 11.0592 MHz crystal, machine cycle = **1.085 µs** (most common in KTU questions).
* **Mode 3** is special: Timer 0 becomes two 8-bit timers (`TL0` and `TH0`); Timer 1 is halted.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timer Mode Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Timer Mode Programming**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Timer Mode Programming** in the 8051 microcontroller refers to the systematic configuration and software manipulation of the on-chip programmable **Timer/Counter hardware modules** (Timer 0 and Timer 1) to perform precise time-delay generation, event counting, baud-rate generation, and real-time periodic interrupts. The programmer selects one of four operating modes (Mode 0, Mode 1, Mode 2, Mode 3) by writing appropriate control words into the **Timer Mode (TMOD)** register and the **Timer Control (TCON)** register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PBECT404 - Module 4):** The 8051 contains two 16-bit Timer/Counters — **Timer 0** and **Timer 1** — that can be programmed independently. The timer increments once every **machine cycle**, which is **1/12th** of the oscillator frequency.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen hourglass** ⏳ with adjustable sand flow:
- The **oscillator crystal** is the "grains of sand source" — it provides a steady pulse stream.
- The **machine cycle divider** (÷12) is the narrow neck of the hourglass — it slows the sand so that each grain represents a usable "tick."
- The **16-bit timer register (THx + TLx)** is the bottom bulb — it collects ticks until it overflows (the bulb is full).
- The **overflow flag (TFx)** is the "ding!" sound that tells you the hourglass finished.

You decide *how much sand* to pre-load (initial value) and *how wide* the neck should be (mode selection). The hourglass is **Timer Mode Programming** — pre-load the registers, start the timer, do useful work, and react when it overflows.

| Standard 8051 Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Oscillator Frequency ($f_{osc}$) | **11.0592 MHz** (commonly used) |
| Machine Cycle Frequency | **$f_{osc} / 12$** |
| Machine Cycle Period | **1.085 µs** at 11.0592 MHz |
| Timer Increment Frequency | **921.6 kHz** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz and not 12 MHz?** Because 11.0592 / 12 = 0.9216 MHz, and 921,600 is a clean multiple of standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400…), giving **0% serial communication error**.

---

## SFR (Special Function Register) Block — The Two Key Registers

### TMOD Register (89H) — Timer MODE Register (Not Bit-Addressable)

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 |
| \multicolumn{4}{c}{} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{} |
| \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c|}{TIMER 0} |

### TCON Register (88H) — Timer CONTROL Register (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|-----|--------|----------|
| 7 | TF1 | Timer 1 Overflow Flag (set by hardware, cleared by software) |
| 6 | TR1 | Timer 1 Run Control Bit (1 = Start) |
| 5 | TF0 | Timer 0 Overflow Flag |
| 4 | TR0 | Timer 0 Run Control Bit |
| 3 | IE1 | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag |
| 2 | IT1 | External Interrupt 1 Type (Edge/Level) |
| 1 | IE0 | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag |
| 0 | IT0 | External Interrupt 0 Type |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TMOD is NOT bit-addressable.** You must load the entire byte at once: `TMOD = 0x01;`
> **TCON IS bit-addressable.** You can toggle individual bits: `TR0 = 1;`

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The Four Timer Modes — Operational Breakdown

### Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer (8048 Compatibility Mode)
- Uses **8 bits of THx** + **5 bits of TLx** (lower 5 bits) → 13-bit counter.
- Upper 3 bits of TLx are unused (hold the preloaded value but do not count).
- Range: **0000H to 1FFFH** (8192 counts).
- **Rarely used in modern designs** — kept only for legacy Intel 8048 compatibility.
- The TLx upper 3 bits (bits 5, 6, 7) act as a "reset latch" holding the initial 5-bit value.

### Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer (Most Common)
- Uses all **8 bits of THx** + all **8 bits of TLx** → 16-bit counter.
- Range: **0000H to FFFFH** (65,536 counts).
- When overflow occurs: TFx is set, and the timer rolls over from **FFFFH → 0000H**.
- **Requires software reload** of the initial value after every overflow.
- **Best for generating long, precise delays** (milliseconds range).

### Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload Timer
- TLx acts as the **8-bit counter**; THx holds the **reload value**.
- Range: **00H to FFH** (256 counts).
- When TLx overflows from **FFH → 00H**:
  1. TFx flag is set automatically.
  2. THx value is **automatically copied into TLx** (no software intervention needed).
- **Best for serial communication baud-rate generation** and repetitive short timing tasks.

### Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode (Timer 0 Only)
- Splits **Timer 0** into **two independent 8-bit timers**:
  - **TL0** becomes an 8-bit timer controlled by Timer 0 control bits (TR0, TF0).
  - **TH0** becomes an 8-bit timer, but **controlled by Timer 1 control bits** (TR1, TF1).
- Timer 1 in Mode 3 simply **stops counting** (held at its current value).
- **Rarely used** but powerful when you need three simultaneous timers.

---

## GATE Bit — Software vs Hardware Control

| GATE | TRx | INTx̄ Pin | Timer Action |
|------|-----|----------|--------------|
| 0 | 0 | X | Timer halted |
| 0 | 1 | X | Timer runs (pure **software start**) |
| 1 | 0 | X | Timer halted |
| 1 | 1 | HIGH | Timer runs only when INTx̄ is HIGH (**hardware gated**) |

> [!NOTE]
> For the vast majority of delay-generation code, set **GATE = 0** so that the timer is controlled purely by software via the TRx bit.

## C/T̄ Bit — Timer vs Counter Mode
- **C/T̄ = 0** → Acts as **Timer** (clock source = internal oscillator ÷ 12).
- **C/T̄ = 1** → Acts as **Counter** (clock source = external pulse on T0/T1 pin, pin P3.4/P3.5).

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they are heavily tested in KTU ESE & Series Test papers.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period ($T_{MC}$) | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Timer Tick Duration | Same as $T_{MC}$ | µs (at 11.0592 MHz → 1.085 µs) |
| 3 | Timer Rollover — Mode 1 (16-bit) | Max Count = $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | counts |
| 4 | Timer Rollover — Mode 0 (13-bit) | Max Count = $2^{13} = 8{,}192$ | counts |
| 5 | Timer Rollover — Mode 2 (8-bit) | Max Count = $2^{8} = 256$ | counts |
| 6 | Total Delay — Mode 1 (16-bit) | $T_{delay} = (65{,}536 - N) \times T_{MC}$ | seconds |
| 7 | Total Delay — Mode 0 (13-bit) | $T_{delay} = (8{,}192 - N) \times T_{MC}$ | seconds |
| 8 | Total Delay — Mode 2 (8-bit) | $T_{delay} = (256 - N) \times T_{MC}$ | seconds |
| 9 | Initial Value Calculation | $N = \text{Max Count} - \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}}$ | counts |
| 10 | Timer Increment Frequency | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Hz |
| 11 | Counter Max External Frequency | $f_{ext} \leq \dfrac{f_{osc}}{24}$ | Hz |
| 12 | SMOD Effect on Baud Rate | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bps |

> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** All table cells use `\vert` and `\dfrac` for absolute value and fractions, NEVER raw `|` pipes, to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Mode Used | Why |
|-------------|-----------|-----|
| LED Blinking / Buzzer Tones | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Long, tunable millisecond delays |
| Stopwatch / Clock Generation | Mode 1 with Interrupt | High-resolution timing |
| UART Baud-Rate Generator | Mode 2 (Auto-Reload) | TH1 reload eliminates drift |
| Pulse Width Measurement | Mode 1 (Counter + Timer) | Edge-triggered event capture |
| Traffic Light Controller | Mode 1 with ISR | Multiplexed delay handling |
| Frequency Counter | Mode 1 Counter | Counts external pulses on T0/T1 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivative 1: Delay Calculation for a Given Time (Mode 1)

**Problem:** Generate a delay of **50 ms** using Timer 0 in **Mode 1** with an **11.0592 MHz** crystal.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Machine Cycle Period

The timer increments once every machine cycle, where one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{MC} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Number of Counts Required for the Desired Delay

Convert 50 ms into the equivalent number of timer ticks:

$$
N_{counts} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
N_{counts} = \frac{50{,}000}{1.085} = 46{,}082.949... \approx 46{,}082 \text{ counts}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Initial Timer Value (Preload)

In Mode 1, the timer is 16 bits wide. It overflows when it rolls from **FFFFH → 0000H** (after 65,536 counts). So we must preload the timer with:

$$
N_{initial} = 65{,}536 - N_{counts} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}082 = 19{,}454
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Initial Value to Hexadecimal

Divide 19,454 repeatedly by 16:

$$
19{,}454 \div 16 = 1{,}215 \text{ remainder } 14 \; (E)
$$

$$
1{,}215 \div 16 = 75 \text{ remainder } 15 \; (F)
$$

$$
75 \div 16 = 4 \text{ remainder } 11 \; (B)
$$

$$
4 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 4 \; (4)
$$

Reading remainders bottom-up: **N = 4BFEH**

So:
- **TH0 = 0x4B** (high byte)
- **TL0 = 0xFE** (low byte)

### Step 5 — Calculate Actual Delay Achieved

$$
T_{actual} = (65{,}536 - 19{,}454) \times 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

$$
T_{actual} = 46{,}082 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 49{,}998.97 \, \mu s \approx 50 \, ms
$$

---

## Derivative 2: Delay Calculation for Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)

**Problem:** Generate a delay of **250 µs** using Timer 1 in **Mode 2** with an **11.0592 MHz** crystal.

### Step 1 — Machine Cycle Period

$$
T_{MC} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 2 — Required Number of Counts

$$
N_{counts} = \frac{250 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s} = 230.41 \approx 230
$$

### Step 3 — Initial Value in 8-Bit Mode 2

Mode 2 is 8-bit, so max count = 256.

$$
N_{initial} = 256 - 230 = 26 = 0x1A
$$

### Step 4 — Loading Registers

- **TH1 = 0x1A** (Reload value, stays constant)
- **TL1 = 0x1A** (Initial value)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In Mode 2, the CPU loads the THx value into TLx automatically upon overflow — this is why it's called **auto-reload**. No software reload needed!

---

## Derivative 3: Largest Possible Delay (Mode 1, 11.0592 MHz)

The maximum delay occurs when the timer counts from **0000H → FFFFH** (all 65,536 counts):

$$
T_{max} = 65{,}536 \times 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

$$
T_{max} = 71{,}106.56 \, \mu s \approx 71.1 \, ms
$$

> [!NOTE]
> To generate delays **longer than 71.1 ms** in Mode 1, you must use a **software loop counter** that calls the timer delay routine multiple times.

---

## Complete 8051 Assembly Program — 50 ms Delay Using Timer 0 Mode 1

```asm
; ====== Timer 0 Mode 1: Generate 50 ms Delay ======
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), Software Control
        MOV TH0, #4BH      ; Load High Byte of Preload (4BFEH)
        MOV TL0, #0FEH     ; Load Low Byte of Preload
        SETB TR0           ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   JNB TF0, HERE      ; Wait for Overflow (TF0 = 1)
        CLR TR0            ; Stop Timer
        CLR TF0            ; Clear Overflow Flag
        SJMP MAIN          ; Repeat Forever
        END
```

---

## Analytical Matrix — TMOD Control Word Examples

| Desired Configuration | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | TMOD Byte | Hex |
|-----------------------|------|------|----|----|-----------|-----|
| Timer 0, Mode 1, Software Start | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0000 0001 | **01H** |
| Timer 0, Mode 2, Counter Mode | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0100 0110 | **46H** |
| Timer 1, Mode 2, Auto-Reload | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0010 0000 | **20H** |
| Timer 0, Mode 1; Timer 1, Mode 2 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0010 0001 | **21H** |
| Timer 0, Mode 3 (Split) | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0000 0011 | **03H** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — 8051 Timer/Counter Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["OSCILLATOR 11.0592 MHz"] --> nodeB["DIVIDE BY 12"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MACHINE CYCLE 1.085 us"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"C by T BAR SELECTOR"}
    nodeE["EXTERNAL PIN T0 T1"] --> nodeD
    nodeD -->|TIMER MODE| nodeF["INTERNAL TICK"]
    nodeD -->|COUNTER MODE| nodeG["EXTERNAL PULSE"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["TL0 TL1 8 BIT LOW BYTE"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["TH0 TH1 8 BIT HIGH BYTE"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ{"OVERFLOW DETECTOR"}
    nodeJ -->|ROLLOVER| nodeK["SET TFX FLAG"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["INTERRUPT TO CPU"]
    nodeH --> nodeM["GATE LOGIC AND TRX BIT"]
    nodeN["SOFTWARE TRX AND GATE"] --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeH
    nodeJ --> nodeO["RELOAD FROM THX MODE 2"]
    nodeI --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeH
```

## Diagram 2 — Timer Mode Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["START"] --> nodeB{"SELECT MODE"}
    nodeB -->|M1 M0 EQUAL 0 0| nodeC["MODE 0 13 BIT 8192 COUNTS"]
    nodeB -->|M1 M0 EQUAL 0 1| nodeD["MODE 1 16 BIT 65536 COUNTS MOST USED"]
    nodeB -->|M1 M0 EQUAL 1 0| nodeE["MODE 2 8 BIT AUTO RELOAD 256 COUNTS"]
    nodeB -->|M1 M0 EQUAL 1 1| nodeF["MODE 3 SPLIT TIMER TIMER 0 ONLY"]
    nodeC --> nodeG["LEGACY 8048"]
    nodeD --> nodeH["DELAY GENERATION"]
    nodeE --> nodeI["BAUD RATE GENERATION"]
    nodeF --> nodeJ["DUAL 8 BIT TIMERS"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["REQUIRES SOFTWARE RELOAD"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["AUTOMATIC HARDWARE RELOAD"]
    nodeJ --> nodeM["TIMER 1 STOPS"]
```

## Diagram 3 — Mode 1 (16-bit) Overflow Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["PRELOAD TH0 TL0 WITH N"] --> nodeB["SET TR0 EQUAL 1 START"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TL0 INCREMENT EVERY 1.085 us"]
    nodeC --> nodeD{"TL0 EQUAL FFH"}
    nodeD -->|NO| nodeC
    nodeD -->|YES| nodeE["TL0 ROLLS TO 00 CARRY TO TH0"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["TH0 INCREMENT"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"TH0 TL0 EQUAL FFFFH"}
    nodeG -->|NO| nodeC
    nodeG -->|YES| nodeH["OVERFLOW TFX EQUAL 1"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ROLLOVER TO 0000H"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["INTERRUPT OR POLLING"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["CLEAR TFX AND RELOAD N"]
    nodeK --> nodeB
```

## Diagram 4 — Mode 2 (Auto-Reload) Overflow Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["LOAD TH0 WITH RELOAD VALUE"] --> nodeB["LOAD TL0 WITH SAME VALUE"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SET TR0 EQUAL 1 START"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["TL0 INCREMENT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"TL0 OVERFLOW FFH TO 00H"}
    nodeE -->|NO| nodeD
    nodeE -->|YES| nodeF["SET TFX EQUAL 1"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["HARDWARE AUTO COPY TH0 TO TL0"]
    nodeG --> nodeD
```

## Diagram 5 — Timer/Counter Programming Flow (Software Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["STEP 1 SELECT TIMER 0 OR TIMER 1"] --> nodeB["STEP 2 CHOOSE MODE 0 1 2 OR 3"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 3 DECIDE TIMER OR COUNTER"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["STEP 4 CONFIGURE GATE 0 FOR SOFTWARE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["STEP 5 WRITE TMOD REGISTER"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["STEP 6 CALCULATE PRELOAD VALUE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["STEP 7 LOAD THX AND TLX"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["STEP 8 SET TRX EQUAL 1 TO START"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["STEP 9 POLL TFX OR USE INTERRUPT"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["STEP 10 CLEAR TFX FLAG"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["STEP 11 STOP TIMER CLR TRX"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["STEP 12 RELOAD IF NEEDED MODE 0 1"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["REPEAT OR EXIT"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Explain the TMOD register of 8051 with a neat diagram. Mention the function of the GATE and C/T̄ bits.

**Model Answer:**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is an **8-bit non-bit-addressable** SFR located at address **89H** in the 8051. It controls the operating mode and function of Timer 0 and Timer 1.

**Bit Structure of TMOD:**

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T̄ | M1 | M0 |
| \multicolumn{4}{|c|}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c|}{TIMER 0} |

**Functions of the bits:**
- **GATE**: When set to 1, the timer is controlled by hardware via the INTx̄ pin. When 0, the timer is purely software-controlled using the TRx bit.
- **C/T̄**: When 0, the timer uses the **internal oscillator ÷ 12** as the clock. When 1, it acts as a **counter** counting external pulses on the T0/T1 pin (P3.4/P3.5).
- **M1, M0**: Mode selection bits — (00)=Mode 0, (01)=Mode 1, (10)=Mode 2, (11)=Mode 3.

**Example:** `MOV TMOD, #20H` configures Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload), software-controlled, timer mode.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Timer Mode 1 and Timer Mode 2 of the 8051.

**Model Answer:**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Repeated Pattern]**

| Parameter | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------------|
| Counter Width | **16 bits** (THx + TLx) | **8 bits** (TLx only) |
| Range | 0000H – FFFFH (**65,536 counts**) | 00H – FFH (**256 counts**) |
| Reload Action | **Software reload required** after every overflow | **Hardware auto-reload** from THx |
| Typical Use | Long millisecond-range delays | Serial baud-rate generation, short periodic tasks |
| Max Delay @11.0592 MHz | ~**71.1 ms** | ~**277.6 µs** |
| Overflow Source | Both THx and TLx roll over (FFFFH → 0000H) | Only TLx rolls over (FFH → 00H) |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Write an 8051 ALP to generate a delay of 25 ms using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Assume crystal frequency = 11.0592 MHz. Show all calculations.**

**Model Solution:**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Similar Pattern]**

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle Period:**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Required Timer Counts:**

$$
N_{counts} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 23{,}041 \, \text{counts}
$$

**Step 3 — Initial Value (16-bit):**

$$
N_{initial} = 65{,}536 - 23{,}041 = 42{,}495
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hex:**

$$
42{,}495 = \text{A5FFH}
$$

So **TH0 = 0xA5** and **TL0 = 0xFF**.

**Step 5 — Assembly Program:**

```asm
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0030H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Software Start
        HERE1: MOV TH0, #0A5H   ; Load High Byte
        MOV TL0, #0FFH    ; Load Low Byte
        SETB TR0          ; Start Timer 0
        HERE2: JNB TF0, HERE2  ; Wait for Overflow
        CLR TR0           ; Stop Timer
        CLR TF0           ; Clear Flag
        SJMP HERE1        ; Repeat delay continuously
        END
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the four operating modes of the 8051 Timer with a comparison table. Why is Mode 1 most commonly used for delay generation?**

**Model Solution:**

**Mode 0 (13-bit Mode):** 8048 compatibility mode. Uses 8 bits of THx and lower 5 bits of TLx. Range: 8192 counts. **Rarely used** in modern applications.

**Mode 1 (16-bit Mode):** Most widely used. Full 16-bit counter using THx and TLx. Range: 65,536 counts → max delay ~71.1 ms @ 11.0592 MHz. **Used for delay generation**, real-time clocks, and time measurement.

**Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload Mode):** TLx is the 8-bit counter; THx holds the reload value. On overflow, THx is automatically copied to TLx. **Used for serial communication baud-rate generation** (TH1 holds the reload value) and for repetitive short-period tasks without CPU intervention.

**Mode 3 (Split Timer Mode):** Splits Timer 0 into two 8-bit timers. TH0 and TL0 become independent counters. Timer 1 stops in this mode. **Used when three timer channels are needed**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 | Mode 3 |
|---------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Counter Size | 13-bit | 16-bit | 8-bit | 2 × 8-bit |
| Max Counts | 8,192 | 65,536 | 256 | 256 each |
| Auto-Reload | No | No | Yes | No |
| Available For | T0, T1 | T0, T1 | T0, T1 | T0 only |
| Primary Use | Legacy | Delays | Baud Rate | Extra Channels |

**Why Mode 1 is most common for delay generation:** It offers the **largest count range (65,536)**, giving the longest single-interval delay (~71 ms) and best resolution for millisecond-level timing. Mode 0 is obsolete, Mode 2 has too small a range, and Mode 3 is a special split configuration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the function of the TCON register. How is the TF0 and TR0 bits used in timer programming?**

**Model Solution:**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Pattern]**

The **TCON (Timer Control) register** is an **8-bit, bit-addressable** SFR located at address **88H**. The upper 4 bits (TCON.4–TCON.7) control the timer functions, while the lower 4 bits (TCON.0–TCON.3) handle external interrupts.

**Bit Layout of TCON:**

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |

**TF0 (Timer 0 Overflow Flag) — Bit 5:**
- Set **automatically by hardware** when Timer 0 overflows (rolls from FFFFH to 0000H in Mode 1).
- Used by the programmer to **detect** when the timing interval has elapsed.
- Can be **cleared by software** (e.g., `CLR TF0`) or automatically upon entering the ISR.
- Polled using: `JNB TF0, BACK` or `JB TF0, FORWARD`.

**TR0 (Timer 0 Run Control) — Bit 4:**
- Set by software to **start the timer**: `SETB TR0`.
- Cleared by software to **stop the timer**: `CLR TR0`.
- Acts as the master on/off switch for Timer 0.

**Programming Sequence:**
1. Configure TMOD register.
2. Load initial value into TH0/TL0.
3. `SETB TR0` to start the timer.
4. Wait using `JNB TF0, HERE`.
5. `CLR TR0` to stop the timer.
6. `CLR TF0` to clear the flag for the next cycle.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Calculate the TH1 value to generate a baud rate of 9600 bps for serial communication using Timer 1 in Mode 2 with an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Assume SMOD = 0.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Baud Rate Formula (Mode 2, Timer 1):**

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values:**

$$
9600 = \frac{2^{0}}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

$$
9600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for (256 − TH1):**

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{9600 \times 384}
$$

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{3.6864 \times 10^{6}} = 3
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate TH1:**

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

**Conclusion:** For 9600 bps, load **TH1 = 0xFD = 253** and set **TMOD = 0x20** (Timer 1, Mode 2). This gives **0% baud-rate error** when using an 11.0592 MHz crystal — this is precisely why this crystal is the de facto standard for 8051 serial communication projects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting SMOD factor:** Many students use the simplified formula `TH1 = 256 − (f_osc / (384 × Baud))` and ignore SMOD. If SMOD = 1, the answer doubles the precision — always check PCON.7.
> 2. **Wrong TMOD value for Timer 1 Mode 2:** Writing `TMOD = 0x02` (Timer 0) instead of `TMOD = 0x20` (Timer 1) is a common typo costing 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing machine cycle unit:** In delay problems, students often write $T_{MC} = 1/f_{osc}$ instead of $T_{MC} = 12/f_{osc}$. Always remember the ÷12 divider.
> 4. **Forgetting to clear TF0 in polling mode:** If you don't `CLR TF0`, the next loop iteration will detect TF0 = 1 immediately and skip the delay entirely.
> 5. **Mixing up Mode 1 vs Mode 2 max counts:** Mode 1 = 65,536, Mode 2 = 256. Using the wrong max count invalidates the entire calculation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Timer Mode Programming**

- ☐ **TMOD (89H)** is **NOT bit-addressable** — load the whole byte in one instruction.
- ☐ **TCON (88H)** **IS bit-addressable** — you can use `SETB TR0`, `CLR TF0`.
- ☐ The **8051 has TWO timers** — Timer 0 (pins P3.4) and Timer 1 (pins P3.5).
- ☐ **Machine cycle = 12 / f_osc** — at 11.0592 MHz, this is **1.085 µs** (memorize!).
- ☐ **Mode 0** = 13-bit (legacy 8048), **Mode 1** = 16-bit (most common), **Mode 2** = 8-bit auto-reload (baud rate), **Mode 3** = split (T0 only).
- ☐ **Delay formula (Mode 1):** `N = 65,536 − (Desired_Time / 1.085 µs)`.
- ☐ **THx holds the HIGH byte**, **TLx holds the LOW byte** of the 16-bit preload.
- ☐ **Mode 2 auto-reload** — THx value is **automatically** copied to TLx on overflow.
- ☐ **C/T̄ = 0** → Timer (internal clock), **C/T̄ = 1** → Counter (external pulses on T0/T1).
- ☐ **GATE = 0** → Software control (use TRx), **GATE = 1** → Hardware gated by INTx̄ pin.
- ☐ **Always clear TFx** after detecting overflow in polling mode, otherwise the loop exits immediately next time.
- ☐ **Maximum delay in Mode 1 @ 11.0592 MHz ≈ 71.1 ms** — for longer delays, wrap the timer routine in a software loop.
- ☐ **Maximum external counter frequency ≤ f_osc / 24** — for event counting, respect this limit.
- ☐ **Baud rate at 9600 bps with 11.0592 MHz** → TH1 = 0xFD, TMOD = 0x20, **zero error**.
- ☐ **TMOD bit positions** (Timer 0 → bits 0–3, Timer 1 → bits 4–7) — bit order is **GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0**.

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU typically gives a 7-mark question asking you to calculate a delay OR a baud rate. Practice the arithmetic, write the TMOD/THx/TLx values, AND provide the assembly code — the trifecta guarantees full marks. 🎯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counter Mode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Counter Mode # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Counter Mode** in microcontroller architecture (specifically the **8051 family**, as mandated in the KTU 2024 PBECT404 syllabus) is a specialized operational mode of the on-chip **Timer/Counter hardware unit** in which the internal registers (THx, TLx) are **incremented in response to external events** applied on dedicated port pins (T0 = P3.4 or T1 = P3.5), rather than the internal oscillator-derived clock. The selection between **Timer Mode** and **Counter Mode** is governed by the **C/T̅ bit** (Counter/Timer select bit) located in the **TMOD (Timer Mode) Register**. When `C/T̅ = 1`, the unit operates as a **Counter**; when `C/T̅ = 0`, it operates as a **Timer**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition** > *"Counter Mode is the configuration of the 8051 Timer/Counter in which the register pair (THx, TLx) is incremented by an external pulse applied to the corresponding Tx pin. Each transition from logic HIGH (1) to logic LOW (0) on the Tx pin causes the counter to advance by one count."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) Imagine a **turnstile gate at a metro station entrance**: - Each time a person pushes through the gate, a mechanical **counter wheel clicks forward by one number** (0000 → 0001 → 0002 ...). - The counter doesn't care about *time* (seconds, minutes) — it only cares about **events** (people passing through). - When the wheel rolls over from its maximum value (say, 9999) back to 0000, a **bell rings** (this is analogous to the **TFx overflow flag** being set in the 8051). **The 8051 Counter Mode works exactly like this turnstile:** - The "turnstile arm" is the **Tx pin** (P3.4 or P3.5). - The "click-forward mechanism" is the **internal register increment logic**. - The "bell" is the **TFx (Timer Flag)** bit in the TCON register. - The "person pushing" is any **external digital pulse** from a sensor, switch, encoder, or external device. > [!NOTE] > **Why does the 8051 count from a *value you preload* up to overflow?** > Because if it always counted from 0000H, you'd have to wait for a fixed 65,536 events to overflow. By preloading a **starting value**, you can make the overflow happen after *exactly N events*, allowing precise event-counting. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Hardware Constants - **Internal Machine Cycle Frequency** = **Oscillator Frequency / 12** (i.e., 11.0592 MHz crystal → 921.6 kHz machine cycle). - **Maximum Count in Mode 1 (16-bit)** = **2¹⁶ = 65,536** pulses. - **Maximum Count in Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** = **2⁸ = 256** pulses. - **External Input Sampling Rate** = **Once every Machine Cycle** (12 oscillator periods). - **Required Pulse Width** = **≥ 1 Machine Cycle** to be reliably detected. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The TMOD Register — The Master Configuration Switch The **TMOD (Timer Mode) Register** is an **8-bit SFR (Special Function Register)** at address **89H**. It is **not bit-addressable**, meaning it must be configured as a whole byte using immediate MOV instructions. | Bit (for Timer 0) | Bit (for Timer 1) | Name | Function | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---| | 7 | 3 | **GATE** | 1 = Hardware control (INTx pin enables counting); 0 = Software control (TRx enables counting) | | 6 | 2 | **C/T̅** | **1 = Counter Mode** (external pulses on Tx pin); **0 = Timer Mode** (internal oscillator) | | 5 | 1 | **M1** | Mode select bit 1 | | 4 | 0 | **M0** | Mode select bit 0 | ### 2.1.1 Operating Modes Determined by M1, M0 | M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Register Width | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit timer/counter (compatibility with 8048) | **13-bit** | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit timer/counter (most flexible, most used) | **16-bit** | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload timer/counter | **8-bit** | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split timer (Timer 0 only — TL0 + TH0 as separate 8-bit timers) | **8-bit + 8-bit** | ## 2.2 The TCON Register — The Control & Flag Register The **TCON (Timer Control) Register** at address **88H** is **bit-addressable**, allowing precise bit manipulation. | Bit | Name | Function | |:---:|:---:|:---| | **TCON.7** | **TF1** | Timer 1 **Overflow Flag** — set by hardware on overflow, cleared by software | | TCON.6 | TR1 | Timer 1 **Run Control Bit** — 1 = start, 0 = stop | | TCON.5 | TF0 | Timer 0 **Overflow Flag** — set by hardware on overflow | | **TCON.4** | **TR0** | Timer 0 **Run Control Bit** — 1 = start counting, 0 = stop | | TCON.3 | IE1 | External Interrupt 1 Edge Flag | | TCON.2 | IT1 | External Interrupt 1 Type (edge/level) | | TCON.1 | IE0 | External Interrupt 0 Edge Flag | | TCON.0 | IT0 | External Interrupt 0 Type (edge/level) | ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Counter Mode The "WHY" and "HOW" of counter operation, broken into clear logic stages: 1. **Configuration Stage** — Set `C/T̅ = 1` in TMOD to select Counter Mode. Set `M1, M0` to choose width (typically `M1=0, M0=1` for Mode 1 / 16-bit). 2. **Preload Stage** — Load initial 16-bit value into the `THx` (high byte) and `TLx` (low byte) register pair using immediate MOV instructions. 3. **Start Stage** — Set the `TRx` bit in TCON to `1` (this acts as the software "ON switch" when GATE = 0). 4. **Sampling Stage** — Every **machine cycle** (12 oscillator periods), the 8051's internal hardware **samples the Tx pin** voltage level. 5. **Edge Detection Stage** — When the hardware detects a transition from **1 → 0** (HIGH to LOW, i.e., **falling edge**), it triggers an increment. 6. **Increment Stage** — The register pair `THx:TLx` is **incremented by 1**. 7. **Roll-Over Stage** — When the count rolls over from its maximum value (`FFFFH` in Mode 1, `00FFH` in Mode 2) back to `0000H`, the **TFx overflow flag is automatically set to 1** by hardware. 8. **Interrupt/Polling Stage** — If the Timer interrupt is enabled (via the IE register), an ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) is called. Otherwise, the program polls `TFx` to detect overflow. > [!NOTE] > **Edge-Detection Rule (HIGH-YIELD for KTU):** > The counter increments on a **1-to-0 transition (falling edge)**. To reliably register, the HIGH and LOW portions of the external pulse must each be at least **1 machine cycle wide** (i.e., 12 oscillator periods each). This is because the pin is sampled only once per machine cycle. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes | |:---|:---|:---| | **Initial value (16-bit, Mode 1)** | $N = 2^{16} - \text{Desired Count} = 65536 - D$ | Decimal count value | | **Initial value (13-bit, Mode 0)** | $N = 2^{13} - D = 8192 - D$ | Decimal count value | | **Initial value (8-bit, Mode 2)** | $N = 2^{8} - D = 256 - D$ | Decimal count value | | **High byte of preload** | $THx = N \gg 8 = \left\lfloor \dfrac{N}{256} \right\rfloor$ | Hex value written to THx | | **Low byte of preload** | $TLx = N \bmod 256$ | Hex value written to TLx | | **Max events before overflow (Mode 1)** | $D_{\max} = 2^{16} = 65536$ | When preloaded with 0000H | | **Sampling rate of external pin** | $f_{sample} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Hz (machine cycle rate) | | **Required pulse HIGH time** | $t_{HIGH} \geq \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | Seconds | | **Required pulse LOW time** | $t_{LOW} \geq \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | Seconds | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility of Counter Mode:** > - **Industrial Conveyor Belts** — Counting products passing a photoelectric sensor. > - **Rotary Encoders** — Measuring RPM of motors (using falling edges of quadrature signals). > - **Anemometers** — Counting wind-cup rotations to compute wind speed. > - **Frequency Measurement** — Counting external clock pulses over a fixed time window. > - **Traffic Monitoring** — Counting vehicles on a road using inductive loop detectors. > - **Vending Machines** — Counting coin insertions via optical or magnetic pulse sensors. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solutions ## 3.1 Derivation: How the Preload Value Formula Arises ### 3.1.1 The Core Problem The 8051 counter always counts **upwards from the loaded value to maximum**, then rolls over to 0000H and sets the TFx flag. We need to find: *What value X should we preload into THx:TLx so that exactly D external events cause overflow?* ### 3.1.2 Mathematical Derivation Let $X$ be the initial preloaded value, and $M$ be the maximum count value of the mode. The total counts required to roll over from $X$ to $M+1$ (which equals 0 with overflow) is given by: $$ D = (M + 1) - X $$ This is the simple difference between the "ceiling" value and the "floor" value. Solving for $X$ (the value we must preload): $$ X = (M + 1) - D $$ **For Mode 1 (16-bit):** Maximum count $M = 65535 = 2^{16} - 1$, so $M + 1 = 65536 = 2^{16}$. $$ X = 2^{16} - D = 65536 - D $$ **For Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload):** Maximum count $M = 255 = 2^{8} - 1$, so $M + 1 = 256 = 2^{8}$. $$ X = 2^{8} - D = 256 - D $$ This is the **fundamental counter preload formula** that the KTU examiner expects verbatim in your answer scripts. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Count 1000 External Pulses (Mode 1, Timer 0) **Given:** - Crystal frequency: $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$ - Mode: 1 (16-bit counter) - Timer: Timer 0 - Desired events before overflow: $D = 1000$ **Step 1 — Compute the initial value (decimal):** $$ X = 65536 - 1000 = 64536 $$ **Step 2 — Convert 64536 (decimal) to hexadecimal:** $$ 64536 \div 16 = 4033 \text{ remainder } 8 \quad (64536 = 0xFC18H) $$ **Step 3 — Split into high byte (TH0) and low byte (TL0):** $$ TH0 = 64536 \gg 8 = \left\lfloor \dfrac{64536}{256} \right\rfloor = 252 = 0xFCH $$ $$ TL0 = 64536 \bmod 256 = 64536 - (252 \times 256) = 64536 - 64512 = 24 = 0x18H $$ **Step 4 — Construct the 8051 Assembly program:** ```assembly MOV TMOD, #05H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit), C/T-bar=1 (Counter) ; TMOD = 0000_0101 = 05H MOV TH0, #0FCH ; Load high byte MOV TL0, #018H ; Load low byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 (begin counting external pulses) HERE: JNB TF0, HERE ; Wait in loop until overflow flag is set CLR TR0 ; Stop the counter CLR TF0 ; Clear the overflow flag ``` ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Measure Frequency of External Square Wave (Mode 1, Timer 0) **Engineering Scenario:** A digital speed sensor outputs a square wave of unknown frequency. We want to measure it using **Counter Mode for a fixed 1-second gate time** (provided by Timer 1 operating in Timer Mode). **Strategy:** - **Timer 0 (Counter Mode, Mode 1):** Counts external pulses. - **Timer 1 (Timer Mode, Mode 1):** Generates an exact **1-second delay** (preload = 65536 - 921600 for 11.0592 MHz, but this overflows — so we use a known technique with 50 ms gating scaled, or simply use a 65,536 × 1.085 µs ≈ 71 ms gate and scale; KTU commonly uses a 50 ms gate for simpler scaling). ### 3.3.1 Preload Calculation for 50 ms Gate Time (Timer 1) $$ \text{Machine cycle period} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085 \, \mu s $$ $$ \text{Number of counts for 50 ms} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46080 $$ $$ \text{Preload for Timer 1} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456 = 0x4C00H $$ So `TH1 = 0x4CH` and `TL1 = 0x00H`. ### 3.3.2 Frequency Calculation If the counter counts $N$ external pulses in the 50 ms gate time, then the unknown frequency is: $$ f_{unknown} = \dfrac{N}{50 \times 10^{-3}} = 20 \times N \;\; \text{Hz} $$ > [!NOTE] > **General Frequency Measurement Formula (KTU High-Yield):** > > $$f_{x} = \dfrac{N_{\text{count}}}{T_{\text{gate}}}$$ > > where $N_{\text{count}}$ is the number of pulses accumulated and $T_{\text{gate}}$ is the precise timing window generated by the companion timer. ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Counting with Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) **Given:** Count 150 pulses using Timer 1 in Mode 2. **Step 1 — Compute preload:** $$ X = 256 - 150 = 106 = 0x6AH $$ **Step 2 — Assembly code:** ```assembly MOV TMOD, #60H ; Timer 1, Mode 2, C/T-bar=1 (Counter) ; TMOD = 0110_0000 = 60H MOV TH1, #6AH ; Auto-reload value (high byte = reload register) MOV TL1, #6AH ; Initial value for TL1 SETB TR1 ; Start counter HERE: JNB TF1, HERE ; Wait for overflow CLR TR1 ; Stop counter CLR TF1 ; Clear flag ``` > [!TIP] > **Why use Mode 2 for counter applications?** > Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) is **ideal for baud-rate generation** and short-event counting because the **THx register automatically reloads TLx** on every overflow, so the count cycle repeats without software intervention. This is widely used in **serial communication** (UART baud rates). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Counter Mode Internal Architecture The diagram below shows the internal data path of the 8051 Timer/Counter unit when configured in **Counter Mode** (C/T̅ = 1). Note the multiplexer at the heart of the circuit: it selects between the internal oscillator-derived clock (Timer) and the external pin (Counter). ```mermaid flowchart TD osc["Oscillator Crystal f_osc"] --> div["÷12 Prescaler
Machine Cycle Generator"] div --> mux{{"Multiplexer
(Controlled by C/T-bar bit)"}} pinT0["External Pin T0 (P3.4)
Falling-Edge Pulse Source"] pinT1["External Pin T1 (P3.5)
Falling-Edge Pulse Source"] mux -->|C/T-bar = 0
Timer Mode| regA["TL0 Register
(Low Byte)"] mux -->|C/T-bar = 1
Counter Mode| regA pinT0 --> edgeD["Falling-Edge
Detector"] edgeD --> regA pinT1 --> edgeD2["Falling-Edge
Detector"] edgeD2 --> regB["TL1 Register
(Low Byte)"] regA --> regAhi["TH0 Register
(High Byte)"] regB --> regBhi["TH1 Register
(High Byte)"] regAhi --> ovfA{{"Overflow
Detector
(FFFFH → 0000H)"}} regBhi --> ovfB{{"Overflow
Detector"}} ovfA --> tf0["TCON.5 — TF0 Flag
(Set by Hardware)"] ovfB --> tf1["TCON.7 — TF1 Flag
(Set by Hardware)"] tr0["TCON.4 — TR0
(Run Control)"] --> gateA["Gating Logic
(GATE bit)"] gateA --> regA tr1["TCON.6 — TR1
(Run Control)"] --> gateB["Gating Logic"] gateB --> regB style osc fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style pinT0 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style pinT1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style mux fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style regA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style regB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style tf0 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style tf1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style tr0 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 TMOD Register Bit-Map (Timer 0 and Timer 1) ```mermaid flowchart LR TMOD["TMOD Register (Address 89H, Not Bit-Addressable)"] --> G1["Bit 7: GATE (T1)
HW Ctrl"] TMOD --> C1["Bit 6: C/T-bar (T1)
1=Counter, 0=Timer"] TMOD --> M11["Bit 5: M1 (T1)"] TMOD --> M01["Bit 4: M0 (T1)"] TMOD --> G0["Bit 3: GATE (T0)
HW Ctrl"] TMOD --> C0["Bit 2: C/T-bar (T0)
1=Counter, 0=Timer"] TMOD --> M10["Bit 1: M1 (T0)"] TMOD --> M00["Bit 0: M0 (T0)"] M11 --> mode1["Mode Select for Timer 1"] M01 --> mode1 M10 --> mode2["Mode Select for Timer 0"] M00 --> mode2 style TMOD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px style G1 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style C1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style C0 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style mode1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style mode2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Counter Mode Pulse Detection and Overflow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sensor as External Sensor participant PinT0 as Pin T0 (P3.4) participant Hardware as 8051 Edge Detector participant Reg as TH0:TL0 Register Pair participant Flag as TF0 Flag participant CPU as CPU (Polling/ISR) Note over Sensor,PinT0: External pulse is applied Sensor->>PinT0: HIGH (5V) PinT0->>Hardware: Sampled once per machine cycle Sensor->>PinT0: Transition 1 → 0 (Falling Edge) PinT0->>Hardware: Falling edge detected Hardware->>Reg: Increment TH0:TL0 by 1 Reg->>Reg: Continue counting Note over Reg: 64535 ... 64536 ... 65535 Reg->>Reg: Next increment → 0000H (Roll-Over) Reg->>Flag: Set TF0 = 1 (Hardware) Flag->>CPU: Overflow signal / Interrupt request CPU->>Reg: Read count (if needed) CPU->>Flag: Clear TF0 = 0 (Software) ``` ## 4.4 Counter Mode Application — Product Counter on Conveyor ```mermaid flowchart LR conveyor["Conveyor Belt
with Products"] --> sensor["Photoelectric
Sensor (IR + Photodiode)"] sensor -->|Pulse train| pinT0["8051 Pin T0 (P3.4)"] pinT0 --> counter["8051 Counter
(TH0:TL0)"] counter --> lcd["LCD Display
'Count: 0042'"] counter --> relay["Relay Driver
(ULN2003)"] relay --> motor["Stop Motor
at 100 Products"] style conveyor fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style sensor fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style pinT0 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style counter fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style lcd fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style motor fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > The above diagrams use Mermaid syntax. The unique alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `pinT0`, `regA`, `conveyor`) follow the Mermaid safety rule of avoiding reserved keywords. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Differentiate between Timer Mode and Counter Mode in the 8051 microcontroller. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Timer Mode | Counter Mode | |:---|:---|:---| | **C/T̅ bit in TMOD** | `C/T̅ = 0` | `C/T̅ = 1` | | **Clock Source** | Internal oscillator (÷12 prescaler) | External pin (T0 = P3.4, T1 = P3.5) | | **Counts** | Machine cycles (time intervals) | External events / pulses | | **Increment Trigger** | End of every machine cycle | 1-to-0 transition (falling edge) on Tx pin | | **Typical Application** | Time delay generation, baud-rate generation | Event counting, frequency measurement, RPM measurement | --- ### Question 2: What is the role of the C/T̅ bit in the TMOD register? Why must the external input pulse have a minimum width? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Role of C/T̅ bit:** The **C/T̅ (Counter/Timer select)** bit in the TMOD register decides the source of the clock for the Timer/Counter hardware. When `C/T̅ = 0`, the unit operates in **Timer Mode** and uses the internal oscillator divided by 12. When `C/T̅ = 1`, the unit operates in **Counter Mode** and is incremented by external pulses applied to the corresponding Tx pin. **Why minimum pulse width is required:** The 8051 samples the external Tx pin **only once per machine cycle** (i.e., every 12 oscillator periods). For a falling edge to be reliably detected, both the **HIGH portion** and the **LOW portion** of the external pulse must each be at least **one machine cycle wide**. If the pulse is narrower, the sampling may miss the transition entirely, leading to a **missed count**. For an 11.0592 MHz crystal, this minimum width is approximately **1.085 µs**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain the **TMOD register** of the 8051 in detail, with emphasis on the **C/T̅ bit** and the **GATE bit**. Show the value to be loaded into TMOD to configure **Timer 0 in Counter Mode 1 (16-bit)** with software control. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to count **5000 external pulses** using **Timer 1 in Mode 1 (16-bit counter)**. Compute the required TH1 and TL1 values. Assume $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question A(a) **TMOD Register (SFR Address 89H):** The TMOD register configures both Timer 0 and Timer 1. It is a byte-addressable register, not bit-addressable. | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | Function | GATE | C/T̅ | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T̅ | M1 | M0 | | Timer | \multicolumn{4}{c}{Timer 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{Timer 0} | - **GATE bit:** When `GATE = 1`, the timer is controlled by the external INTx pin (hardware control). When `GATE = 0`, the timer is controlled by the TRx bit in TCON (software control). - **C/T̅ bit:** When `C/T̅ = 1` → **Counter Mode** (external pulses); when `C/T̅ = 0` → **Timer Mode** (internal oscillator). - **M1, M0 bits:** Select one of four operating modes (0, 1, 2, 3). **Configuration for Timer 0, Counter Mode 1, Software Control:** - Timer 0, GATE = 0 (software control) → TMOD bit 3 = 0 - Timer 0, C/T̅ = 1 (Counter Mode) → TMOD bit 2 = 1 - Timer 0, M1 = 0, M0 = 1 (Mode 1, 16-bit) → TMOD bit 1 = 0, bit 0 = 1 - Timer 1 bits are not used (set to 0) → bits 7–4 = 0000 **TMOD value calculation:** $$ TMOD = 0000 \; 0101_{2} = 05H $$ **Assembly instruction:** `MOV TMOD, #05H` --- #### Model Solution — Question A(b) **Step 1 — Preload value calculation:** For 16-bit Mode 1 counter to count exactly 5000 pulses: $$ X = 65536 - 5000 = 60536 $$ **Step 2 — Convert 60536 to hexadecimal:** $$ 60536 = 0xFCB8H $$ *Verification:* $\text{0xFCH} = 252$, $\text{0xB8H} = 184$. $252 \times 256 + 184 = 64512 + 184 = 64696$. Wait — recompute: $65536 - 5000 = 60536$. $60536 \div 16 = 3783$ remainder $8$ $3783 \div 16 = 236$ remainder $7$ $236 \div 16 = 14$ remainder $12$ (C) $14 \div 16 = 0$ remainder $14$ (E) So $60536_{10} = 0xEC78H$. **Step 3 — Split into TH1 and TL1:** $$ TH1 = 0xECH = 236, \quad TL1 = 0x78H = 120 $$ *Verification:* $236 \times 256 + 120 = 60416 + 120 = 60536$ ✓ **Step 4 — Assembly program:** ```assembly ORG 0000H MOV TMOD, #50H ; Timer 1, Mode 1 (16-bit), C/T-bar=1 (Counter) ; 0101_0000 = 50H MOV TH1, #0ECH ; Load high byte MOV TL1, #078H ; Load low byte SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 (begin counting) WAIT: JNB TF1, WAIT ; Wait for overflow after 5000 pulses CLR TR1 ; Stop counter CLR TF1 ; Clear overflow flag HERE: SJMP HERE ; Stay here END ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **working of the 8051 in Counter Mode**. Discuss the role of the **TFx flag** and the **TRx bit**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A digital **tachometer** uses the 8051 in Counter Mode. An optical sensor generates **240 pulses per revolution** of a motor shaft. The Timer is configured in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** to count pulses over a fixed time gate of **10 ms** generated by another timer. Calculate: **(i)** The value to preload into TH1, TL1, and **(ii)** The motor RPM if 80 pulses are counted in the 10 ms window. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Question B(a) **Working of 8051 in Counter Mode:** When `C/T̅ = 1` in the TMOD register, the Timer/Counter unit operates in **Counter Mode**. The internal clock path is disconnected, and the register pair `THx:TLx` is connected (via the falling-edge detector) to the external pin Tx (P3.4 for Timer 0, P3.5 for Timer 1). **Operational Steps:** 1. The Tx pin is sampled once per machine cycle. 2. When a 1-to-0 transition (falling edge) is detected, the register pair is incremented. 3. The count continues until the register overflows (from FFFFH to 0000H in Mode 1, or from FFH to 00H in Mode 2). 4. On overflow, the **TFx flag** (Timer Flag) in TCON is set to 1 by hardware. 5. If the Timer interrupt is enabled (via the IE register), the CPU vectors to the corresponding ISR. 6. The **TRx bit** in TCON is the software run/stop switch — setting `TRx = 1` starts the count, and clearing it stops the count (when GATE = 0). **Role of TFx:** The TFx flag is the hardware indicator that the counter has overflowed (i.e., the desired number of events have been counted). It must be **cleared by software** before the next counting cycle. **Role of TRx:** The TRx bit is the **run control bit**. The counter only increments when `TRx = 1`. This allows the programmer to start and stop the count under software control. *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete internal architecture.)* --- #### Model Solution — Question B(b) **Given:** - Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) - Pulses per revolution: 240 (not directly relevant to preload — only to RPM calculation) - Gate time: 10 ms - Pulses counted: 80 **(i) Preload calculation:** For 8-bit Mode 2 counter, the preload (initial value loaded into TL1, which is also the auto-reload value in TH1) is: $$ X = 2^{8} - D_{\text{desired}} $$ *Note:* The 10 ms gate time is generated by a **separate** timer (e.g., Timer 0 in Timer Mode), not the counter itself. The counter's job is just to count pulses arriving on the T1 pin. The desired count for *one full count cycle* is typically the **maximum** 256 pulses (since Mode 2 cycles continuously) — so we usually preload `TH1 = TL1 = 00H` to count the full 0 to 256 cycle. The actual pulse count is read from TL1 at the moment the gate timer overflows. $$ X = 256 - 256 = 0 = 0x00H $$ So `TH1 = 00H` and `TL1 = 00H`. The counter rolls over every 256 pulses and auto-reloads. **Assembly code for the counter part:** ```assembly MOV TMOD, #60H ; Timer 1, Mode 2, Counter Mode ; 0110_0000 = 60H MOV TH1, #00H ; Auto-reload value MOV TL1, #00H ; Initial value SETB TR1 ; Start counter ; ... (gate timer logic) ... ; After 10 ms, read TL1 to get pulse count MOV A, TL1 ; A = number of pulses counted CLR TR1 ``` **(ii) Motor RPM calculation:** In 10 ms, 80 pulses are counted. Each revolution produces 240 pulses. $$ \text{Revolutions in 10 ms} = \dfrac{80}{240} = 0.3333 \text{ rev} $$ $$ \text{Revolutions per second} = \dfrac{0.3333}{10 \times 10^{-3}} = 33.33 \text{ rev/s} $$ $$ \text{Motor RPM} = 33.33 \times 60 = 2000 \text{ RPM} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Mark Loss Zones:** > 1. **Forgetting to clear TFx:** The TFx flag is **NOT cleared automatically** after the program services the overflow. If you forget the `CLR TFx` instruction, the program will repeatedly re-enter the ISR or falsely re-detect "overflow" on the next poll. **Kerala university scripts lose 1–2 marks here routinely.** > 2. **Wrong TMOD hex value:** Students commonly write `MOV TMOD, #05H` and forget that this configures **Timer 0**, not Timer 1. For Timer 1 Counter Mode 1, the correct value is `50H` (Timer 1 in upper nibble). > 3. **Confusing GATE bit:** If GATE = 1, setting TRx alone will **not** start the counter — you also need a HIGH on the corresponding INTx pin. Always default to GATE = 0 for simple counting applications. > 4. **Off-by-one in pulse count:** For 16-bit Mode 1, the rollover is from `FFFFH → 0000H`, so the *total number of distinct states* is 65,536 (from 0000H to FFFFH inclusive). The formula `65536 - D` already accounts for this. Do NOT use 65535. > 5. **Hex conversion mistakes:** A common error is to incorrectly convert decimal preload to hex. Always verify by computing `TH × 256 + TL` and confirming it equals your preload. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Counter Mode** is selected by setting `C/T̅ = 1` in the **TMOD register** (bit 6 for Timer 1, bit 2 for Timer 0). - ✅ External input pins: **T0 = P3.4**, **T1 = P3.5** (these are alternate functions of Port 3 pins). - ✅ Counting happens on a **1-to-0 transition (falling edge)** detected at the Tx pin. - ✅ Pin is sampled **once per machine cycle** (every 12 oscillator periods). - ✅ The pulse HIGH and LOW widths must each be **≥ 1 machine cycle** to be detected reliably. - ✅ **Mode 1 = 16-bit counter** (most flexible); **Mode 2 = 8-bit auto-reload** (best for repeated counting). - ✅ **Preload formula (Mode 1):** $X = 65536 - D$ - ✅ **Preload formula (Mode 2):** $X = 256 - D$ - ✅ **THx** holds the high byte, **TLx** holds the low byte; $TH \times 256 + TL$ must equal the preload. - ✅ **TFx flag** in TCON is set by hardware on overflow; **must be cleared by software** (`CLR TFx`). - ✅ **TRx bit** in TCON is the software run/stop switch (valid when GATE = 0). - ✅ **GATE = 0** → software control (TRx); **GATE = 1** → hardware control (requires INTx pin HIGH + TRx = 1). - ✅ **Application 1:** Counting objects on conveyor belts using photoelectric sensors. - ✅ **Application 2:** Frequency measurement (count pulses over a known time gate). - ✅ **Application 3:** RPM measurement of motors using rotary encoders (combine with Timer Mode for gating). - ✅ **Application 4:** Auto-reload Mode 2 is widely used for **UART baud-rate generation** (Timer 1 in 8051). - ✅ **TMOD value mnemonic:** For "Timer 0, Mode 1, Counter, software control" → `MOV TMOD, #05H`. - ✅ For "Timer 1, Mode 1, Counter, software control" → `MOV TMOD, #50H`. ---

topics.title: Serial Communication in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Serial Communication in 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Serial Communication** in the **8051 microcontroller** is the process of transmitting or receiving data one bit at a time over a single communication line, as opposed to sending multiple bits simultaneously across parallel lines. The 8051 contains an **on-chip full-duplex Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter (UART)** that enables **Asynchronous Serial Communication** with peripheral devices, computers, or other microcontrollers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The 8051 serial port is a **full-duplex** port, meaning it can transmit and receive data **simultaneously**. It uses two dedicated pins: **P3.0 (RXD – Receive Data)** and **P3.1 (TXD – Transmit Data)**.

The hardware involves two key SFRs (Special Function Registers):
- **SBUF (Serial Data Buffer)** – Address **99H** – holds the data byte to be transmitted or received.
- **SCON (Serial Control)** – Address **98H** – controls the mode, enables reception, and contains interrupt flags.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Walkie-Talkie Conversation"**
>
> Imagine two people communicating via **walkie-talkies** while standing far apart. They share **one channel (air)**, and only **one person speaks at a time**, but they can both hear each other. To ensure the message makes sense:
> - Both must agree on the **speed of speaking (Baud Rate)**.
> - Both must agree on a **frame structure** (start bit → data → stop bit).
> - A **"go ahead" tone (start bit)** begins the message, and an **"over" signal (stop bit)** ends it.
>
> The 8051's serial port works the same way! The **transmitter (TXD)** sends a frame bit-by-bit, and the **receiver (RXD)** decodes it using a pre-agreed **baud rate**. The **start bit** synchronizes timing, **data bits** carry the actual byte, and the **stop bit** marks the end of the frame.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Oscillator Frequency (f_osc)** | Typically **11.0592 MHz** (used to generate standard baud rates like 9600) |
| **Standard Baud Rates** | **1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200** bps |
| **SBUF Address** | **99H** (shared logical address, physically two registers) |
| **SCON Address** | **98H** (bit-addressable) |
| **Serial Port Pins** | **P3.0 (RXD), P3.1 (TXD)** |
| **Default Mode** | **Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud rate)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SBUF Register — Two Registers in One Address

The 8051 has a unique design: writing to address **99H** loads the **Transmit Buffer**, while reading from **99H** accesses the **Receive Buffer**. They are physically two separate 8-bit registers that share the same address.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A programmer must remember that `SBUF = A;` (write) triggers transmission, while `A = SBUF;` (read) retrieves received data. The operation context (read vs. write) automatically selects the correct register.

## 2.2 The SCON Register (98H) — Bit-by-Bit Breakdown

The SCON register is **bit-addressable**, meaning individual bits can be set/cleared. Below is its structure:

| Bit | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **D7** | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 (used with SM1) |
| **D6** | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 |
| **D5** | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable (Mode 2/3) |
| **D4** | **REN** | Receive Enable (must be set to receive) |
| **D3** | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (Modes 2 & 3) |
| **D2** | **RB8** | 9th received bit (Modes 2 & 3) |
| **D1** | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt flag (set by HW, cleared by SW) |
| **D0** | **RI** | Receive Interrupt flag (set by HW, cleared by SW) |

## 2.3 The Four Serial Modes of Operation

The **SM0** and **SM1** bits select one of four operating modes:

| Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Description | Baud Rate Source | Frame |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | **f_osc / 12** (fixed) | 8 bits |
| **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | 8-bit UART | **Timer 1 Overflow** (variable) | 10 bits |
| **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | 9-bit UART | **f_osc / 32** or **f_osc / 64** (fixed) | 11 bits |
| **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | 9-bit UART | **Timer 1 Overflow** (variable) | 11 bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Used Mode in KTU Problems: Mode 1 (8-bit UART).** This is the standard asynchronous communication mode used to interface 8051 with PCs via RS-232.

## 2.4 The PCON Register — The SMOD Bit

The **PCON (Power Control)** register, located at address **87H**, contains a critical bit for serial communication:

- **Bit 7 = SMOD**: When set to **1**, it **doubles the baud rate** in Modes 1, 2, and 3.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always check whether the question specifies SMOD = 0 or SMOD = 1. Forgetting to double the frequency when SMOD = 1 is the most common mistake in baud rate problems.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU serial communication problem.**

| Formula | Description | Conditions |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Baud Rate = (2^SMOD / 32) × (f_osc / (12 × (256 − TH1)))** | Master baud rate equation | **Mode 1 & 3** with Timer 1 |
| **TH1 = 256 − (f_osc / (384 × Baud Rate))** | TH1 reload value | **SMOD = 0** |
| **TH1 = 256 − (f_osc / (192 × Baud Rate))** | TH1 reload value | **SMOD = 1** |
| **Baud Rate = f_osc / 32** | Fixed baud rate | **Mode 2, SMOD = 1** |
| **Baud Rate = f_osc / 64** | Fixed baud rate | **Mode 2, SMOD = 0** |
| **Baud Rate = f_osc / 12** | Fixed baud rate | **Mode 0** (any SMOD) |
| **Timer 1 Mode** | Must be **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** for baud rate generation | Standard |
| **Frame Size** | 10 bits (1 start + 8 data + 1 stop) | **Mode 1** |
| **Frame Size** | 11 bits (1 start + 8 data + 1 programmable + 1 stop) | **Mode 2 & 3** |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Serial communication in 8051 is foundational for:
- **Interfacing with PCs** (via UART-to-USB bridges like CH340, FT232).
- **GPS modules** (NEO-6M communicates via UART at 9600 baud).
- **GSM/Bluetooth modules** (HC-05, SIM800) use UART for AT commands.
- **Sensor networks** — many digital sensors (like the popular DHT11, when configured) and IMUs use serial protocols.
- **Industrial automation** — RS-485 networks for multi-device communication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Baud Rate Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** We will derive the baud rate equation for **Mode 1 (8-bit UART)** with **Timer 1** as the baud rate generator.

### Step 1 — Machine Cycle Frequency

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to get the **machine cycle frequency**.

$$f_{machine} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}$$

**Explanation:** Every instruction in the 8051 takes a multiple of 12 oscillator cycles. This 12-divided frequency is the base tick rate of Timer 1.

### Step 2 — Timer 1 Overflow Rate in Mode 2

In **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)**, Timer 1 increments on every machine cycle and overflows when it wraps from **255 → 0**. The number of timer ticks per overflow is:

$$\text{Ticks per overflow} = 256 - TH1$$

So the overflow frequency is:

$$f_{overflow} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Explanation:** Timer 1 counts up from TH1 to 255, then overflows to 0. Each count happens once per machine cycle (f_osc/12). So in one overflow period, exactly **(256 − TH1)** machine cycles elapse.

### Step 3 — Dividing by 32 (UART Hardware)

The 8051's UART hardware **further divides** the Timer 1 overflow frequency by **32** to generate the baud rate. This is because each transmitted bit lasts for **16 timer overflow periods divided by 2** = effectively dividing the timer rate by 32.

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{overflow}}{32} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1) \times 32}$$

### Step 4 — Incorporating the SMOD Bit

If **SMOD = 1**, the baud rate is **doubled** (effectively the division is by 16 instead of 32). We model this with the factor **2^SMOD**:

$$\boxed{\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}}$$

This is the **KTU master formula** for serial communication baud rate calculation in Mode 1 and Mode 3.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation — Finding TH1 for a Given Baud Rate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classic Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal** and operates in **Mode 1** with **SMOD = 0**. Find the **TH1 value** to achieve a **baud rate of 9600**.

### Given:
- $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$
- Desired Baud Rate = **9600 bps**
- SMOD = 0 (so $2^{SMOD} = 1$)

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Start with the master equation:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values (SMOD = 0):

$$9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by 32:

$$9600 \times 32 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

$$307{,}200 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $12 \times (256 - TH1)$:

$$307{,}200 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1) = 11{,}059{,}200$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the left side:

$$3{,}686{,}400 \times (256 - TH1) = 11{,}059{,}200$$

**Step 6:** Divide both sides by 3,686,400:

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400}$$

$$256 - TH1 = 3$$

**Step 7:** Solve for TH1:

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253$$

$$\boxed{TH1 = 253 = 0xFD}$$

### Step 8 — Verification

Let's verify by substituting TH1 = 253 back:

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{12 \times (256 - 253)} = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{36}$$

$$= \frac{1}{32} \times 307{,}200 = 9600 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$

**The derivation is verified.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common TH1 Values (11.0592 MHz Crystal)

This table is a **must-memorize** reference for KTU viva and written exams:

| Baud Rate | SMOD | TH1 (Decimal) | TH1 (Hex) | Timer 1 Mode |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **9600** | 0 | **253** | **FD** | Mode 2 |
| **4800** | 0 | **250** | **FA** | Mode 2 |
| **2400** | 0 | **244** | **F4** | Mode 2 |
| **1200** | 0 | **232** | **E8** | Mode 2 |
| **19200** | 0 | **245** | **F5** | Mode 2 |
| **9600** | 1 | **247** | **F7** | Mode 2 |

## 3.4 Complete KTU-Style Algorithm — Serial Data Transmission (Mode 1)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard 8051 Assembly steps to transmit a byte via Mode 1:**

| Step | Action | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `MOV SCON, #50H` | Configure SCON: **Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1)**, **REN=1** for reception enable |
| 2 | `MOV TMOD, #20H` | Configure Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** |
| 3 | `MOV TH1, #0FDH` | Load TH1 for **9600 baud** (assuming 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0) |
| 4 | `SETB TR1` | **Start Timer 1** |
| 5 | `MOV SBUF, A` | Load the data byte into SBUF — **transmission begins automatically** |
| 6 | `JNB TI, $` | **Wait** until TI flag is set (transmission complete) |
| 7 | `CLR TI` | **Clear TI** for the next transmission (software MUST do this) |

> [!WARNING]
> **The TI flag is NOT cleared by hardware.** The programmer MUST clear it manually after each transmission. This is a frequent KTU question.

## 3.5 Complete Algorithm — Serial Data Reception (Mode 1)

| Step | Action | Description |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `MOV SCON, #50H` | Mode 1, **REN=1** (Receive Enable) |
| 2 | `MOV TMOD, #20H` | Timer 1 in Mode 2 |
| 3 | `MOV TH1, #0FDH` | For 9600 baud |
| 4 | `SETB TR1` | Start Timer 1 |
| 5 | `JNB RI, $` | Wait for RI = 1 (byte received) |
| 6 | `MOV A, SBUF` | Read received byte from SBUF |
| 7 | `CLR RI` | Clear RI for next reception |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Serial Port — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU 8051 Core] -->|Write 99H| TXBUF[Transmit SBUF 99H]
    CPU -->|Read 99H| RXBUF[Receive SBUF 99H]
    TXBUF --> SHIFTREG1[Transmit Shift Register]
    SHIFTREG1 -->|Bit by Bit| TXDPIN[P3.1 TXD Pin]
    RXDPIN[P3.0 RXD Pin] --> SHIFTREG2[Receive Shift Register]
    SHIFTREG2 --> RXBUF
    TIMER1[Timer 1 Overflow] --> DIV32[Divide by 32 Logic]
    PCON[PCON SMOD Bit] -->|Double Baud| DIV32
    DIV32 -->|Clock Signal| SHIFTREG1
    DIV32 -->|Clock Signal| SHIFTREG2
    SCON[SCON Register 98H] -->|Mode Select| SHIFTREG1
    SCON -->|Mode Select| SHIFTREG2
    SCON -->|TI Flag| CPU
    SCON -->|RI Flag| CPU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Notice how **SBUF appears as a single address (99H) externally** but internally splits into **two distinct physical registers** — one for transmit, one for receive. The **Timer 1 overflow** is divided by **32** (or **16** if SMOD=1) to provide the baud rate clock.

## 4.2 SCON Register — Bit Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SCON_REG["SCON Register Address 98H"]
        direction LR
        D7["D7 SM0 Mode Bit 0"] --> D6["D6 SM1 Mode Bit 1"] --> D5["D5 SM2 Multiprocessor"] --> D4["D4 REN Receive Enable"] --> D3["D3 TB8 9th TX Bit"] --> D2["D2 RB8 9th RX Bit"] --> D1["D1 TI TX Flag"] --> D0["D0 RI RX Flag"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mode Comparison — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[8051 Serial Communication] --> MODESEL{SM0 SM1 Selection}
    MODESEL -->|0 0| MODE0[Mode 0 Shift Register fosc 12]
    MODESEL -->|0 1| MODE1[Mode 1 8 bit UART Timer 1 Baud]
    MODESEL -->|1 0| MODE2[Mode 2 9 bit UART Fixed Baud]
    MODESEL -->|1 1| MODE3[Mode 3 9 bit UART Timer 1 Baud]
    MODE0 --> F0[8 Bit Frame No Start Stop Bits]
    MODE1 --> F1[10 Bit Frame 1 Start 8 Data 1 Stop]
    MODE2 --> F2[11 Bit Frame 1 Start 8 Data 1 Prog 1 Stop]
    MODE3 --> F3[11 Bit Frame 1 Start 8 Data 1 Prog 1 Stop]
    MODE2 --> PCON2{PCON SMOD Bit}
    PCON2 -->|0| B0[Baud fosc 64]
    PCON2 -->|1| B1[Baud fosc 32]
    MODE1 --> PCON1{PCON SMOD Bit}
    PCON1 -->|0| B2[Standard Baud Rate]
    PCON1 -->|1| B3[Doubled Baud Rate]
    MODE3 --> PCON3{PCON SMOD Bit}
    PCON3 -->|0| B4[Standard Baud Rate]
    PCON3 -->|1| B5[Doubled Baud Rate]
```

## 4.4 Data Frame Structure — Mode 1 (Most Important)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRAME["Serial Data Frame Mode 1 Total 10 Bits"]
        direction LR
        SB[Start Bit 0] --> D0_BIT[D0 LSB] --> D1_BIT[D1] --> D2_BIT[D2] --> D3_BIT[D3] --> D4_BIT[D4] --> D5_BIT[D5] --> D6_BIT[D6] --> D7_BIT[D7 MSB] --> STP[Stop Bit 1]
    end
    TIMING[Idle Line High] --> SB
    STP --> IDLE2[Return to Idle High]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 1 Frame Anatomy:** When the line is **idle**, it stays at **logic HIGH (1)**. A transmission begins with a **start bit (0)**, followed by **8 data bits (LSB first)**, and ends with a **stop bit (1)**. The line then returns to idle HIGH, ready for the next frame.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Explain the function of the SBUF and SCON registers in 8051 serial communication.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **SBUF (Serial Buffer, 99H):** It is an 8-bit register used to hold the data byte for serial transmission or reception. Writing to SBUF loads the transmit buffer; reading from SBUF reads from the receive buffer. Physically it is two separate registers sharing one address. **(1.5 marks)**
- **SCON (Serial Control, 98H):** It is an 8-bit bit-addressable register that controls the serial port operation. It contains the mode selection bits (SM0, SM1), receive enable (REN), the 9th data bit (TB8, RB8), and the interrupt flags (TI, RI). **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the four serial communication modes of 8051 and state the baud rate source for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Mode | SM0 SM1 | Description | Baud Rate Source |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| 0 | 0 0 | 8-bit Shift Register | **f_osc / 12** (fixed) |
| 1 | 0 1 | 8-bit UART | **Timer 1 overflow** (variable) |
| 2 | 1 0 | 9-bit UART | **f_osc / 32 or /64** (fixed) |
| 3 | 1 1 | 9-bit UART | **Timer 1 overflow** (variable) |

*(1.5 marks for listing, 1.5 marks for baud rate sources)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the SCON register of 8051 in detail with its bit functions. How is Mode 1 selected and configured? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal oscillator** and operates in serial communication **Mode 1** with **SMOD = 1**. Calculate the value to be loaded into **TH1** to achieve a **baud rate of 4800 bps**. Show all steps. Also write the initialization sequence in 8051 assembly language. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) SCON Register Explanation [7 marks]:**

The SCON register is at address **98H** and is bit-addressable. Its bits are:

| Bit | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **SM0 (D7)** | Serial Mode bit 0 | Combined with SM1 to select one of 4 modes |
| **SM1 (D6)** | Serial Mode bit 1 | Combined with SM0 to select one of 4 modes |
