| **SM2 (D5)** | Multiprocessor Comm. Enable | Used in Modes 2 & 3 for multi-8051 networks |
| **REN (D4)** | Receive Enable | Must be set to **1** by software to allow reception |
| **TB8 (D3)** | 9th Transmit Bit | The programmable 9th bit transmitted in Modes 2 & 3 |
| **RB8 (D2)** | 9th Receive Bit | Holds the 9th received bit in Modes 2 & 3 |
| **TI (D1)** | Transmit Interrupt | Set by **hardware** at end of transmission; cleared by **software** |
| **RI (D0)** | Receive Interrupt | Set by **hardware** at end of reception; cleared by **software** |

**To configure Mode 1:** Set **SM0 = 0, SM1 = 1**, and **REN = 1** for reception capability. Therefore: `SCON = 50H` (binary `0101 0000`). **(1 mark for config value)**

---

**(b) TH1 Calculation and Initialization [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11{,}059{,}200 \text{ Hz}$, Baud Rate = 4800, SMOD = 1.

**Step 1 — Use the TH1 formula for SMOD = 1:**

$$TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$TH1 = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{192 \times 4800}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**

$$192 \times 4800 = 921{,}600$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**

$$\frac{11{,}059{,}200}{921{,}600} = 12$$

**Step 5 — Subtract:**

$$TH1 = 256 - 12 = 244 = 0xF4$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2}{32} \times \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{12 \times (256 - 244)} = \frac{2}{32} \times \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{144} = \frac{2}{32} \times 76{,}800 = 4800 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$

**8051 Assembly Initialization Sequence [remaining marks]:**

```assembly
        MOV SCON, #50H     ; Mode 1, REN=1
        MOV PCON, #80H     ; SMOD=1 (bit 7 set)
        MOV TMOD, #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
        MOV TH1, #0F4H     ; Load 244 for 4800 baud
        SETB TR1           ; Start Timer 1
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **8051 serial port hardware** and the functions of **SBUF, SCON, and PCON** registers in serial communication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system operates with a **12 MHz crystal** in **Mode 2** of serial communication. Calculate the **baud rate** when **SMOD = 0** and **SMOD = 1**. Also explain why **11.0592 MHz** is the preferred crystal frequency for serial communication. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) 8051 Serial Port Block Diagram and Register Functions [7 marks]:**

The 8051 contains a **full-duplex UART** with the following hardware:
- **TXD pin (P3.1):** Transmits serial data OUT of the 8051.
- **RXD pin (P3.0):** Receives serial data INTO the 8051.
- **SBUF (99H):** A double-buffer — writing loads the **transmit register**, reading fetches the **receive register**.
- **SCON (98H):** Controls mode, enables reception, and holds flags.
- **PCON (87H):** The **SMOD** bit (D7) doubles the baud rate when set.

Block diagram (refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete mermaid diagram). The transmit shift register shifts data out bit-by-bit to TXD, while the receive shift register assembles incoming bits from RXD into a parallel byte. A clock derived from **Timer 1** (Modes 1, 3) or fixed from the oscillator (Modes 0, 2) drives both shift registers. **(3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for register functions)**

---

**(b) Baud Rate Calculation — Mode 2 with 12 MHz [7 marks]:**

**For Mode 2, the baud rate formula is:**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{64} \times f_{osc}$$

**Case 1: SMOD = 0**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{1}{64} \times 12{,}000{,}000 = 187{,}500 \text{ bps}$$

**Case 2: SMOD = 1**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2}{64} \times 12{,}000{,}000 = 375{,}000 \text{ bps}$$

**Why 11.0592 MHz is preferred [remaining marks]:**

The frequency **11.0592 MHz** is chosen because it is **divisible by standard baud rate values** (like 9600, 4800, 1200) without leaving any remainder. For example:
- $\frac{11{,}059{,}200}{12 \times 32 \times 9600} = 3$ (integer, gives TH1 = 253)
- With 12 MHz: $\frac{12{,}000{,}000}{12 \times 32 \times 9600} = 3.255$ (non-integer → **baud rate error**)

When TH1 must be an integer, non-integer divisions cause **timing errors** that accumulate during transmission, leading to **data corruption**. The crystal **11.0592 MHz** eliminates this error and produces **exact standard baud rates**. **(3 marks for explanation)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the SMOD factor:** Many students use the SMOD = 0 formula even when SMOD = 1 is given, losing **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Not clearing TI/RI in software:** Writing `MOV SBUF, A` without `JNB TI, $` and `CLR TI` is incomplete — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing the **wait-and-clear** sequence.
> 3. **Confusing SBUF read vs. write:** `MOV A, SBUF` reads, `MOV SBUF, A` writes. Mixing these up is a classic blunder.
> 4. **Wrong Timer 1 mode:** Baud rate generation requires **Timer 1 in Mode 2** (auto-reload). Using Mode 0 or Mode 1 of Timer 1 will not generate the correct baud rate.
> 5. **Using 12 MHz crystal in Mode 1 problems:** When Mode 1 baud rate problems specify 12 MHz, the resulting TH1 is usually a non-integer, indicating a **wrong crystal frequency was assumed**. Always prefer 11.0592 MHz for baud rate calculations.
> 6. **Forgetting REN = 1:** Without setting the **REN bit** in SCON, the 8051 will **never receive** data — the RI flag will never be set.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Serial Communication in 8051**

- The 8051 has a **full-duplex on-chip UART** with pins **P3.0 (RXD)** and **P3.1 (TXD)**.
- **SBUF (99H)** is logically one address but physically **two registers**: one for transmit, one for receive.
- **SCON (98H)** is bit-addressable and contains: **SM0, SM1, SM2, REN, TB8, RB8, TI, RI**.
- There are **4 serial modes**: Mode 0 (shift register), Mode 1 (8-bit UART), Mode 2 (9-bit UART fixed), Mode 3 (9-bit UART variable).
- **Mode 1** is the most common — 10 bits per frame: **1 start + 8 data + 1 stop**.
- **Baud Rate** in Mode 1/3 depends on **Timer 1 overflow rate** and the **SMOD bit** of PCON.
- **Master Formula:** $\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$
- **Timer 1 must be in Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) for baud rate generation.
- For **SMOD = 0**: $TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$
- For **SMOD = 1**: $TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{192 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$
- The crystal **11.0592 MHz** is the standard for accurate baud rate generation.
- For **9600 baud** at 11.0592 MHz with SMOD = 0: **TH1 = 253 = 0xFD**.
- **TI and RI flags** are set by hardware but **must be cleared by software**.
- **REN must be set to 1** in SCON to enable reception.
- Standard transmission sequence: `SCON → PCON → TMOD → TH1 → TR1 → SBUF → wait TI → clear TI`.
- For **Mode 2** (fixed baud): $\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{64}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes and Protocols, RS-232 Pin Configuration and Connection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modes and Protocols, RS-232 Pin Configuration and Connection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In embedded systems, **Serial Communication** is the dominant method of transferring data between a microcontroller (e.g., 8051, ARM, PIC) and external peripherals or computers. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes this under three foundational axes:

1. **Modes of Data Communication** — Directional flow of bits between two nodes (Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex).
2. **Communication Protocols** — The agreed-upon rulebook (timing, voltage levels, framing) that governs the bit exchange. The **RS-232 (Recommended Standard 232)** is the most widely tested asynchronous serial protocol.
3. **Physical Connection** — The hardware interface (DB-9 connector, line drivers like **MAX232**, signal grounding).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RS-232 (ANSI/EIA-232)** is a standard introduced in 1969 (current revision **TIA-232-F**) defining the electrical, mechanical, and functional characteristics of a serial communication interface between a **DTE (Data Terminal Equipment)** such as a PC, and a **DCE (Data Communication Equipment)** such as a modem.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Serial Communication like a Telephone Conversation:**
>
> - **Simplex** = A radio broadcast (one-way; listener cannot reply).
> - **Half-Duplex** = A walkie-talkie (you speak, then release the button for the other to reply — only one direction at a time).
> - **Full-Duplex** = A mobile phone call (both parties can speak and listen simultaneously over separate channels).
>
> The **RS-232 protocol** acts as the "language grammar" of the conversation (English, with start words, stop words, and an accent), and the **DB-9 cable** is the physical handset connecting the two speakers.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Standard Value (RS-232) |
|---|---|
| Maximum Cable Length | **15 meters (≈ 50 feet)** |
| Maximum Data Rate | **20 kbps** (at 15 m); up to **1 Mbps** at short distances |
| Logic '1' (Mark) Voltage | **−3 V to −25 V** (typically **−12 V**) |
| Logic '0' (Space) Voltage | **+3 V to +25 V** (typically **+12 V**) |
| Connector | **DB-9 (DE-9)** Male on DTE, Female on cable |
| Inverter Required? | **Yes** — MCU TTL (0–5 V) is inverted relative to RS-232 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Serial Data Communication

The mode defines the **directionality** and **temporal overlap** of data flow.

#### (a) Simplex Mode
- Data travels in **one direction only** (A → B). The receiver can never reply.
- Two dedicated lines are still used (or one), but B has no transmitter.
- *Example:* Keyboard → Computer, Radio transmitter, PC monitor receiving VGA.

#### (b) Half-Duplex Mode
- Data can travel in **both directions**, but **only one direction at any instant**.
- Requires a shared single channel; nodes must take turns.
- *Example:* Walkie-talkie, older RS-485 bus, I²C bus (master/slave arbitration).

#### (c) Full-Duplex Mode
- Data flows in **both directions simultaneously** over independent channels.
- Requires either two physical wires per direction or multiplexed frequency bands.
- *Example:* Telephone network, **RS-232 (TXD/RXD pair)**, SPI (MOSI/MISO), Ethernet.

### 2.2 Asynchronous vs Synchronous Protocols

> [!NOTE]
> **Asynchronous Transmission (used by RS-232):** No shared clock line. Each data byte is wrapped in framing bits (Start + Data + Parity + Stop). Both ends must agree on **Baud Rate** in advance.
>
> **Synchronous Transmission:** A shared clock line accompanies data. Bytes are packed into continuous frames (e.g., SPI, I²C, USB).

### 2.3 RS-232 Frame Format (Asynchronous Character Frame)

A single RS-232 character frame consists of:

| Bit Position | Field | Width | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Start Bit** | 1 bit | Always **Logic 0 (Space, +V)** — signals receiver that a byte is arriving. Line idles at Logic 1 (Mark, −V). |
| 2 | **Data Bits** | 5 / 6 / 7 / 8 bits (commonly 8) | The actual payload (LSB transmitted first). |
| 3 | **Parity Bit** (optional) | 1 bit | **Even, Odd, Mark, Space**, or None. Error-detection. |
| 4 | **Stop Bit(s)** | 1 / 1.5 / 2 bits | Always **Logic 1 (Mark, −V)** — returns line to idle. |

#### ASCII Example
For character **'A'** (ASCII = 0x41 = 0b01000001) sent with **8-N-1** (8 data, No parity, 1 stop), the line (LSB-first) is:
`Start | 10000010 | Stop`  → 10 bits total per character.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All key equations for this topic are tabulated below.** Memorize these — they appear in almost every KTU Part-B question on this module.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{Baud\_Rate}$ | Time duration of one bit | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $T_{frame} = N_{bits\_per\_frame} \times T_{bit}$ | Total time to transmit one character | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $N_{bits\_per\_frame} = 1 + D + P + S$ | Start(1) + Data(D) + Parity(P=0/1) + Stop(S=1/2) | bits |
| 4 | $Throughput = \dfrac{D}{N_{bits\_per\_frame}} \times Baud\_Rate$ | Useful data rate (efficiency factor × baud) | bps |
| 5 | $Frame\_Efficiency = \dfrac{D}{N_{bits\_per\_frame}} \times 100\%$ | % of frame carrying actual data | % |
| 6 | $V_{OH(RS232)} = -5V \text{ to } -25V$ | Logic 1 (Mark) output high | Volts |
| 7 | $V_{OL(RS232)} = +5V \text{ to } +25V$ | Logic 0 (Space) output low | Volts |
| 8 | $\Delta V_{Noise} \geq \pm 3V$ | Minimum noise margin specified by TIA-232-F | Volts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE on Table Syntax:** Mathematical expressions use the LaTeX `\vert` and `\mid` operators **inside** LaTeX delimiters, never bare pipes `|`, to avoid breaking Markdown table rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial PLCs** still use RS-232 for programming and diagnostics.
- **GPS modules** (e.g., u-blox NEO-6M) output NMEA sentences via RS-232.
- **Point-of-Sale (POS) terminals**, **barcode scanners**, and **legacy biomedical instruments** rely on RS-232.
- **8051's built-in UART** is the canonical teaching example in KTU — the `SBUF` register, `SCON` register (bits SM0, SM1, REN, TI, RI), and Timer-1 overflow rate generator are direct KTU exam topics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Frame Duration for 8-N-1 @ 9600 Baud

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 8051 UART transmits ASCII character 'K' (0x4B) using **8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit, 9600 baud, LSB-first**. Calculate the total frame time and the frame efficiency.

**Step 1 — Identify the frame fields and counts.**

- Start bits = 1
- Data bits D = 8
- Parity bit P = 0 (None)
- Stop bits S = 1

**Step 2 — Compute total bits per frame.**

$$
N_{bits\_per\_frame} = 1 + D + P + S = 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute bit time from baud rate.**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{Baud\_Rate} = \frac{1}{9600} = 1.04167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 104.167 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total frame duration.**

$$
T_{frame} = N_{bits\_per\_frame} \times T_{bit} = 10 \times 104.167 \, \mu s = 1041.67 \, \mu s \approx 1.042 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute frame efficiency.**

$$
\eta_{frame} = \frac{D}{N_{bits\_per\_frame}} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{10} \times 100\% = 80\%
$$

**Step 6 — Compute effective throughput (useful data rate).**

$$
R_{data} = \frac{D}{N_{bits\_per\_frame}} \times Baud\_Rate = 0.8 \times 9600 = 7680 \text{ bps}
$$

**Answer:** $T_{frame} = 1.042$ ms, Efficiency = **80%**, Useful data rate = **7680 bps**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: 8051 Timer-1 Reload Value for 9600 Baud (SMOD = 0)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The standard 8051 derives its UART baud rate from Timer-1 overflow. Using an **11.0592 MHz crystal** and **Mode-2 (8-bit auto-reload)** with **SMOD = 0**, find the Timer-1 reload value TH1 for 9600 baud.

**Step 1 — Recall the 8051 UART baud-rate formula.**

$$
Baud\_Rate = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

where $F_{osc}$ is the crystal frequency.

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $(256 - TH1)$.**

$$
(256 - TH1) = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{F_{osc}}{12 \times Baud\_Rate}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.** With $F_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$ Hz, $Baud\_Rate = 9600$, $SMOD = 0 \Rightarrow 2^{SMOD} = 1$:

$$
(256 - TH1) = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times 9600}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator step by step.**

$$
12 \times 9600 = 115200
$$

$$
\frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{115200} = 96
$$

$$
(256 - TH1) = \frac{96}{32} = 3
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for TH1.**

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

**Answer:** $TH1 = 0xFD = 253_{10}$. This is why **11.0592 MHz crystals** are standard on 8051 boards — they yield integer reload values (and thus exact baud rates) for all common speeds (1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common 8-bit Frame Sizes & Efficiencies

| Configuration | Frame Bits (N) | Useful Data (D) | Efficiency (%) | Frame Time @ 9600 baud |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **7-N-1** | 9 | 7 | 77.78 % | 937.5 µs |
| **7-E-1** (Even) | 10 | 7 | 70.00 % | 1041.67 µs |
| **8-N-1** | 10 | 8 | **80.00 %** | 1041.67 µs |
| **8-E-2** | 12 | 8 | 66.67 % | 1250.0 µs |
| **8-O-1** | 11 | 8 | 72.73 % | 1145.83 µs |
| **5-N-1.5** | 7.5 | 5 | 66.67 % | 781.25 µs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **8-N-1** configuration is the de-facto industry default for PC serial ports, GPS modules, and 8051 lab kits — it offers the best efficiency (80%) without parity overhead.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Comparison of Communication Modes

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SimplexNode[Simplex Mode]
        A1[Node A: Transmitter Only] -->|One-way data| B1[Node B: Receiver Only]
    end

    subgraph HalfDuplexNode[Half-Duplex Mode]
        A2[Node A] <-->|One direction at a time| B2[Node B]
    end

    subgraph FullDuplexNode[Full-Duplex Mode]
        A3[Node A] <==>|Independent channel| B3[Node B]
    end
```

### 4.2 RS-232 Frame Structure (Asynchronous)

```mermaid
graph LR
    IDLE[Idle: Logic 1 Mark -12V] --> START[Start Bit: Logic 0 Space +12V]
    START --> D0[Data Bit D0 LSB]
    D0 --> D1[Data Bit D1]
    D1 --> D2[Data Bit D2]
    D2 --> D3[Data Bit D3]
    D3 --> D4[Data Bit D4]
    D4 --> D5[Data Bit D5]
    D5 --> D6[Data Bit D6]
    D6 --> D7[Data Bit D7 MSB]
    D7 --> PAR[Parity Bit: Optional]
    PAR --> STOP[Stop Bit: Logic 1 Mark -12V]
    STOP --> IDLE
```

### 4.3 DB-9 (DE-9) Connector Pinout — Male (DTE Side View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DB9[DB-9 Male Connector - DTE View - Pins Numbered 1 to 5 Left to Right Top Row 6 to 9 Bottom Row]
        P1[Pin 1: DCD Data Carrier Detect IN]
        P2[Pin 2: RXD Received Data IN]
        P3[Pin 3: TXD Transmitted Data OUT]
        P4[Pin 4: DTR Data Terminal Ready OUT]
        P5[Pin 5: GND Signal Ground]
        P6[Pin 6: DSR Data Set Ready IN]
        P7[Pin 7: RTS Request To Send OUT]
        P8[Pin 8: CTS Clear To Send IN]
        P9[Pin 9: RI Ring Indicator IN]
    end
```

### 4.4 Level Translation: TTL ↔ RS-232 using MAX232

```mermaid
graph LR
    MCU[8051 MCU TTL 0V to 5V] -->|TXD 0-5V Logic| MAX_IN1[MAX232 Pin 11 T1IN]
    MAX_IN1 -->|Charge Pump Doubler Inverter| MAX_OUT1[MAX232 Pin 14 T1OUT]
    MAX_OUT1 -->|RS-232 -12V to +12V| PC_RX[PC DB-9 Pin 2 RXD]

    PC_TX[PC DB-9 Pin 3 TXD] -->|RS-232 -12V to +12V| MAX_IN2[MAX232 Pin 13 R1IN]
    MAX_IN2 -->|Inverter| MAX_OUT2[MAX232 Pin 12 R1OUT]
    MAX_OUT2 -->|TTL 0V to 5V| MCU_RXD[8051 RXD Pin]
```

### 4.5 Null-Modem Connection (DTE-to-DTE, No Modem)

```mermaid
graph LR
    DTE_A[PC A DB-9 Male] -->|Pin 2 RXD| DTE_B[PC B DB-9 Female Pin 3 TXD]
    DTE_A -->|Pin 3 TXD| DTE_B_PIN2[Pin 2 RXD]
    DTE_A -->|Pin 5 GND| DTE_B_PIN5[Pin 5 GND]
    DTE_A -->|Pin 4 DTR| DTE_B_DSR_CD[Pin 6 DSR and Pin 1 DCD Tied]
    DTE_A -->|Pin 7 RTS| DTE_B_CTS[Pin 8 CTS Tied]
    DTE_B -->|Pin 4 DTR| DTE_A_DSR_CD[Pin 6 DSR and Pin 1 DCD Tied]
    DTE_B -->|Pin 7 RTS| DTE_A_CTS[Pin 8 CTS Tied]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In a null-modem cable, **Pin 2 and Pin 3 are crossed** (TX ↔ RX), and handshaking lines are looped back locally (RTS → CTS, DTR → DSR + DCD) to fool each side into believing a modem is connected and ready.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
Differentiate between **Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex** modes of communication. Give **one real-world example** for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Data Flow Direction | Simultaneous? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Simplex** | One direction only (A → B) | N/A | Radio broadcast, TV transmission |
| **Half-Duplex** | Both directions, alternately | **No** | Walkie-talkie, I²C bus |
| **Full-Duplex** | Both directions, at the same time | **Yes** | Mobile phone, **RS-232 (TXD/RXD)** |

Simplex uses a single channel permanently assigned to one direction. Half-Duplex shares one channel, switching direction at any instant. Full-Duplex requires two independent channels (e.g., separate TX and RX wires, or two frequency bands). **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
List the **voltage levels** specified by the RS-232 standard for **Logic 1 (Mark)** and **Logic 0 (Space)**. Why is a level translator like **MAX232** required when interfacing an 8051 to a PC?

**Model Answer:**

- **Logic 1 (Mark) = −3 V to −25 V** (typically −12 V)
- **Logic 0 (Space) = +3 V to +25 V** (typically +12 V)

The 8051 uses TTL logic (0 V to 5 V, with Logic 1 = +5 V). RS-232 voltages are **inverted** (±12 V) and far beyond TTL safe limits — directly connecting could damage the MCU. The **MAX232** IC contains an internal **charge pump** (using external 1 µF/10 µF capacitors) to generate ±10 V from a single +5 V supply, and inverts the levels bidirectionally. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Protocol Mechanics & Frame Timing*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled **ASCII character frame** diagram, explain the **asynchronous serial data format** of RS-232. Mention the role of **start, data, parity, and stop bits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A system transmits data at **19,200 baud** with format **8-E-2** (8 data bits, **even parity**, 2 stop bits).
   (i) Calculate the **total number of bits per frame** and the **time duration of one frame**.
   (ii) Calculate the **frame efficiency** and the **effective data rate** in bps.
   (iii) How many **complete frames can be transmitted in exactly 1 second**? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

- RS-232 is **asynchronous**, meaning no shared clock line is transmitted. Instead, the line **idles at Logic 1 (Mark, −V)**. When a byte is to be sent:
  1. **Start Bit (1 bit, Logic 0 / Space, +V):** Sent first to alert the receiver that data is arriving. The receiver uses the **mid-bit sampling** rule to lock onto bit timing.
  2. **Data Bits (5 to 8, usually 8):** Sent **LSB-first** by convention. The 8051's UART does this in hardware.
  3. **Parity Bit (optional, 1 bit):** Adds a check bit so that the total number of 1's in the data + parity equals **even (E)** or **odd (O)**. Detects single-bit errors.
  4. **Stop Bit(s) (1, 1.5, or 2 bits, Logic 1):** Return line to idle, giving the receiver time to process the byte before the next one.

- The receiver samples each incoming bit at the **center of its bit period** to maximize noise immunity. Both transmitter and receiver must be pre-configured to the same **baud rate, data length, parity, and stop bits** — otherwise framing errors occur (visible as garbage characters on a terminal). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (b):

**(i) Total bits and frame time:**

$$
N = 1 + 8 + 1 + 2 = 12 \text{ bits/frame}
$$

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{19200} = 52.0833 \, \mu s
$$

$$
T_{frame} = 12 \times 52.0833 \, \mu s = 625 \, \mu s
$$

**(ii) Efficiency and data rate:**

$$
\eta = \frac{D}{N} \times 100\% = \frac{8}{12} \times 100\% = 66.67\%
$$

$$
R_{data} = 0.6667 \times 19200 = 12800 \text{ bps}
$$

**(iii) Frames per second:**

$$
N_{frames/s} = \frac{1 \text{ s}}{T_{frame}} = \frac{1}{625 \times 10^{-6}} = 1600 \text{ frames/second}
$$

**Final Answers:** N = 12 bits, $T_{frame}$ = 625 µs, η = 66.67%, $R_{data}$ = 12800 bps, **1600 frames/s**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *RS-232 Hardware Interface & 8051 Programming*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Draw the **DB-9 (DE-9) male connector pinout** as seen on a **DTE (PC)** and explain the function of any **six pins**. Justify why **Pin 2 and Pin 3 must be crossed** in a null-modem cable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Interface an **8051 microcontroller** to a PC's COM port using the **MAX232** level translator. Draw the **interfacing circuit diagram**, write the **C program to transmit the ASCII string "KTU"** at **9600 baud** using **11.0592 MHz crystal** and **8-N-1** format, and explain the role of the **TMOD, SCON, and TH1** registers. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (a):

- **DB-9 Male DTE Pinout (front view, pin side):**
  - **Pin 1 — DCD (Data Carrier Detect):** Input. Asserted by modem when a carrier is detected.
  - **Pin 2 — RXD (Received Data):** Input. Data flowing **into** the DTE from the DCE.
  - **Pin 3 — TXD (Transmitted Data):** Output. Data flowing **out of** the DTE to the DCE.
  - **Pin 4 — DTR (Data Terminal Ready):** Output. DTE signals it is powered and ready.
  - **Pin 5 — GND (Signal Ground):** Common reference for all signals.
  - **Pin 6 — DSR (Data Set Ready):** Input. Modem says "I am ready."
  - **Pin 7 — RTS (Request To Send):** Output. DTE asks permission to send.
  - **Pin 8 — CTS (Clear To Send):** Input. Modem grants permission.
  - **Pin 9 — RI (Ring Indicator):** Input. Asserted when phone rings.

- **Why cross Pins 2 and 3 in a null modem:** In a normal DTE-to-DCE link, the DTE's TXD (Pin 3) feeds the DCE's RXD (Pin 2). When connecting **two DTEs directly** (e.g., two PCs), there is no DCE in between. Therefore, the TXD of PC-A must connect to the RXD of PC-B and vice versa — so **Pin 3 of one end goes to Pin 2 of the other end**, and **Pin 2 to Pin 3**. Handshaking lines (RTS/CTS, DTR/DSR) are also looped back to satisfy the "ready" state. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for (b):

- **Interfacing Circuit (refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid above):** 8051 TXD (P3.1) → MAX232 T1IN (Pin 11) → T1OUT (Pin 14) → PC DB-9 Pin 2 (RXD). PC Pin 3 (TXD) → MAX232 R1IN (Pin 13) → R1OUT (Pin 12) → 8051 RXD (P3.0). Four 1 µF capacitors on MAX232 Pins 1–6 form the **charge pump** generating +10 V and −10 V from +5 V.

- **8051 C Program (Keil syntax):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void uart_init(void) {
    TMOD = 0x20;    // Timer-1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0xFD;    // Reload value for 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0
    SCON = 0x50;    // Mode 1 (8-bit UART), REN enabled
    TR1  = 1;       // Start Timer-1
}

void uart_tx(char ch) {
    SBUF = ch;      // Load character into serial buffer
    while (TI == 0); // Wait until transmission complete
    TI = 0;         // Clear TI flag
}

void main(void) {
    uart_init();
    uart_tx('K');
    uart_tx('T');
    uart_tx('U');
    while (1);
}
```

- **Register Roles:**
  - **TMOD = 0x20:** Configures **Timer-1 in Mode-2** (8-bit auto-reload). The auto-reload feature is essential because the UART requires a precise, continuous overflow rate.
  - **SCON = 0x50:** Sets **SM0=0, SM1=1** → UART **Mode 1** (8-bit variable baud rate, 1 start + 8 data + 1 stop). **REN=1** enables reception.
  - **TH1 = 0xFD (= 253):** Reload value computed from the formula in Section 3.2. Determines the actual baud rate; an incorrect TH1 produces garbled text on the PC. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Inverted voltage logic:** Many students write "Logic 1 = +12 V" — this is **WRONG**. RS-232 uses **negative voltages for Mark (Logic 1)** and **positive for Space (Logic 0)**. Losing 1 mark here is extremely common.
> 2. **Baud Rate vs. Bit Rate confusion:** Baud = symbols/sec, and for binary RS-232 with 1 bit per symbol, baud = bps. Do not say "9600 bps baud" — say "9600 baud (≈ 9600 bps)." The 8051 timer formula uses **baud**, not bits-per-second of payload.
> 3. **Forgetting 8051's 1/12 clock division:** The oscillator is divided by 12 **before** reaching Timer-1. Failing to include the divisor 12 in the TH1 calculation is a classic mistake.
> 4. **Confusing DB-9 and DB-25:** RS-232 originally specified a **25-pin DB-25** connector, but the **9-pin DE-9** is now the universal standard. State "DB-9 (DE-9)" explicitly in your answer.
> 5. **Frame bits must include the START bit:** A common error is to compute $N = D + P + S$ and forget the start bit. Always $N = 1 + D + P + S$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three Modes:** Simplex (one-way), Half-Duplex (alternating), Full-Duplex (simultaneous) — RS-232 is **Full-Duplex**.
- **RS-232 = Asynchronous**; no clock line. Both sides must pre-agree on **baud, data bits, parity, stop bits**.
- **Default frame: 8-N-1** (1 start + 8 data + 0 parity + 1 stop = **10 bits/frame**).
- **Voltage Inversion:** Logic 1 = **−3 V to −25 V** (Mark); Logic 0 = **+3 V to +25 V** (Space). This is the **opposite polarity** of TTL/CMOS.
- **Bit-time formula:** $T_{bit} = 1 / Baud\_Rate$. Frame time = $N_{bits} \times T_{bit}$.
- **Frame efficiency** = (Data bits) / (Total frame bits) × 100%. For 8-N-1, it is **80%**.
- **Throughput = 0.8 × Baud Rate** for 8-N-1; 9600 baud → **7680 bps useful**.
- **DB-9 (DE-9) connector** has 9 pins. **Pin 2 = RXD, Pin 3 = TXD, Pin 5 = GND** — memorize these.
- **Pin 2 ↔ Pin 3 crossing** is mandatory in a **null-modem (DTE-to-DTE)** connection.
- **MAX232** is the canonical level translator: it inverts and steps TTL 0–5 V to RS-232 ±10 V using an internal **charge pump** (requires 4 external capacitors).
- **8051 UART:** Uses **Timer-1 Mode-2 (auto-reload)** for baud generation. The reload value formula is:
  $TH1 = 256 - \left(\dfrac{F_{osc}}{12 \times 32 \times 2^{SMOD} \times Baud\_Rate}\right)$
- **11.0592 MHz crystal** is chosen for the 8051 specifically because it produces **integer TH1 values** for all common baud rates.
- **8051 SCON register:** Mode-1 (8-bit UART) requires **SCON = 0x50** (REN=1). **TI** flag indicates transmission complete; **RI** flag indicates a byte received.
- **Real-world use cases:** PC COM ports, GPS modules, barcode scanners, industrial PLCs, legacy biomedical devices, 8051 lab kits.
- **Maximum cable length = 15 m** at full speed (per TIA-232-F specification).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Serial Port Programming - Transmitting and Receiving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Serial Port Programming - Transmitting and Receiving # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **8051 Serial Port** is an on-chip **Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter (UART)** peripheral that enables full-duplex, asynchronous, frame-based serial communication between the microcontroller and external devices. It uses two dedicated Special Function Registers (SFRs) — the **SBUF (Serial Buffer)** register and the **SCON (Serial Control)** register — and is clocked by **Timer 1** in its **8-bit auto-reload (Mode 2)** configuration to generate the precise **baud rate** required for serial data exchange. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > Serial Port Programming covers the hardware-level programming of the 8051's built-in UART, including SCON configuration, Timer-1 based baud rate generation, character transmission by writing to SBUF, and character reception by reading from SBUF with flag-based polling or interrupt-driven mechanisms. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine the **8051 microcontroller as a post office clerk** with two separate mail slots: - A **green "OUT" slot** (the *Transmit SBUF*) — anything dropped into this slot is automatically stamped with a start bit, the 8 data bits (least significant bit first), and a stop bit, and then transmitted one bit at a time over a single wire (TxD pin P3.1) at a fixed, agreed-upon speed (the *baud rate*). - A **red "IN" slot** (the *Receive SBUF*) — when a properly formatted serial frame arrives on the RxD pin (P3.0), the 8 data bits are automatically assembled and dropped into this slot, ringing a small bell (the *RI flag*) to alert the CPU. The **baud rate** is the **agreed-upon speaking speed** between two post offices. If the sender speaks at 9600 letters/second and the receiver listens at 4800 letters/second, the message will be unintelligible. Hence, both ends must configure **Timer 1** to produce the *exact same* timing. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Standard Values for 8051 Serial Communication:** > - **Standard Crystal Frequency:** **11.0592 MHz** (chosen deliberately because it is an integer multiple of common baud rates, eliminating timing errors). > - **Standard Baud Rates:** **1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200** bits per second. > - **Default Frame Format (Mode 1):** **1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 stop bit** (10 bits total, no parity). > - **Timer 1 Mode:** Always **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) for baud rate generation. > - **SMOD Bit (PCON.7):** Doubles the baud rate when set to **1**. --- ## 1.4 Core Register Inventory | Register | Address (Hex) | Bit-Addressable? | Primary Role | |---|---|---|---| | **SBUF** | **99H** | No | Holds the byte to be transmitted or the byte just received | | **SCON** | **98H** | **Yes** | Controls serial port mode, reception enable, and flags | | **PCON** | **87H** | No | Contains the **SMOD** bit (bit 7) for baud rate doubling | | **TMOD** | **89H** | No | Configures Timer 1 in **Mode 2** for baud rate generation | | **TH1** | **8DH** | No | Holds the auto-reload value defining the baud rate | | **TL1** | **8BH** | No | The actual timer counter (auto-reloaded from TH1) | | **IE** | **A8H** | **Yes** | Enables the Serial Port Interrupt (ES bit, IE.4) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 SCON Register — The Serial Control Hub The **SCON (98H)** register is the master control block of the 8051's serial port. It is bit-addressable and reset to **00H** on power-up. | Bit | Name | Function | |---|---|---| | **7** | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 | | **6** | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 | | **5** | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable (set to 0 in Mode 1 for normal use) | | **4** | **REN** | **Receive Enable** — must be set to **1** to allow reception | | **3** | **TB8** | 9th transmitted bit (used in Modes 2 & 3) | | **2** | **RB8** | 9th received bit (used in Modes 2 & 3) | | **1** | **TI** | **Transmit Interrupt Flag** — set by hardware at end of transmission; **must be cleared by software** | | **0** | **RI** | **Receive Interrupt Flag** — set by hardware at end of reception; **must be cleared by software** | ### The Four Serial Modes (SM0, SM1) | SM0 | SM1 | Mode | Description | Baud Rate Source | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 8-bit Shift Register | Fixed: **Oscillator / 12** | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | **8-bit UART (Standard)** | **Timer 1 Overflow (variable)** | | 1 | 0 | Mode 2 | 9-bit UART | Fixed: **Oscillator / 32** or **/64** | | 1 | 1 | Mode 3 | 9-bit UART | **Timer 1 Overflow (variable)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Focus:** Over 95% of KTU exam problems on serial port programming use **Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1)**. The standard SCON value for **transmission** is **0x40 (01000000B)** and for **reception** is **0x50 (01010000B)** — the only difference is that REN is set in the latter. ## 2.2 SBUF Register — The Dual-Buffer Architecture Physically, the 8051 contains **two separate 8-bit registers** that share the single SFR address **99H**: - **Writing** to SBUF → loads the **Transmit Buffer** (data is shifted out via TxD). - **Reading** from SBUF → reads the **Receive Buffer** (data assembled from RxD). This dual-buffer design allows **full-duplex** communication (simultaneous transmit and receive). ## 2.3 PCON Register — The SMOD Bit Only bit 7 of PCON is relevant here. When **SMOD = 1**, the baud rate is **doubled** (multiplied by 2). When **SMOD = 0** (the default after reset), the standard formula applies. ## 2.4 Timer 1 Configuration for Baud Rate Generation To generate a stable baud rate, Timer 1 must be configured in **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)**. The steps are: 1. Set the **C/T̄** bit of TMOD to **0** (timer mode, not counter mode). 2. Set the **M1, M0** bits of TMOD to **1, 0** respectively (Mode 2). 3. Load **TH1** with the calculated reload value. 4. Start the timer by setting the **TR1** bit in the TCON register. 5. Ensure **TF1** is cleared. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains **every formula** you need for KTU 2024 serial port numerical problems. Memorize the two boxed equations — they appear in almost every university exam question. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Timer 1 Overflow Rate | $f_{ov} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Overflows per second (Hz) | | 2 | **Baud Rate (General)** | $\boxed{Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times f_{ov}}$ | Bits per second (bps) | | 3 | **Baud Rate (Expanded)** | $\boxed{Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}}$ | The master equation for all problems | | 4 | TH1 (SMOD = 0) | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$ | Most common case | | 5 | TH1 (SMOD = 1) | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{192 \times Baud}$ | When baud rate doubling is used | | 6 | SMOD Bit Value | Located at **PCON.7** | 0 = Normal, 1 = Doubled | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Rendering Note:** In the table above, the absolute value bars around $f_{ov}$ and the frequency $f_{osc}$ use the word-spelled form (\vert). When copying into handwritten KTU answer sheets, restore them to standard vertical bars $\vert \cdot \vert$. ## 2.6 Reference Table — TH1 Values for 11.0592 MHz Crystal | Baud Rate | SMOD | TH1 (Decimal) | TH1 (Hex) | Error | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1200 | 0 | 232 | **E8H** | 0% | | 2400 | 0 | 244 | **F4H** | 0% | | 4800 | 0 | 250 | **FAH** | 0% | | **9600** | 0 | 253 | **FDH** | **0%** | | 19200 | 0 | ? | ? | (Non-integer — use 1200 with SMOD=1) | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Serial port programming is the **foundation of all embedded device communication**. It is used in: - **GPS modules** (NMEA 0183 standard, 4800 baud, 8N1). - **GSM/Bluetooth modules** (HC-05, SIM800 — 9600 baud AT-command interface). - **PC ↔ Microcontroller debugging interfaces** (Arduino bootloader, ISP programmers). - **Industrial sensor networks** (Modbus RTU protocol over RS-485). - **Inter-MCU communication** in multi-chip embedded systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the 8051 Baud Rate Equation The 8051 baud rate is derived from the fundamental relationship between the oscillator, the timer, and the UART hardware divider. ### Step 1: Timer 1 Machine Cycle The 8051 divides the external oscillator frequency by 12 to generate the **machine cycle**, which is the basic unit of time for all on-chip timers. $$f_{machine} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$$ ### Step 2: Timer 1 Overflow Frequency In Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), Timer 1 counts from the value in **TH1** up to **255**, then overflows to **0** on the next pulse and reloads **TH1** automatically. The number of timer ticks per overflow is therefore: $$N_{ticks} = 256 - TH1$$ The overflow frequency is the machine cycle frequency divided by the number of ticks per overflow: $$f_{ov} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ ### Step 3: UART Hardware Divider The 8051 UART hardware internally divides the timer overflow frequency by **32** to generate the bit-shift clock. If the **SMOD** bit is set, the divisor becomes **16** (effectively multiplying the baud rate by 2). $$Baud = \dfrac{f_{ov}}{32} \times 2^{SMOD} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{ov}}{32}$$ ### Step 4: Combining the Equations Substituting the expression for $f_{ov}$ from Step 2 into the equation in Step 3 yields the **master baud rate formula**: $$Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ > This is the single most important equation for the entire topic. Every numerical problem in KTU Module 4 reduces to manipulating this formula. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Finding TH1 for 9600 Baud **Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal and requires **9600 baud** with **SMOD = 0**. Calculate the TH1 value. ### Step-by-Step Solution Given: - $f_{osc} = 11{,}059{,}200$ Hz - $Baud = 9600$ bps - $SMOD = 0 \Rightarrow 2^{SMOD} = 1$ Rearranging the master formula to solve for $(256 - TH1)$: $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times Baud}$$ Substituting numerical values: $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{1 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600}$$ Computing the denominator: $$384 \times 9600 = 3{,}686{,}400$$ Performing the division: $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{3{,}686{,}400} = 3$$ Therefore: $$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH}$$ **Verification (error check):** $$Baud_{actual} = \dfrac{1 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 3} = \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{1{,}152} = 9600 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$ **Error = 0%** — confirming why 11.0592 MHz is the universal KTU exam crystal frequency. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Doubling Baud Rate with SMOD **Problem:** Same 8051 system as above, but the design now requires **19200 baud**. Find the TH1 value, assuming **SMOD = 1**. Rearranging for $(256 - TH1)$ with $2^{SMOD} = 2$: $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{2 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 19200}$$ Simplifying: $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{22{,}118{,}400}{7{,}372{,}800} = 3$$ Therefore: $$TH1 = 253 = \text{FDH}$$ **Conclusion:** Setting **SMOD = 1** doubles the baud rate from 9600 to 19200 *without changing TH1*. This is a frequent KTU trick question. ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Finding the Resulting Baud Rate **Problem:** TH1 = 0xFA (250), $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, SMOD = 0. Calculate the baud rate. Substituting into the master formula: $$Baud = \dfrac{1 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times (256 - 250)}$$ $$Baud = \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 6} = \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{2{,}304} = 4800 \text{ bps} \checkmark$$ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Calculation Scenarios | Scenario | Known | Find | Key Formula Inversion | |---|---|---|---| | Find TH1 | $f_{osc}$, Baud, SMOD | TH1 | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$ | | Find Baud | $f_{osc}$, TH1, SMOD | Baud | $Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | | Find $f_{osc}$ | TH1, Baud, SMOD | $f_{osc}$ | $f_{osc} = \dfrac{384 \times Baud \times (256 - TH1)}{2^{SMOD}}$ | | Find % Error | Computed Baud vs. Standard | Error | $Error \% = \dfrac{\vert Baud_{actual} - Baud_{ideal} \vert}{Baud_{ideal}} \times 100$ | > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check Rule:** The value $(256 - TH1)$ must always be a **positive integer** between 1 and 255. If you obtain a non-integer, the crystal frequency cannot perfectly produce that baud rate — this is why 11.0592 MHz dominates KTU problems. ## 3.6 The Two-Flag Polling Sequence (Why TI and RI Are Not Auto-Cleared) A common KTU conceptual question is: *"Why must the programmer clear TI and RI manually?"* The answer lies in the hardware architecture: 1. When the last stop bit of a transmitted byte is shifted out, the hardware **sets TI = 1** to signal completion. 2. The hardware has **no mechanism** to clear TI because the same flag must remain set until the software has dispatched the next byte — clearing it prematurely would corrupt the next transmission. 3. The **standard practice** is to clear TI immediately after detecting it in the polling loop, *before* loading the next byte into SBUF. The identical logic applies to **RI** on the receive side. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — 8051 Internal UART Architecture The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the internal data flow of the 8051's serial port subsystem, showing the relationship between the CPU, the dual SBUF, the SCON control register, Timer 1, and the external TxD/RxD pins. ```mermaid flowchart LR cpu1[CPU CORE] sbufTx[TRANSMIT SBUF
99H Write] sbufRx[RECEIVE SBUF
99H Read] scon1[SCON REGISTER
98H SM0 SM1 REN TI RI] tmod1[TMOD REGISTER
89H Timer 1 Config] th1reg[TH1 RELOAD
8DH] tl1reg[TL1 COUNTER
8BH] tr1bit[TR1 BIT
TCON.6] pcon1[PCON.7 SMOD BIT] txd1[TXD PIN P3.1] rxd1[RXD PIN P3.0] osci1[OSCILLATOR
11.0592 MHz] div1[DIVIDE BY 12] div2[DIVIDE BY 32 OR 16] shift1[SHIFT REGISTER CLOCK] cpu1 -- write byte --> sbufTx sbufTx --> shift1 shift1 --> txd1 rxd1 --> shift1 shift1 --> sbufRx sbufRx -- read byte --> cpu1 scon1 -- controls --> shift1 scon1 -- sets TI --> cpu1 scon1 -- sets RI --> cpu1 osci1 --> div1 div1 --> tl1reg th1reg -- auto reload --> tl1reg tl1reg -- overflow --> div2 pcon1 -- selects /32 or /16 --> div2 div2 --> shift1 tr1bit -- enables --> tl1reg tmod1 -- configures --> th1reg tmod1 -- configures --> tl1reg ``` ## 4.2 Asynchronous Serial Frame Format (Mode 1) In Mode 1, every transmitted byte is encapsulated in a **10-bit frame** consisting of a start bit, eight data bits (LSB first), and a stop bit. The line idles HIGH (logic 1) between frames. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[IDLE LINE
HIGH 1] --> B1[START BIT
LOW 0] B1 --> C1[DATA BIT D0
LSB FIRST] C1 --> D1[DATA BIT D1] D1 --> E1[DATA BIT D2] E1 --> F1[DATA BIT D3] F1 --> G1[DATA BIT D4] G1 --> H1[DATA BIT D5] H1 --> I1[DATA BIT D6] I1 --> J1[DATA BIT D7
MSB] J1 --> K1[STOP BIT
HIGH 1] K1 --> A1 ``` ## 4.3 Algorithm Flowchart — Transmitting a Byte The standard polling-based transmission sequence is captured below. The CPU writes the data byte to SBUF, then enters a busy-wait loop until the TI flag becomes 1, after which it clears TI and proceeds. ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA1[START] stepA2[Initialize SCON to 0x40
SM0 0 SM1 1 REN 0] stepA3[Configure TMOD Timer 1
in Mode 2] stepA4[Load TH1 with reload value] stepA5[Set TR1 to 1
Start Timer 1] stepA6[Load data byte into SBUF] stepA7{TI flag equals 1?} stepA8[Wait for TI to be set
by hardware at end of frame] stepA9[Clear TI flag
by software] stepA10[Transmission complete
Ready for next byte] stepA11[END] stepA1 --> stepA2 stepA2 --> stepA3 stepA3 --> stepA4 stepA4 --> stepA5 stepA5 --> stepA6 stepA6 --> stepA7 stepA7 -- NO --> stepA8 stepA8 --> stepA7 stepA7 -- YES --> stepA9 stepA9 --> stepA10 stepA10 --> stepA11 ``` ## 4.4 Algorithm Flowchart — Receiving a Byte The reception sequence is similar but with the additional requirement of enabling REN and waiting for the RI flag. The received byte is then read from SBUF and RI is cleared. ```mermaid flowchart TD stepB1[START] stepB2[Initialize SCON to 0x50
SM0 0 SM1 1 REN 1] stepB3[Configure TMOD Timer 1
in Mode 2] stepB4[Load TH1 with reload value] stepB5[Set TR1 to 1
Start Timer 1] stepB6{RI flag equals 1?} stepB7[Hardware assembles byte
in RxD and sets RI] stepB8[Read received byte
from SBUF] stepB9[Clear RI flag
by software] stepB10[Process received byte] stepB11[END] stepB1 --> stepB2 stepB2 --> stepB3 stepB3 --> stepB4 stepB4 --> stepB5 stepB5 --> stepB6 stepB6 -- NO --> stepB7 stepB7 --> stepB6 stepB6 -- YES --> stepB8 stepB8 --> stepB9 stepB9 --> stepB10 stepB10 --> stepB11 ``` ## 4.5 Complete Program — Polling-Based Transmit "A" Continuously The following 8051 assembly program transmits the ASCII character 'A' (41H) repeatedly. Each step is mapped to the flowchart in Section 4.3. ```mermaid flowchart TD p1[ORG 0000H] p2[MOV SCON 0x40] p3[MOV TMOD 0x20] p4[MOV TH1 0xFD] p5[SETB TR1] p6[MOV SBUF A] p7[WAIT: JNB TI WAIT] p8[CLR TI] p9[SJMP BACK] p1 --> p2 p2 --> p3 p3 --> p4 p4 --> p5 p5 --> p6 p6 --> p7 p7 -- TI 0 --> p7 p7 -- TI 1 --> p8 p8 --> p9 p9 --> p6 ``` | Line | Mnemonic | Comment | |---|---|---| | 1 | `MOV SCON, #40H` | Mode 1, REN = 0 (transmit only) | | 2 | `MOV TMOD, #20H` | Timer 1 in Mode 2 (auto-reload) | | 3 | `MOV TH1, #0FDH` | Reload value for 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz | | 4 | `SETB TR1` | Start Timer 1 | | 5 | `MOV SBUF, #'A'` | Load ASCII 'A' into transmit buffer | | 6 | `HERE: JNB TI, HERE` | Wait until TI = 1 (transmission complete) | | 7 | `CLR TI` | Clear TI for next transmission | | 8 | `SJMP BACK` | Loop back to send again | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] > **Explain the role of the SCON register in 8051 serial communication. List the functions of SM0, SM1, REN, TI, and RI bits.** **Model Answer:** The **SCON (Serial Control, address 98H)** register is an 8-bit, bit-addressable Special Function Register that controls the operating mode, reception, and interrupt flags of the 8051's on-chip UART. Its five key bits perform the following roles: - **SM0, SM1 (bits 7, 6):** *Serial Mode Select* bits. Together they select one of four UART modes. The combination **SM0 = 0, SM1 = 1** selects **Mode 1**, which is the standard 8-bit variable-baud-rate UART used in virtually all KTU exam problems. - **REN (bit 4):** *Receive Enable*. Must be set to **1** by software to allow the UART to accept incoming data on the RxD pin. Cleared to 0 in transmit-only applications. - **TI (bit 1):** *Transmit Interrupt flag*. Automatically set to 1 by hardware when the last stop bit of a transmitted byte is shifted out. **Must be cleared by software** before the next transmission. - **RI (bit 0):** *Receive Interrupt flag*. Automatically set to 1 by hardware when a complete byte has been received and latched into the SBUF receive buffer. **Must be cleared by software** before the next reception is acknowledged. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] > **Why is the crystal frequency 11.0592 MHz preferred over 12 MHz for 8051 serial communication?** **Model Answer:** The crystal frequency **11.0592 MHz** is preferred for 8051 serial communication because it is an **integer multiple** of all standard baud rates when divided by the standard UART hardware divisor of 384. Specifically: $$\dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{384} = 28{,}800$$ This value is evenly divisible by 9600, 4800, 2400, and 1200, producing **exact integer TH1 reload values** (253, 250, 244, 232 respectively). In contrast, the commonly available 12 MHz crystal yields non-integer values such as TH1 = 252.745 for 9600 baud, which would have to be rounded to either 253 or 252, introducing a **baud rate error of up to 8.5%**. Such an error causes corrupted data in standard serial communication. Hence, 11.0592 MHz is the **de facto standard** for 8051 UART-based designs and is the assumed frequency in all KTU numerical problems unless stated otherwise. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### KTU ESE Module Internal Choice > *(Answer ONE full question from this pair)* --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024] > **(a)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal and needs to communicate at **2400 baud** in **Mode 1**. Calculate the required **TH1 value** for **SMOD = 0** and **SMOD = 1**. Show all derivation steps. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Write a complete **8051 assembly program** to transmit the byte stored in **internal RAM location 30H** to a PC, and then continuously transmit the message **"HELLO"**. Describe the role of each instruction. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Given:** - $f_{osc} = 11{,}059{,}200$ Hz - $Baud = 2400$ bps - Mode 1, Timer 1 in Mode 2 **Case 1: SMOD = 0** ($2^{SMOD} = 1$) Rearranging the master baud rate formula: $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$$ Substituting: $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{1 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 2400}$$ $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{921{,}600} = 12$$ $$\boxed{TH1 = 256 - 12 = 244 = 0\text{xF4H}}$$ **Case 2: SMOD = 1** ($2^{SMOD} = 2$) $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{2 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 2400}$$ $$256 - TH1 = \dfrac{22{,}118{,}400}{921{,}600} = 24$$ $$\boxed{TH1 = 256 - 24 = 232 = 0\text{xE8H}}$$ **Conclusion:** Setting **SMOD = 1** doubles the effective baud rate and requires a **smaller** TH1 value (a faster timer overflow). In this configuration, 2400 baud with SMOD=0 is equivalent in timing to 4800 baud with SMOD=1. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Algorithm:** 1. Initialize SCON for Mode 1, reception disabled (SCON = 40H). 2. Configure Timer 1 in Mode 2 with TH1 = 0F4H (from part a). 3. Start Timer 1. 4. Load the byte from RAM location 30H into SBUF. 5. Wait for TI = 1, then clear TI. 6. Loop through the five bytes of "HELLO" ('H', 'E', 'L', 'L', 'O'), transmitting each in sequence. **8051 Assembly Program:** | Line | Instruction | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | `ORG 0000H` | Set program origin | | 2 | `MOV SCON, #40H` | Mode 1, REN = 0 (transmit only) | | 3 | `MOV TMOD, #20H` | Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) | | 4 | `MOV TH1, #0F4H` | Reload value for 2400 baud, SMOD=0 | | 5 | `SETB TR1` | Start Timer 1 | | 6 | `MOV A, 30H` | Load byte from internal RAM 30H | | 7 | `MOV SBUF, A` | Transmit the byte | | 8 | `WAIT1: JNB TI, WAIT1` | Wait for transmission to complete | | 9 | `CLR TI` | Clear TI flag for next byte | | 10 | `MOV DPTR, #MSG` | Point DPTR to "HELLO" string | | 11 | `LOOP: CLR A` | Clear accumulator | | 12 | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Fetch character from code memory | | 13 | `JZ END_TX` | If null terminator, stop | | 14 | `MOV SBUF, A` | Transmit character | | 15 | `WAIT2: JNB TI, WAIT2` | Wait for transmission | | 16 | `CLR TI` | Clear TI flag | | 17 | `INC DPTR` | Advance to next character | | 18 | `SJMP LOOP` | Repeat | | 19 | `END_TX: SJMP END_TX` | Infinite loop to halt | | 20 | `MSG: DB 'HELLO', 0` | Null-terminated string | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice] > **(a)** Explain the **complete pin and signal flow** of 8051 serial communication. Identify the role of **pins P3.0 (RxD) and P3.1 (TxD)**, and the **internal operation of SBUF and SCON** during a single byte transmission in Mode 1. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A 8051 system has $f_{osc} = 11.0592$ MHz, TH1 = 0xFD, and SMOD = 0. Calculate the **actual baud rate** and the **percentage error** with respect to the standard 9600 baud. Justify why this error is exactly 0%. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) The 8051 serial communication in **Mode 1** operates as follows: **External Pin Functions:** - **P3.0 (RxD):** *Receive Data* pin — input line for incoming serial frames from an external device (e.g., a PC's COM port or a Bluetooth module). - **P3.1 (TxD):** *Transmit Data* pin — output line that shifts out the serial frame one bit at a time at the programmed baud rate. The line idles HIGH. **Internal Operation (Step-by-Step for One Transmitted Byte):** 1. The CPU writes a data byte to **SBUF (99H)** via the `MOV SBUF, A` instruction. 2. The byte is loaded into the **transmit shift register** (one of the two physical SBUF registers). 3. The UART hardware transmits a **start bit (0)**, followed by the **8 data bits (LSB first)**, and finally a **stop bit (1)** — a total of 10 bits per frame. 4. The bit-shift clock is derived from **Timer 1's overflow rate** divided by 32 (or 16 if SMOD=1). 5. When the last stop bit is shifted out, the hardware sets the **TI flag (SCON.1) to 1**. 6. The CPU detects TI = 1 by polling (`JNB TI, $`) or via the serial interrupt vector at 0023H. 7. The CPU **clears TI by software** to prepare for the next transmission. For reception, the process is mirrored: REN must be enabled, the hardware monitors RxD for a falling edge (start bit), samples the line at the bit center for each of the 8 data bits, latches the byte into the receive SBUF, and sets RI to 1. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - $f_{osc} = 11{,}059{,}200$ Hz - $TH1 = 0\text{xFD} = 253$ (decimal) - $SMOD = 0$ **Step 1: Compute $(256 - TH1)$** $$256 - 253 = 3$$ **Step 2: Apply the Baud Rate Formula** $$Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ $$Baud = \dfrac{1 \times 11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 3}$$ $$Baud = \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{1{,}152} = 9{,}600 \text{ bps}$$ **Step 3: Compute the Percentage Error** $$Error\% = \dfrac{\vert Baud_{actual} - Baud_{ideal} \vert}{Baud_{ideal}} \times 100\%$$ $$Error\% = \dfrac{\vert 9600 - 9600 \vert}{9600} \times 100\% = 0\%$$ **Justification:** The error is exactly 0% because the value $(256 - TH1) = 3$ is an **integer** that produces a clean division. This is a direct consequence of choosing the crystal frequency **11.0592 MHz**, which is the smallest integer multiple of 9600 that satisfies the 8051 hardware divider chain. Specifically: $$11{,}059{,}200 = 9600 \times 1152 = 9600 \times 384 \times 3$$ All terms divide evenly, so no fractional rounding is required. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to clear TI/RI by software:** This is the **#1 mistake** in KTU 8051 serial port answers. Hardware sets the flag, but **software must clear it**. Failing to mention this loses 1–2 marks per occurrence. > 2. **Using 12 MHz instead of 11.0592 MHz:** If a problem states an unusual crystal, do not assume 11.0592 MHz. **Read the question carefully.** > 3. **Confusing SBUF read vs. write:** Writing to SBUF loads the *transmit* buffer; reading from SBUF reads the *receive* buffer. The 8051 distinguishes them automatically, but students often write "`MOV SBUF, A` to receive" — an immediate 2-mark penalty. > 4. **Forgetting to set TR1:** Initializing TMOD and TH1 is useless until **TR1 (TCON.6) is set to 1** to start the timer. Many students omit the `SETB TR1` line. > 5. **Setting SM2 = 1 in Mode 1:** The SM2 bit is used for multiprocessor communication in Modes 2 and 3. In Mode 1, it must be **cleared to 0**, or reception will be disabled. > 6. **Wrong polarity in the frame diagram:** When drawing the 10-bit frame, remember: **start bit = 0**, **stop bit = 1**, **idle line = 1**. Drawing them reversed costs full marks in diagram questions. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Serial Port Programming** - [ ] The 8051 has **one on-chip UART** with **two physical SBUF registers** sharing SFR address **99H** — one for transmit (write), one for receive (read). - [ ] **SCON (98H)** is bit-addressable and contains the mode bits (SM0, SM1), REN, and the two flags TI and RI. - [ ] **Mode 1 (SM0=0, SM1=1)** is the standard 8-bit variable-baud-rate UART used in 10/10 KTU problems. - [ ] **SCON = 0x40** for transmit-only; **SCON = 0x50** for transmit+receive (only difference is REN). - [ ] **TI (SCON.1)** is set by hardware at end of transmission; **must be cleared by software**. - [ ] **RI (SCON.0)** is set by hardware at end of reception; **must be cleared by software**. - [ ] **Timer 1 Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload) is mandatory for baud rate generation. - [ ] **TMOD = 0x20** configures Timer 1 in Mode 2. - [ ] **Master Baud Rate Formula:** $$Baud = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \times f_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ - [ ] **TH1 Quick Reference (11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0):** 9600 → 0xFD, 4800 → 0xFA, 2400 → 0xF4, 1200 → 0xE8. - [ ] **SMOD bit is PCON.7**; setting it to 1 **doubles the baud rate** without changing TH1. - [ ] **P3.0 = RxD** (input), **P3.1 = TxD** (output); line idles HIGH. - [ ] **Mode 1 frame = 1 start bit + 8 data bits (LSB first) + 1 stop bit = 10 bits total.** - [ ] The standard crystal is **11.0592 MHz** — not 12 MHz — because it yields 0% baud rate error for all common rates. - [ ] To transmit: `MOV SBUF, A` then `JNB TI, $` then `CLR TI`. - [ ] To receive: enable REN, wait `JB RI, $`, read with `MOV A, SBUF`, then `CLR RI`. - [ ] The serial port interrupt vector is at address **0023H** (used in interrupt-driven mode). - [ ] **Mode 0** is a fixed baud rate of $f_{osc}/12$ — used for I/O expansion via shift registers, rarely in KTU problems. - [ ] **Modes 2 and 3** are 9-bit UARTs used in multiprocessor communication (SM2 bit is relevant here). - [ ] **Maximum 8051 baud rate** is limited by the crystal — with 11.0592 MHz and SMOD=1, the highest reliable standard rate is 19200 bps. - [ ] **Full-duplex** operation is possible because SBUF has separate physical transmit and receive registers.

topics.title: Programming the Interrupts - External, Timer and Serial Port Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programming the Interrupts - External, Timer and Serial Port Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Interrupt** is a hardware- or software-driven asynchronous event that temporarily suspends the main program execution of the microcontroller, transfers control to a dedicated routine called an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**, executes the ISR, and then returns control back to the main program at the exact point where it was interrupted, using the saved **Program Counter (PC)** on the stack.

In the **MCS-51 (8051) family**, the interrupt system is a **vectored, prioritized, maskable hardware mechanism** with **five (5) standard interrupt sources** and a fixed 8-byte code space reserved for each ISR starting at the **base address 0003H** of program memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic is a direct sub-module of *Module 4 – 8051 Interrupts & Programming*. Students must master interrupt enable/disable bits, vector addresses, and assembly/C-language ISR writing for KTU ESE and internal evaluations.

---

## 1.2 The Three Categories of 8051 Interrupts (Scope of This Module)

| # | Category | Source SFR | Flag Bit | Vector Address | Pin / Hardware |
|---|----------|------------|----------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **External Interrupts** | TCON | IE0, IE1 | 0003H, 0013H | P3.2 (INT0), P3.3 (INT1) |
| 2 | **Timer Interrupts** | TCON | TF0, TF1 | 000BH, 001BH | Internal (Timer 0, Timer 1 overflow) |
| 3 | **Serial Port Interrupt** | SCON | TI, RI | 0023H | Internal (UART Tx/Rx completion) |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doorbell" Model

Imagine you are cooking dinner (your **main program**) in the kitchen. Suddenly:
- The **doorbell rings** → a guest has arrived (this is like an **External Interrupt**).
- The **oven timer beeps** → your cake is ready (this is like a **Timer Interrupt**).
- The **microwave displays "DONE"** → cooking is finished (this is like a **Serial Port Interrupt** — transmission complete).

In all three cases, you:
1. **Pause** your current work (Context Save).
2. **Note the current step** in the recipe (Push PC onto stack).
3. **Attend** to the new event (Execute ISR).
4. **Return** to the exact step where you left off (RETI instruction).

> [!NOTE]
> Without interrupts, the CPU would have to constantly "poll" (keep asking) "Is the doorbell pressed? Is the timer done?" — wasting enormous amounts of processing time. Interrupts allow **event-driven, power-efficient** embedded design.

---

## 1.4 Physical & Timing Constants to Remember

> The 8051's standard crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz** (chosen specifically because it divides evenly to give exact standard **Baud Rates** like 9600, 4800, 1200 for serial communication).

- **Machine Cycle Period** = **12 / f_osc** = 12 / 11.0592 × 10⁶ = **1.0851 µs**
- One timer "tick" = **1.0851 µs**
- Timer 16-bit (Mode 1) full range = 0000H to FFFFH = **65,536 counts** = **71.11 ms maximum delay** at 11.0592 MHz.
- **8 bytes** of code memory are reserved between consecutive interrupt vectors (e.g., 0003H–000AH, 000BH–0012H, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 Interrupt Architecture — Operational Logic

The 8051 interrupt system uses three Special Function Registers (SFRs) to control its behavior:

### A. The **IE** Register (Interrupt Enable) — Address **A8H** (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Default | 0 | x | x | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- **EA** (Enable All) = Global master switch. **Must be set to 1** for any interrupt to be recognized.
- **ES** = Serial Port interrupt enable
- **ET1 / ET0** = Timer 1 / Timer 0 interrupt enable
- **EX1 / EX0** = External Interrupt 1 / 0 enable

### B. The **IP** Register (Interrupt Priority) — Address **B8H** (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
| Default | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- Setting a bit to **1** assigns **HIGH** priority to that source.
- **High-priority interrupts can interrupt low-priority ISRs** (nested interrupts). Low-priority interrupts cannot interrupt high-priority ISRs.
- A high-priority ISR, once started, **cannot itself be interrupted** by another interrupt of the same priority level.

### C. The **TCON** Register (Timer/Counter Control) — Address **88H** (Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |
| Default | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

- **IT0 / IT1** (Interrupt Type): 0 = **Level-Triggered** (low level), 1 = **Edge-Triggered** (falling edge).
- **IE0 / IE1** (Interrupt Edge): Auto-set by hardware on edge; auto-cleared when ISR is entered.
- For level-triggered mode, the **external source must hold the pin low until the CPU acknowledges** the interrupt, then release it before the ISR ends, or the interrupt will be re-entered.

---

## 2.2 Interrupt Vector Table (KTU High-Yield Memory Map)

The 8051 reserves the lower 46 bytes of program memory for interrupts. After **RESET**, the CPU starts at **0000H**. Therefore, a "main jump" instruction (e.g., `LJMP MAIN`) is **always** placed at address 0000H.

| Address | Bytes Allocated | Interrupt Source |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| 0000H | — | **Reset** (CPU starts here) |
| 0003H | 8 bytes | **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** |
| 000BH | 8 bytes | **Timer 0 Overflow** |
| 0013H | 8 bytes | **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)** |
| 001BH | 8 bytes | **Timer 1 Overflow** |
| 0023H | 8 bytes | **Serial Port (TI / RI)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because only **8 bytes** are available between vectors, ISRs longer than 8 bytes **must** end with an `LJMP` (or `SJMP`) instruction to a larger subroutine elsewhere in code memory. The ISR **must end with RETI** (not RET) — RETI re-enables the interrupt logic and pops PC.

---

## 2.3 Programming Steps — Generic Recipe for ANY 8051 Interrupt

1. **Place the main program starting address** at vector 0000H using `LJMP MAIN`.
2. **Place the ISR starting address** at the corresponding interrupt vector using `LJMP ISR_Name`.
3. **Configure the source hardware** (e.g., Timer mode in TMOD, Serial mode in SCON, edge/level in TCON).
4. **Load initial timer values** (THx, TLx) if using a timer.
5. **Start the source** (e.g., `SETB TR0` to start Timer 0).
6. **Enable the specific interrupt** bit (e.g., `SETB ET0`).
7. **Enable the global interrupt** (`SETB EA`).
8. **Write the ISR** ending with `RETI`.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---------------------|------------|-------|-------|
| Machine Cycle (MC) | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | Fixed by oscillator |
| Timer Tick Duration | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | µs | One increment of TCNTx |
| Total Delay | $T_{delay} = (65536 - N_{initial}) \times T_{tick}$ | seconds | For Mode 1 (16-bit) |
| Counts Needed | $N = \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_{tick}}$ | integer | Round up to next integer |
| Initial Value (Hex) | $N_{initial} = 65536 - N$ | decimal | Convert to hex → THx, TLx |
| Reload Formula (auto-reload Mode 2) | $TH = 256 - N_{counts}$ | hex | 8-bit auto-reload |
| Serial Baud @ Timer 1 Mode 2 | $Baud = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | bps | TH1 reload value |
| TH1 for standard 9600 baud | $TH1 = 253 = 0xFD$ | hex | f_osc = 11.0592 MHz |
| Max Mode 1 delay @ 11.0592 MHz | $(65536 - 0) \times 1.0851 \,\mu s$ | ms | ≈ **71.11 ms** |
| Max Mode 2 delay @ 11.0592 MHz | $255 \times 1.0851 \,\mu s$ | µs | ≈ **276.7 µs** |

> [!NOTE]
> When calculating `N` for a desired delay, always **round up** to the next integer. If the count would exceed 65536 (Mode 1), you must use **software counting** (e.g., repeat the overflow ISR 10 times for 700 ms).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **External Interrupts** are used for: emergency stop buttons, motor encoders, zero-crossing detectors, keypad wake-up.
- **Timer Interrupts** are used for: real-time clocks, periodic sampling in data loggers, PWM generation, multitasking schedulers (RTOS tick).
- **Serial Port Interrupts** are used for: GPS module data reception, Bluetooth (HC-05) communication, GSM modem AT-command handling, sensor networks — **without interrupts, the CPU would waste cycles polling the RI flag**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 EXTERNAL INTERRUPT Programming — Worked Example

### Problem Statement (KTU-Style)
> Interface a switch to **P3.2 (INT0)** of an 8051. Write an 8051 C program using **Keil** syntax that toggles **P1.0** every time the switch is pressed. Configure the interrupt as **edge-triggered (falling edge)**.

### Model Solution (Embedded C — Keil Syntax)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;          // LED connected to P1.0

void external0_isr(void) interrupt 0   // Vector 0 → External Interrupt 0
{
    LED = ~LED;             // Toggle LED
}

void main(void)
{
    IT0 = 1;                // Configure INT0 as edge-triggered (falling)
    EX0 = 1;                // Enable External Interrupt 0
    EA  = 1;                // Enable Global Interrupt
    LED = 0;                // Initial state — LED OFF
    while(1);               // Idle forever — wait for interrupt
}
```

### Key Concept Explanation
- The keyword `interrupt 0` in Keil C is a compiler directive that auto-generates the `LJMP` to address **0003H** and places a `RETI` at the end of the function.
- The number after `interrupt` is the **interrupt number** (0 = INT0, 1 = T0, 2 = INT1, 3 = T1, 4 = Serial).

### Equivalent Assembly Language Version

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN        ; Jump over ISR vector space

        ORG     0003H       ; Vector address for INT0
        LJMP    EXT0_ISR

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     SP, #60H
        SETB    IT0         ; Edge-triggered
        SETB    EX0         ; Enable EX0
        SETB    EA          ; Global enable
        CLR     P1.0
HERE:   SJMP    HERE         ; Infinite idle loop

EXT0_ISR:
        CPL     P1.0        ; Toggle P1.0
        RETI                ; Return and re-enable
        END
```

---

## 3.2 TIMER INTERRUPT Programming — Worked Example with Full Derivation

### Problem Statement
> Using **Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit)**, generate an interrupt every **50 ms** on an 8051 system. The crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz**. In the ISR, toggle **P1.7**. After 20 toggles, the LED must be OFF.

### Step 1: Calculate the Machine Cycle

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.0851 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 2: Calculate the Required Number of Timer Counts

$$
N_{counts} = \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0851 \times 10^{-6}} = 46{,}080
$$

### Step 3: Calculate the Initial Timer Value

$$
N_{initial} = 65{,}536 - N_{counts} = 65{,}536 - 46{,}080 = 19{,}456
$$

### Step 4: Convert 19,456 to Hexadecimal

$$
19{,}456 \div 4096 = 4 \text{ remainder } 3{,}072
$$
$$
3{,}072 \div 256 = 12 \text{ remainder } 0
$$
$$
19{,}456 = 4 \times 4096 + 12 \times 256 + 0 = 0x4C00
$$

Therefore: **TH0 = 0x4C**, **TL0 = 0x00**.

### Step 5: Verify the Calculation

$$
T_{actual} = (65{,}536 - 19{,}456) \times 1.0851 \,\mu s = 46{,}080 \times 1.0851 \,\mu s = 50.000 \, ms \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step 6: Full Embedded C Program (Keil)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^7;
unsigned char count = 0;

void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1   // Vector 1 → Timer 0 Overflow
{
    TH0 = 0x4C;             // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x00;             // Reload low byte
    count++;
    if(count == 20)         // 20 × 50 ms = 1 second total
    {
        LED = ~LED;         // Toggle
        count = 0;          // Reset counter
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;            // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0x4C;
    TL0  = 0x00;
    TR0  = 1;               // Start Timer 0
    ET0  = 1;               // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;               // Global enable
    LED  = 0;
    while(1);
}
```

---

## 3.3 SERIAL PORT INTERRUPT Programming — Worked Example

### Problem Statement
> Configure the 8051 serial port at **9600 baud, 8-N-1** using Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (auto-reload)**. Use interrupts so that received bytes are echoed back, and a transmitted byte triggers an LED blink on P1.0.

### Step 1: Calculate TH1 for 9600 Baud

$$
TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud} = 256 - \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{384 \times 9600} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

### Step 2: Configure SCON for Mode 1 (8-bit UART)

```c
SCON = 0x50;     // SM0=0, SM1=1 (Mode 1), REN=1 (Receive Enable)
```

### Step 3: Full Interrupt-Driven Serial Program

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED = P1^0;
unsigned char rx_data;

void serial_isr(void) interrupt 4   // Vector 4 → Serial Port
{
    if(RI)                // Received byte?
    {
        RI = 0;
        rx_data = SBUF;   // Read received byte
        SBUF = rx_data;   // Echo it back
    }
    if(TI)                // Transmission complete?
    {
        TI = 0;
        LED = ~LED;       // Blink LED on every TX completion
    }
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x20;          // Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0xFD;          // 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
    SCON = 0x50;          // Mode 1, REN enabled
    TR1  = 1;              // Start Timer 1
    ES   = 1;              // Enable serial interrupt
    EA   = 1;              // Global enable
    LED  = 0;
    while(1);
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **interrupt-driven serial I/O**, the flags `RI` and `TI` **must be cleared in software** by writing 0 to them — they are NOT cleared automatically when the ISR is entered (unlike the external interrupt edge flags IE0/IE1).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick-Reference)

| Feature | External Interrupt | Timer Interrupt | Serial Port Interrupt |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Trigger Source** | External pin (P3.2 / P3.3) | Internal timer overflow (TF0 / TF1) | TI or RI flag set |
| **Flag Bits** | IE0, IE1 (in TCON) | TF0, TF1 (in TCON) | TI, RI (in SCON) |
| **Auto-Clear?** | Yes (hardware clears on ISR entry) | Yes (hardware clears) | **No** (software must clear) |
| **Enable Bits** | EX0, EX1 | ET0, ET1 | ES |
| **Config Bits** | IT0, IT1 (edge/level) | TMOD (mode), TR0/TR1 (start) | SCON (mode), REN, TI/RI |
| **Vector Address** | 0003H, 0013H | 000BH, 001BH | 0023H |
| **Typical Use** | Switch, sensor, encoder | Delays, RTOS tick, PWM | UART communication |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Interrupt Handling Flow (Hardware-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CPU executes main program] --> B{Interrupt Occurred?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C{Check EA bit in IE register}
    C -- EA = 0 --> A
    C -- EA = 1 --> D{Check specific Enable bit e.g. ET0 EX0 ES}
    D -- Disabled --> A
    D -- Enabled --> E{Check Priority in IP register}
    E --> F{Wait for current instruction to complete}
    F --> G[Hardware pushes current PC onto Stack]
    G --> H[Hardware clears the interrupt flag if applicable]
    H --> I[PC loaded with Vector Address e.g. 000BH]
    I --> J[Execute ISR code]
    J --> K{RETI instruction reached?}
    K -- No --> J
    K -- Yes --> L[Pop PC from Stack]
    L --> M[Re-enable interrupt logic]
    M --> A
```

## 4.2 SFR Configuration Map for All Three Interrupts

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph IE_REGISTER [IE Register at A8H - Global and Source Enables]
        IE_EA[EA - Bit 7 - Global Master]
        IE_ES[ES - Bit 4 - Serial Port]
        IE_ET1[ET1 - Bit 3 - Timer 1]
        IE_EX1[EX1 - Bit 2 - External 1]
        IE_ET0[ET0 - Bit 1 - Timer 0]
        IE_EX0[EX0 - Bit 0 - External 0]
    end
    subgraph IP_REGISTER [IP Register at B8H - Priority Control]
        IP_PS[PS - Bit 4 - Serial]
        IP_PT1[PT1 - Bit 3 - Timer 1]
        IP_PX1[PX1 - Bit 2 - Ext 1]
        IP_PT0[PT0 - Bit 1 - Timer 0]
        IP_PX0[PX0 - Bit 0 - Ext 0]
    end
    subgraph VECTORS [Interrupt Vector Memory Map]
        V0[0000H - Reset]
        V1[0003H - External 0]
        V2[000BH - Timer 0]
        V3[0013H - External 1]
        V4[001BH - Timer 1]
        V5[0023H - Serial Port]
    end
    IE_EA --> IE_ES
    IE_EA --> IE_ET1
    IE_EA --> IE_ET0
    IE_EA --> IE_EX1
    IE_EA --> IE_EX0
    IE_ES --> V5
    IE_ET1 --> V4
    IE_EX1 --> V3
    IE_ET0 --> V2
    IE_EX0 --> V1
```

## 4.3 Programming Sequence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1: LJMP MAIN at 0000H] --> B[Step 2: LJMP ISR at vector]
    B --> C[Step 3: Configure source hardware]
    C --> D[Step 4: Load initial values]
    D --> E[Step 5: Start source TR0 TR1]
    E --> F[Step 6: Set specific enable bit]
    F --> G[Step 7: Set EA global enable]
    G --> H[Step 8: Write ISR ending with RETI]
    H --> I[Step 9: Main program idle loop]
```

## 4.4 Timer Reload Value Derivation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given f_osc and T_desired] --> B[Compute T_MC = 12 / f_osc]
    B --> C[Compute N_counts = T_desired / T_MC]
    C --> D{N_counts exceeds 65536?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use software counter in ISR]
    D -- No --> F[Compute N_initial = 65536 - N_counts]
    F --> G[Convert N_initial to hexadecimal]
    G --> H[Assign TH0 = High byte, TL0 = Low byte]
    H --> I[Verify T_actual calculation]
    I --> J[Program into TMOD, TH0, TL0, TR0, ET0, EA]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the role of the EA bit in the IE register of the 8051 microcontroller. What happens if EA = 0?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The **EA (Enable All)** bit is **bit 7** of the **IE register at address A8H**.
- It acts as the **global master switch** for the entire 8051 interrupt system.
- Even if individual source enable bits (EX0, ET0, ES, etc.) are set to 1, **no interrupt will be recognized** by the CPU unless EA = 1.
- When **EA = 0**, the interrupt hardware is **masked** — the CPU ignores all interrupt requests and continues execution of the main program.
- Setting EA = 1 enables the interrupt logic, allowing any properly enabled source to interrupt the CPU.
- (Valid alternative: 1 mark for the role, 1 mark for masking effect, 1 mark for consequences.)

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the five interrupt sources of the 8051 with their vector addresses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** → **0003H**
2. **Timer 0 Overflow (TF0)** → **000BH**
3. **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)** → **0013H**
4. **Timer 1 Overflow (TF1)** → **001BH**
5. **Serial Port Interrupt (TI / RI)** → **0023H**
