(½ mark each for source, ½ mark for vector, total 3 × 1 = 3 marks — acceptable distribution.)

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the structure of the IE (Interrupt Enable) register of the 8051. Describe how external and timer interrupts are enabled.**

**Model Solution:**

The **IE register** is located at **SFR address A8H** and is **bit-addressable**, meaning any individual bit can be set/cleared using bit-manipulation instructions.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Function | Global | Reserved | Reserved | Serial | Timer 1 | Ext 1 | Timer 0 | Ext 0 |

**Enabling procedure:**
1. Set the **specific source bit** (e.g., `SETB EX0` for External 0, `SETB ET0` for Timer 0).
2. Set the **global EA bit** (`SETB EA`).
3. If EA = 0, the source enable bits have no effect.

**Example assembly snippet:**

```asm
SETB    EX0        ; Enable External Interrupt 0
SETB    ET0        ; Enable Timer 0 Interrupt
SETB    EA         ; Global enable
```

(3 marks for diagram/table, 2 marks for individual enable bits, 2 marks for global enable explanation.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an 8051 C program to generate a square wave of 1 kHz frequency on pin P1.0 using Timer 0 interrupts. Assume f_osc = 11.0592 MHz.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Derivation of Timer Reload Value:**

A 1 kHz square wave has a period of $T = 1/1000 = 1000 \, \mu s$. Half-period = **500 µs**.

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.0851 \, \mu s
$$

$$
N_{counts} = \frac{500}{1.0851} = 460.8 \approx 461
$$

$$
N_{initial} = 65536 - 461 = 65075 = 0xFE33
$$

Therefore: **TH0 = 0xFE**, **TL0 = 0x33**.

**Step 2 — Embedded C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit WAVE = P1^0;

void timer0_isr(void) interrupt 1
{
    TH0 = 0xFE;          // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x33;          // Reload low byte
    WAVE = ~WAVE;        // Toggle P1.0
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;         // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
    TH0  = 0xFE;
    TL0  = 0x33;
    TR0  = 1;            // Start Timer 0
    ET0  = 1;            // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;            // Global enable
    while(1);
}
```

(3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for configuration, 2 marks for ISR logic.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Differentiate between level-triggered and edge-triggered external interrupts in 8051. How is the choice made using TCON bits?**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Level-Triggered (ITx = 0) | Edge-Triggered (ITx = 1) |
|---------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Activation | Pin held **LOW** | **Falling edge** (HIGH→LOW transition) |
| Hold Time | Source must hold low until ISR starts | Only a transition is needed |
| Re-trigger | Will re-interrupt if still low at RETI | Will not re-trigger unless a new edge occurs |
| Suitable for | Slowly changing signals, emergency lines | Switches, encoders, pulse signals |
| Flag (IEx) | Hardware-set, hardware-cleared | Hardware-set on edge, hardware-cleared on ISR entry |

**Setting via TCON (88H):**
- `SETB IT0` → Edge-triggered for INT0
- `CLR IT0` → Level-triggered for INT0

(4 marks for comparison, 3 marks for configuration explanation.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Write an 8051 assembly program to receive data serially at 9600 baud and echo it back. Use interrupt-driven serial communication.**

**Model Solution:**

```asm
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN

        ORG     0023H        ; Serial port vector
        LJMP    SERIAL_ISR

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #20H  ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
        MOV     TH1,  #0FDH ; 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
        MOV     SCON, #50H  ; Mode 1, REN enabled
        SETB    TR1         ; Start Timer 1
        MOV     IE,   #90H  ; EA=1, ES=1
HERE:   SJMP    HERE

SERIAL_ISR:
        JNB     RI, CHECK_TI
        CLR     RI
        MOV     A, SBUF
        MOV     SBUF, A
CHECK_TI:
        RETI
        END
```

(2 marks for TMOD/SCON/TH1, 2 marks for IE setup, 3 marks for ISR logic.)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to set EA** — the program compiles, runs, but interrupts NEVER fire. Always show `EA = 1` explicitly.
> 2. **Using RET instead of RETI** at the end of the ISR — the interrupt logic is NOT re-enabled, and the CPU may hang on subsequent interrupts.
> 3. **Forgetting to clear RI and TI** in the serial ISR — they are NOT auto-cleared like TFx or IEx. The ISR will keep re-entering.
> 4. **Incorrect hex conversion** of timer initial values — re-verify by computing the actual delay after the conversion.
> 5. **Using wrong interrupt number in Keil C** — the order is: 0=Ext0, 1=T0, 2=Ext1, 3=T1, 4=Serial. Students often write `interrupt 5` by mistake.
> 6. **Not placing `LJMP MAIN` at 0000H** — the CPU will execute garbage code in the vector area and crash.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **5 interrupts** in 8051: INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial.
- [ ] **Vector addresses**: 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H.
- [ ] **EA must = 1** for any interrupt to fire.
- [ ] **RETI** (not RET) must end every ISR.
- [ ] **RI and TI must be software-cleared**; all other interrupt flags are auto-cleared.
- [ ] **Machine cycle = 12 / f_osc**; for 11.0592 MHz, T_MC = 1.0851 µs.
- [ ] **Timer Mode 1**: 16-bit, manual reload, max delay = 71.11 ms.
- [ ] **Timer Mode 2**: 8-bit auto-reload, max delay = 276.7 µs (used for baud-rate generation).
- [ ] **TH1 = 0xFD** gives 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz (must be remembered).
- [ ] **Edge-triggered** (ITx=1) is preferred for switches to avoid multiple triggering.
- [ ] **Priority register IP** allows nested interrupts — high priority can interrupt low priority.
- [ ] **Keil C keyword** `interrupt N` where N = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 (NOT the vector address).
- [ ] **8 bytes** of code memory are reserved between vectors — ISRs longer than this must `LJMP` to a full routine.
- [ ] **Level-triggered interrupts** must release the pin before RETI, or will retrigger.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Priority Settings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Priority Settings**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Interrupt Priority** is a hardware-defined or software-configurable mechanism in a microcontroller's interrupt controller (such as the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** in ARM Cortex-M) that determines the order in which multiple pending interrupt requests are serviced when they occur simultaneously, and also governs whether a lower-priority interrupt service routine (ISR) can itself be preempted (interrupted) by a higher-priority interrupt request.

In the ARM Cortex-M architecture, every interrupt source is assigned a numerical **priority value** stored in an **Interrupt Priority Register (IPR)**. The fundamental rule is:

> **Lower numerical priority value = Higher logical priority of service.**

A unique architectural feature of NVIC is the splitting of the priority field into two sub-fields:
- **Preemption Priority** — controls whether an incoming interrupt can preempt an ongoing ISR.
- **Sub-Priority** — controls the servicing order among multiple pending interrupts of the *same* preemption priority.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "Interrupt priority defines the relative urgency of an interrupt request. The NVIC supports programmable priority levels using a configurable number of priority bits, with a built-in mechanism for nested (preemptive) interrupts based on preemption priority."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **Hospital Emergency Triage Ward**:

| Triage Concept | Embedded System Equivalent |
|----------------|---------------------------|
| Critical (heart attack) patient arriving | **High-priority interrupt** (e.g., Hard Fault) |
| Moderate (fracture) patient | **Mid-priority interrupt** (e.g., ADC EOC) |
| Minor (cold) patient | **Low-priority interrupt** (e.g., UART TX complete) |
| Doctor attending a fracture patient is asked to attend a heart attack patient | **Preemption** (higher-priority ISR interrupting lower-priority ISR) |
| Two heart-attack patients arrive together | **Sub-priority** decides who is treated *first* if both have same urgency tier |

The **triage nurse (NVIC)** continuously monitors all incoming "patient signals" (interrupt requests) and decides, using a strict priority ladder, which one gets the doctor's attention. Just as you cannot have the cold patient preempt the heart-attack patient, an MCU cannot let a UART interrupt preempt a system fault interrupt.

---

## 1.3 Why Interrupt Priority is Critical in Embedded Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Engineering Reasons Why Priority Matters:**
> 1. **Determinism** — Real-time systems (automotive ECUs, motor controllers, medical pacemakers) must guarantee response within bounded latency. Priority makes this guarantee possible.
> 2. **Deadlock Prevention** — Without priority, two interrupts could starve each other, or critical data could be lost.
> 3. **Resource Hierarchy** — Shared resources (buffers, buses, DMA channels) require ordered access, enforced through priority-based scheduling.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined for Quick Recall)

- **NVIC** — Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller; the interrupt management unit in ARM Cortex-M cores.
- **Preemption Priority** — The priority level that determines if an ISR can be interrupted by another ISR.
- **Sub-Priority** — Tie-breaker priority used only when two interrupts share the same preemption priority.
- **Priority Grouping** — Bit-field configuration via the **AIRCR.PRIGROUP** register that splits the priority byte into preemption and sub-priority fields.
- **Tail-Chaining** — NVIC optimization where consecutive ISRs execute back-to-back without unstacking/stacking overhead.
- **Interrupt Latency** — Time between interrupt request assertion and the first instruction of the ISR; for Cortex-M, this is **12 clock cycles** minimum (4 for stacking + 8 for vector fetch).
- **IPR** — Interrupt Priority Register array; base address **0xE000E400** in Cortex-M3/M4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NVIC Priority Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The ARM Cortex-M NVIC implements priority using a hardware-efficient design. The internal logic flows as follows:

**Step 1 — Interrupt Request (IRQ) Assertion**
An external or internal peripheral asserts its interrupt request line. NVIC samples this on every clock cycle.

**Step 2 — Priority Comparison**
The NVIC compares the incoming interrupt's priority value with the currently active (executing) ISR's priority value.

**Step 3 — Decision Branch**
- If the new IRQ has a **strictly lower numerical priority value** (i.e., higher logical priority) **AND** it differs in **preemption priority** → **Preempt the current ISR**.
- If the new IRQ has the same preemption priority but lower numerical sub-priority → **Tail-chain or Pend** until current ISR completes.
- If the new IRQ has higher or equal numerical priority → **Remain pending** until current ISR finishes.

**Step 4 — Vector Fetch & Stacking**
If preemption is warranted, the CPU performs an automatic hardware stacking of **8 registers** (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) onto the active stack pointer, then fetches the vector address from the Vector Table.

**Step 5 — ISR Execution Begins**
The first instruction of the ISR executes within **12 clock cycles** of the IRQ assertion (zero-wait-state Flash assumed).

**Step 6 — Return & Tail-Chaining**
On `BX LR` / `POP {PC}`, the NVIC checks the pending bit. If another interrupt is pending, **tail-chaining** enters its ISR in **6 clock cycles** (no stacking overhead).

---

## 2.2 The Priority Grouping — `AIRCR.PRIGROUP`

The **Application Interrupt and Reset Control Register (AIRCR)** at address **0xE000ED0C** contains the `PRIGROUP` field (bits [10:8]) that controls the split of the 8-bit priority field.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The number of implemented priority bits is **vendor-specific**. Most Cortex-M3/M4 implementations provide only the **upper 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, or 8 bits** of the 8-bit field. The lower unimplemented bits read as zero and writes to them are ignored.

### 2.2.1 Priority Grouping Lookup Table

| PRIGROUP Value | Preemption Bits (g) | Sub-Priority Bits (s) | Preemption Levels | Sub-Priority Levels |
|----------------|---------------------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 0b000 (0)      | 8                   | 0                    | 256               | 1                  |
| 0b001 (1)      | 7                   | 1                    | 128               | 2                  |
| 0b010 (2)      | 6                   | 2                    | 64                | 4                  |
| 0b011 (3)      | 5                   | 3                    | 32                | 8                  |
| 0b100 (4)      | 4                   | 4                    | 16                | 16                 |
| 0b101 (5)      | 3                   | 5                    | 8                 | 32                 |
| 0b110 (6)      | 2                   | 6                    | 4                 | 64                 |
| 0b111 (7)      | 0                   | 8                    | 1 (no nesting)    | 256                |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "more preemption bits = better." In reality, the grouping should be chosen to match the application: a complex RTOS needs more preemption levels, while a simple super-loop may use all bits as sub-priority.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board-examinable** for interrupt-priority numerical and analytical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Priority byte layout | `PRI = [Preemption (g bits) : Sub-priority (s bits)]` where `g + s = implemented_bits` | bits |
| 2 | Implemented priority bits | Typically **3 to 8** depending on vendor | bits |
| 3 | Preemption levels | `L_preempt = 2^g` | integer |
| 4 | Sub-priority levels | `L_sub = 2^s` | integer |
| 5 | Lower number = higher priority | `PRI_a < PRI_b  ⇒  a is serviced before b` | numeric |
| 6 | Preemption condition | `PRI_new_preempt < PRI_active_preempt` | numeric |
| 7 | Interrupt latency (stacking + vector) | `t_latency = 12 / f_CPU` (zero-wait-state) | seconds |
| 8 | Tail-chain latency | `t_tail = 6 / f_CPU` | seconds |
| 9 | Worst-case nested latency | `t_worst = (N_nested × 12) / f_CPU` | seconds |
| 10 | IPR base address (Cortex-M3/M4) | `0xE000E400` | hex |
| 11 | AIRCR register address | `0xE000ED0C` | hex |
| 12 | VECTKEY for AIRCR write | `0x05FA` must be written to bits [31:16] | hex |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR / ISO 26262):** Engine control units use priority grouping `PRIGROUP = 0b011` (5 preemption, 3 sub) to allow 32 preemption levels for safety-critical task isolation.
- **Industrial Motor Control:** Field-Oriented Control (FOC) loops require deterministic 1 µs response. Timer interrupts are assigned the **lowest numerical value** (highest preemption priority) to preempt ADC end-of-conversion.
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers):** Fault interrupts are programmed to preemption priority `0x00` (highest) with no sub-priority to guarantee non-interruptible servicing.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Low-power modes rely on priority configuration to ensure that wake-up interrupts (e.g., GPIO) preempt background telemetry tasks.

---

## 2.5 8051 Background Priority (Comparisons Frequently Asked in KTU)

For KTU continuity (since 8051 is the foundational MCU in earlier modules), the **8051 IP (Interrupt Priority) Register at 0xB8** provides a simpler **2-level priority** model:

| IP Bit | Interrupt Source | Reset Value |
|--------|------------------|-------------|
| `IP.0` (PX0) | External 0 | 0 |
| `IP.1` (PT0) | Timer 0 | 0 |
| `IP.2` (PX1) | External 1 | 0 |
| `IP.3` (PT1) | Timer 1 | 0 |
| `IP.4` (PS)  | Serial Port | 0 |
| `IP.5` (PT2) | Timer 2 (8052) | 0 |

**Default polling order** (when all priorities are 0 and simultaneous requests occur):
1. External 0 → 2. Timer 0 → 3. External 1 → 4. Timer 1 → 5. Serial Port
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Priority Byte Construction from PRIGROUP

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A Cortex-M4 MCU has **4 implemented priority bits**. The firmware configures `PRIGROUP = 0b011` (i.e., 5 preemption, 3 sub-priority in the 8-bit model — but only the **upper 4 bits** are physical). An engineer wants to assign:
- Interrupt A: Preemption level `2`, Sub-priority level `1`
- Interrupt B: Preemption level `2`, Sub-priority level `0`

**Find the byte to write into the IPR for Interrupt A and Interrupt B, and determine which is serviced first if both become pending simultaneously.**

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the actual bit field widths in the hardware.**

The vendor implements only 4 bits. With `PRIGROUP = 0b011`, the conceptual split is 5 preemption bits and 3 sub-priority bits, but only the **upper 4** are physical. The split is taken from the **MSB side**. So the physical layout is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical byte} &= b_7 \, b_6 \, b_5 \, b_4 \, b_3 \, b_2 \, b_1 \, b_0 \\
\text{Used bits} &= b_7 \, b_6 \, b_5 \, b_4 \quad \text{(upper 4 only)} \\
\text{MSB-side split at } 0b011 &\Rightarrow b_7 b_6 b_5 \, \vert \, b_4 \\
&\Rightarrow \text{3 preemption bits } (b_7 b_6 b_5), \text{ 1 sub-priority bit } (b_4)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the priority byte for Interrupt A.**

Preemption level = 2 = `010₂` (3 bits). Sub-priority level = 1 = `1₂` (1 bit).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte}_A &= (\text{Preemption}_A \ll 1) \,\vert\, \text{Sub}_A \\
&= (010_2 \ll 1) \,\vert\, 1_2 \\
&= 010_2 \times 2^1 + 1_2 \\
&= 0100_2 + 0001_2 \\
&= 0101_2 \\
&= 0x05
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** Shift the 3-bit preemption field left by 1 (because sub-priority occupies 1 bit) and OR with the 1-bit sub-priority field.

**Step 3 — Compute the priority byte for Interrupt B.**

Preemption level = 2 = `010₂`. Sub-priority level = 0 = `0₂`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte}_B &= (010_2 \ll 1) \,\vert\, 0_2 \\
&= 0100_2 \\
&= 0x04
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare the two priority values numerically.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte}_A &= 0x05 = 5_{10} \\
\text{Byte}_B &= 0x04 = 4_{10} \\
\text{Since } 0x04 &< 0x05 \Rightarrow \text{Interrupt B has higher logical priority.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Service order determination.**

Both interrupts have the **same preemption level** (2). The preemption field bits are identical, so **no preemption** can occur. The NVIC then uses the **sub-priority** field as a tie-breaker.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sub}_A = 1, \quad \text{Sub}_B = 0 \\
\text{Since Sub}_B < \text{Sub}_A \Rightarrow \text{Interrupt B is serviced first.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- Write `0x05` to IPR_A and `0x04` to IPR_B.
- If both pend simultaneously, **Interrupt B is serviced first**, then **Interrupt A** (via tail-chaining).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Preemption vs Sub-Priority Decision

| Scenario | New IRQ Preemption | New IRQ Sub | Active ISR Preemption | Active ISR Sub | Outcome |
|----------|-------------------|-------------|----------------------|----------------|---------|
| 1 | 0x1 | 0x2 | 0x3 | 0x0 | **Preempt** (lower preemption value) |
| 2 | 0x3 | 0x0 | 0x3 | 0x1 | **Pend** (same preemption, new sub is higher value = lower priority) |
| 3 | 0x3 | 0x0 | 0x3 | 0x0 | **Pending (tie, undefined tie-breaker)** |
| 4 | 0x5 | 0x1 | 0x2 | 0x3 | **Pend** (new has higher numerical value = lower priority) |
| 5 | 0x0 | 0x0 | 0x7 | 0x7 | **Preempt immediately** (highest possible) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A frequent exam question asks *"Can two interrupts with identical preemption and sub-priority preempt each other?"* The answer is **No** — they execute in **vector table order** (lower IRQ number first), since the NVIC has no further tie-breaker.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Tail-Chain Latency Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> A Cortex-M4 runs at **72 MHz**. Two ISRs (A then B) execute back-to-back. Calculate the total time from A's IRQ assertion to B's first instruction.

**Step 1 — Latency for first ISR (A):**
$$
t_A = \frac{12}{72 \times 10^6} = 166.67 \ \text{ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Tail-chain entry for ISR B (no stacking):**
$$
t_{tail} = \frac{6}{72 \times 10^6} = 83.33 \ \text{ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Total response time for B's first instruction:**
$$
t_{total} = t_A + t_{tail} = 166.67 + 83.33 = 250 \ \text{ns}
$$

This compact derivation shows why tail-chaining is essential for **low-overhead real-time** interrupt pipelining.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 NVIC Priority Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New IRQ asserted] --> B[Read pending bit]
    B --> C[Compare new preemption priority with active ISR preemption priority]
    C --> D{New preempt value strictly less than active?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Preempt: stack 8 registers, fetch vector, execute new ISR]
    D -- No --> F{New preempt value equals active?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Compare sub-priority values]
    G --> H{New sub value strictly less than active?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Mark pending: service after current ISR via tail-chain]
    H -- No --> J[Remain pending: lower vector number wins if sub is equal]
    F -- No --> K[Remain pending: lower priority interrupt waits]
    E --> L[ISR executes]
    I --> M[Current ISR finishes]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    M --> N[Tail-chain to next pending ISR in 6 cycles]
    N --> L
```

---

## 4.2 Priority Byte Bit-Field Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph BYTE["8-bit Priority Field (IPR)"]
        direction LR
        P7["Bit 7"] --> P6["Bit 6"]
        P6 --> P5["Bit 5"]
        P5 --> P4["Bit 4"]
        P4 --> P3["Bit 3"]
        P3 --> P2["Bit 2"]
        P2 --> P1["Bit 1"]
        P1 --> P0["Bit 0"]
    end
    subgraph IMPL["Implemented bits (vendor-specific, typically 3-8)"]
        direction LR
        U7["Implemented: upper n bits"]
        U6["Unimplemented: read as 0, writes ignored"]
    end
    subgraph SPLIT["Split based on AIRCR.PRIGROUP"]
        direction LR
        PRE["Preemption field (g bits, MSB side)"]
        SUB["Sub-priority field (s bits, LSB side)"]
    end
    BYTE --> IMPL
    IMPL --> SPLIT
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Interrupt Nesting Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CPU["ARM Cortex-M4 Core"]
        direction TB
        PC_RUN["Main program executing"]
        ISR_A["ISR A in execution\n(Preempt = 0x2)"]
        ISR_B["ISR B in execution\n(Preempt = 0x1)"]
        ISR_C["ISR C in execution\n(Preempt = 0x0 - HIGHEST)"]
    end
    subgraph NVIC["NVIC Controller"]
        PRI_REG["Interrupt Priority Registers\n0xE000E400 - 0xE000E4EF"]
        AIRCR["AIRCR 0xE000ED0C\nPRIGROUP field"]
        PEND["Pending bits\nSETPEND / CLRPND"]
    end
    subgraph PERIPH["Peripherals"]
        TIM["Timer 2"]
        ADC["ADC"]
        UART["UART 1"]
        GPIO["GPIO EXTI"]
    end
    TIM --> NVIC
    ADC --> NVIC
    UART --> NVIC
    GPIO --> NVIC
    NVIC --> CPU
    PC_RUN -->|IRQ higher preempt| ISR_A
    ISR_A -->|IRQ higher preempt| ISR_B
    ISR_B -->|IRQ higher preempt| ISR_C
    ISR_C -->|Return| ISR_B
    ISR_B -->|Return| ISR_A
    ISR_A -->|Return| PC_RUN
```

---

## 4.4 8051 Two-Level Priority Comparison Block

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LOW["LOW PRIORITY GROUP"]
        direction TB
        L1["External 0 (PX0 = 0)"]
        L2["Timer 0 (PT0 = 0)"]
        L3["External 1 (PX1 = 0)"]
        L4["Timer 1 (PT1 = 0)"]
        L5["Serial Port (PS = 0)"]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH PRIORITY GROUP"]
        direction TB
        H1["Any IRQ with corresponding IP bit = 1"]
    end
    LOW -->|Cannot preempt| HIGH
    HIGH -->|Can preempt| LOW
    H1 -.->|E.g., Timer 1 with PT1=1| L1
    L1 -.->|Default polling order| L2
    L2 -.-> L3
    L3 -.-> L4
    L4 -.-> L5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define interrupt priority. Differentiate between preemption priority and sub-priority in the ARM Cortex-M NVIC.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Interrupt Priority** is the mechanism that determines the order in which multiple pending interrupt requests are serviced and whether an active ISR can itself be interrupted by another IRQ (3 marks for complete answer).

| Aspect | Preemption Priority | Sub-Priority |
|--------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Function** | Decides if an IRQ can preempt an active ISR | Decides order of service among same-preemption-level pending IRQs |
| **Granularity** | Configurable, MSB side of priority byte | Configurable, LSB side of priority byte |
| **Trigger for context switch** | If new preempt value < active preempt value | Only when preemption levels are equal |
| **Configurable via** | `AIRCR.PRIGROUP` field | `AIRCR.PRIGROUP` field |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** State the rule "lower numerical value = higher logical priority" explicitly for full marks.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of the `AIRCR` register in configuring NVIC priorities? Mention the address and the significance of the VECTKEY field.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Application Interrupt and Reset Control Register (AIRCR)** at address **0xE000ED0C** contains the `PRIGROUP` field (bits [10:8]) which controls how the priority byte is split between preemption and sub-priority fields (1 mark).

The `PRIGROUP` value ranges from `0` to `7` (1 mark).

The **VECTKEY** field (bits [31:16]) must be written with the value **`0x05FA`** for any write to AIRCR to take effect. This prevents accidental corruption by runaway code. Writing any other value to the upper 16 bits is ignored (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)** in an ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller. Highlight the functions of the IPR, ISER, ICER, ISPR, and ICPR registers. (7 marks)

**(b)** A Cortex-M4 system has **3 implemented priority bits**. The `PRIGROUP` is set to `0b100`. For two interrupts, X and Y, the following configurations are given:

- Interrupt X: Preemption level = 3, Sub-priority level = 2
- Interrupt Y: Preemption level = 3, Sub-priority level = 2

Both interrupts are enabled and become pending at the **same instant**. Explain in detail, with calculated priority bytes, which interrupt will be serviced first and why. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks):**

The **NVIC** is a tightly coupled peripheral inside the Cortex-M4 core. Its primary role is **interrupt detection, prioritization, vectoring, and nested preemption management**.

**Major NVIC Registers:**

| Register | Address Range | Function |
|----------|---------------|----------|
| **ISER[0]-ISER[7]** | `0xE000E100` - `0xE000E11C` | Interrupt Set-Enable Register — writing 1 enables the corresponding IRQ |
| **ICER[0]-ICER[7]** | `0xE000E180` - `0xE000E19C` | Interrupt Clear-Enable Register — writing 1 disables the IRQ |
| **ISPR[0]-ISPR[7]** | `0xE000E200` - `0xE000E21C` | Interrupt Set-Pending Register — software-triggered interrupt assertion |
| **ICPR[0]-ICPR[7]** | `0xE000E280` - `0xE000E29C` | Interrupt Clear-Pending Register — clears the pending state |
| **IABR[0]-IABR[7]** | `0xE000E300` - `0xE000E31C` | Interrupt Active Bit Register — read-only, indicates active IRQs |
| **IPR[0]-IPR[59]** | `0xE000E400` - `0xE000E4EF` | Interrupt Priority Registers — 8-bit per IRQ, only upper N bits physical |
| **STIR** | `0xE000EF00` | Software Trigger Interrupt Register |

The NVIC also features:
- **Hardware stacking** of 8 registers (R0–R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR) on interrupt entry.
- **Vector table** at address `0x00000000` (relocatable via `VTOR`) containing 32-bit ISR addresses.
- **Tail-chaining** for back-to-back interrupts.
- **Late-arrival preemption** for higher-priority IRQs arriving mid-stack.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** 2 marks for block diagram, 3 marks for register functions, 2 marks for additional features (tail-chaining, stacking).

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Determine the priority byte layout.**

With 3 implemented priority bits, only the **upper 3 bits** (b7, b6, b5) of the 8-bit IPR are physical. Lower bits read as 0 and writes are ignored (1 mark).

For `PRIGROUP = 0b100`, the split is **4 preemption bits and 4 sub-priority bits** in the 8-bit model. Since only 3 bits exist, the actual split is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Preemption bits} &= \min(4, 3) = 3 \\
\text{Sub-priority bits} &= 0 \quad \text{(no sub-priority when only 3 bits physical and PRIGROUP} \geq 0b101)
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — with 3 implemented bits and `PRIGROUP = 0b100` (4 preemption, 4 sub), the **3 MSBs are preemption** and **0 LSBs are sub-priority**. The preemption field is bits [7:5] (1 mark).

**Step 2 — Encode Interrupt X.**

Preemption level = 3 = `011₂` (3 bits). Sub-priority = 2, but with 0 sub-bits this is rounded to 0.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Byte}_X &= 011_2 \ll 0 = 0110\,0000_2 = 0x60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Encode Interrupt Y.**

Identical to X (1 mark):

$$
\text{Byte}_Y = 0x60
$$

**Step 4 — Service order resolution (3 marks).**

Since both interrupts have:
- **Identical preemption level** (3)
- **Identical (or zero) sub-priority**
- **Identical priority byte** (`0x60`)

The NVIC uses the **vector table ordering** as the final tie-breaker. The interrupt with the **lower IRQ number** is serviced first. (E.g., if X is IRQ 5 and Y is IRQ 7, then X is serviced first.)

If IRQ numbers are also equal (impossible in practice), the behaviour is **architecturally undefined**; however, the typical implementation services the request that arrived first on the bus.

**Conclusion:** Both interrupts have the same priority configuration. The actual servicing order is determined by the **vector table position (IRQ number)**, with the lower IRQ number being serviced first.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **interrupt priority structure of the 8051 microcontroller** using the IP (Interrupt Priority) register. List the default polling order and describe how a high-priority interrupt can preempt a low-priority ISR. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the 8051 two-level priority system with the **ARM Cortex-M NVIC multi-level priority system**. Use a tabular comparison covering at least **five parameters** and explain with a real-time example why NVIC's design is superior for complex embedded applications. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**IP Register Bits (Bit-Addressable, 0xB8):**

| IP Bit | Name | Interrupt |
|--------|------|-----------|
| `IP.7` | — | Reserved |
| `IP.6` | — | Reserved |
| `IP.5` | PT2 | Timer 2 overflow (8052 only) |
| `IP.4` | PS  | Serial Port (TI/RI) |
| `IP.3` | PT1 | Timer 1 overflow |
| `IP.2` | PX1 | External Interrupt 1 |
| `IP.1` | PT0 | Timer 0 overflow |
| `IP.0` | PX0 | External Interrupt 0 |

**Setting logic:** If the corresponding IP bit is `1`, that interrupt is assigned **high priority**; if `0`, **low priority** (1 mark).

**Default Polling Order (when all IP bits = 0):**

1. External Interrupt 0 (highest default)
2. Timer 0 Interrupt
3. External Interrupt 1
4. Timer 1 Interrupt
5. Serial Port Interrupt (lowest default)

(1 mark for the order)

**Preemption Mechanism (3 marks):**

When the 8051 is executing a **low-priority ISR** and a **high-priority interrupt** occurs:
1. The current instruction of the low-priority ISR completes.
2. The 8051 hardware performs a **long call (LCALL)** to the high-priority ISR's vector address.
3. The high-priority ISR executes.
4. On `RETI` from the high-priority ISR, control returns to the **low-priority ISR** (not the main program).
5. The low-priority ISR resumes until completion, then returns to the main program.

This preemption is **one-level deep** — a high-priority ISR cannot itself be interrupted by another low-priority interrupt, but it *can* be interrupted by **another high-priority** interrupt (which then takes over and returns to the first high-priority ISR's remaining instructions).

**Vector addresses:**

| Interrupt | Vector Address |
|-----------|----------------|
| Reset | 0x0000 |
| External 0 | 0x0003 |
| Timer 0 | 0x000B |
| External 1 | 0x0013 |
| Timer 1 | 0x001B |
| Serial Port | 0x0023 |
| Timer 2 | 0x002B |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Comparative Analysis Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | 8051 IP Register | ARM Cortex-M NVIC |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Number of priority levels** | 2 (high / low) | Up to 256 (depending on `PRIGROUP`) |
| **Programmability** | Fixed two-tier with set/clear of individual IP bits | Per-IRQ 8-bit programmable priority byte |
| **Tie-breaking** | Fixed polling sequence | Configurable sub-priority + vector order fallback |
| **Hardware stacking** | Manual stack push/pop in ISR | Automatic 8-register hardware stacking |
| **Vector table** | Fixed addresses at 0x0003, 0x000B, … | Relocatable, configurable entry count |
| **Latency** | Variable, depends on longest instruction (~13 cycles) | Deterministic 12 cycles |
| **Nesting depth** | Effectively 1 level for high-priority ISRs | Up to 256 levels (one per preemption level) |
| **Tail-chaining** | Not supported | Hardware-supported 6-cycle back-to-back |

**Real-Time Example (2 marks):**

Consider an **automotive Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** system:
- An **SPI transaction with the brake ECU** must complete within 100 µs (a UART-like low-priority interrupt).
- An **accelerometer sample-ready** signal must preempt all lower tasks and execute within 1 µs (highest preemption priority).
- A **CAN bus off-state error** needs response within 10 µs (mid-priority).

With 8051's two-level scheme, the developer cannot guarantee deterministic ordering between the accelerometer and the CAN error — both could be assigned "high priority" and preempt each other unpredictably. With NVIC, the accelerometer is assigned **preemption priority 0x00**, the CAN error **0x10**, and the SPI **0x80**, guaranteeing exact nested behaviour and bounded worst-case latency (1 µs for accelerometer, 12 cycles + SPI ISR time for CAN, etc.). This **deterministic, multi-level, hardware-accelerated** priority makes NVIC strictly superior for complex, safety-critical embedded applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Interrupt Priority Questions:**
> 1. **Inverted priority logic** — Many students write "higher number = higher priority." This is **wrong** for NVIC. The correct rule is: **lower numerical value = higher logical priority.** Marks are cut immediately for this confusion.
> 2. **Confusing preemption and sub-priority** — Sub-priority cannot cause preemption. If two ISRs share the same preemption level, the sub-priority only determines the order of service *after* the current ISR finishes, not during it.
> 3. **Forgetting VECTKEY** — Writing to `AIRCR` without `0x05FA` in the upper 16 bits silently fails. Students writing code or register configuration in answers must include this.
> 4. **Assuming 8 implemented bits** — Most Cortex-M3/M4 implementations use only 3–5 bits. Always check the device datasheet; do not assume.
> 5. **Missing tail-chaining** in latency questions — If two ISRs are involved and the question asks for total time, students often add two 12-cycle latencies. The correct approach is **12 + 6 = 18 cycles** (with tail-chain), or **12 + 12 = 24 cycles** (with full preemption stack).
> 6. **8051 vs ARM mix-up** — KTU Module 4 focuses on ARM NVIC. Do not answer 8051 IP-register questions unless the question explicitly asks for it (e.g., the alternative choice).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Interrupt Priority Settings**

- ☐ **NVIC** is the interrupt controller in ARM Cortex-M; it is tightly coupled to the core and supports **hardware stacking**, **vector fetching**, and **tail-chaining**.
- ☐ The **priority field** is **8 bits wide** conceptually but only the **upper N bits are implemented** (N = 3 to 8 depending on vendor).
- ☐ The **PRIGROUP** field in `AIRCR` (address `0xE000ED0C`) splits the priority byte into **preemption** (MSB side) and **sub-priority** (LSB side) fields.
- ☐ **Lower numerical value = higher logical priority** (mnemonic: "0 is the king").
- ☐ **Preemption priority** controls whether an IRQ can interrupt an active ISR. A new IRQ preempts the active ISR **only if its preemption value is strictly less**.
- ☐ **Sub-priority** is a tie-breaker used when preemption values are equal. It determines the order of tail-chained execution.
- ☐ **Interrupt latency** is 12 cycles (stacking + vector fetch) for the first IRQ, and **6 cycles** for subsequent tail-chained IRQs.
- ☐ The **vector table** can be relocated via the **VTOR** register (address `0xE000ED08`).
- ☐ The **IPR** array starts at `0xE000E400` with one byte per interrupt; for devices with N < 8 implemented bits, the lower `(8 − N)` bits are read-as-zero and writes are ignored.
- ☐ The **ISER, ICER, ISPR, ICPR** registers are used for enable/disable and set/clear pending operations, each requiring a write of `1` to the relevant bit.
- ☐ The **AIRCR** register requires the **VECTKEY = `0x05FA`** in the upper 16 bits for any write to take effect.
- ☐ **8051 IP register** (address `0xB8`) provides a simpler **2-level priority** (high/low) with default polling order: **Ext0 → T0 → Ext1 → T1 → Serial**.
- ☐ In 8051, a **high-priority ISR can preempt a low-priority ISR**; two high-priority ISRs cannot preempt each other (single-level preemption depth).
- ☐ **Late-arrival preemption** in NVIC allows a higher-priority IRQ to take over even if it arrives during the stacking sequence of a lower-priority IRQ.
- ☐ Always **disable interrupts** (`CPSID I`) when modifying priority registers to avoid race conditions with concurrent interrupt handling.
- ☐ The **BASEPRI** register masks all interrupts below a threshold; **PRIMASK** masks all exceptions except NMI and HardFault; **FAULTMASK** escalates all faults to HardFault.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microproject Implementation using 8051 Microcontroller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Microproject Implementation using 8051 Microcontroller ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Microproject** in the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme PBECT404 syllabus is defined as a small-scale, hands-on, integrative hardware-software engineering task that requires students to design, develop, implement, test, and document a functional embedded system based on the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller (MCS-51 Family)**. It bridges the gap between theoretical instruction (assembly language, peripherals, interfacing) and real-world product engineering by demanding the application of Course Outcomes (COs) related to embedded C/Assembly programming, peripheral interfacing, and system debugging. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech regulations (NEP 2020 aligned)**, a microproject carries defined Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) marks, and is evaluated on three pillars: **Conceptual Design, Working Demonstration, and Technical Documentation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)** > The microproject is NOT just a coding exercise. It is a *systems engineering activity* that requires interfacing the 8051 with at least one external peripheral (LED matrix, LCD, keypad, ADC, sensor, relay, motor, UART device) to solve a defined real-world problem statement. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The "Mini Kitchen" Before the "Restaurant"** > > Imagine you are a chef who has just learned knife skills, sauce-making, and baking in separate theory classes. A microproject is your **practice kitchen** before you open a real restaurant. You are given a small problem — "build a sandwich vending machine that accepts coins" — and you must: > - Plan the recipe (requirements analysis) > - Arrange ingredients and tools (hardware design) > - Write down the steps (algorithm/flowchart) > - Cook and taste-test iteratively (implementation & debugging) > - Present the dish to the examiner with a recipe card (documentation & viva) > > The 8051 is your **smart cooking stove** — a versatile, well-documented platform that every kitchen (industry) recognizes. Mastering it on small projects prepares you for industrial-grade ARM/STM32 kitchens. ### 1.3 Why 8051 for Microprojects? The **8051 microcontroller** remains the academic gold standard for microprojects because of: | Feature | Engineering Significance | |---|---| | **8-bit Harvard architecture** | Separate program + data memory simplifies student learning | | **4 KB on-chip ROM**, **128 Bytes RAM** | Sufficient for small automation tasks | | **Four 8-bit I/O ports (P0–P3)** | Direct interfacing with LEDs, relays, LCDs | | **Two 16-bit Timers (T0, T1)** | Precise delay/event generation | | **Full-duplex UART** | Serial communication with PC/GSM/Bluetooth | | **5 interrupt sources** | Real-time event handling | | **Cheap, available, well-supported** | AT89C51 / P89V51RD2 cost < ₹100 in India | ### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bold Constants) - **Oscillator Frequency (f_osc):** typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz** crystal - **Machine Cycle Period (T_mc):** **12 / f_osc** seconds - **Instruction Cycle:** 1 to 4 machine cycles - **Standard UART Baud Rates:** **9600, 4800, 2400, 1200 bps** - **Power Supply:** **+5V DC** (regulated) - **Logic HIGH (TTL):** **> 2.0 V**; Logic LOW: **< 0.8 V** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Phases of Microproject Implementation (Engineering Lifecycle) The implementation of a microproject using the 8051 follows a well-defined **5-phase V-Model-inspired lifecycle**, mapped directly to industry practices. #### **Phase 1: Problem Definition & Requirement Analysis** - Identify the real-world problem (e.g., "automatic streetlight controller"). - List **Functional Requirements** (what the system MUST do). - List **Non-Functional Requirements** (response time, cost, power). - **Deliverable:** Problem Statement Document (PSD). #### **Phase 2: System Design** - **Hardware Design:** Select 8051 variant, draw schematic (using KiCad/Proteus), decide peripherals. - **Software Design:** Write the algorithm in pseudo-code, then draw the flowchart (using standard ISO 5807 symbols). - **Deliverable:** Schematic diagram + Flowchart. #### **Phase 3: Implementation (Coding + Hardware Assembly)** - Translate flowchart into **8051 Assembly** OR **Embedded C (Keil µVision IDE)**. - Assemble/burn the HEX file using a programmer (e.g., **Flash Magic**, **ProgISP**). - **Deliverable:** Source code + HEX file + working breadboard/PCB. #### **Phase 4: Testing & Debugging** - **Unit testing:** test each subroutine (e.g., LCD init, ADC read). - **Integration testing:** combine modules. - **Validation:** compare output with problem statement. - Tools: **Keil Debugger, Proteus Simulation, Logic Analyzer, Digital Multimeter (DMM)**. #### **Phase 5: Documentation & Presentation** - Prepare a **Project Report** (typical KTU format: 20–30 pages). - Prepare a **Working Demo** for the viva panel. ### 2.2 Why Each Phase Matters — The "Why & How" - **Why Phase 1?** "If you don't know what to build, you build the wrong thing." — Reduces re-work. - **Why Phase 2?** Schematics and flowcharts are *contracts* between the designer's intent and the implementer's action. - **Why Phase 3?** Translates abstract logic into voltage levels on silicon pins. - **Why Phase 4?** Bugs are inevitable. Hardware bugs are *electrical*; software bugs are *logical*. Both need distinct tools. - **Why Phase 5?** Engineering is communication. A working circuit without documentation is a liability. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Viva & Exam) > [!NOTE] > These formulas are **frequently tested** in KTU university exams under the Microcontroller module. Memorize the boundary conditions carefully. | # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Machine Cycle Time** | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $f_{osc}$ in **Hz**, $T_{mc}$ in **seconds** | | 2 | **Timer Tick Period (Mode 1)** | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | one timer increment | | 3 | **Total Delay (Mode 1, 16-bit)** | $T_{delay} = (65536 - X) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $X$ = initial timer value (TH + TL) | | 4 | **Total Delay (Mode 2, 8-bit auto-reload)** | $T_{delay} = (256 - X) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $X$ = TH value (TL auto-reloads) | | 5 | **Baud Rate (Timer 1, Mode 2)** | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | SMOD = 0 or 1 (PCON.7) | | 6 | **TH1 reload value (9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0)** | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{384 \times \text{Baud}} = 256 - 3 = \mathbf{253} = \mathbf{0xFD}$ | Standard "magic number" | | 7 | **ADC0804 Clock (if used with 8051)** | $f_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{1.1 \cdot R \cdot C}$ | R and C form the clock RC network | | 8 | **LCD Initialization Time (per HD44780 spec)** | $15\text{ ms} \rightarrow 4.1\text{ ms} \rightarrow 100\text{ µs} \rightarrow 100\text{ µs}$ | 4-step power-on sequence | | 9 | **Power Dissipation (8051 active)** | $P = V_{CC} \times I_{CC}$ | $V_{CC}=5V$, $I_{CC} \approx 20\text{ mA}$ typical | | 10 | **Maximum I/O Sink Current (per pin, P1/P2/P3)** | $\vert I_{OL} \vert \le 10\text{ mA}$ (TTL) | Do not exceed to prevent damage | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Substitution Rule:** For 8051 timing problems, **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz** is almost always used in exam questions because it generates *exact* integer baud rates. If f_osc = 12 MHz is given, the baud rate becomes 0.16% off — still usable but introduces rounding errors. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications Microprojects using 8051 directly translate to the following industrial domains: - **Home Automation:** Light/fan control via relay, BLDC motor speed control - **Industrial Automation:** Conveyor belt sequencing, tank-level monitoring - **Automotive:** Digital speedometer, parking sensor display - **Agriculture:** Greenhouse temperature/humidity controller - **Security:** Password-based door lock (keypad + LCD + solenoid) - **Healthcare:** Digital thermometer with LCD display - **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machine controller, microwave oven timer ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Generating a 50 ms Delay using Timer 0 in Mode 1 **Problem Statement (typical KTU 4-mark sub-question):** "Calculate the value to be loaded into TH0 and TL0 of an 8051 to generate a delay of 50 ms. Assume f_osc = 11.0592 MHz, Timer 0 in Mode 1." **Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation:** > [!NOTE] > **Logic Behind the Setup:** > In Mode 1, the timer is a **16-bit counter** that increments from its initial value $X$ up to $2^{16} = 65536$ (which is equivalent to 0x0000 after one full overflow). The number of timer ticks needed is therefore $N = 65536 - X$. Each tick consumes exactly one machine cycle, $T_{mc}$. Step 1 — Calculate the Machine Cycle Time: $$T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$$ $$T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}\ \text{seconds}$$ $$T_{mc} = 1.085\ \mu s\ \text{(per machine cycle, and per timer tick)}$$ Step 2 — Calculate the Number of Timer Ticks Required: Let $T_{delay} = 50\text{ ms} = 50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$. $$N_{ticks} = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}}$$ $$N_{ticks} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$N_{ticks} = 46080\ \text{ticks}$$ Step 3 — Calculate the Initial Timer Value $X$: $$X = 65536 - N_{ticks}$$ $$X = 65536 - 46080$$ $$X = 19456\ \text{(decimal)}$$ Step 4 — Convert 19456 to Hexadecimal (16-bit split into TH0 and TL0): $$19456 \div 16 = 1216\ \text{remainder}\ 0\ \rightarrow\ \text{LSB} = 0$$ $$1216 \div 16 = 76\ \text{remainder}\ 0$$ $$76 \div 16 = 4\ \text{remainder}\ 12\ \rightarrow\ \text{next} = C$$ $$4 \div 16 = 0\ \text{remainder}\ 4\ \rightarrow\ \text{MSB} = 4$$ Reading remainders from bottom to top: $0x4C00$. Step 5 — Split into TH0 and TL0 (High byte → TH0, Low byte → TL0): $$TH0 = 0x4C$$ $$TL0 = 0x00$$ Step 6 — Verify the Calculated Delay: $$T_{delay} = (65536 - 0x4C00) \times 1.085\ \mu s$$ $$T_{delay} = 46080 \times 1.085\ \mu s$$ $$T_{delay} = 50004.8\ \mu s \approx 50\ \text{ms}\ \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** $\boxed{TH0 = 0x4C,\ TL0 = 0x00}$ produces a **50.0048 ms** delay (rounding error < 0.01%, acceptable in KTU exam). --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Timer 1 Reload Value for 9600 Baud Rate Serial Communication **Problem Statement:** "Find the value of TH1 to be loaded for serial communication at 9600 baud rate using an 8051 with 11.0592 MHz crystal. Assume SMOD = 0." **Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation:** Step 1 — Recall the Standard 8051 Baud Rate Formula (from Formula Sheet #5): $$\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{osc}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ Step 2 — Substitute Known Values: - $\text{SMOD} = 0 \implies 2^{0} = 1$ - $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$ - $\text{Baud} = 9600$ $$9600 = \dfrac{1 \cdot 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ Step 3 — Simplify the Denominator: $$32 \times 12 = 384$$ $$9600 = \dfrac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$$ Step 4 — Solve for $(256 - TH1)$: $$(256 - TH1) = \dfrac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600}$$ $$(256 - TH1) = \dfrac{11059200}{3686400}$$ $$(256 - TH1) = 3$$ Step 5 — Solve for $TH1$: $$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253$$ $$TH1 = 0xFD\ \text{(hexadecimal)}$$ Step 6 — Verify: $$\text{Baud} = \dfrac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times (256 - 253)} = \dfrac{11059200}{384 \times 3} = \dfrac{11059200}{1152} = 9600\ \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** $\boxed{TH1 = 0xFD = 253}$ — this is the universally memorized "magic value" for 9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0, and is set via `MOV TH1, #0xFD` followed by `MOV SCON, #0x50` and `MOV TMOD, #0x20`. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Microproject Sub-Systems The table below maps **typical microproject functional blocks** to **8051 hardware registers/instructions**. This is the most frequently tested matrix in KTU viva. | Subsystem Function | 8051 Register(s) | Key Instruction(s) | Typical Peripheral | |---|---|---|---| | Generate 1 second delay | T0, TH0/TL0, TMOD, TCON | `MOV TMOD,#01H`, `SETB TR0` | Used as time-base for clock display | | Read a digital switch | P1.x (input mode) | `JB P1.0, label` | Push-button, limit switch | | Drive an LED | P1.x (output mode) | `SETB P1.0` / `CLR P1.0` | Single LED, 7-segment display | | Multiplexed 7-segment | P0 (data), P2 (digit select) | `MOV P0, #pattern` | 4-digit counter | | LCD (16x2) interface | P0 (data), P2.0–P2.2 (RS,RW,EN) | `LCALL lcd_cmd`, `LCALL lcd_data` | HD44780 LCD | | Matrix keypad scan | P1 (rows output, cols input) | `MOV P1, #0x0F` then read cols | 4x4 keypad | | ADC read (parallel) | P1 (8 data lines), P3.0 (RD), P3.1 (WR) | `CLR P3.1; SETB P3.1` then read P1 | ADC0804 | | Stepper motor (full step) | P1.0–P1.3 (4 phase sequence) | `MOV P1, #0x09; CALL delay` | 4-phase unipolar stepper | | DC motor PWM | P1.0 (via driver), T0 overflow for PWM | `SETB/CLR P1.0` in loop | L293D driver | | UART transmit | SBUF, SCON, T1 | `MOV SBUF, A`; wait TI=1 | PC/GSM/Bluetooth | | UART receive | SBUF, SCON | `JNB RI, $; MOV A, SBUF` | GPS module | --- ### 3.4 Derivation 3: Power Calculation for a Microproject **Problem Statement:** "Estimate the total current drawn by an 8051 system that drives 8 LEDs (each 10 mA) and one 16x2 LCD (5 mA). The 8051 itself draws 20 mA. Find the total power at 5V." **Step-by-Step Solution:** Step 1 — Sum the currents: $$I_{total} = I_{8051} + I_{LEDs} + I_{LCD}$$ $$I_{total} = 20 + (8 \times 10) + 5$$ $$I_{total} = 20 + 80 + 5 = 105\ \text{mA}$$ Step 2 — Calculate the power: $$P = V \times I = 5\text{ V} \times 105\text{ mA} = 0.525\ \text{W}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** A standard 7805 regulator in TO-220 package can dissipate up to 1 W without a heat sink. At 0.525 W, it is safe **without** a heat sink. If you added a relay (≈ 70 mA) and a buzzer (30 mA), the total jumps to **205 mA / 1.025 W**, requiring a heat sink. KTU students frequently lose marks for ignoring thermal design. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Generic 8051 Microproject Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB power["5V DC Power Supply"]:::power xtal["11.0592 MHz Crystal + 33pF Caps"]:::clk rst["Reset Circuit 10uF + 10k + Button"]:::rst mcu["8051 Microcontroller AT89C51"]:::mcu isp["ISP Header for HEX Burn"]:::isp sensor["Input Peripherals Sensors Keypad Switches"]:::in display["Output Peripherals LCD 7-seg LED Motor Relay"]:::out uart["UART Interface MAX232 to PC GSM GPS"]:::uart pwrCtrl["Driver ULN2003 L293D Relay"]:::drv power --> mcu xtal --> mcu rst --> mcu isp --> mcu sensor --> mcu mcu --> pwrCtrl pwrCtrl --> display mcu --> uart mcu --> display classDef power fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,color:#000 classDef clk fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#6a5acd,color:#000 classDef rst fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,color:#000 classDef mcu fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef isp fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 classDef in fill:#add8e6,stroke:#4682b4,color:#000 classDef out fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#c71585,color:#000 classDef uart fill:#dda0dd,stroke:#8b008b,color:#000 classDef drv fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#cd5c5c,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **How to read this diagram:** The `8051` block is the central processing node. The **arrows represent the direction of signal/control flow**, not necessarily physical wires. The `Driver` node is a *power amplification stage* (e.g., ULN2003 for relays, L293D for motors) — the 8051 GPIO pins (max 10 mA sink) cannot directly drive high-current loads. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Microproject Development Lifecycle (V-Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR A([Problem Statement]):::stage --> B[Requirements Analysis]:::stage B --> C[Hardware Design Schematic]:::stage B --> D[Software Design Flowchart]:::stage C --> E[PCB or Breadboard Build]:::stage D --> F[Embedded C or Assembly Code]:::stage E --> G[Unit Test Each Module]:::stage F --> G G --> H[Integration Test]:::stage H --> I[Validation vs Problem Statement]:::stage I --> J{Demo Working?}:::decision J -->|Yes| K[Documentation and Report]:::stage J -->|No| L[Debug Hardware or Software]:::stage L --> G K --> M([Final Viva Demo]):::stage classDef stage fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Note:** The diamond shape is the **Debug Loop**. In industry, this is called a "feedback iteration." In academic microprojects, KTU evaluators give **extra marks for the number of times you debugged and re-tested**, not just for a working demo on Day 1. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Typical Microcontroller-LED Microproject ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as Programmer participant IDE as Keil uVision participant HEX as HEX File participant MCU as 8051 Chip participant LED as External LED P->>IDE: Write Embedded C code IDE->>IDE: Compile and link IDE->>HEX: Generate .hex file P->>MCU: Burn via ISP programmer Note over MCU: Power-on reset
PC jumps to 0x0000 MCU->>MCU: Execute main() MCU->>MCU: Configure P1.0 as output loop Forever MCU->>LED: SETB P1.0 (HIGH) MCU->>MCU: Delay 500ms using Timer0 MCU->>LED: CLR P1.0 (LOW) MCU->>MCU: Delay 500ms using Timer0 end ``` --- ### 4.4 Mermaid Architecture: Layered View of a Microproject Software ```mermaid flowchart TB L5["Application Layer main routine"]:::app L4["Algorithm Layer delay, ADC_read, LCD_print"]:::algo L3["Driver Layer GPIO_init, UART_init, I2C_start"]:::drv L2["Hardware Abstraction Layer SFR definitions"]:::hal L1["Physical Layer 8051 silicon + peripherals"]:::phys L5 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 classDef app fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000 classDef algo fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 classDef drv fill:#ce93d8,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 classDef hal fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 classDef phys fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this layered architecture matters:** In KTU viva, when an examiner asks "What happens if you change the LCD from port P0 to port P2?", the correct answer is "I only modify the **Driver Layer (L3)** — the Algorithm (L4) and Application (L5) layers remain untouched." This demonstrates **modular design thinking** and is highly rewarded in microproject evaluation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** **What is a microproject in the context of the 8051 microcontroller course? List any two real-world examples of 8051-based microprojects.** **Model Answer:** A *microproject* in the KTU 2024 Scheme PBECT404 syllabus is a small-scale, integrative hardware-software engineering task where students design, build, program, and test a working embedded system using the 8051 microcontroller. It typically involves interfacing one or more external peripherals (sensor, actuator, display) and demonstrates the application of assembly/C programming, timer operations, interrupts, and serial communication concepts learned in the module. **Two Real-World Examples:** 1. **Digital Thermometer with LCD Display** — uses an LM35 temperature sensor (via ADC0804) and 16x2 LCD to display the room temperature in °C. 2. **Password-Protected Door Lock System** — uses a 4x4 matrix keypad for password entry, a 16x2 LCD for feedback, and a solenoid/relay to unlock the door on correct password. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** **Differentiate between a "software-only simulation" and a "hardware implementation" of an 8051 microproject. Mention one tool used for each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Software-Only Simulation | Hardware Implementation | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Running the HEX file in a virtual environment to test logic | Burning the HEX file into a physical 8051 IC and observing I/O pins | | **Realism** | Ideal (no noise, no signal integrity issues) | Real (affected by voltage drops, EMI, timing jitter) | | **Cost** | Free | Requires components, breadboard, power supply | | **Tool** | **Keil µVision** debugger OR **Proteus ISIS** simulator | **AT89C51** chip + **Flash Magic** programmer + DMM + oscilloscope | Software simulation is used during development for early debugging; hardware implementation is the final validation step before viva demonstration. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model QP, May 2024 Equivalent]** **(a)** Explain the **five phases of microproject implementation** using the 8051 microcontroller, with one example activity in each phase. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Design an 8051-based **automatic streetlight controller** microproject. Draw the block diagram, list the hardware components, write the algorithm (or detailed pseudo-code), and identify the SFRs used. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** The five phases of microproject implementation are: **Phase 1 — Problem Definition & Requirement Analysis:** Identify the real-world problem. *Example activity:* "Visit a campus parking lot at night and observe that streetlights remain ON during daytime → formulate the problem: 'Design a system that switches streetlights ON at dusk and OFF at dawn using ambient light sensing.'" **Phase 2 — System Design (Hardware + Software):** *Hardware:* Select 8051 (AT89C51), LDR sensor with voltage divider, relay driver (ULN2003 + 5V relay), bulb load, 16x2 LCD for status display, 5V regulated supply. Draw schematic using KiCad. *Software:* Draw flowchart — start → init LCD → read ADC → compare threshold → control relay → loop. **Phase 3 — Implementation:** Write Embedded C in Keil. Sample code skeleton: ``` #include sbit RELAY = P1^0; sbit LDR = P1^1; void main() { P1 = 0x00; // Configure port TMOD = 0x01; // Timer 0, Mode 1 while(1) { if (LDR == 1) // Dark detected RELAY = 0; // Relay ON else RELAY = 1; // Relay OFF } } ``` Burn the HEX file using Flash Magic. **Phase 4 — Testing & Debugging:** - *Unit test:* Verify LDR sensor output on DMM (0V dark, 5V light). - *Integration test:* Check relay clicks when LDR is covered. - *Validation:* Cover LDR → bulb should glow within 100 ms. **Phase 5 — Documentation & Presentation:** Prepare a 20-page report with: Title, Abstract (½ page), Introduction (1 page), Block Diagram, Circuit Diagram, Source Code, Testing Results (with photographs), Conclusion, References. Conduct a live demo for the evaluator. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Block Diagram of Automatic Streetlight Controller:** ```mermaid flowchart LR LDR["LDR + Resistor Voltage Divider"]:::in ADC["ADC0804 8-bit"]:::adc MCU["8051 Microcontroller"]:::mcu LCD["16x2 LCD Display"]:::out DRV["ULN2003 + 5V Relay"]:::drv BULB["230V AC Streetlight Bulb"]:::load PSU["5V Regulated Power Supply 7805"]:::psu LDR --> ADC ADC --> MCU MCU --> LCD MCU --> DRV DRV --> BULB PSU --> MCU PSU --> ADC PSU --> LCD PSU --> DRV classDef in fill:#add8e6,stroke:#4682b4,color:#000 classDef adc fill:#fffacd,stroke:#daa520,color:#000 classDef mcu fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef out fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#c71585,color:#000 classDef drv fill:#ffa07a,stroke:#cd5c5c,color:#000 classDef load fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000 classDef psu fill:#e6e6fa,stroke:#6a5acd,color:#000 ``` **Hardware Components List:** 1. AT89C51 microcontroller (×1) 2. LDR (Light Dependent Resistor, GL5516) + 10 kΩ resistor (voltage divider) 3. ADC0804 (8-bit parallel ADC) 4. 16x2 LCD (HD44780-based, JHD162A) 5. ULN2003 driver IC (×1) + 5V SPDT relay (×1) 6. 230V AC bulb with holder (the load) 7. 11.0592 MHz crystal + 2 × 33 pF capacitors 8. 10 µF electrolytic + 10 kΩ resistor + push-button (reset circuit) 9. 7805 voltage regulator + 9V DC adapter 10. 10 µF + 0.1 µF decoupling capacitors **Algorithm (Pseudo-code):** ``` BEGIN PROGRAM INITIALIZE LCD to 2-line, 5x7 mode INITIALIZE ADC0804 (CS = 0, RD = 1, WR = 1) DISPLAY "STREETLIGHT CTRL" on LCD first line FOREVER LOOP: START conversion: CLR P3.1 (WR pin) // give pulse WAIT until INTR (P3.0) goes LOW READ digital value: P1 = (P1 & 0x00) | (P1 high byte) // read P1 IF value < THRESHOLD (e.g., 100) THEN SET RELAY = 0 // Relay ON, bulb glows DISPLAY "LIGHT: ON " ELSE SET RELAY = 1 // Relay OFF DISPLAY "LIGHT: OFF" ENDIF DELAY 500 ms END FOREVER END PROGRAM ``` **Special Function Registers (SFRs) Used:** - **P1** — parallel data port for reading ADC and driving relay - **P3.0 (INT0)** — connected to ADC0804's INTR line - **P3.1 (TXD pin reused)** — connected to ADC0804's WR line - **P0** — connected to LCD data lines (D0–D7) - **P2.0, P2.1, P2.2** — LCD control (RS, RW, EN) - **TMOD, TH0, TL0, TCON** — for delay generation --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model QP, Dec 2023 Equivalent]** **(a)** Describe the **hardware and software design methodology** for a microproject, with a flow diagram showing the iterative debug loop. Explain why testing and documentation are critical phases. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A microproject uses an 8051 with an **11.0592 MHz crystal** to **transmit the character 'A' (ASCII 0x41)** over UART at **9600 baud** to a PC. Write the complete assembly language program, calculate TH1, and explain each instruction's purpose. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Hardware and Software Design Methodology:** The design methodology for a microproject is split into two parallel tracks that converge at the **integration** phase. **Hardware Design Track:** - Requirement study → Component selection (8051 variant, sensors, actuators) - Schematic capture (KiCad / Proteus) → Power budget calculation - Breadboard prototype → PCB fabrication (if applicable) - Continuity test with DMM → Power-on test **Software Design Track:** - Problem decomposition → Algorithm design (step-by-step logic) - Flowchart drawing (ISO 5807 symbols) → Pseudo-code writing - Choice of language (Keil C for portability, Assembly for timing precision) - Modular coding (functions for each sub-task) - Compile and fix syntax errors → Simulate in Proteus **Iterative Debug Loop (Flow Diagram):** ```mermaid flowchart TB S([Start]):::start --> W[Write Code Module]:::step W --> C[Compile in Keil]:::step C --> E{Build OK?}:::decision E -->|No| X[Fix Syntax or Linker Errors]:::fix X --> W E -->|Yes| L[Load HEX in Proteus]:::step L --> T[Run Simulation, Observe Outputs]:::step T --> V{Behavior Correct?}:::decision V -->|No| D[Debug with Breakpoints Logic Analyzer]:::fix D --> W V -->|Yes| H[Burn HEX to Hardware]:::step H --> R{Real HW OK?}:::decision R -->|No| P[Debug HW Continuity Driver Timing]:::fix P --> W R -->|Yes| Z([Module Complete]):::start classDef start fill:#90ee90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef step fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef fix fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 ``` **Why Testing is Critical:** - *Verification* ensures the code does what the programmer *intended*. - *Validation* ensures the code does what the *customer/stakeholder* expected. - Without testing, the system may work in simulation but fail on real hardware due to signal integrity, contact bounce, or power supply noise. - KTU evaluators typically **deduct 3–5 marks** if a student cannot demonstrate debugging skills. **Why Documentation is Critical:** - A microproject is a *team deliverable*; documentation enables handover. - Documents serve as a legal record (in industry) of the design decisions and trade-offs. - Documentation = marks in university evaluation. A working demo without a report can yield 70% marks; with a complete report, students often achieve 90%+. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Calculate TH1 (already derived in Section 3.2):** $$\boxed{TH1 = 0xFD = 253}$$ **Step 2 — Complete Assembly Program:** ```assembly ;============================================================== ; Program: UART Transmission of 'A' (0x41) at 9600 baud ; Crystal: 11.0592 MHz ; Controller: 8051 ;============================================================== ORG 0000H ; Reset vector LJMP MAIN ; Jump to main program ORG 0030H ; Main program starts here MAIN: MOV TMOD, #20H ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) MOV TH1, #0FDH ; Reload value for 9600 baud MOV SCON, #50H ; Mode 1 (8-bit UART), REN enabled SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 TRANSMIT: MOV SBUF, #'A' ; Load ASCII 'A' (0x41) into serial buffer WAIT: JNB TI, WAIT ; Wait until TI flag is set (transmission complete) CLR TI ; Clear TI for next transmission SJMP TRANSMIT ; Loop and transmit 'A' continuously END ; End of assembly source ``` **Step 3 — Instruction-by-Instruction Explanation:** | Instruction | Purpose | |---|---| | `ORG 0000H` | Sets the program origin to reset vector address | | `LJMP MAIN` | Long jump to skip interrupt vector area | | `ORG 0030H` | Places main code safely above vector space | | `MOV TMOD, #20H` | `0010 0000` — Timer 1 in **Mode 2** (8-bit auto-reload), Timer 0 untouched | | `MOV TH1, #0FDH` | Reload value = 253 = 0xFD (derived for 9600 baud) | | `MOV SCON, #50H` | `0101 0000` — **Mode 1** (8-bit UART), receiver enabled (REN=1) | | `SETB TR1` | Start Timer 1 clock; baud rate generator is now active | | `MOV SBUF, #'A'` | Write 0x41 to the serial buffer; transmission begins automatically | | `JNB TI, WAIT` | TI is set by hardware when the last bit has shifted out; loop until then | | `CLR TI` | Software must clear TI (unlike RI, TI is NOT cleared by hardware) | | `SJMP TRANSMIT` | Infinite loop — keeps sending 'A' | **Step 4 — Verification on PC (Hyperterminal / PuTTY / Tera Term):** - Open COM port at 9600 baud, 8-N-1. - Observing character 'A' continuously on screen confirms successful implementation. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 5–10 Marks in KTU Microproject Evaluation:** > > 1. **Forgetting to set SMOD bit context** in baud rate derivations. If the question says "SMOD = 1", students often write the same answer as SMOD = 0, losing 1–2 marks. > 2. **Using f_osc = 12 MHz** in UART problems and getting non-integer TH1 values. The examiner expects you to *flag* this as a design flaw and suggest 11.0592 MHz instead. > 3. **Confusing TH0 and TL0 assignments** in 16-bit timer mode. Always write `TH0 = HIGH`, `TL0 = LOW`. Mixing them gives garbage delays. > 4. **Not drawing a reset circuit** in schematics. KTU evaluators frequently deduct 1 mark for missing the 10 µF + 10 kΩ + push-button reset network. > 5. **Writing code without comments** in viva demonstration. The examiner *cannot* verify your understanding if the code is undocumented. > 6. **Forgetting power supply decoupling capacitors (0.1 µF)** near each IC. This is a hardware-engineering expectation, not an option. > 7. **Not specifying the baud rate format (8-N-1, 8-E-1, etc.)** in serial communication problems. Always explicitly state **8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit**. > 8. **Hard-coding all logic** in `main()`. A modular microproject with separate functions (e.g., `lcd_init()`, `adc_read()`) is rewarded; a monolithic code is penalized. > 9. **Failing to mention the watchdog/reset scenario** in the report. Examiners love seeing "What happens if the program hangs?" addressed. > 10. **Using P0 directly without external pull-ups** for output — P0 is open-drain and needs 10 kΩ pull-ups. This is a classic hardware trap. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Summary — Print This Before Exam** - [ ] **Microproject = 5-phase lifecycle** — Define → Design → Implement → Test → Document. - [ ] **8051 default crystal = 11.0592 MHz** (for integer UART baud rates). - [ ] **Machine cycle = 12 / f_osc** — memorize this first. - [ ] **Timer Mode 1** = 16-bit, manual reload, used for long delays. - [ ] **Timer Mode 2** = 8-bit auto-reload, used for **baud rate generation** (UART). - [ ] **TH1 = 0xFD** = the magic value for **9600 baud, 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0**. - [ ] **P0 needs external pull-ups** (4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ); P1, P2, P3 have internal pull-ups. - [ ] **8051 cannot drive high-current loads** (motors, relays) directly — use ULN2003 or L293D. - [ ] **ADC0804** is the most common 8-bit parallel ADC in academic 8051 projects. - [ ] **LCD pins:** RS, RW, E (control); D0–D7 (data) — initialize with `0x38`, `0x0C`, `0x06`, `0x01` commands. - [ ] **Keypad scanning** uses the technique: drive all rows LOW, then read columns to detect key press. - [ ] **UART transmission** flag TI must be **manually cleared** by software; **RI** too. - [ ] **Interrupt vector addresses** — Reset: 0000H, INT0: 0003H, T0: 000BH, INT1: 0013H, T1: 001BH, Serial: 0023H. - [ ] **Power calculation** for project: $P = 5\text{V} \times I_{total}$; check 7805 thermal budget. - [ ] **Standard baud rate formula:** $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{osc}}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}$. - [ ] **KTU viva evaluation** is based on: Working Demo (40%) + Report (30%) + Viva Q&A (20%) + Innovation (10%). - [ ] **Documentation is non-negotiable** — Include: Abstract, Block Diagram, Circuit Diagram, Source Code (commented), Test Results, Conclusion, References. - [ ] **The 8051 stack is in internal RAM** (08H to 1FH by default for registers; 30H–7FH for general use). SP initializes to 07H on reset. - [ ] **Modular programming** always scores higher than monolithic code in KTU microproject evaluation. - [ ] **Proteus simulation** can save hours of hardware debugging — always simulate first, then burn to chip.

topics.title: Example Projects (ADC/LCD Interface, DAC/Sine Wave Generation, DC Motor Control, Stepper Motor Control, Temperature Sensor/LCD Display)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBECT404 - MICROCONTROLLERS & EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Example Projects (ADC/LCD Interface, DAC/Sine Wave Generation, DC Motor Control, Stepper Motor Control, Temperature Sensor/LCD Display)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — Example Projects in Embedded Systems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded System Project**: A purposeful combination of a microcontroller (MCU), dedicated peripheral hardware (sensors/actuators), and firmware designed to perform a single, well-defined real-world task under real-time constraints. The five canonical example projects under Module 4 demonstrate the **sensor → MCU → actuator** paradigm that forms the bedrock of industrial embedded design.

The five example projects mandated under KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 of PBECT404 are:

| # | Project | Sensor/Input Stage | Actuator/Output Stage |
|---|---------|--------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **ADC / LCD Interface** | Analog voltage (potentiometer / transducer) | 16×2 Character LCD |
| 2 | **DAC / Sine Wave Generation** | Digital look-up table in ROM | DAC0808 / R-2R Network |
| 3 | **DC Motor Control** | Push-buttons / PWM command | L293D H-Bridge + DC Motor |
| 4 | **Stepper Motor Control** | Direction & step-rate logic | ULN2003 / L297 + Stepper Motor |
| 5 | **Temperature Sensor / LCD Display** | LM35 (10 mV/°C) transducer | 16×2 Character LCD |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Workshop" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition**: Imagine the microcontroller as the **head chef** of a smart kitchen. Each project is one specialised recipe.
> - **ADC/LCD** is like a *digital weighing scale* — it converts the "weight" (analog voltage) into a number you can read on a screen.
> - **DAC/Sine Wave** is the *musical synthesizer* — the chef writes down every note (lookup table) and the DAC plays the melody as a smooth waveform.
> - **DC Motor Control** is the *fan speed regulator* — the chef pulses power to slow or speed up the fan using PWM.
> - **Stepper Motor Control** is the *clock hand mechanism* — the chef moves a pointer in precise, countable steps.
> - **Temperature Sensor/LCD** is the *digital thermometer* — the chef measures heat and announces the number on the display.

### 1.3 Why These Five Projects?

These projects cover the **three fundamental signal-flow classes** in any embedded product:

1. **Analog-In / Human-Out** → ADC + LCD (Project 1 & 5)
2. **Digital-In / Analog-Out** → DAC + Waveform (Project 2)
3. **Digital-In / Power-Out** → Motor control (Projects 3 & 4)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Every KTU exam question from this module tests the student's ability to *trace a signal* from sensor → ADC/DAC → MCU → actuator, identify the **register-level configuration**, and **calculate the resulting physical quantity** (voltage, temperature, RPM, step angle, or waveform frequency).

---

### 1.4 Standard Reference Conventions Used in This Module

- **V<sub>ref</sub> = 5 V** (default ADC/DAC reference for 8051-based KTU labs)
- **ADC Resolution = 8 bits (ADC0804)** or **10 bits (8051 on-chip ADC)**
- **DAC Resolution = 8 bits (DAC0808)**
- **LM35 Sensitivity = 10 mV/°C**
- **LCD = HD44780-compatible 16×2 character display**
- **Stepper Motor = 1.8° per step (200 steps/rev) — 4-phase unipolar**

> [!NOTE]
> All numeric constants in the formulae below follow these **KTU lab-standard values** unless explicitly stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Project 1 — ADC0804 with 8051 and 16×2 LCD

### 2.1.1 Operational Theory

The **ADC0804** is an 8-bit, successive-approximation analog-to-digital converter. The microcontroller sends a low-to-high pulse on the **WR** pin to start a conversion, then polls (or is interrupted by) the **INTR** pin to know when the digital byte is ready on ports DB0–DB7.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**
1. Apply analog input V<sub>in</sub> between V<sub>in</sub>(+) and V<sub>in</sub>(–) of ADC0804.
2. Pull **CS** low and pulse **WR** low→high → conversion starts.
3. ADC0804 performs successive approximation over **8 clock cycles**.
4. **INTR** goes HIGH when conversion completes.
5. MCU reads DB0–DB7 by pulsing **RD** low.
6. MCU converts the digital byte to ASCII and writes it to the LCD.

### 2.1.2 Interface Pins of ADC0804

| Pin | Function | Connection to 8051 |
|-----|----------|--------------------|
| CS | Chip Select (active LOW) | GND (permanently enabled) |
| RD | Read (active LOW) | P3.7 |
| WR | Write/Start (active LOW) | P3.6 |
| INTR | Interrupt (HIGH when done) | P3.5 (polled) |
| DB0–DB7 | Digital output bus | P1 (input to MCU) |
| CLK R, CLK IN | Internal/External clock | RC network (typical 606 kHz) |
| V<sub>ref</sub>/2 | Reference mid-point | Decoupled capacitor (≈ 2.5 V for 5 V span) |
| V<sub>in</sub>+, V<sub>in</sub>– | Differential analog input | Potentiometer wiper |

### 2.1.3 LCD (HD44780) Pin Map

| LCD Pin | Symbol | Function |
|---------|--------|----------|
| 1 | VSS | Ground |
| 2 | VDD | +5 V |
| 3 | VEE | Contrast voltage (pot wiper) |
| 4 | RS | Register Select (0=Command, 1=Data) |
| 5 | R/W | Read/Write (0=Write for MCU) |
| 6 | E | Enable (HIGH→LOW latches data) |
| 7–14 | D0–D7 | 8-bit data bus |
| 15 | A | Backlight anode |
| 16 | K | Backlight cathode |

## 2.2 Project 2 — DAC0808 / R-2R Ladder for Sine Wave Generation

### 2.2.1 Operational Theory

The **DAC0808** is an 8-bit multiplying DAC with current outputs. A summing amplifier (typically LF351 or µA741) converts the I-out current into a 0–5 V analog voltage.

To generate a **sine wave**, the MCU walks a **lookup table** of equally spaced sine samples stored in ROM and updates the DAC at a fixed interval T<sub>s</sub>. The smoother the output, the higher the number of samples per cycle **N** (commonly 36, 48, 64, or 256).

### 2.2.2 Lookup Table Math

For **N** samples per cycle of a sine wave of frequency **f**:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{N \cdot f}$$

The **k-th sample value** (0-indexed) for the DAC is:
