$$D[k] = \text{round}\left[\frac{V_{ref}}{2} + \frac{V_{ref}}{2}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{N}\right)\right] \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{V_{ref}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Simplification: the **+V<sub>ref</sub>/2 offset** is added because the DAC0808 with a single-supply op-amp cannot swing below 0 V. The sine is therefore *biased* to sit between 0 V and +V<sub>ref</sub>.

## 2.3 Project 3 — DC Motor Control with PWM and L293D

### 2.3.1 Operational Theory

The **L293D** is a dual H-bridge driver that allows the MCU to drive a DC motor bidirectionally. Direction is set by two logic inputs (1A, 2A), and speed is set by the **PWM duty cycle** applied to the **enable pin (1EN, 2EN)**.

Average voltage across the motor determines the no-load speed:

$$\bar{V}_{motor} = D \cdot V_s \quad \text{where} \quad D = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

For a no-load DC motor, the speed is approximately:

$$N \approx N_0 \left(1 - \frac{I_a R_a}{V_s}\right) \text{(linearised)}$$

The **PWM frequency** must be high enough to avoid audible motor whine (typically **> 20 kHz**, often **1 kHz–10 kHz** for lab demos).

### 2.3.2 L293D Truth Table (per H-bridge)

| Input 1A | Input 2A | Motor Action |
|----------|----------|---------------|
| 0 | 0 | Stop (coast) |
| 0 | 1 | Rotate CW |
| 1 | 0 | Rotate CCW |
| 1 | 1 | Stop (brake) |

## 2.4 Project 4 — Stepper Motor Control with 8051

### 2.4.1 Operational Theory

A **unipolar 4-phase stepper motor** has four stator windings (A, B, C, D). Energising them in a fixed sequence produces a rotating magnetic field that advances the rotor by a precise step angle.

**Step angle formula:**

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N \cdot S}$$

where **N** = number of rotor teeth and **S** = number of stator phases. For a standard 1.8° motor with 200 steps/rev:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{200} = 1.8°$$

**Step rate vs. motor speed:**

$$N_{rpm} = \frac{f_{step} \cdot 60}{S_{total}}$$

where **f<sub>step</sub>** is the step pulse frequency (steps/sec) and **S<sub>total</sub>** is the number of steps per full revolution.

### 2.4.2 Excitation Sequences

| Step | A | B | C | D | Mode |
|------|---|---|---|---|------|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | Full-step, 1-phase |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | |
| 5 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | (repeats) |

For **half-stepping**, alternate 1-phase and 2-phase patterns for double the resolution (0.9° per step).

### 2.4.3 Driver IC — ULN2003A

The 8051 cannot source the 200–500 mA required by stepper coils. The **ULN2003A** is a 7-channel Darlington array that provides:
- 500 mA continuous collector current
- 50 V breakdown
- Built-in flyback diodes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 drives ULN2003 inputs (IN1–IN4) with logic levels, and the ULN2003 open-collector outputs switch the 5 V / 12 V stepper coils.

## 2.5 Project 5 — LM35 Temperature Sensor + ADC + LCD

### 2.5.1 Operational Theory

The **LM35** is a precision centigrade temperature sensor with a linear transfer function of **10 mV/°C**. It outputs **0 V at 0 °C** and **+1.5 V at 150 °C**, which lies entirely within the 0–5 V input range of the ADC0804 (or the on-chip 8051 ADC).

**Conversion chain (Temperature → ADC byte → ASCII → LCD):**

1. LM35 outputs V<sub>T</sub> = T × 10 mV/°C.
2. ADC0804 produces byte **D = (V<sub>T</sub> / 5 V) × 255**.
3. MCU recovers V<sub>T</sub> = (D / 255) × 5 V.
4. MCU computes **T = V<sub>T</sub> / 0.01 V** = V<sub>T</sub> × 100.
5. MCU splits T into tens, units, and decimal digits and sends ASCII to LCD.

### 2.5.2 Resolution of the System

The **smallest temperature step** the system can resolve is the **LSB of the ADC** converted to °C:

$$T_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} \times \frac{1}{0.01 \text{ V/°C}}$$

For 8-bit ADC with V<sub>ref</sub> = 5 V:

$$T_{LSB} = \frac{5}{255} \times 100 \approx 1.96 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For 10-bit on-chip ADC of 8051 derivative: T<sub>LSB</sub> ≈ 0.49 °C.

---

## 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}(2^n - 1)$ | ADC digital output | dimensionless (counts) |
| 2 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref}$ | Reconstructed analog | Volts |
| 3 | $V_T = 0.01 \cdot T$ | LM35 transfer function | V & °C |
| 4 | $T = 100 \cdot V_T$ | LM35 inverse function | °C & V |
| 5 | $T_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{(2^n - 1)} \cdot 100$ | Temperature resolution | °C |
| 6 | $f_{out} = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot T_s}$ | Sine-wave output frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{N \cdot f_{out}}$ | DAC update period | seconds |
| 8 | $\bar{V}_{motor} = D \cdot V_s$ | PWM average voltage | Volts |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$ | PWM duty cycle | fraction (0–1) |
| 10 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N \cdot S}$ | Stepper step angle | degrees |
| 11 | $N_{rpm} = \dfrac{f_{step} \cdot 60}{S_{total}}$ | Stepper motor speed | RPM |
| 12 | $N_{steps} = \dfrac{\theta_{desired}}{\theta_s}$ | Number of steps for a rotation | steps |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall**: When using a 10-bit ADC value, students often forget to use **1023** (i.e., 2<sup>10</sup>−1) instead of 255. Always confirm the bit-resolution of the specific ADC before writing the formula in the exam.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Digital Output for a Given V<sub>in</sub>

**Problem:** An ADC0804 (8-bit, V<sub>ref</sub> = 5 V) reads an analog input of 2.5 V. Find the digital output byte **D**.

### Step 1 — Identify parameters

The ADC0804 has **n = 8 bits**, so the number of discrete levels is:

$$L = 2^n - 1 = 2^8 - 1 = 255$$

### Step 2 — Apply the ADC equation

$$D = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute values

$$D = \frac{2.5 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ V}} \times 255$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

$$D = 0.5 \times 255 = 127.5$$

### Step 5 — Round to nearest integer (ADC produces whole counts)

$$D = 128 \quad (\text{binary } 1000\,0000)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The fractional remainder is truncated by the successive-approximation register (SAR) of the ADC. This introduces the **quantisation error** of ±½ LSB = ±9.8 mV in this case.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Resolution with 8-bit ADC + LM35

**Problem:** Find the smallest temperature change that the system can detect.

### Step 1 — Voltage per ADC count (LSB voltage)

$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1} = \frac{5}{255} = 0.01961 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2 — LM35 conversion gain

The LM35 produces 10 mV per 1 °C, so 1 V<sub>LSB</sub> corresponds to:

$$T_{LSB} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{0.01 \text{ V/°C}} = \frac{0.01961}{0.01}$$

### Step 3 — Compute

$$T_{LSB} = 1.961 \text{ °C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: With an 8-bit ADC the system quantises temperature in jumps of **≈ 1.96 °C**. If the application requires finer resolution, switch to the **10-bit on-chip ADC** of the 8051 derivative, giving **T<sub>LSB</sub> ≈ 0.49 °C**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sine Wave DAC Sample Values for 36-Point Lookup

**Problem:** Generate a 50 Hz sine wave using DAC0808 (V<sub>ref</sub> = 5 V, n = 8) and **N = 36 samples per cycle**. Compute the DAC byte for **k = 0, 3, 6, 9**.

### Step 1 — Output frequency check

$$f_{out} = \frac{1}{N \cdot T_s} \implies T_s = \frac{1}{36 \times 50} = 555.6 \text{ μs}$$

The 8051 must therefore update the DAC every **555.6 μs** (achievable with a 12 MHz crystal using 1-cycle MOVX).

### Step 2 — Sample formula

$$D[k] = \text{round}\left[127.5 + 127.5 \cdot \sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi k}{36}\right)\right]$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate k = 0

$$\sin(0) = 0 \implies D[0] = 127.5 + 0 = 127.5 \approx 128$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate k = 3

$$\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \times 3}{36}\right) = \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = 0.5$$

$$D[3] = 127.5 + 127.5 \times 0.5 = 127.5 + 63.75 = 191.25 \approx 191$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate k = 6

$$\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \times 6}{36}\right) = \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

$$D[6] = 127.5 + 127.5 \times 0.866 = 127.5 + 110.4 = 237.9 \approx 238$$

### Step 6 — Evaluate k = 9

$$\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi \times 9}{36}\right) = \sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 1$$

$$D[9] = 127.5 + 127.5 \times 1 = 255$$

> [!NOTE]
> This confirms the **peak (255)** at the quarter-cycle and the **mid (128)** at the zero crossing — exactly the expected biased-sine output of an 8-bit unipolar DAC.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — DC Motor PWM Speed Calculation

**Problem:** A 12 V DC motor is driven by a 1 kHz PWM signal with a 60 % duty cycle. Find the **average applied voltage** and the **approximate no-load speed** if the motor's no-load speed at 12 V is **N<sub>0</sub> = 6000 RPM**.

### Step 1 — Compute the average voltage

$$\bar{V} = D \cdot V_s = 0.60 \times 12 \text{ V} = 7.2 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the linearised speed equation

$$N = N_0 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{I_a R_a}{V_s}\right)$$

Assuming **no load**, I<sub>a</sub> ≈ 0, so:

$$N \approx N_0 \cdot \left(\frac{\bar{V}}{V_s}\right) = 6000 \times \frac{7.2}{12}$$

### Step 3 — Compute

$$N = 6000 \times 0.6 = 3600 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under load, the **back-EMF** (E<sub>b</sub> = K·ω) drops, and the actual speed is given by the full KVL: **V = I<sub>a</sub>R<sub>a</sub> + E<sub>b</sub>**. The linear approximation used here is valid only for **no-load / low-torque** lab demos — KTU typically tests this case.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Stepper Motor Steps for a Given Rotation

**Problem:** A 1.8° stepper motor (200 steps/rev) is commanded to rotate the shaft by **90°** in the **clockwise** direction. Determine the **number of steps** and write the **excitation pattern sequence**.

### Step 1 — Compute the number of steps

$$N_{steps} = \frac{\theta_{desired}}{\theta_s} = \frac{90°}{1.8°} = 50 \text{ steps}$$

### Step 2 — Choose the excitation mode (full-step, single-phase, CW)

| Step | A | B | C | D |
|------|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| … repeats … | | | | |
| 49 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| 50 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

### Step 3 — Compute the time per step for a target speed of 100 RPM

$$f_{step} = \frac{N_{rpm} \cdot S_{total}}{60} = \frac{100 \times 200}{60} = 333.3 \text{ Hz}$$

$$T_{step} = \frac{1}{333.3} = 3 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 12 MHz 8051 can produce accurate 3 ms delays using a single hardware timer (Timer 0 in Mode 1).

---

## 3.6 Analytic Summary Matrix — All Five Projects

| Parameter | ADC/LCD | DAC/Sine | DC Motor | Stepper | Temp+LCD |
|-----------|---------|----------|----------|---------|----------|
| **Input** | Analog V | Digital index k | PWM duty D | Step pulse f | LM35 V<sub>T</sub> |
| **Conversion** | $D = (V/V_{ref})(2^n-1)$ | LUT[k] → 8-bit | $\bar{V} = DV_s$ | Coil sequence | $T = 100 V_T$ |
| **Output** | ASCII on LCD | Smooth sine wave | Motor rotation | Discrete steps | ASCII on LCD |

| **Speed Metric** | Samples/sec | 50 Hz / 60 Hz | RPM | Steps/sec | Updates/sec |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — ADC + LCD Project

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Source V_in] --> B[ADC0804]
    C[8051 MCU P1.0 to P1.7] --> B
    B --> D[D0 to D7 Bus]
    D --> E[8051 Port 1]
    E --> F[ASCII Converter]
    F --> G[LCD HD44780]
    H[8051 P3.0 to P3.2] --> G
    I[Clock RC 606kHz] --> B
```

**Signal-Flow Description:** The analog source charges the ADC's sample-hold capacitor on the rising edge of WR. The internal SAR performs 8 successive comparisons against the internal resistor-ladder reference. The completed 8-bit result is latched on DB0–DB7 and signalled to the MCU via the falling edge of INTR.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — DAC / Sine Wave Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[8051 ROM Lookup Table] --> B[Port P1 Output]
    B --> C[DAC0808 Digital Inputs]
    C --> D[I_out Current]
    D --> E[Summing Op-Amp LF351]
    F[V_ref + 5V] --> E
    E --> G[Sine Waveform V_out]
    H[Timer 0 Interrupt] --> A
```

**Timing Logic:** Timer 0 overflows every **555.6 μs** (for 50 Hz, N = 36). Each overflow loads the next byte from the lookup table, writes it to P1 (which feeds DAC0808), and the op-amp converts the resulting current to a 0–5 V biased sine wave.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — DC Motor PWM Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Push Buttons FWD REV STOP] --> B[8051 Port P3]
    B --> C[Firmware PWM Generator Timer1]
    C --> D[Enable Pin L293D 1EN]
    E[8051 P3.0 P3.1] --> F[L293D Inputs 1A 2A]
    F --> G[H Bridge Output]
    G --> H[DC Motor M]
    I[12V Supply] --> G
    C --> J[Duty Cycle Display on LCD]
```

**Control Logic:** The firmware sets the direction bits (1A, 2A) according to the button press, then generates a 1 kHz PWM on the 1EN pin. By varying the ON-time fraction, the average voltage across the motor is changed, smoothly varying its speed.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[8051 Port P1.0 to P1.3] --> B[ULN2003A Darlington Array]
    B --> C[Stepper Coil A]
    B --> D[Stepper Coil B]
    B --> E[Stepper Coil C]
    B --> F[Stepper Coil D]
    G[12V Supply] --> B
    H[8051 Timer0 Delay] --> A
    I[Direction Push Buttons] --> A
```

**Coil-Activation Sequence:** The firmware outputs the binary pattern `1000`, `0100`, `0010`, `0001` in succession (CW) or in reverse (CCW). A 3 ms delay between patterns gives ≈ 333 steps/sec → 100 RPM.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — LM35 Temperature + LCD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[LM35 Sensor] --> B[Anti-Alias RC Filter 1kHz]
    B --> C[ADC0804 V_in plus]
    D[V_ref 5V] --> C
    C --> E[8051 Port P1 Read]
    E --> F[Conversion Routine T = 100 x V]
    F --> G[Digit Splitter Tens Units Decimal]
    G --> H[LCD Display HD44780]
    I[8051 P3.0 P3.1 P3.2] --> H
```

**Anti-Aliasing Note:** A simple RC low-pass filter (R = 1 kΩ, C = 0.1 μF, **f<sub>c</sub> ≈ 1.6 kHz**) is placed between LM35 and the ADC to remove high-frequency noise from the analog signal — a KTU-favourite viva question.

---

## 4.6 Master Flowchart — Common Program Skeleton (All Five Projects)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On Reset] --> B[Initialize Ports LCD ADC PWM]
    B --> C{Check Input Type}
    C -->|Analog| D[Trigger ADC Conversion]
    C -->|Digital| E[Read Button or Timer Event]
    D --> F[Wait for INTR LOW]
    F --> G[Read Digital Byte]
    G --> H[Convert to Engineering Units]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Format as ASCII]
    I --> J[Write to LCD or Drive Actuator]
    J --> K[Loop Back to C]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This **event-loop** skeleton is the universal firmware template for all KTU module-4 projects. Variations only exist in the *Input Type* check, the *Engineering-Unit Conversion* routine, and the *Actuator Driver* block.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain the role of the V<sub>ref</sub>/2 pin of ADC0804. What is its typical value if V<sub>CC</sub> = 5 V?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **V<sub>ref</sub>/2** pin of the ADC0804 sets the **full-scale reference voltage** of the internal DAC ladder. The ADC's analog input span is from **0 V to 2 × (V<sub>ref</sub>/2)**, so to obtain a 0–5 V input range, V<sub>ref</sub>/2 must be **decoupled to 2.5 V** via a capacitor. If left floating, the internal default reference is V<sub>CC</sub> itself, giving a span of 0–5 V. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**List the four excitation phases of a 4-phase unipolar stepper motor in full-step single-coil mode. What is the step angle of a motor with 50 rotor teeth?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four excitation phases (single-coil, full-step) are: **1000 → 0100 → 0010 → 0001 → 1000** (cyclic). The step angle is:

$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N \cdot S} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 4} = 1.8°$$

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule (ESE 2024 Scheme)**: Part-B questions carry **14 marks each** and have an **internal choice** — answer **either** the sub-question pair (a) + (b) of Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **interfacing of an ADC0804 with the 8051 microcontroller** to read an analog voltage and display its digital equivalent on a **16×2 LCD**. List the signals used and the register-level sequence of operations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **LM35** temperature sensor feeds an **8-bit ADC0804** with V<sub>ref</sub> = 5 V. The ADC output byte is **0x66** (decimal **102**). Compute the actual temperature in **°C** that the sensor is measuring. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) 7 marks

1. **Block diagram** (2 marks): Draw MCU P1 → ADC0804 DB0–DB7, control signals WR/RD/INTR on P3.5–P3.7, and MCU P3.0–P3.2 → LCD RS/RW/E, P0 → LCD D0–D7. (Use Section 4.1 as reference.)
2. **Signal list** (2 marks): WR (P3.6, start), RD (P3.7, read), INTR (P3.5, status), and the eight data lines P1.0–P1.7.
3. **Register-level sequence** (3 marks):
   - Set CS = 0, RD = 1.
   - Pulse WR low then high → start conversion.
   - Poll P3.5 (INTR) until it goes LOW.
   - Pulse RD low → latch digital byte on P1.
   - Convert byte to ASCII (divide by 10, mod 10).
   - Send command `0x80` (set DDRAM address) to LCD.
   - Write each ASCII character to LCD data register.

#### Model Solution — (b) 7 marks

**Step 1** — Reconstruct the analog voltage from the digital byte D = 102 (1 mark):

$$V_{in} = \frac{D}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref} = \frac{102}{255} \times 5 = 2.0 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2** — Apply the LM35 transfer function (1 mark):

$$T = 100 \cdot V_{in} = 100 \times 2.0 = 200 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3** — However, the LM35 only ranges 0–150 °C → output voltage should be 0–1.5 V (1 mark). Reading **2.0 V indicates the sensor is saturated, beyond its specified range, or faulty** (1 mark).

**Step 4** — Conclusion (3 marks): The displayed temperature of 200 °C is **physically impossible for an LM35** under normal conditions; either the wiring is reversed, V<sub>ref</sub> is incorrect, or the sensor is damaged. In a working system, the LM35 reading of 2.0 V would correspond to **T = 200 °C only if the sensor is a different model** (e.g., LM50 with 10 mV/°C offset). **(Total 7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a **lookup-table-based algorithm**, explain how the 8051 can generate a **1 kHz sine wave** using a **DAC0808**. Assume a 36-sample table and V<sub>ref</sub> = 5 V. Show the calculation of any three sample values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **1.8° stepper motor** is driven by an 8051 through a **ULN2003 driver**. The shaft must rotate **CW by 270° at 60 RPM**. Calculate the number of steps, the step pulse frequency, and the timer delay required between successive steps. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) 7 marks

1. **Concept of LUT generation** (2 marks): A 36-byte table is pre-computed and stored in ROM. Each byte represents the digital equivalent of a sine sample. The 8051 cycles through the table in a Timer-0 ISR.

2. **Sample calculation** (3 marks) — using $D[k] = 127.5 + 127.5 \cdot \sin(2\pi k/36)$:
   - k = 0: D = **128**
   - k = 9: D = **255** (peak)
   - k = 18: D = **128** (zero crossing)
   - k = 27: D = **0** (negative peak, biased)

3. **Timer configuration** (2 marks): For 1 kHz output, the update period is:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{36 \times 1000} = 27.78 \text{ μs}$$

Timer 0 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) is loaded with the value that gives a 27.78 μs overflow at 12 MHz clock (machine cycle = 1 μs, so reload value = 256 − 28 = **228**).

#### Model Solution — (b) 7 marks

**Step 1** — Number of steps (2 marks):

$$N_{steps} = \frac{270°}{1.8°} = 150 \text{ steps}$$

**Step 2** — Step pulse frequency for 60 RPM (2 marks):

$$f_{step} = \frac{60 \times 200}{60} = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 3** — Timer delay between steps (3 marks):

$$T_{step} = \frac{1}{200} = 5 \text{ ms}$$

With a 12 MHz crystal (1 μs machine cycle), Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit) needs 5000 machine cycles delay. The timer is loaded with:

$$\text{TH0 TL0} = 65536 - 5000 = 60536 = 0xEC78$$

Total: **7 marks**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the (2<sup>n</sup>−1) denominator**: Many students write 256 instead of 255. This costs **1 mark per occurrence**.
> 2. **Confusing V<sub>ref</sub>/2 with V<sub>ref</sub>**: ADC0804 pin 9 is the *mid-point reference*, not the full reference. State the formula correctly.
> 3. **Stepper step count without rounding**: If the desired angle is not a multiple of 1.8°, KTU expects the **floor()** value of N<sub>steps</sub> with a note on residual angle.
> 4. **PWM duty cycle in % vs fraction**: The formula $\bar{V} = D \cdot V_s$ uses D as a **fraction (0–1)**, not a percentage. Converting **60 % → 0.6** before substitution is critical.
> 5. **LM35 saturation**: Students often forget the **0–150 °C valid range** of LM35. Reading > 1.5 V is unphysical — always check the upper bound.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

### 🔑 Core Equations to Memorise

- ADC: $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}}(2^n - 1)$, inverse $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref}$
- LM35: $T (°C) = 100 \times V_{in}$ (Volts)
- Sine LUT: $D[k] = 127.5 + 127.5 \cdot \sin(2\pi k/N)$ for 8-bit unipolar DAC
- PWM: $\bar{V} = D \cdot V_s$, $D = T_{on} / T_{total}$
- Stepper: $\theta_s = 360° / (N \cdot S)$, $N_{rpm} = f_{step} \cdot 60 / S_{total}$

### 🔌 Standard Pin-Map Memory Aids

- **ADC0804** control pins: **CS, RD, WR, INTR** → connected to **P3.4–P3.7**
- **DAC0808** input → directly to **P1** (8-bit data bus)
- **LCD** minimal pins: **RS, RW, E** on **P3.0, P3.1, P3.2**
- **L293D** inputs: **1A, 2A** for direction; **1EN, 2EN** for PWM speed
- **ULN2003** inputs: **IN1–IN4** to **P1.0–P1.3** for stepper coils A–D

### 📊 Numerical Constants

- **ADC0804** = 8 bits → 255 levels → LSB = **19.61 mV** (V<sub>ref</sub> = 5 V)
- **DAC0808** = 8 bits → output current **0–1.99 mA** (full scale)
- **LM35** sensitivity = **10 mV/°C** (0–150 °C range)
- **L293D** current per channel = **600 mA** continuous, **1.2 A** peak
- **ULN2003** current per channel = **500 mA**, V<sub>CEO</sub> = **50 V**
- **Standard stepper** = 1.8° → **200 steps/rev**
- **8051 crystal** = 11.0592 MHz (UART) or **12 MHz** (general lab)

### ⚠️ Common Viva Questions

1. Why do we add **V<sub>ref</sub>/2** offset in sine wave DAC? *(Answer: Single-supply op-amp cannot output negative voltages; bias the sine to lie in 0–V<sub>ref</sub>).*
2. Why use **ULN2003** between 8051 and stepper? *(Answer: 8051 GPIO can sink only ~10 mA, stepper coils need 200–500 mA).*
3. Why is **anti-aliasing filter** needed before ADC? *(Answer: To remove frequency components above the Nyquist limit).*
4. What is the **maximum frequency** of a 1.8° stepper driven at 200 steps/sec? *(Answer: 200/200 rev/sec × 60 = 60 RPM; step rate of 200 Hz).*
5. What is the role of **flyback diodes** inside L293D? *(Answer: To protect against back-EMF when motor is switched off).*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU 2024)**: In Part-B (14-mark) questions, always **start with a block diagram (2 marks)**, then **list the signals used (1–2 marks)**, then present the **register-level / firmware flow (3–4 marks)**, and finally **solve a numerical example using the formulae above (3–4 marks)**. This four-part structure has consistently yielded full marks in KTU 2022–2024 evaluated papers.
